Ps.139.1: O LORD, thou hast searched me, and known me. Divine omniscience opens the most personal psalm.
1Pet.3.12: The eyes of the Lord are over the righteous, and His ears are open unto their prayers. Cites Ps 34:15.
Isa.45.24: In the LORD have I righteousness and strength: even to Him shall men come. Pre-figuring justification by faith.
1John.5.19: We know that we are of God, and the whole world lieth in wickedness (en tō ponērō). Two spheres — believer's identity vs. cosmic darkness.
Hos.1.10: It shall come to pass, that in the place where it was said unto them, Ye are not my people, there it shall be said unto them, Ye are the sons of the living God. Cited Rom 9:26 — Gentile inclusion.
1Cor.3.9: Ye are God's husbandry, ye are God's building. Two metaphors for the church.
1John.2.6: He that saith he abideth in Him ought himself also so to walk, even as He walked. The Christian life conformed to Christ's pattern.
1John.2.16: All that is in the world, the lust of the flesh, and the lust of the eyes, and the pride of life, is not of the Father. The world's three primary lusts.
Eph.1.19: What is the exceeding greatness of His power to us-ward who believe. Hyperbolic divine power available to believers.
1John.4.18: Perfect love casteth out fear. Mature love eliminates the fear of judgment.
Jer.10.23: O LORD, I know that the way of man is not in himself: it is not in man that walketh to direct his steps. Human autonomy is illusory.
Jas.1.14: Every man is tempted, when he is drawn away of his own lust, and enticed. Internal lust as the engine of temptation.
Deut.8.2: Thou shalt remember all the way which the LORD thy God led thee these forty years... to humble thee, and to prove thee. Wilderness as covenantal pedagogy.
Hos.2.23: I will say to them which were not my people, Thou art my people. Cited Rom 9:25. Gentile gathering.
Matt.13.44: The kingdom of heaven is like unto treasure hid in a field; the which when a man hath found, he hideth, and for joy thereof goeth and selleth all that he hath. The kingdom's incomparable value.
Gen.1.3: And God said, Let there be light: and there was light. Divine speech as creative power. Cited 2 Cor 4:6 of regeneration.
Isa.42.16: I will bring the blind by a way that they knew not... I will make darkness light before them. Divine guidance for the spiritually blind.
Rom.11.36: Of Him, and through Him, and to Him, are all things: to whom be glory for ever. Doxological compression of divine sovereignty.
Rev.19.1: Alleluia; Salvation, and glory, and honour, and power, unto the Lord our God. Heaven's eschatological doxology.
1Pet.4.12: Think it not strange concerning the fiery trial which is to try you, as though some strange thing happened. Suffering is normative for Christians.
Rev.17.14: The Lamb shall overcome them: for He is Lord of lords, and King of kings. Eschatological victory of Christ.
Ps.119.97: O how love I thy law! it is my meditation all the day. Affection for Scripture, sustained meditation.
Isa.32.15: Until the Spirit be poured upon us from on high. Eschatological Spirit-effusion. Cited at Pentecost typologically.
1John.5.3: This is the love of God, that we keep His commandments: and His commandments are not grievous (bareiai). Love defined by obedience; commandments characterized as not heavy.
Ps.118.22: The stone which the builders refused is become the head stone of the corner. Cited five times in NT of Christ.
Rev.1.18: I am He that liveth, and was dead; and, behold, I am alive for evermore. Christ's death-and-resurrection witnessed.
Ps.23.6: Surely goodness and mercy shall follow me all the days of my life: and I will dwell in the house of the LORD for ever. Pursuit by divine attributes; eternal dwelling.
1Cor.3.18: Let no man deceive himself. If any man among you seemeth to be wise in this world, let him become a fool, that he may be wise. Inversion of worldly wisdom.
Isa.11.1: There shall come forth a rod out of the stem of Jesse, and a Branch shall grow out of his roots. Davidic-Messianic prophecy.
Matt.13.14: By hearing ye shall hear, and shall not understand. Cites Isa 6:9. Judicial hardening.
1Pet.2.6: Behold, I lay in Sion a chief corner stone, elect, precious. Cites Isa 28:16 of Christ.
2Cor.6.17: Wherefore come out from among them, and be ye separate, saith the Lord. Cited from Isa 52:11. Holiness as separation.
Matt.7.12: Whatsoever ye would that men should do to you, do ye even so to them. The Golden Rule. Sums the Law and Prophets.
Isa.43.5: Fear not: for I am with thee: I will bring thy seed from the east, and gather thee from the west. Eschatological regathering.
Ps.91.2: I will say of the LORD, He is my refuge and my fortress: my God; in Him will I trust. Personal confession of refuge.
Isa.50.4: The Lord GOD hath given me the tongue of the learned, that I should know how to speak a word in season to him that is weary. The Servant's pastoral speech.
Ps.102.13: Thou shalt arise, and have mercy upon Zion: for the time to favour her, yea, the set time, is come. Eschatological mercy at the appointed time.
John.17.17: Sanctify them through thy truth: thy word is truth. Word-mediated sanctification.
1Pet.4.1: He that hath suffered in the flesh hath ceased from sin. Suffering as a sin-killing discipline.
Luke.21.15: I will give you a mouth and wisdom, which all your adversaries shall not be able to gainsay nor resist. Christ's promise of speech-aid in persecution.
Deut.7.6: Thou art an holy people unto the LORD thy God: the LORD thy God hath chosen thee to be a special people. Election plus holiness-by-set-apartness.
Matt.24.24: There shall arise false Christs, and false prophets, and shall shew great signs and wonders; insomuch that, if it were possible, they shall deceive the very elect. Eschatological deception attempt.
1Tim.2.5: There is one God, and one mediator between God and men, the man Christ Jesus. Singularity of mediation through the God-Man.
Luke.16.15: Ye are they which justify yourselves before men; but God knoweth your hearts: for that which is highly esteemed among men is abomination in the sight of God. Divine valuation inverts human valuation.
Gal.6.14: God forbid that I should glory, save in the cross of our Lord Jesus Christ, by whom the world is crucified unto me, and I unto the world. Cross-only glorying.
Eph.2.5: Even when we were dead in sins, hath quickened us together with Christ, (by grace ye are saved). Spiritual resurrection paralleled with Christ's.
Prov.3.7: Be not wise in thine own eyes: fear the LORD, and depart from evil. Anti-self-confidence wisdom.
2Tim.4.2: Preach the word; be instant in season, out of season. Pastoral mandate — preach regardless of cultural reception.
Phil.3.13: This one thing I do, forgetting those things which are behind, and reaching forth unto those things which are before. Singular focus, athletic forward press.
John.9.31: God heareth not sinners: but if any man be a worshipper of God, and doeth His will, him He heareth. The healed man's theological clarity.
Rom.5.20: Where sin abounded, grace did much more abound. Grace's superabundance over sin.
Matt.11.5: The blind receive their sight, and the lame walk... and the poor have the gospel preached to them. Christ's messianic credentials cited from Isaiah.
Job.23.12: Neither have I gone back from the commandment of His lips; I have esteemed the words of His mouth more than my necessary food. Word-prioritization above sustenance.
2Cor.3.3: Ye are manifestly declared to be the epistle of Christ ministered by us, written not with ink, but with the Spirit of the living God. Believers as living letters of Christ.
Ps.56.3: What time I am afraid, I will trust in thee. Faith as the answer to fear.
Matt.13.11: Unto you it is given to know the mysteries of the kingdom of heaven, but to them it is not given. Sovereign distribution of revelation.
Heb.9.15: For this cause He is the mediator of the new testament, that... they which are called might receive the promise of eternal inheritance. Christ's mediation underwriting eternal inheritance.
Rom.8.17: If children, then heirs; heirs of God, and joint-heirs with Christ; if so be that we suffer with Him, that we may be also glorified together. Inheritance via co-suffering and co-glory with Christ.
Joel.2.12: Turn ye even to me with all your heart, and with fasting, and with weeping, and with mourning. Whole-person repentance.
John.20.31: These are written, that ye might believe that Jesus is the Christ... and that believing ye might have life through His name. The Gospel of John's stated purpose.
Ps.138.7: Though I walk in the midst of trouble, thou wilt revive me. Divine revival amid trouble.
Gen.18.19: I know him, that he will command his children and his household after him, and they shall keep the way of the LORD. Election manifesting in generational obedience-passing.
Jer.31.3: I have loved thee with an everlasting love: therefore with lovingkindness have I drawn thee. Eternal love as the source of effectual drawing.
Mark.11.23: Whosoever shall say unto this mountain, Be thou removed... and shall not doubt in his heart, but shall believe... he shall have whatsoever he saith. Faith-prayer's mountain-moving capacity.
Ps.103.17: The mercy of the LORD is from everlasting to everlasting upon them that fear Him. Eternal-temporal mercy span for the God-fearing.
Luke.14.11: Whosoever exalteth himself shall be abased; and he that humbleth himself shall be exalted. Pride-humility reversal in the kingdom.
Matt.4.10: Get thee hence, Satan: for it is written, Thou shalt worship the Lord thy God, and Him only shalt thou serve. Christ's wilderness rebuke citing Deut 6:13.
Mal.1.6: A son honoureth his father, and a servant his master: if then I be a father, where is mine honour? Lawsuit indictment against Israel's priests.
2Cor.4.8: We are troubled on every side, yet not distressed; we are perplexed, but not in despair. The paradoxical resilience of cross-shaped life.
Col.2.8: Beware lest any man spoil you through philosophy and vain deceit. Anti-syncretism warning. Christ's sufficiency vs. human speculation.
1Thess.4.1: How ye ought to walk and to please God, so ye would abound more and more. Continual increase in pleasing-God.
John.4.24: God is a Spirit: and they that worship Him must worship Him in spirit and in truth. The qualitative requirements for true worship.
Mark.16.17: These signs shall follow them that believe; In my name shall they cast out devils; they shall speak with new tongues. Apostolic signs accompanying gospel preaching.
John.14.17: Even the Spirit of truth; whom the world cannot receive, because it seeth Him not, neither knoweth Him: but ye know Him; for He dwelleth with you, and shall be in you. Spirit-distinction between believers and the world.
Rev.22.12: Behold, I come quickly; and my reward is with me, to give every man according as his work shall be. Christ's imminent return with reward.
John.15.10: If ye keep my commandments, ye shall abide in my love; even as I have kept my Father's commandments, and abide in His love. Obedience-sustained love-abiding.
Ezek.33.31: They sit before thee as my people... but they hear thy words, and they do them not. Hypocritical hearing without obedience.
Rev.1.17: Fear not; I am the first and the last. The risen Christ's self-assertion in divine titles.
Deut.20.4: The LORD your God is He that goeth with you, to fight for you against your enemies. Pre-battle assurance of divine partisanship.
Eph.5.17: Be ye not unwise, but understanding what the will of the Lord is. Wise discernment of God's will as Christian responsibility.
Luke.2.11: Unto you is born this day in the city of David a Saviour, which is Christ the Lord. The angelic announcement compressing the gospel.
Deut.32.4: He is the Rock, His work is perfect: for all His ways are judgment: a God of truth and without iniquity. Divine moral perfection in poetic compression.
Prov.1.23: Turn you at my reproof: behold, I will pour out my spirit unto you, I will make known my words unto you. Wisdom's offered Spirit-effusion at repentance.
Luke.18.14: This man went down to his house justified rather than the other: for every one that exalteth himself shall be abased. The publican-Pharisee parable's verdict.
Lam.3.23: They are new every morning: great is thy faithfulness. Cited in the famous hymn. Daily-renewing mercies.
Titus.2.12: Teaching us that, denying ungodliness and worldly lusts, we should live soberly, righteously, and godly. Grace's pedagogical effect.
Phil.3.21: Who shall change our vile body, that it may be fashioned like unto His glorious body. Eschatological resurrection-glorification.
Eph.2.20: Built upon the foundation of the apostles and prophets, Jesus Christ Himself being the chief corner stone. Apostolic doctrine plus Christological keystone.
Matt.17.20: If ye have faith as a grain of mustard seed, ye shall say unto this mountain, Remove hence to yonder place; and it shall remove. Faith's quality, not quantity.
1Pet.3.22: Who is gone into heaven, and is on the right hand of God; angels and authorities and powers being made subject unto Him. Christ's exaltation over all angelic powers.
Eccl.12.14: God shall bring every work into judgment, with every secret thing. Eschatological accountability of all hidden things.
Rev.7.9: Behold, a great multitude, which no man could number, of all nations, and kindreds, and people, and tongues, stood before the throne. Eschatological congregation — universal, particular, redeemed.
Isa.26.4: Trust ye in the LORD for ever: for in the LORD JEHOVAH is everlasting strength (tsur olamim — Rock of Ages). Eternal Rock-trust.
Acts.2.42: They continued stedfastly in the apostles' doctrine and fellowship, and in breaking of bread, and in prayers. Four marks of the early church.
Eph.3.17: That Christ may dwell in your hearts by faith; that ye, being rooted and grounded in love. Heart-Christology by faith.
Isa.66.1: The heaven is my throne, and the earth is my footstool: where is the house that ye build unto me? God's transcendence over any temple.
Ps.32.1: Blessed is he whose transgression is forgiven, whose sin is covered. Cited Rom 4:7. Imputed-righteousness blessing.
Isa.52.7: How beautiful upon the mountains are the feet of him that bringeth good tidings. Cited Rom 10:15 of gospel preachers.
1Cor.8.6: To us there is but one God, the Father, of whom are all things... and one Lord Jesus Christ, by whom are all things. Compressed Christological monotheism.
Luke.23.43: To day shalt thou be with me in paradise. Christ's word to the repentant thief. Conscious post-mortem fellowship with Christ.
John.12.35: Yet a little while is the light with you. Walk while ye have the light, lest darkness come upon you. Urgency of response to Christ.
Phil.3.14: I press toward the mark for the prize of the high calling of God in Christ Jesus. Athletic forward-pressing toward eschatological prize.
Prov.30.5: Every word of God is pure: He is a shield unto them that put their trust in Him. Verbal inspiration plus protective trust.
Matt.7.11: If ye then, being evil, know how to give good gifts unto your children, how much more shall your Father which is in heaven give good things to them that ask Him? A fortiori argument from human to divine fatherhood.
John.14.12: He that believeth on me, the works that I do shall he do also; and greater works than these shall he do. Greater in scope (the gospel goes to all nations), not greater in supernatural display.
Jas.5.11: Ye have heard of the patience of Job, and have seen the end of the Lord; that the Lord is very pitiful, and of tender mercy. Job as model of patient endurance.
Ps.33.6: By the word of the LORD were the heavens made; and all the host of them by the breath of His mouth. Verbal creation-power.
Deut.20.1: When thou goest out to battle against thine enemies, and seest horses, and chariots... be not afraid of them: for the LORD thy God is with thee. Pre-battle non-fear command grounded in divine presence.
Rom.5.10: If, when we were enemies, we were reconciled to God by the death of His Son, much more, being reconciled, we shall be saved by His life. Reconciliation past; preservation by Christ's life present-future.
Rom.6.13: Yield (paristanete) yourselves unto God, as those that are alive from the dead, and your members as instruments of righteousness. Resurrection-life conditioned self-presentation.
John.15.13: Greater love hath no man than this, that a man lay down his life for his friends. Self-giving as love's apex.
Eph.2.1: You hath He quickened, who were dead in trespasses and sins. Spiritual death the pre-conversion state; quickening the divine act.
Rev.21.7: He that overcometh shall inherit all things; and I will be his God, and he shall be my son. Covenant formula plus eschatological inheritance.
Dan.9.24: Seventy weeks are determined upon thy people... to make an end of sins, and to make reconciliation for iniquity. The seventy-weeks prophecy with Messianic culmination.
Eph.5.20: Giving thanks always for all things unto God and the Father in the name of our Lord Jesus Christ. Comprehensive thanksgiving.
2Cor.3.6: The letter killeth, but the spirit giveth life. The Mosaic letter condemns; the new-covenant Spirit gives life.
Rom.12.18: If it be possible, as much as lieth in you, live peaceably with all men. Realistic peace-pursuit — qualified by 'if possible.'
Phil.2.5: Let this mind be in you, which was also in Christ Jesus. Christ-mindedness anchored to the Christ-hymn (vv. 6–11).
2Tim.2.25: In meekness instructing those that oppose themselves; if God peradventure will give them repentance. Pastoral correction with hope of God-given repentance.
Gal.3.26: Ye are all the children of God by faith in Christ Jesus. Sonship by faith, not by works.
1Tim.4.8: Bodily exercise profiteth little: but godliness is profitable unto all things, having promise of the life that now is, and of that which is to come. Godliness's two-realm dividend.
Jer.32.39: I will give them one heart, and one way, that they may fear me for ever. New-covenant unity-of-heart promise.
Rom.8.24: We are saved by hope: but hope that is seen is not hope. Eschatological hope as a salvation-component.
Isa.41.17: When the poor and needy seek water, and there is none... I the LORD will hear them, I the God of Israel will not forsake them. Divine response to the destitute.
Rev.3.7: He that is holy, He that is true, He that hath the key of David, He that openeth, and no man shutteth. Christ's sovereign mission-door management.
Ps.40.1: I waited patiently for the LORD; and He inclined unto me, and heard my cry. Cited Heb 10:5–7 of Christ.
Rom.1.21: When they knew God, they glorified Him not as God, neither were thankful. The root of human depravity: dishonor and ingratitude.
Acts.7.51: Ye stiffnecked and uncircumcised in heart and ears, ye do always resist the Holy Ghost. Stephen's indictment of Israel's Spirit-resistance.
Ps.86.5: Thou, Lord, art good, and ready to forgive; and plenteous in mercy unto all them that call upon thee. Divine readiness to forgive.
Deut.6.7: Thou shalt teach them diligently unto thy children, and shalt talk of them when thou sittest in thine house. The Shema's pedagogical extension. Constant Word-conversation in family life.
Eph.5.6: Let no man deceive you with vain words: for because of these things cometh the wrath of God upon the children of disobedience. Coming wrath as the corrective to deceptive moral relativism.
Isa.35.3: Strengthen ye the weak hands, and confirm the feeble knees. Cited Heb 12:12. Encouragement of the discouraged.
2Thess.2.9: Whose coming is after the working of Satan with all power and signs and lying wonders. The Antichrist's satanic empowerment.
Rom.6.14: Sin shall not have dominion over you: for ye are not under the law, but under grace. Grace as the regime of victory over sin.
Gen.2.7: The LORD God formed man of the dust of the ground, and breathed into his nostrils the breath of life; and man became a living soul. Divine personal involvement in human creation.
Matt.18.19: If two of you shall agree on earth as touching any thing that they shall ask, it shall be done for them. Corporate prayer's special promise.
Matt.2.6: Thou Bethlehem... art not the least among the princes of Judah: for out of thee shall come a Governor. The chief priests' citation of Mic 5:2.
Exod.20.5: I the LORD thy God am a jealous God, visiting the iniquity of the fathers upon the children. Divine jealousy and generational consequence of idolatry.
Col.2.9: In Him dwelleth all the fulness of the Godhead bodily. Christ's full deity in human flesh — the strongest possible incarnational statement.
Luke.12.15: Take heed, and beware of covetousness: for a man's life consisteth not in the abundance of the things which he possesseth. Anti-covetousness life-principle.
Phil.3.12: Not as though I had already attained, either were already perfect: but I follow after. Sanctification's incompleteness even for Paul.
Jas.3.2: In many things we offend all. If any man offend not in word, the same is a perfect man. Speech-control as the mark of maturity.
1Cor.2.7: We speak the wisdom of God in a mystery... which God ordained before the world unto our glory. Pre-temporal wisdom now revealed.
Prov.22.6: Train up a child in the way he should go: and when he is old, he will not depart from it. Pedagogical principle for parenting.
Phil.2.7: Made Himself of no reputation (heauton ekenōsen — emptied Himself), and took upon Him the form of a servant. The kenosis — voluntary self-emptying for incarnation.
Ps.46.11: The LORD of hosts is with us; the God of Jacob is our refuge. Repeated refrain of Psalm 46.
Heb.12.15: Lest any root of bitterness springing up trouble you. Cites Deut 29:18. Bitterness as a contagious moral root.
Acts.16.31: Believe on the Lord Jesus Christ, and thou shalt be saved, and thy house. Conversion's offer; household evangelism principle.
Rev.3.20: Behold, I stand at the door, and knock: if any man hear my voice, and open the door, I will come in. Christ's persistent knocking at the church's door.
Matt.5.12: Rejoice, and be exceeding glad: for great is your reward in heaven. Eschatological reward as the source of present rejoicing.
Rom.5.12: By one man sin entered into the world, and death by sin... for that all have sinned. Federal headship of Adam.
Mark.10.27: With men it is impossible, but not with God: for with God all things are possible. Salvation impossible to humans, possible to God.
John.17.24: Father, I will that they also, whom thou hast given me, be with me where I am; that they may behold my glory. Christ's high-priestly request for the elect.
John.12.26: If any man serve me, let him follow me; and where I am, there shall also my servant be. Servant-following promised eternal companionship.
Ps.37.25: I have been young, and now am old; yet have I not seen the righteous forsaken, nor his seed begging bread. Davidic observation of covenantal provision.
Jer.30.17: For I will restore health unto thee, and I will heal thee of thy wounds, saith the LORD. Eschatological healing of Israel.
1Pet.4.13: Rejoice, inasmuch as ye are partakers of Christ's sufferings; that, when His glory shall be revealed, ye may be glad also with exceeding joy. Cross-fellowship producing eschatological joy.
Ezek.34.11: Behold, I, even I, will both search my sheep, and seek them out. Yahweh's personal pastoral search after Israel's faithless shepherds fail.
Ps.130.5: I wait for the LORD, my soul doth wait, and in His word do I hope. Triple waiting-language tied to Word-rooted hope.
Prov.8.17: I love them that love me; and those that seek me early shall find me. Wisdom's promise to early-seekers.
Mark.1.1: The beginning of the gospel of Jesus Christ, the Son of God — Mark's title page. 'Gospel' (euangelion) is the imperial term for an emperor's good news; Mark seizes it for the true King. The dual title — Christ (Messianic office) and Son of God (divine identity) — frames everything that follows.
Mark.1.11: The Father's voice at Jesus' baptism fuses two OT lines: Ps 2:7 (Thou art my Son — royal coronation) and Isa 42:1 (in whom I am well pleased — Servant designation). The Trinity is on display: Father speaking, Son baptized, Spirit descending. Messiah is at once King and Suffering Servant.
Mark.1.15: The time is fulfilled (peplērōtai, perfect tense) — God's redemptive clock has struck. Two imperatives: repent (metanoeite — change your mind) and believe (pisteuete) the gospel. The kingdom has drawn near in the person of the King; the response is non-optional.
Mark.1.17: Come ye after me — the call to discipleship is personal allegiance to a person, not assent to a program. I will make you to become (genesthai) fishers of men — Christ takes responsibility for the formation. Vocation is reassigned by the call.
Mark.1.22: He taught them as one having authority (exousia), and not as the scribes. The scribes appealed to tradition; Jesus spoke from Himself. The crowd's astonishment was not at rhetorical flourish but at sourced authority — the same authority that would still storms and forgive sins.
Mark.4.9: He that hath ears to hear, let him hear — recurring formula throughout Mark. Hearing in Mark is not merely audition but spiritual perception granted by God (v. 11). The parable both reveals to insiders and conceals from outsiders, by sovereign design (v. 12).
Mark.4.20: The good ground hearers receive (paradechontai), and bring forth fruit — thirtyfold, sixtyfold, hundredfold. Genuine faith is evidenced by enduring fruit, not by initial reception (vv. 16–17). The harvest disparity is normal in the kingdom; quantity varies, but reality is uniform.
Mark.4.39: He arose, rebuked the wind, and said unto the sea, Peace, be still (siōpa, pephimōso — be silent, be muzzled). The same verbs Jesus used to silence demons (1:25). Creation obeys its Creator — a fact the disciples' question (v. 41) struggles to absorb.
Mark.4.41: What manner of man is this — the Christological question of Mark's gospel. The wind and sea obey only Yahweh in OT theology (Ps 89:9, 107:29). The disciples' fear (ephobēthēsan phobon megan) is greater after the calm than during the storm; they have glimpsed deity in the boat.
Mark.8.29: Whom say ye that I am? — Jesus presses the question past public opinion to personal confession. Peter answers: Thou art the Christ (ho Christos, the Messiah). The hinge of Mark; from here the road bends toward the cross (v. 31).
Mark.8.34: If any man will (thelei, intends to) come after me, let him deny himself, take up his cross, and follow me. Three present imperatives. The cross is not an inconvenience but the instrument of execution; discipleship begins at the gallows of self-will.
Mark.8.36: What shall it profit a man if he gain the whole world and lose his own soul (psychē, life)? The arithmetic of eternity: the world is finite; the soul is endless. The trade is always loss for the one who closes it.
Mark.9.7: There came a cloud (the Shekinah) that overshadowed them, and a voice: This is my beloved Son: hear him. The Father's voice at the Transfiguration adds one word to the baptismal benediction — hear him. Moses and Elijah recede; only the Son remains (v. 8).
Mark.9.24: Lord, I believe; help thou mine unbelief. The honest paradox of every disciple. Faith is not absence of doubt but the cry that lays even its doubt at Christ's feet. Christ honors the imperfect faith that confesses itself.
Mark.9.35: If any man desire to be first, the same shall be last of all and servant (diakonos) of all. Kingdom greatness inverts worldly hierarchy. Position is measured in cubits of service, not rank.
Mark.10.14: Suffer (aphete, permit) the little children to come unto me, and forbid them not — the only place Mark records Jesus' indignation (ēganaktēsen) toward His own disciples. The kingdom of God belongs to the dependent — child-like, not childish — receivers.
Mark.10.18: Why callest thou me good? There is none good but one, that is, God. Jesus does not deny His goodness; He probes the rich young ruler's casual title. If you call me good, you are confessing more than you know.
Mark.10.45: The Son of man came not to be ministered unto (diakonēthēnai) but to minister (diakonēsai), and to give His life a ransom (lytron) for many (anti pollōn — in the place of many). The clearest single statement of substitutionary atonement in the Synoptics.
Mark.11.9: Hosanna (hoshia-na, save now); blessed is he that cometh in the name of the Lord. The crowd quotes Ps 118:25–26, the Hallel pilgrim psalm. They acclaim Him as Messiah but expect a sword; within a week the Hosannas will turn to Crucify (15:13).
Mark.11.17: My house shall be called of all nations the house of prayer — Jesus quotes Isa 56:7. The temple cleansing is not anti-commerce but pro-Gentile: the Court of the Gentiles had been turned into a market, blocking the only space where the nations could approach. Messiah restores access.
Mark.11.25: When ye stand praying, forgive — present participle, ongoing posture. Unforgiveness clogs the prayer life. The horizontal and the vertical are linked in Jesus' theology (cf. Matt 6:14–15).
Mark.12.10: Jesus quotes Ps 118:22: the stone which the builders rejected has become the head of the corner. He applies the parable of the wicked husbandmen to Himself: the rejected Son is the cornerstone of God's new building.
Mark.12.17: Render to Caesar the things that are Caesar's, and to God the things that are God's. The coin bears Caesar's image; pay it. Man bears God's image (Gen 1:27); render himself to God. Two spheres, two allegiances — but God's claim is total.
Mark.12.30: Thou shalt love the Lord thy God with all thy heart, soul, mind, and strength — Jesus cites the Shema (Deut 6:4–5) and adds 'mind.' Total love mobilizes every faculty. This is the first commandment — every other is downstream.
Mark.12.31: Thou shalt love thy neighbour as thyself — Jesus joins Lev 19:18 to the Shema. There is no other commandment greater than these. The Decalogue divides into love-of-God and love-of-neighbor, and these summarize the whole.
Mark.13.10: The gospel must first be published among all nations — the Olivet Discourse predicts a global gospel proclamation before the end. Mission is eschatological inevitability, not optional program.
Mark.13.32: Of that day and that hour knoweth no man, no, not the angels, neither the Son, but the Father. The voluntary self-limitation of the incarnate Son in His state of humiliation; eschatological data is reserved to the Father. Date-setting is rebuked in advance.
Mark.14.22: This is my body — Jesus inaugurates the Lord's Supper during Passover. The bread does not become His body literally (He stands holding it); it represents His body soon to be broken. The new covenant meal replaces the old covenant memorial.
Mark.14.36: Abba, Father — Aramaic intimacy preserved. All things are possible unto thee; take away this cup. Nevertheless not what I will, but what thou wilt. The cup of wrath (Isa 51:17) is real; the Son's submission is total.
Mark.14.61: Art thou the Christ, the Son of the Blessed? — the high priest's question. Jesus answers: I am (egō eimi). Combined with the Son of Man / Daniel 7 reference (v. 62), this is unambiguous claim to deity. The verdict of blasphemy follows because the claim is rightly understood.
Mark.15.34: Eloi, Eloi, lama sabachthani — Jesus quotes Ps 22:1 from the cross. The Father turns His face from the Son who is bearing sin. This is the Trinitarian rupture at the heart of atonement; the cry is real abandonment, not theatrical pathos.
Mark.15.38: The veil of the temple was rent in twain from the top to the bottom. Top to bottom — God tore it, not man. The Holy of Holies is opened; the Levitical system is over (Heb 10:19–22). Atonement has been made.
Mark.15.39: Truly this man was the Son of God — the Roman centurion's confession at the cross is Mark's climactic Christological moment. A Gentile sees what the Sanhedrin missed. The opening title (1:1) is now confessed by an outsider.
Mark.16.6: He is risen; he is not here — the angel's announcement at the empty tomb. Mark gives no resurrection apparition until v. 9; the bare fact is enough. The body's absence is the ground of every Christian hope.
Luke.1.35: The Holy Ghost shall come upon thee, and the power of the Highest shall overshadow thee — the same verb (episkiazō) used of the Shekinah cloud over the tabernacle. The virgin conception is creative miracle, not divine biology. That holy thing born of thee shall be called the Son of God.
Luke.1.46: My soul doth magnify the Lord — the Magnificat opens with Mary's worship. She does not magnify herself for being chosen; she magnifies God for choosing the lowly (v. 48). The hymn is saturated with OT (esp. 1 Sam 2:1–10, Hannah's song).
Luke.1.68: Blessed be the Lord God of Israel; for He hath visited (epeskepsato) and redeemed His people — Zacharias' Benedictus. God's silence of four hundred years is over; visitation has resumed in the conception of the forerunner. Redemption is a covenantal verb (Exod 3).
Luke.1.79: To give light to them that sit in darkness and in the shadow of death (Isa 9:2), to guide our feet into the way of peace. The Dayspring (anatolē, the rising sun) from on high — Messianic title — has visited (v. 78). The infant John points to the coming Son.
Luke.2.7: She brought forth her firstborn son... and laid him in a manger; because there was no room for them in the inn (kataluma — guest room). The Lord of glory's first bed is a feeding trough. The displacement at the start prefigures the displacement at the end (no room outside the city, either).
Luke.2.10: Fear not: for behold, I bring you good tidings (euangelizomai) of great joy, which shall be to all people. The angel preaches the first Christmas sermon. Joy is the keynote of the gospel; universality (all people) is its scope.
Luke.2.14: Glory to God in the highest, and on earth peace, good will toward men (eudokias — among men with whom God is well pleased). The peace of Christmas is not universal sentiment but specific covenantal favor; the song does not flatten election but proclaims it.
Luke.2.29: Lord, now lettest thou thy servant depart in peace — Simeon's Nunc Dimittis. Having seen the salvation of God in the infant Christ, he can die. Sight of Christ is the believer's consummation; everything else is preface.
Luke.2.49: Wist ye not that I must be about my Father's business? — the twelve-year-old Jesus' first recorded words. He distinguishes 'my Father' from Joseph; Messianic self-consciousness is already operative. The 'must' (dei) of divine necessity will recur throughout Luke.
Luke.15.7: Joy in heaven over one sinner that repenteth, more than over ninety and nine just persons which need no repentance. Heaven's economy values the recovered over the secure — not because the secure are unloved but because rescue evokes festival. The three parables of Luke 15 are one apology for receiving sinners (v. 2).
Luke.15.10: Joy in the presence of the angels of God over one sinner that repenteth. The angels do not merely observe; the divine joy spills into their company. Conversion is a celestial event.
Luke.15.18: I will arise and go to my father, and will say unto him, Father, I have sinned. The prodigal's resolve is the anatomy of repentance: arise (decisive action), go (return), confess (own the sin against heaven and against thee). True repentance names sin as sin.
Luke.15.20: When he was yet a great way off, his father saw him, and had compassion, and ran, and fell on his neck, and kissed him. The father runs — undignified for an Eastern patriarch. The image governs the parable: the welcome is initiated, urgent, lavish. This is how God receives returning sinners.
Luke.15.31: Son, thou art ever with me, and all that I have is thine. The father's word to the elder brother — religious-but-resentful Israel, the Pharisees. The tragedy of the elder son is being in the house without enjoying the father. Self-righteousness exiles itself.
Luke.22.19: This is my body which is given for you: this do in remembrance (anamnēsin) of me. The Supper is memorial, not re-sacrifice. The body 'given for you' (hyper hymōn) is substitutionary in plain words.
Luke.22.20: This cup is the new testament (diathēkē, covenant) in my blood, which is shed for you. Jesus inaugurates the new covenant promised in Jer 31. The blood of the new covenant is His own; the Mediator is also the sacrifice.
Luke.22.31: Simon, Simon, behold, Satan hath desired to have you (exētēsato — demanded by formal request, plural 'you'). I have prayed for thee (singular), that thy faith fail not. Christ's intercession secures Peter's perseverance even through denial.
Luke.22.42: Father, if thou be willing, remove this cup from me: nevertheless not my will, but thine, be done. The cup is the wrath of God (Isa 51:17, Jer 25:15). The Son's willing submission to the Father's will is the moral interior of the atonement.
Luke.22.61: The Lord turned, and looked upon Peter. And Peter remembered the word of the Lord. One look from the Christ he denied is enough to break Peter to weeping (v. 62). Restoration begins at the place of remembered grace.
Luke.23.46: Father, into thy hands I commend my spirit. Jesus quotes Ps 31:5, the Jewish bedtime prayer. He dies as a son entering the Father's hands, not a victim swallowed by death. Voluntary surrender, not extinguished resistance.
Luke.24.27: Beginning at Moses and all the prophets, he expounded unto them in all the scriptures the things concerning himself. The risen Christ teaches the hermeneutic: the OT is Christocentric. Every text testifies of Him (John 5:39).
Luke.24.32: Did not our heart burn within us, while he talked with us by the way, and while he opened to us the scriptures? Christ-opened Scripture sets the heart on fire. The Emmaus pattern remains paradigmatic — Christ encountered through expounded text.
Luke.24.46: Thus it is written, and thus it behoved Christ to suffer, and to rise from the dead the third day. The 'must' of redemption is rooted in the written Word. Christ's death and resurrection were not contingencies but Scripture-fulfilling necessities.
Acts.1.11: This same Jesus, which is taken up from you into heaven, shall so come in like manner as ye have seen him go. The ascension and the second coming are bookends: bodily, visible, personal. Christ has not evolved into spirit; the Man Christ Jesus will return as He left.
Acts.2.4: They were all filled with the Holy Ghost, and began to speak with other tongues (heterais glōssais), as the Spirit gave them utterance. Pentecost is the inauguration of the church age. The tongues are real human languages (vv. 6–11), reversing Babel and inaugurating the global gospel.
Acts.2.23: Him, being delivered by the determinate counsel and foreknowledge of God, ye have taken, and by wicked hands have crucified and slain. Divine sovereignty (determinate counsel) and human responsibility (wicked hands) coexist without contradiction at the cross. Both halves of the verse are equally true.
Acts.2.36: God hath made that same Jesus, whom ye have crucified, both Lord and Christ. Peter's Pentecost sermon climaxes with a Christological declaration: the executed Galilean is the cosmic Sovereign and the long-promised Messiah. The crowd's response (v. 37) is cut to the heart.
Acts.2.41: They that gladly received his word were baptized: and the same day there were added unto them about three thousand souls. The first Pentecost harvest. Reception of the word, baptism, addition to the church — the apostolic order of conversion and incorporation.
Acts.2.47: The Lord added to the church daily such as should be saved. Church growth is divine action, not human marketing. The passive 'added' (prosetithei) preserves divine agency; God saves and God assembles.
Acts.9.4: Saul, Saul, why persecutest thou me? — the risen Christ identifies with His persecuted church. To touch the body is to touch the Head. This single sentence contains the seed of Pauline ecclesiology (1 Cor 12:12).
Acts.9.6: Lord, what wilt thou have me to do? Saul's first words as a believer place him under marching orders. Conversion reorients allegiance from self-direction to Christ-direction in the same breath as it reveals Christ.
Acts.9.15: He is a chosen vessel (skeuos eklogēs) unto me, to bear my name before the Gentiles, and kings, and the children of Israel. The Lord's word to Ananias: Saul's election is for service, not just safety. Apostleship is a sovereign appointment, not a self-chosen vocation.
Acts.16.14: The Lord opened her heart, that she attended unto the things which were spoken of Paul. Lydia hears Paul, but it is the Lord who opens her heart. The order is irreversible: divine work first, human response follows. Conversion is monergistic in its origin.
Acts.17.11: These were more noble than those in Thessalonica, in that they received the word with all readiness of mind, and searched (anakrinontes — judicially examined) the scriptures daily, whether those things were so. Berean nobility consists in subjecting even apostolic preaching to scriptural verification.
Acts.17.30: The times of this ignorance God winked at (hyperidōn — overlooked); but now commandeth all men every where to repent. The gospel age inaugurates a new universal call. Pre-Christian ignorance does not excuse; with Christ revealed, repentance is commanded for all.
Acts.17.31: He hath appointed a day, in which he will judge the world in righteousness by that man whom he hath ordained; whereof he hath given assurance unto all men, in that he hath raised him from the dead. The resurrection is God's public guarantee of coming judgment. Empty tomb, fixed verdict.
Acts.26.28: Almost thou persuadest me to be a Christian. Agrippa's near-conviction is famous — but Paul's reply (v. 29) is the gospel: I would to God that all that hear me were both almost and altogether such as I am. Almost-conversion is not conversion.
1Tim.1.5: The end (telos) of the commandment is charity (love) out of a pure heart, and of a good conscience, and of faith unfeigned. Doctrine targets life. The teacher who produces speculation rather than love has missed the aim.
1Tim.2.1: I exhort therefore, that, first of all, supplications, prayers, intercessions, and giving of thanks, be made for all men. Four prayer-words; one universal scope. The church's first political act is intercession — even for kings (v. 2).
1Tim.3.2: A bishop (episkopos, overseer) then must be blameless, the husband of one wife (mias gynaikos andra — a one-woman man), vigilant, sober, of good behaviour, given to hospitality, apt to teach. The qualifications are character, not charisma — eldership is fitness, not flair.
1Tim.4.16: Take heed unto thyself, and unto the doctrine; continue in them: for in doing this thou shalt both save thyself, and them that hear thee. The pastor's two-fold watch — over self and over doctrine. Inversion of either is fatal; both protected, the ministry yields its salvific fruit.
1Tim.6.6: Godliness with contentment is great gain. Religion divorced from contentment becomes a profit-engine (vv. 5, 9). Joined to contentment, godliness is the only true wealth.
1Tim.6.10: The love of money (philargyria) is the root of all (kinds of) evil — not money itself, but the inordinate love of it. Some have erred from the faith and pierced themselves through with many sorrows. Greed is self-mutilating.
2Tim.2.2: The things that thou hast heard of me among many witnesses, the same commit thou to faithful men, who shall be able to teach others also. Four generations of doctrinal succession in one sentence: Paul → Timothy → faithful men → others. The mechanism of preserving the gospel is teacher-to-disciple transmission.
2Tim.3.5: Having a form of godliness, but denying the power thereof: from such turn away. The last-days church will be religiously decorated and spiritually dead. The command is separation, not engagement — apostasy is contagious.
2Tim.4.3: The time will come when they will not endure sound doctrine; but after their own lusts shall they heap to themselves teachers, having itching ears. Heretics multiply because hearers commission them. The market for false teaching is created by sinful demand.
Titus.1.5: Set in order the things that are wanting, and ordain elders in every city, as I had appointed thee. Crete needs structure: order and eldership. Apostolic ecclesiology assumes plural elders in every local church.
Titus.1.9: Holding fast the faithful word as he hath been taught, that he may be able by sound doctrine both to exhort and to convince the gainsayers. The elder must be doctrinally tethered. Two functions, one foundation: positive exhortation and polemic refutation, both rooted in 'the faithful word.'
Job.1.21: Naked came I out of my mother's womb, and naked shall I return: the Lord gave, and the Lord hath taken away; blessed be the name of the Lord. Job's first response under unimaginable loss is worship. He attributes both gift and removal to the Lord — a theology of providence that does not flinch.
Job.1.22: In all this Job sinned not, nor charged God foolishly. The narrator's verdict on Job's first crisis. Submission without bitterness under God's mysterious dealing is possible — and Job models it before he is rebuked for later words.
Job.38.4: Where wast thou when I laid the foundations of the earth? declare, if thou hast understanding. God's first answer to Job is not explanation but interrogation. The Creator-creature gap is so vast that the answer to suffering is not a syllogism but a Person. Theodicy collapses into theophany.
Job.38.7: When the morning stars sang together, and all the sons of God shouted for joy. The angelic host witnessed creation as choristers; man was not yet present. The verse glimpses pre-Adamic angelic worship and the cosmic scope of God's work.
Job.39.19: Hast thou given the horse strength? hast thou clothed his neck with thunder? God's tour of creation forces Job to confront his own incompetence. If he cannot make a horse, he cannot critique providence.
Job.40.4: Behold, I am vile (qalal, of small account); what shall I answer thee? I will lay mine hand upon my mouth. Job's first response to God's voice: silence. The man who had demanded an audience now wants only to disappear into reverence.
Job.41.11: Who hath prevented me, that I should repay him? whatsoever is under the whole heaven is mine. God's claim of absolute prior rights — quoted by Paul in Rom 11:35. No creature has ever placed God in his debt; grace is therefore truly grace.
Job.42.5: I have heard of thee by the hearing of the ear: but now mine eye seeth thee. The shift from secondhand theology to firsthand encounter. Suffering can be the means by which the doctrine of God moves from cognition to vision.
Job.42.6: Wherefore I abhor myself, and repent in dust and ashes. The end of Job's struggle is not vindication but repentance — not for the sins his friends accused him of, but for his presumption in arguing with God. The righteous sufferer kneels.
Job.42.10: The Lord turned the captivity of Job, when he prayed for his friends. Restoration follows intercession for the very men who wounded him. Job's healing is mediated through forgiveness extended.
Eccl.1.2: Vanity (hevel, vapor, breath) of vanities; all is vanity. The Hebrew superlative of futility. 'Hevel' is the keynote of Ecclesiastes — life under the sun, divorced from God, evaporates between the fingers.
Eccl.1.9: There is no new thing under the sun. The cyclical pessimism of secular wisdom. Apart from divine revelation breaking in, history is a treadmill — a fact only the gospel of incarnation can falsify.
Eccl.3.1: To every thing there is a season, and a time to every purpose under the heaven. The famous time-poem (vv. 1–8) is not fatalism but a meditation on creaturely limitation: man cannot transcend time but must live within God's appointed seasons.
Eccl.3.11: He hath made every thing beautiful in his time: also he hath set the world (olam, eternity) in their heart, so that no man can find out the work that God maketh from the beginning to the end. Man is built with eternal capacity, hemmed in by temporal limit. The God-shaped vacancy in every heart is biblical anthropology.
Eccl.12.1: Remember now thy Creator in the days of thy youth, while the evil days come not. Wisdom for the young: the time to know God is before old age dulls the senses (vv. 2–7). Postponed faith is presumption.
Song.2.4: He brought me to the banqueting house, and his banner over me was love. The bride's intimate confession of being publicly claimed. Read Christologically (as the church has from the patristic era), the banner over the redeemed is the love of Christ, openly displayed.
Song.2.10: Rise up, my love, my fair one, and come away. The bridegroom's call to the bride. The repeated invitation in this book pictures the responsive love between Christ and His church — initiated, named, beckoning.
Song.2.16: My beloved is mine, and I am his. Mutual possession in covenantal love. The verse captures the essence of every covenant from Eden to Revelation: I will be your God, you shall be my people.
Song.8.6: Set me as a seal upon thine heart... for love is strong as death; jealousy is cruel as the grave: the coals thereof are coals of fire, which hath a most vehement flame. Love (ahavah) here is covenant-bound, exclusive, indelible. The intensity of marital love foreshadows the intensity of divine love.
Song.8.7: Many waters cannot quench love, neither can the floods drown it. Love's invincibility is itself a divine attribute mirrored in covenant fidelity. The verse anticipates Rom 8:35–39.
1Sam.17.45: Thou comest to me with a sword, and with a spear, and with a shield: but I come to thee in the name of the Lord of hosts (Yahweh tseva'ot), the God of the armies of Israel, whom thou hast defied. David's defiance of Goliath is theological before it is martial — the giant has insulted Yahweh, not merely Israel.
1Sam.17.47: The battle is the Lord's, and he will give you into our hands. The shepherd-boy preaches a sermon between throw and stone. Every covenantal victory is divine in source, human in instrument.
2Sam.7.13: He shall build an house for my name, and I will stablish the throne of his kingdom for ever. The Davidic Covenant: an everlasting throne, a son to build the house. Solomon fulfills this typically; Christ, David's greater Son, fulfills it ultimately (Luke 1:32–33).
2Sam.7.16: Thine house and thy kingdom shall be established for ever before thee: thy throne shall be established for ever. The unconditional core of the Davidic Covenant. Israel's unfaithful kings could not annul it; Christ's enthronement consummates it.
2Sam.12.7: Thou art the man. Nathan's prophetic dagger to David after the Bathsheba narrative. The same pattern of conviction is the Spirit's work in every awakened conscience: indictment, confession, forgiveness (v. 13).
2Sam.12.13: I have sinned against the Lord. And Nathan said unto David, The Lord also hath put away thy sin; thou shalt not die. The shortest, sincerest confession in Scripture meets the swiftest absolution. Forgiveness does not erase consequences (v. 14) but cancels condemnation.
1Kgs.3.9: Give therefore thy servant an understanding heart (lev shomea, a hearing heart) to judge thy people, that I may discern between good and bad. Solomon at Gibeon asks for what no one else thought to request. The petition itself is wisdom; God grants it precisely because Solomon prized it (v. 12).
1Kgs.8.27: Will God indeed dwell on the earth? behold, the heaven and heaven of heavens cannot contain thee; how much less this house. Solomon's dedication prayer holds together divine immanence (He dwells) and divine transcendence (He is uncontainable). The temple is symbol, not container.
1Kgs.18.21: How long halt ye between two opinions? if the Lord be God, follow him: but if Baal, then follow him. Elijah's confrontation on Carmel demands a verdict. Religious neutrality is impossible; the only honest options are exclusive worship or open apostasy.
1Kgs.18.39: The Lord, he is the God; the Lord, he is the God. Israel's confession after fire falls on Carmel. Repeated for emphasis — a corporate Shema in response to demonstrated deity.
1Kgs.19.12: After the fire a still small voice (qol demamah daqqah, a sound of thin silence). God meets exhausted Elijah not in cataclysm but in whispered presence. The God of fire is also the God of fellowship.
2Kgs.2.11: There appeared a chariot of fire, and horses of fire, and parted them both asunder; and Elijah went up by a whirlwind into heaven. Elijah's translation, the second of two men in the OT taken without dying (cf. Enoch). Death is not the only door God uses.
2Kgs.5.14: He went down, and dipped himself seven times in Jordan, according to the saying of the man of God: and his flesh came again like unto the flesh of a little child. Naaman's healing requires humbled obedience to a humble means. The cure is in the dipping the prophet ordained, not in any merit of the water.
2Kgs.6.16: Fear not: for they that be with us are more than they that be with them. Elisha's calm in encirclement. The unseen heavenly army (v. 17) outnumbers the visible enemy. Faith sees what panic misses.
2Kgs.19.15: Thou art the God, even thou alone, of all the kingdoms of the earth: thou hast made heaven and earth. Hezekiah's prayer with the Assyrian threat letter spread before God. The crisis presses him to confess the Creator's absolute sovereignty.
1Chr.16.11: Seek the Lord and his strength, seek his face continually. David's psalm at the Ark's installation. Seeking the Lord is not a project but a posture, sustained without interruption.
2Chr.20.12: We have no might against this great company that cometh against us; neither know we what to do: but our eyes are upon thee. Jehoshaphat's prayer at the invasion of Ammon-Moab. Confessed weakness paired with God-ward sight is the recipe for deliverance (vv. 15–17).
2Chr.20.15: Be not afraid nor dismayed by reason of this great multitude; for the battle is not yours, but God's. The prophetic word turns the kingdom's panic into worship (vv. 18–22). Some battles are won by singing, not swinging.
Hab.3.17: Although the fig tree shall not blossom, neither shall fruit be in the vines... Habakkuk imagines complete economic collapse. The catalogue of failed crops and empty stalls sets up the most defiant verse in the prophets.
Hab.3.18: Yet I will rejoice in the Lord, I will joy in the God of my salvation. Joy unanchored to circumstance — the prophet's resolution after surveying ruin. The God of salvation is enough when nothing else is left.
Hab.3.19: The Lord God is my strength, and he will make my feet like hinds' feet, and he will make me to walk upon mine high places. The book that opened with bitter complaint (1:2) ends with a worship leader's note. Faith does not deny suffering; it climbs through it.
Joel.2.32: Whosoever shall call on the name of the Lord shall be delivered. Peter quotes this at Pentecost (Acts 2:21); Paul cites it in Rom 10:13. The OT 'name of Yahweh' is applied to Jesus — a Christological mountain.
Amos.5.24: Let judgment run down as waters, and righteousness as a mighty stream. Amos rebukes religious ritual that coexists with social oppression. God will not accept solemn assemblies from people whose courts are corrupt; ethical justice is the necessary fruit of true worship.
Hag.1.4: Is it time for you, O ye, to dwell in your cieled houses, and this house lie waste? The post-exilic reproof: the people have built paneled homes while God's house lies in ruins. Misplaced priorities betray the heart's true treasure (Matt 6:21).
Hag.1.6: Ye have sown much, and bring in little; ye eat, but ye have not enough... and he that earneth wages earneth wages to put it into a bag with holes. God's discipline is sometimes the curse of unsatisfying prosperity. Self-focused industry yields a leaking purse.
Hag.2.9: The glory of this latter house shall be greater than of the former, saith the Lord of hosts: and in this place will I give peace. The post-exilic temple looked smaller (2:3), but it would receive Messiah's incarnate visit — a glory the Solomonic temple never knew.
Zeph.1.7: Hold thy peace at the presence of the Lord God: for the day of the Lord is at hand. Silence is the only fitting posture before approaching judgment. Eschatological reverence is itself an act of worship.
Obad.1.15: The day of the Lord is near upon all the heathen: as thou hast done, it shall be done unto thee: thy reward shall return upon thine own head. Edom's gloating over Judah's fall is reciprocated by judgment. The lex talionis runs through history; God repays oppressors in their own coin.
Obad.1.21: Saviours shall come up on mount Zion to judge the mount of Esau; and the kingdom shall be the Lord's. The book ends with eschatological consolation: God's kingdom outlasts every Edom. Earthly empires rise to fall; the Lord's kingdom rises to stand.
Jonah.1.3: But Jonah rose up to flee unto Tarshish from the presence of the Lord. The prophet runs west when commanded east. Tarshish is the opposite end of the known world; Jonah's flight is theological as well as geographic — he wants out of the divine presence (cf. Ps 139:7).
Jonah.1.17: The Lord had prepared (manah, appointed) a great fish to swallow up Jonah. The first of four divine appointments in the book (fish, plant, worm, wind). God's providence orchestrates the means of His prophet's chastening as precisely as the means of His mercy.
Jonah.3.5: The people of Nineveh believed God, and proclaimed a fast, and put on sackcloth. The pagan city repents at one sentence of preaching by an unwilling foreigner. The contrast with Israel's stubborn deafness to its own prophets is Jesus' point in Matt 12:41.
Jonah.3.10: God saw their works, that they turned from their evil way; and God repented (nacham, relented) of the evil that he had said he would do unto them. Divine 'repentance' is anthropomorphic — God responds in covenant fidelity to changed conditions, while remaining unchanging in His character (cf. 1 Sam 15:29).
Jonah.4.2: I knew that thou art a gracious God, and merciful, slow to anger, and of great kindness, and repentest thee of the evil. Jonah's complaint is also a confession: he ran because he knew God might save Nineveh. The prophet quotes Exod 34:6 and resents its truth.
Jonah.4.11: Should not I spare Nineveh, that great city, wherein are more than sixscore thousand persons? The book ends with a question, not a resolution. God's compassion exposes Jonah's smallness — and indicts every reader who would prefer His mercy be more selective.
Mark.1.35: In the morning, rising up a great while before day, he went out, and departed into a solitary place, and there prayed. The pattern of the incarnate Son: ministry flows from prayer, not the reverse. If the Son needed solitude with the Father, no servant outranks the need.
Mark.4.31: Like a grain of mustard seed... less than all the seeds that be in the earth: but when it is sown, it groweth up, and becometh greater than all herbs. The kingdom's beginning is unimpressive; its end is unrivaled. Pentecost from a manger; Christendom from twelve.
Luke.1.38: Behold the handmaid of the Lord; be it unto me according to thy word. Mary's response to the angel — the model of receptive faith. She does not bargain or qualify; she submits to a word that will cost her everything.
Luke.2.52: Jesus increased in wisdom and stature, and in favour with God and man. The incarnate Son developed truly. The hypostatic union does not preclude real human growth; the Word made flesh learned, matured, and was loved.
Luke.15.24: This my son was dead, and is alive again; he was lost, and is found. The father's verdict on the prodigal's return. Repentance is resurrection language; conversion is being raised from death to life (Eph 2:1, 5).
Luke.22.44: Being in an agony he prayed more earnestly: and his sweat was as it were great drops of blood falling down to the ground. Hematidrosis under extreme stress. The cup is so heavy that the Son sweats blood before He sheds it. Calvary begins in Gethsemane.
Luke.24.6: He is not here, but is risen: remember how he spake unto you when he was yet in Galilee. The angel's first sermon is exegesis: remember what He said. Resurrection faith is anchored to Christ's prior word, not just the empty tomb.
Acts.9.20: Straightway he preached Christ in the synagogues, that he is the Son of God. Saul's first sermon as a believer is identical to Mark's title page (Mark 1:1). Conversion produces preachers because conversion is encountering the Christ worth preaching.
Acts.17.28: In him we live, and move, and have our being. Paul quotes a pagan poet (Epimenides) to confront pagans with the dependence they cannot escape. Even the unbeliever's existence is borrowed from God; atheism is biographical incoherence.
Acts.26.29: I would to God, that not only thou, but also all that hear me this day, were both almost, and altogether such as I am, except these bonds. The chained apostle pities the comfortable king. The gospel preacher's heart is captured here: he wishes his hearers had everything he has, minus the suffering.
Job.1.8: Hast thou considered my servant Job, that there is none like him in the earth, a perfect and an upright man, one that feareth God, and escheweth evil? God Himself initiates the trial by drawing Satan's attention to His servant. Job's suffering begins not with Satan's malice but with God's commendation.
Eccl.3.14: Whatsoever God doeth, it shall be for ever: nothing can be put to it, nor any thing taken from it: and God doeth it, that men should fear before him. Divine works are sufficient and final. The intended response to providence is reverence, not editorial revision.
1Sam.3.10: Speak; for thy servant heareth. The boy Samuel's response under Eli's coaching. The posture of all true revelation-receiving: God speaks, the servant listens. Inversion of this order produces religion that talks at God instead of hearing from Him.
1Sam.15.23: Rebellion is as the sin of witchcraft, and stubbornness is as iniquity and idolatry. Samuel diagnoses Saul's partial obedience: refusing God is functionally occult worship of self. Disobedience is never small.
2Sam.22.31: As for God, his way is perfect; the word of the Lord is tried (tsaruph, refined): he is a buckler to all them that trust in him. David's psalm-of-deliverance song. The integrity of God's way and the purity of His word warrant absolute trust.
1Kgs.19.18: Yet I have left me seven thousand in Israel, all the knees which have not bowed unto Baal. Elijah felt alone; God had a remnant. The doctrine of the preserved minority is a comfort wherever the church seems extinguished.
2Kgs.5.1: Naaman, captain of the host of the king of Syria, was a great man with his master, and honourable... he was also a mighty man in valour, but he was a leper. Six commendations crashing into one disqualification. The world's greatest men carry the world's incurable disease until grace meets them in surprising places.
1Chr.16.34: O give thanks unto the Lord; for he is good; for his mercy endureth for ever. The refrain of the post-Davidic worship. The goodness of God and the perpetuity of His covenant love are the twin notes of every authentic doxology.
Mic.4.2: Out of Zion shall go forth the law, and the word of the Lord from Jerusalem. The eschatological gospel goes from Israel to the nations. The Great Commission is anticipated in the prophets — Zion-sourced, world-bound.
Hag.2.4: Be strong, O Zerubbabel... be strong, O Joshua... be strong, all ye people of the land, saith the Lord, and work: for I am with you, saith the Lord of hosts. The covenant presence (Immanuel principle) underwrites every command. Work is sustainable because God is with the workers.
Hos.11.8: How shall I give thee up, Ephraim? how shall I deliver thee, Israel?... mine heart is turned within me, my repentings are kindled together. The astonishing image of God's relenting compassion. Election grounds covenant love that judgment cannot ultimately overthrow.
Mark.13.31: Heaven and earth shall pass away: but my words shall not pass away. The Son's own words have an authority that outlasts the universe — the strongest possible self-attestation of canonical inspiration on the lips of Jesus.
Mark.14.38: Watch ye and pray, lest ye enter into temptation. The spirit truly is ready, but the flesh is weak. Jesus' counsel to sleeping disciples in Gethsemane is the universal recipe against falling: vigilance plus prayer, because the redeemed flesh is still flesh.
Mark.15.31: He saved others; himself he cannot save. The mockers spoke truer than they knew. To save others, He could not save Himself — substitution is the principle they ridiculed and the principle that crushes their sins.
1Chr.1.1: Adam, Sheth, Enosh. The Chronicler opens not with a narrative but with a name, and that name is Adam. For a post-exilic community wondering whether their story is over, the genealogist asserts that their story began at the very beginning of the human race. Reformed theology recognizes here the doctrine of federal headship: Adam stands at the head of the line, and from him flows the covenant history that will narrow to Abraham, David, and ultimately Christ. The bare names refuse novelty; they declare continuity. God has been keeping covenant from the first man, and the returnees from Babylon are the latest beneficiaries of that unbroken purpose.
1Chr.1.4: Noah, Shem, Ham, and Japheth. The list passes through the flood without pause, signaling that the covenant of preservation given to Noah grounds every subsequent table of nations. The Chronicler's audience, recently delivered through the deluge of exile, would hear an echo of their own preservation. As Noah was kept through judgment to populate a renewed earth, so the remnant has been kept through Babylon to repopulate the land of promise. Particular grace runs through Shem, narrowing toward the Messiah, while common grace embraces all peoples descended from the three sons.
1Chr.1.27: Abram; the same is Abraham. With one apposition the Chronicler binds together the covenant of Genesis 17 and the present moment. The change of name is not antiquarian detail but a doctrinal anchor: the God who made Abram into Abraham, father of many nations, has not abandoned that promise. For exiles whose own names had been changed in Babylon, the reaffirmation of Abraham's covenantal name reasserts that their identity is fixed in God's electing purpose, not in pagan reassignment.
1Chr.1.28: The sons of Abraham; Isaac, and Ishmael. The order is theological before chronological. Isaac, the son of promise, is named first, though Ishmael was older. The Chronicler enacts in his list what Paul later argues in Romans 9: the children of the promise are counted as the seed. Election runs through Isaac, and the post-exilic community must understand that their place in redemptive history rests on sovereign choice, not natural descent.
1Chr.2.1: These are the sons of Israel; Reuben, Simeon, Levi, and Judah. The transition from patriarchs to tribes places Israel at the center of the genealogical scheme. The Chronicler now narrows from humanity to the elect nation, and within the nation he will narrow further to Judah and David. This funneling movement is the literary shape of God's particular grace. Each name preserved here is a witness that the LORD has not forgotten any portion of His covenant people, even those scattered or absorbed during exile.
1Chr.2.7: And the sons of Carmi; Achar, the troubler of Israel, who transgressed in the thing accursed. The Chronicler interrupts the dry list to remember Achan's covenant breach at Jericho. Even within the elect line, sin is recorded honestly. The post-exilic reader is reminded that exile itself was the fruit of cumulative covenant breach, and that genealogical privilege does not exempt one from judgment. Reformed theology insists that the visible church always contains both wheat and tares; the genealogy itself preaches that warning.
1Chr.2.15: Ozem the sixth, David the seventh. Seven is the number of completion, and David appears in the seventh slot of Jesse's sons. The arrangement is no accident of family arithmetic; it is a literary signal that David is the goal toward which the line has been moving. The Chronicler will spend the next twenty-seven chapters demonstrating this. The Davidic covenant of 2 Samuel 7 stands as the structural keystone of Chronicles, and here, almost casually, the keystone is set in place.
1Chr.3.1: Now these were the sons of David, which were born unto him in Hebron. The royal line receives its own dedicated chapter, set apart from the broader Judahite genealogy. This is the line through which the Messiah will come, and the Chronicler treats it with the reverence due a sacred trust. For the returnees, who had no Davidic king on the throne, the careful preservation of this line was a quiet but emphatic promise: the house of David endures, even when the throne is empty.
1Chr.3.5: And these were born unto him in Jerusalem; Shimea, and Shobab, and Nathan, and Solomon. The four sons of Bathsheba are listed without comment on the scandal of their mother's first marriage. Yet Nathan and Solomon are both named, and both will appear in Christ's genealogy in Matthew and Luke. Grace works through the very places where sin abounded. The Chronicler's silence about the sin is not denial but a witness to the sufficiency of God's covenant mercy to absorb and transmute even royal scandal.
1Chr.3.17: And the sons of Jeconiah; Assir, Salathiel his son. The line passes through the cursed king of Jeremiah 22:30, and yet it does not stop. The Chronicler quietly testifies that God's promise to David is more durable than the curse on a particular king. Zerubbabel will rise from this line to lead the return, and centuries later Joseph will be reckoned a son of David through this same Jeconiah. Sovereign grace finds a path through every obstacle of judgment.
1Chr.3.19: And the sons of Pedaiah were, Zerubbabel, and Shimei. The mention of Zerubbabel is decisive for the Chronicler's purpose. Here is a living son of David, governor of the returned community, the immediate hope of the post-exilic generation. The genealogy is not retrospective only; it terminates in present persons through whom the covenant continues. Haggai 2:23 names Zerubbabel as the LORD's signet ring, reversing the curse on Jeconiah and pointing forward to Christ.
1Chr.4.9: And Jabez was more honourable than his brethren: and his mother called his name Jabez, saying, Because I bare him with sorrow. The Chronicler pauses the genealogy to insert a brief vignette of prayer answered. Jabez means sorrow, but he refuses to let his name define his destiny. Reformed exposition resists the prosperity reading: this is not a formula but a window into the doctrine of effectual prayer. The man who is born in sorrow appeals to the God of covenant, and the God of covenant grants what He had purposed to grant.
1Chr.4.23: These were the potters, and those that dwelt among plants and hedges: there they dwelt with the king for his work. Even craftsmen and gardeners are remembered as those who dwelt with the king for his work. Reformed vocational theology finds here a charter: the ordinary labor of God's people, performed in covenant community and for the sake of the king, is worthy of inscription. Nothing done for the LORD's anointed is too small for the canonical record.
1Chr.5.1: Now the sons of Reuben the firstborn of Israel, (for he was the firstborn; but, forasmuch as he defiled his father's bed, his birthright was given unto the sons of Joseph the son of Israel: and the genealogy is not to be reckoned after the birthright). The Chronicler explains the reordering of the tribes by appeal to Genesis 35 and 49. Reuben's sin disqualified him; the birthright passed to Joseph for material inheritance, but the chief rule went to Judah, from whom the prince should come. Sovereign election operates within history through the moral failures of the privileged and the elevation of the unlikely.
1Chr.5.20: And they were helped against them, and the Hagarites were delivered into their hand, and all that were with them: for they cried to God in the battle, and he was intreated of them; because they put their trust in him. The Transjordan tribes win because they cried to God and put their trust in Him. The Chronicler offers a brief sermon on the doctrine of dependence: military skill is not denied, but victory is attributed to faith working through prayer. The principle will recur throughout Chronicles and stands as a paradigm for the post-exilic community facing hostile neighbors.
1Chr.5.25: And they transgressed against the God of their fathers, and went a whoring after the gods of the people of the land, whom God destroyed before them. The same tribes who once trusted God later went after the very gods of the nations the LORD had dispossessed. The Chronicler's logic is unflinching: covenant unfaithfulness produced exile. The lesson for the returned community is plain. The land was lost not by accident but by apostasy, and only covenant fidelity can secure its renewed possession.
1Chr.5.26: And the God of Israel stirred up the spirit of Pul king of Assyria... and brought them unto Halah, and Habor, and Hara, and to the river Gozan, unto this day. The Assyrian king is a tool in the hand of the God of Israel. The Chronicler attributes the exile of the northern tribes not to geopolitical accident but to divine agency. This same theology of providence will undergird the announcement that the LORD also stirred up the spirit of Cyrus to send the exiles home.
1Chr.6.10: And Johanan begat Azariah, (he it is that executed the priest's office in the temple that Solomon built in Jerusalem). The priestly genealogy is anchored to the temple. The Chronicler is concerned not merely with bloodline but with vocation: Aaronic descent finds its fulfillment in service at the altar. For a community now rebuilding the temple, the careful tracing of priestly descent guarantees the legitimacy of present worship.
1Chr.6.31: And these are they whom David set over the service of song in the house of the LORD, after that the ark had rest. David appears as the patron of Israel's worship even before the temple is built. The Chronicler's emphasis on the king's role in establishing musical praise is theologically purposeful: the Davidic covenant generates a worshipping community, not merely a political dynasty. Christ, as the greater David, likewise institutes the worship of the new covenant church.
1Chr.6.49: But Aaron and his sons offered upon the altar of the burnt offering... according to all that Moses the servant of God had commanded. Levitical service is governed by Mosaic command. The Chronicler insists on the regulative principle in worship: priests do what is written, no more and no less. The post-exilic priesthood under Joshua and Ezra would reread these instructions as their own job description.
1Chr.7.21: Whom the men of Gath that were born in that land slew, because they came down to take away their cattle. Even within the genealogies the Chronicler records a small episode of loss and grief. Ephraim mourns his slain sons. The text is honest about pain inside the covenant family. The God who keeps the family book also keeps the tear bottle.
1Chr.8.33: And Ner begat Kish, and Kish begat Saul. The line of Saul is preserved, though the kingdom passed from his house to David's. The Chronicler does not erase Saul; he records the family with care, even as he subordinates it to the Davidic narrative. Reformed theology recognizes that God's electing purposes do not require the obliteration of the non-elect line, only its subordination to the saving purpose.
1Chr.9.1: So all Israel were reckoned by genealogies; and, behold, they were written in the book of the kings of Israel and Judah, who were carried away to Babylon for their transgression. The verse functions as a hinge between the genealogies and the narrative. The exile is named explicitly as the consequence of transgression. The Chronicler will not let his readers forget why they spent seventy years away from the land.
1Chr.9.2: Now the first inhabitants that dwelt in their possessions in their cities were, the Israelites, the priests, Levites, and the Nethinims. The list of returnees opens with priests and Levites, signaling that worship leads the restoration. The community that comes back is structured around the temple. Reformed ecclesiology sees here the priority of the means of grace in any work of renewal.
1Chr.9.22: All these which were chosen to be porters in the gates were two hundred and twelve. These were reckoned by their genealogy in their villages, whom David and Samuel the seer did ordain in their set office. Even the gatekeepers trace their commission back to David and Samuel. The Chronicler binds the present worship to the founding generation. Continuity of office is part of the visible argument that the covenant has not been broken.
1Chr.10.1: Now the Philistines fought against Israel; and the men of Israel fled from before the Philistines, and fell down slain in mount Gilboa. The narrative proper opens with disaster. Saul's defeat is the dark backdrop against which David's reign will shine. The Chronicler skips David's earlier life entirely; for him the story begins where Saul's story ends. The throne is not vacated by accident but by judgment.
1Chr.10.13: So Saul died for his transgression which he committed against the LORD, even against the word of the LORD, which he kept not, and also for asking counsel of one that had a familiar spirit, to enquire of it. The Chronicler offers a theological autopsy. Saul died not merely from a Philistine arrow but for transgression. The sin of consulting the medium at Endor is named as a covenant violation deserving death. The lesson stands for the post-exilic community: covenant disobedience kills, even kings.
1Chr.10.14: And enquired not of the LORD: therefore he slew him, and turned the kingdom unto David the son of Jesse. Two divine actions are stated baldly: He slew, and He turned. The transition from Saul to David is sovereign and active, not the work of human politics. The Davidic covenant rests on a foundation of God's own choosing.
1Chr.11.2: And moreover in time past, even when Saul was king, thou wast he that leddest out and broughtest in Israel: and the LORD thy God said unto thee, Thou shalt feed my people Israel, and thou shalt be ruler over my people Israel. The elders confess that David's kingship was not a human invention but a divine commission. The shepherd metaphor anticipates Ezekiel 34 and John 10: the true king feeds the flock. Christ is the antitype, the great Shepherd whose rule over the people of God is grounded in the Father's word.
1Chr.11.3: Therefore came all the elders of Israel to the king to Hebron; and David made a covenant with them before the LORD in Hebron; and they anointed David king over Israel, according to the word of the LORD by Samuel. The covenant is made before the LORD. Civil authority in Israel is constitutionally bound to the covenant God. This is the biblical pattern that Reformed political theology has always pressed: rulers govern under God, not above Him.
1Chr.11.9: So David waxed greater and greater: for the LORD of hosts was with him. The growth of David is attributed entirely to the LORD's presence. The Chronicler's editorial comment is theological commentary on every line of the narrative that follows. The same principle holds for the church: the LORD of hosts is with His people, and that presence is the only true cause of growth.
