1Chr.12.18: Then the spirit came upon Amasai, who was chief of the captains, and he said, Thine are we, David, and on thy side, thou son of Jesse: peace, peace be unto thee, and peace be to thine helpers; for thy God helpeth thee. The Spirit produces confession of allegiance to David. The pneumatology of the Old Testament is here glimpsed: the same Spirit who would later be poured out at Pentecost causes warriors to declare loyalty to the LORD's anointed. Christ likewise gathers His people by the Spirit's effectual call.
1Chr.12.32: And of the children of Issachar, which were men that had understanding of the times, to know what Israel ought to do. The men of Issachar are commended for discernment. Reformed application has long seen here a mandate for the church to read its own moment with prayerful intelligence. The post-exilic community needed such men, and so does every generation of the people of God.
1Chr.12.38: All these men of war, that could keep rank, came with a perfect heart to Hebron, to make David king over all Israel: and all the rest also of Israel were of one heart to make David king. Unity around the anointed king is described as one heart. The image foreshadows the body of Christ gathered around her Lord, of which the prophet Ezekiel and the apostle Paul will speak.
1Chr.13.3: And let us bring again the ark of our God to us: for we enquired not at it in the days of Saul. David identifies the neglect of the ark as a defining failure of the previous reign. The new administration will be characterized by attention to the presence of God. Reformed worship retains this priority: nothing is more central than the ordained presence of God in the assembly of His people.
1Chr.13.10: And the anger of the LORD was kindled against Uzza, and he smote him, because he put his hand to the ark: and there he died before God. The death of Uzza is a sober reminder that good intentions do not justify unauthorized worship. The ark was to be carried by Levites on poles, not transported on a Philistine cart. The regulative principle in worship is written in Uzza's blood: God is to be approached only as He has commanded.
1Chr.14.2: And David perceived that the LORD had confirmed him king over Israel, for his kingdom was lifted up on high, because of his people Israel. David's kingship is for the sake of the people. The Chronicler refuses any vision of the throne as personal aggrandizement. The greater Son of David rules likewise for the sake of His church.
1Chr.14.10: And David enquired of God, saying, Shall I go up against the Philistines? And wilt thou deliver them into mine hand? And the LORD said unto him, Go up; for I will deliver them into thine hand. David inquires, in pointed contrast to Saul who did not. The pattern of dependence by inquiry runs through Chronicles as the mark of legitimate rule. The same pattern applies to the church: dependence on the Word read and proclaimed.
1Chr.15.2: Then David said, None ought to carry the ark of God but the Levites: for them hath the LORD chosen to carry the ark of God, and to minister unto him for ever. After the disaster with Uzza, David returns to Scripture. The corrective is not abandonment of the ark but reformation according to the Word. The post-exilic community reading this would learn the pattern of repentance: not flight from worship but the recovery of right worship.
1Chr.15.13: For because ye did it not at the first, the LORD our God made a breach upon us, for that we sought him not after the due order. The phrase due order is foundational for Reformed worship. God is to be sought according to His prescribed manner. Innovation in worship, however well-intended, invites breach.
1Chr.15.29: And it came to pass, as the ark of the covenant of the LORD came to the city of David, that Michal the daughter of Saul looking out at a window saw king David dancing and playing: and she despised him in her heart. The contempt of Michal stands as a counter-witness to the joy of David. Worship that is genuinely God-centered will appear undignified to the world and even to formal religion. The Chronicler records her scorn without comment, letting the contrast speak.
1Chr.16.8: Give thanks unto the LORD, call upon his name, make known his deeds among the people. The first line of David's psalm of dedication, which composites portions of Psalms 105, 96, and 106. The Chronicler is binding the historical narrative to the inspired hymnody of Israel. The temple worship he is anticipating is not novel but is the public proclamation of the LORD's deeds among the nations.
1Chr.16.22: Saying, Touch not mine anointed, and do my prophets no harm. Even the patriarchs are called the LORD's anointed. The category of anointing extends beyond kings and priests; it embraces all whom God sets apart for His purposes. In the new covenant the entire church is anointed in Christ.
1Chr.17.4: Go and tell David my servant, Thus saith the LORD, Thou shalt not build me an house to dwell in. The first word of the Davidic covenant is a refusal. David will not build the house. God's purposes do not always align with the desires of even His best servants. Yet the refusal is the prelude to a far greater promise.
1Chr.17.11: And it shall come to pass, when thy days be expired that thou must go to be with thy fathers, that I will raise up thy seed after thee, which shall be of thy sons; and I will establish his kingdom. The Davidic covenant proper. The seed promised will be both Solomon and, ultimately, Christ. Reformed federal theology recognizes this verse as the third great covenant: after Adam and Abraham, the LORD now binds Himself to David and his line forever.
1Chr.17.12: He shall build me an house, and I will stablish his throne for ever. The throne is established for ever. The post-exilic reader, with no Davidic king on the throne, must read this verse as a promise yet awaiting full realization. The Chronicler's whole work invites his audience to lift their eyes from Zerubbabel the governor to the King who is to come.
1Chr.17.13: I will be his father, and he shall be my son: and I will not take my mercy away from him, as I took it from him that was before thee. The Father-Son relationship is established here in covenantal terms and finds its full referent in the Lord Jesus Christ. The promise that mercy will not be removed as it was from Saul guarantees that the Davidic line will not fail. Christ is the mercy that never departs.
1Chr.17.16: And David the king came and sat before the LORD, and said, Who am I, O LORD God, and what is mine house, that thou hast brought me hitherto? David's prayer of astonishment at sovereign grace is a model for every saint contemplating the covenant. The first response to God's promises is humility. Whoever boasts before the covenant has not understood it.
1Chr.17.27: Now therefore let it please thee to bless the house of thy servant, that it may be before thee for ever: for thou blessest, O LORD, and it shall be blessed for ever. The prayer ends in faith resting on the divine word. What God blesses is blessed. Reformed assurance is precisely this: confidence not in the strength of our prayer but in the unbreakable promise of the One to whom we pray.
1Chr.18.14: So David reigned over all Israel, and executed judgment and justice among all his people. Justice is the proper work of the king. The Chronicler frames David's reign in terms that prepare the reader for the Messianic king of Isaiah 9 and 11. Judgment and justice are the marks of the kingdom of God in any age.
1Chr.19.13: Be of good courage, and let us behave ourselves valiantly for our people, and for the cities of our God: and let the LORD do that which is good in his sight. Joab's exhortation is a pattern of biblical courage. Human duty and divine sovereignty meet without tension. We act, and the LORD does what is good. The post-exilic community rebuilding under threat could find here a charter for faithful action under providence.
1Chr.21.1: And Satan stood up against Israel, and provoked David to number Israel. The Chronicler unveils the spiritual reality behind the census. The parallel in 2 Samuel 24 says the LORD moved David; the Chronicler says Satan provoked him. Both are true. God is sovereign; Satan is the proximate tempter; David is responsible. Reformed theology has long appealed to such concurrence: God ordains, the creature acts.
1Chr.21.8: And David said unto God, I have sinned greatly, because I have done this thing: but now, I beseech thee, do away the iniquity of thy servant; for I have done very foolishly. True repentance names sin without excuse. David does not blame Satan, the tempter, or the people. He confesses I have sinned. The post-exilic community, called to confess corporate and personal sin, is given a paradigm.
1Chr.21.13: And David said unto Gad, I am in a great strait: let me fall now into the hand of the LORD; for very great are his mercies: but let me not fall into the hand of man. David chooses the rod of God over the rod of men. The mercy of God, even in chastening, is greater than any human mercy. The exiles, who had fallen into the hand of Babylon, would find this verse an aching consolation.
1Chr.21.26: And David built there an altar unto the LORD, and offered burnt offerings and peace offerings, and called upon the LORD; and he answered him from heaven by fire upon the altar of burnt offering. The site of judgment becomes the site of sacrifice and the foundation of the temple. The pattern is gospel-shaped: where wrath falls and is averted by sacrifice, there God will dwell. Calvary is the antitype.
1Chr.22.1: Then David said, This is the house of the LORD God, and this is the altar of the burnt offering for Israel. The threshing floor of Ornan, purchased through repentance and atoned by sacrifice, becomes the temple site. The Chronicler insists that the place of God's dwelling is established by mercy and atonement, not by raw human ambition.
1Chr.22.5: And David said, Solomon my son is young and tender, and the house that is to be builded for the LORD must be exceeding magnifical, of fame and of glory throughout all countries: I will therefore now make preparation for it. David accepts that the house will be built by another and devotes himself to preparation. Reformed pastoral wisdom finds here a doctrine of generational labor: the saint plants what others will reap and rejoices to do so.
1Chr.22.10: He shall build an house for my name; and he shall be my son, and I will be his father; and I will establish the throne of his kingdom over Israel for ever. The Davidic covenant is restated to the next generation. Sonship language carries forward to the New Testament, where Christ is named the eternal Son and the true Builder of the house of God.
1Chr.22.13: Then shalt thou prosper, if thou takest heed to fulfil the statutes and judgments which the LORD charged Moses with concerning Israel: be strong, and of good courage; dread not, nor be dismayed. The promise of prosperity is conditioned on covenant obedience and accompanied by the encouragement once given to Joshua. Solomon, like Joshua, faces a great task in dependence on Mosaic Torah. The principle holds for every leader of God's people.
1Chr.22.19: Now set your heart and your soul to seek the LORD your God; arise therefore, and build ye the sanctuary of the LORD God. The summons unites heart, soul, and hand. Worship and labor are not opposites but partners. The post-exilic community rebuilding the second temple under Haggai and Zechariah is being addressed here from across the centuries.
1Chr.23.1: So when David was old and full of days, he made Solomon his son king over Israel. The peaceful succession contrasts with the violent transitions of pagan dynasties. Covenant succession is orderly because it rests on divine promise, not on military conquest. The greater Son of David will receive the kingdom by appointment of the Father.
1Chr.23.13: The sons of Amram; Aaron and Moses: and Aaron was separated, that he should sanctify the most holy things, he and his sons for ever, to burn incense before the LORD, to minister unto him, and to bless in his name for ever. The Aaronic priesthood is described in three perpetual functions: sanctify, minister, bless. These will all find fulfillment in Christ, our great High Priest, who sanctifies His people, ministers in the heavenly sanctuary, and blesses His church with every spiritual blessing.
1Chr.23.30: And to stand every morning to thank and praise the LORD, and likewise at even. Worship is patterned to the rhythm of creation: morning and evening. The post-exilic temple recovers this rhythm. Reformed family worship and the church's daily prayers stand in continuity with this pattern.
1Chr.24.5: Thus were they divided by lot, one sort with another; for the governors of the sanctuary, and governors of the house of God, were of the sons of Eleazar, and of the sons of Ithamar. The casting of lots places the order of priestly service in the hand of the LORD. Proverbs 16:33 supplies the principle: the lot is cast in the lap, but the whole disposing of it is of the LORD. Even sacred rotations are ordained by sovereign providence.
1Chr.25.1: Moreover David and the captains of the host separated to the service of the sons of Asaph, and of Heman, and of Jeduthun, who should prophesy with harps, with psalteries, and with cymbals. The musicians prophesy. The Chronicler treats inspired song as a form of prophetic ministry. The Psalter is not human creativity dressed up but the very voice of God put on the lips of His people.
1Chr.26.27: Out of the spoils won in battles did they dedicate to maintain the house of the LORD. The plunder of war is consecrated to the worship of God. The principle that all things belong to the LORD and are to be returned to Him in worship governs even the materials of the sanctuary. Reformed stewardship sees here a pattern for the use of all wealth.
1Chr.27.23: But David took not the number of them from twenty years old and under: because the LORD had said he would increase Israel like to the stars of the heavens. David remembers the Abrahamic promise and refuses to circumscribe what God has expanded. Faith refuses to count what God has promised to multiply. The post-exilic community, small in number, must learn to read its size in the light of promise.
1Chr.28.2: Then David the king stood up upon his feet, and said, Hear me, my brethren, and my people: As for me, I had in mine heart to build an house of rest for the ark of the covenant of the LORD. David's farewell speech opens with a reminder of his thwarted desire. The aged king does not complain that he was denied the privilege; he uses his final breath to commission the next generation. Pastoral succession in the church follows this pattern.
1Chr.28.6: And he said unto me, Solomon thy son, he shall build my house and my courts: for I have chosen him to be my son, and I will be his father. Election is invoked again. Solomon's role is grounded not in primogeniture nor in popular consent but in divine choice. The pattern of sovereign election running through Chronicles, from Isaac to Judah to David to Solomon, points always to the eternal election that culminates in Christ.
1Chr.28.10: Take heed now; for the LORD hath chosen thee to build an house for the sanctuary: be strong, and do it. The charge is brief, urgent, and grounded in election. The work assigned is undertaken because chosen. Christian ministry is built on this foundation: not self-appointment, but the One who calls.
1Chr.28.19: All this, said David, the LORD made me understand in writing by his hand upon me, even all the works of this pattern. The pattern of the temple comes by revelation, just as the tabernacle pattern was given to Moses on the mountain. Reformed theology recognizes here the divine origin of the place and forms of worship. Innovation is excluded; reception is required.
1Chr.29.10: Wherefore David blessed the LORD before all the congregation: and David said, Blessed be thou, LORD God of Israel our father, for ever and ever. The doxology of David at the end of his life is a pattern of biblical praise. He addresses the LORD God of Israel our father, binding personal worship to covenant identity. The Chronicler is teaching his community how to pray.
1Chr.29.14: But who am I, and what is my people, that we should be able to offer so willingly after this sort? for all things come of thee, and of thine own have we given thee. The doctrine of stewardship is here in its purest form. We give to God only what we first received from Him. Reformed practice of tithing and offering rests on this confession.
1Chr.29.15: For we are strangers before thee, and sojourners, as were all our fathers: our days on the earth are as a shadow, and there is none abiding. David, at the height of his power, confesses pilgrim identity. The post-exilic community, recently displaced and now resettled, is reminded that even in the land they remain sojourners. Reformed eschatology preserves this pilgrim consciousness.
1Chr.29.18: O LORD God of Abraham, Isaac, and of Israel, our fathers, keep this for ever in the imagination of the thoughts of the heart of thy people, and prepare their heart unto thee. David prays for the perseverance of the people. He knows that without divine preservation the right disposition will not last. Reformed soteriology rests on this same intercession, fulfilled in Christ who keeps His own.
1Chr.29.22: And did eat and drink before the LORD on that day with great gladness. And they made Solomon the son of David king the second time, and anointed him unto the LORD to be the chief governor, and Zadok to be priest. King and priest are anointed together, prefiguring the union of these offices in Christ. The post-exilic reader, awaiting both, is prepared to recognize their true convergence in the Messiah.
1Chr.29.28: And he died in a good old age, full of days, riches, and honour: and Solomon his son reigned in his stead. The Chronicler's verdict on David is a benediction. Despite his sins, recorded honestly elsewhere, David died in covenant faithfulness, full of days. The hope held out to every saint is to die in the LORD, full of His mercy.
1Chr.1.34: And Abraham begat Isaac. The sons of Isaac; Esau and Israel. The patriarchal line condenses with stark simplicity. Esau and Israel together represent the two trajectories of grace and rejection. Romans 9 will read this very verse as a window into election. The Chronicler is content to record the names; the doctrine has always been there for those with eyes to see.
1Chr.2.3: The sons of Judah; Er, and Onan, and Shelah: which three were born unto him of the daughter of Shua the Canaanite. And Er, the firstborn of Judah, was evil in the sight of the LORD; and he slew him. The line of Judah, from which Messiah will come, is preserved despite the moral failures of its members. The Chronicler does not airbrush the family. Sovereign grace runs through compromised lines because it must.
1Chr.2.55: And the families of the scribes which dwelt at Jabez; the Tirathites, the Shimeathites, Suchathites. These are the Kenites that came of Hemath, the father of the house of Rechab. The scribes are remembered. In the post-exilic period, when Ezra the scribe will rise to prominence, the careful preservation of scribal lineage signals the importance of those who preserve and teach the Word.
1Chr.4.43: And they smote the rest of the Amalekites that were escaped, and dwelt there unto this day. The Amalekite remnant, target of Saul's failed obedience, is finally destroyed by Simeonites. The Chronicler closes a long arc that began in Exodus 17. God's word does not return void; what He has decreed, He performs, even across centuries.
1Chr.5.18: The sons of Reuben, and the Gadites, and half the tribe of Manasseh, of valiant men, men able to bear buckler and sword, and to shoot with bow, and skilful in war, were four and forty thousand seven hundred and threescore, that went out to the war. Skill in war is recorded honestly, but the next verses will assign victory to prayer and trust. The Chronicler does not despise human means; he simply refuses to trust them.
1Chr.6.32: And they ministered before the dwelling place of the tabernacle of the congregation with singing, until Solomon had built the house of the LORD in Jerusalem: and then they waited on their office according to their order. Worship has continuity through transitions. The Levites who served the tabernacle continue to serve the temple. The post-exilic community is part of the same continuous worshipping body.
1Chr.7.40: All these were the children of Asher, heads of their father's house, choice and mighty men of valour, chief of the princes. And the number throughout the genealogy of them that were apt to the war and to battle was twenty and six thousand men. Even the tribes that figure least in the narrative are remembered with honor. The Chronicler will not let any tribe be forgotten. The whole of Israel matters to the God of the covenant.
1Chr.9.13: And their brethren, heads of the house of their fathers, a thousand and seven hundred and threescore; very able men for the work of the service of the house of God. The phrase very able men for the work of the service of the house of God deserves notice. Service in the temple is described in terms that any vocation might envy. Reformed theology refuses any sacred-secular split, but it does insist that no work is more honorable than the service of God's house.
1Chr.10.6: So Saul died, and his three sons, and all his house died together. The end of Saul's house is total. The Chronicler underscores the discontinuity to highlight the new beginning in David. The judgment on the previous covenant breaker is the dark backdrop for the mercy that constitutes the new king.
1Chr.11.10: These also are the chief of the mighty men whom David had, who strengthened themselves with him in his kingdom, and with all Israel, to make him king, according to the word of the LORD concerning Israel. The mighty men strengthen themselves with David according to the word of the LORD. Their loyalty is theological before it is personal. They side with the LORD's anointed because the LORD has spoken.
1Chr.13.6: And David went up, and all Israel, to Baalah, that is, to Kirjath-jearim, which belonged to Judah, to bring up thence the ark of God the LORD, that dwelleth between the cherubims, whose name is called on it. The God who dwells between the cherubim is named here. The Chronicler is careful to identify worship with the particular God of Israel, not with a generic deity. The named God is the only God to be sought.
1Chr.15.16: And David spake to the chief of the Levites to appoint their brethren to be the singers with instruments of musick, psalteries and harps and cymbals, sounding, by lifting up the voice with joy. Joy is the marked tone of true worship. The Chronicler returns again and again to the gladness of God's people in His presence. The post-exilic community is invited into the same joy.
1Chr.16.36: Blessed be the LORD God of Israel for ever and ever. And all the people said, Amen, and praised the LORD. Congregational amen seals the leader's praise. Reformed worship preserves this responsive shape: the people are not spectators but participants, joining their voice to the appointed leader's words.
1Chr.17.20: O LORD, there is none like thee, neither is there any God beside thee, according to all that we have heard with our ears. The Shema is invoked in David's prayer. The exclusive claim of Yahweh is the bedrock of Israelite worship and the foundation of all post-exilic monotheism. There is no rival god to be appeased.
1Chr.18.6: Then David put garrisons in Syria-damascus; and the Syrians became David's servants, and brought gifts. Thus the LORD preserved David whithersoever he went. The Chronicler interprets military success as divine preservation. The same agency will be invoked when the LORD preserves the post-exilic community against Sanballat and Tobiah.
1Chr.20.4: And it came to pass after this, that there arose war at Gezer with the Philistines; at which time Sibbechai the Hushathite slew Sippai, that was of the children of the giant: and they were subdued. The Chronicler records the defeat of giants but omits the Bathsheba narrative entirely. He is not interested in covering up sin (it is recorded in 2 Samuel and assumed here) but in showing how the LORD continues to give victory to His people.
1Chr.21.17: And David said unto God, Is it not I that commanded the people to be numbered? even I it is that have sinned and done evil indeed; but as for these sheep, what have they done? David seeks to bear the punishment that has fallen on the people. He cannot do so fully, but the prayer prefigures the One who could and did. Christ is the true Shepherd-King who bore the sin of His people in their place.
1Chr.22.18: Is not the LORD your God with you? and hath he not given you rest on every side? for he hath given the inhabitants of the land into mine hand; and the land is subdued before the LORD, and before his people. The land subdued before the LORD is the precondition for the temple. Rest precedes worship. The pattern recurs in the New Testament: the rest Christ provides is the platform for the worship of His people.
1Chr.23.25: For David said, The LORD God of Israel hath given rest unto his people, that they may dwell in Jerusalem for ever. The promise of rest forever is theologically charged. Hebrews 4 will pick up this theme to point to the eternal Sabbath rest that remains for the people of God. The post-exilic community, still without full rest, is being pointed to a deeper fulfillment.
1Chr.24.19: These were the orderings of them in their service to come into the house of the LORD, according to their manner, under Aaron their father, as the LORD God of Israel had commanded him. According to the LORD's command. The phrase recurs throughout Chronicles to ground worship practice in revealed Word. Reformed worship insists on the same anchor: nothing is to be done in the LORD's house that the LORD has not commanded.
1Chr.25.7: So the number of them, with their brethren that were instructed in the songs of the LORD, even all that were cunning, was two hundred fourscore and eight. The musicians are skilled and instructed. Worship requires both spiritual reverence and trained craft. The Reformed tradition has always valued the union of doctrine and excellent praise.
1Chr.26.32: And his brethren, men of valour, were two thousand and seven hundred chief fathers, whom king David made rulers over the Reubenites, the Gadites, and the half tribe of Manasseh, for every matter pertaining to God, and affairs of the king. Civil and religious affairs are entrusted to the same officers. There is no Cartesian split between sacred and secular under the Old Covenant. The whole of life is brought under the kingship of the LORD.
1Chr.27.24: Joab the son of Zeruiah began to number, but he finished not, because there fell wrath for it against Israel; neither was the number put in the account of the chronicles of king David. Even the records of the kingdom carry the marks of judgment. The aborted census remains in the memory of the community as a warning. Sin's traces are kept visible so that the next generation might not repeat them.
1Chr.28.20: And David said to Solomon his son, Be strong and of good courage, and do it: fear not, nor be dismayed: for the LORD God, even my God, will be with thee; he will not fail thee, nor forsake thee, until thou hast finished all the work for the service of the house of the LORD. The promise once given to Joshua is given to Solomon. The same promise is given to every minister of the gospel: He will not fail thee, nor forsake thee. The work assigned will be brought to completion by the One who assigns it.
1Chr.29.1: Furthermore David the king said unto all the congregation, Solomon my son, whom alone God hath chosen, is yet young and tender, and the work is great: for the palace is not for man, but for the LORD God. The temple is not for man, but for the LORD God. The Chronicler closes his first volume with this declaration, which doubles as the constitutional principle of all true worship. The church exists not for itself but for the glory of its Lord.
1Chr.29.20: And David said to all the congregation, Now bless the LORD your God. And all the congregation blessed the LORD God of their fathers, and bowed down their heads, and worshipped the LORD, and the king. The whole congregation worships the LORD. The Chronicler ends the reign of David not in human triumph but in corporate worship. The post-exilic community is summoned to do the same in their day, and the church to do likewise in hers.
1Kgs.1.1: The opening verse depicts David, the warrior-king of covenant promise, as aged and unable to keep warm despite covering. The Hebrew zaqen ba bayyamim ('old, advanced in days') signals the closing of an era. Reformed exegesis recognizes here the inadequacy of every earthly Davidic king to fulfill the eternal covenant of 2 Samuel 7. The shadow of mortality falls across the throne, preparing the reader to look beyond David to a greater Son who will not grow old, whose kingdom shall not pass away. The narrative immediately raises the succession crisis, demonstrating that human kingship totters even at its zenith, magnifying our need for the Messianic King.
1Kgs.1.5: Adonijah's self-exaltation, 'I will be king' (ani emlok), echoes the satanic ambition of Isaiah 14 and the usurpation of Absalom. Reformed commentators note that he 'exalted himself,' a verb (mitnasse) used elsewhere of presumptuous pride. He prepared chariots and runners in imitation of his slain brother Absalom (2 Sam 15:1), revealing the persistent corruption of the heart even within the covenant household. David's failure to discipline him ('his father had not displeased him at any time') stands as a sober warning to covenantal fathers. The flesh ever seeks to seize what only sovereign election can bestow.
1Kgs.1.30: David's solemn oath confirms Solomon's accession by divine appointment, not merely paternal preference. 'As the LORD liveth' (chai-YHWH) binds the king's word to the living covenant God. This fulfills the prophetic word given to Nathan (2 Sam 12:24-25, where Solomon is named Jedidiah, 'beloved of YHWH'). The Reformed reader sees here that the line of promise is preserved not by primogeniture but by sovereign election, foreshadowing the gospel principle that 'they are not all Israel which are of Israel' (Rom 9:6). The kingdom is given, not seized.
1Kgs.1.39: Zadok the priest takes the horn of oil from the tabernacle and anoints Solomon. The convergence of priest, prophet (Nathan), and king at this anointing prefigures the threefold office of Christ. The horn (qeren) symbolizes strength and royal vigor; the oil (shemen) symbolizes the consecrating Spirit. The shout 'Long live king Solomon!' (yechi ha-melek) anticipates the eschatological acclamation of the true Anointed One. Calvin notes that legitimate authority is established by God's appointed means, not by self-promotion. The trumpet blast publishes the king's reign, as the gospel trumpet publishes Christ's.
1Kgs.2.3: David's deathbed charge to Solomon centers on covenantal obedience: 'keep the charge of the LORD thy God, to walk in his ways, to keep his statutes... as it is written in the law of Moses.' The fivefold piling up of Torah-vocabulary (mishmeret, derek, chuqqot, mitzvot, mishpatim) shows that the Davidic king must himself be a Torah-king (Deut 17:18-20). Reformed theology stresses that covenant promises do not nullify covenant responsibility; the conditional 'that thou mayest prosper' frames the throne under the Lord's law. Yet the eternal promise of v.4 will ultimately rest in Christ alone, who perfectly kept the way.
1Kgs.2.4: The conditional formulation 'if thy children take heed' coexists with the unconditional Davidic promise of 2 Samuel 7. Reformed covenant theology resolves this tension Christologically: the conditional dimension is fulfilled vicariously in the obedient Son, Jesus Christ, who alone walks before the LORD in truth with all His heart. Every earthly Davidide will fail this test; the throne therefore must rest on a perfect King. The phrase 'there shall not fail thee a man on the throne of Israel' finds its eternal Yes and Amen in the resurrection and ascension of Christ.
1Kgs.2.10: 'David slept with his fathers' employs the covenantal idiom for the death of a saint in hope. Though buried in the city named for him, David, like Abraham, sought 'a better country, that is, an heavenly' (Heb 11:16). His forty-year reign typifies the wilderness generation's testing yet ends in covenant rest. The Reformed reader sees in David's death the limitation of every type and the certainty of resurrection through David's greater Son. The royal sepulchre will one day be empty, as Peter argues in Acts 2:29-32.
1Kgs.2.27: The deposition of Abiathar fulfills the word spoken against the house of Eli (1 Sam 2:30-36). The narrator's note 'that he might fulfill the word of the LORD' (lemalleot et-debar YHWH) is theologically pivotal: human political acts serve sovereign prophetic purpose. Calvin observes that God's threats, like His promises, never fall to the ground. Zadok's exclusive priesthood now anticipates the new covenant priesthood of Christ, after the order of Melchizedek, displacing every faithless line.
1Kgs.3.5: At Gibeon, the LORD appears to Solomon in a dream and offers, 'Ask what I shall give thee.' This open-handed condescension reveals divine generosity toward the covenant king. The dream-revelation, valid in the pre-canonical period, was an extraordinary means now ceased. Reformed exegetes see this as a moment of testing: what the heart desires reveals what the heart truly worships. Solomon stands as a representative of Adamic kingship offered the choice between self-aggrandizement and submission to divine purpose. His response will define his reign and prefigure both the wisdom and the failure of human dominion.
1Kgs.3.12: 'Lo, I have given thee a wise and an understanding heart.' The verb natan ('I have given') marks wisdom as pure gift, not human achievement. James 1:5 echoes this: God gives wisdom liberally. Solomon's incomparable wisdom prefigures Christ, in whom 'are hid all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge' (Col 2:3). Yet Christ is greater than Solomon (Matt 12:42), for His wisdom is not bestowed but essential, not received but eternally His own. The greater than Solomon now dwells in His people by His Spirit, the spirit of wisdom and revelation.
1Kgs.3.16: The case of the two harlots vindicates Solomon's wisdom in concrete judicial form. The narrator deliberately introduces socially marginal women, demonstrating that Davidic justice extends to the outcast. The proposed division of the living child reveals true motherhood through love that yields possession. Reformed commentators have seen here a parable of the church: the false mother (compromised religion) is willing to see Christ divided; the true mother (faithful church) treasures Him whole. True wisdom in pastoral judgment discerns the heart, not merely the surface claim.
1Kgs.4.25: 'Judah and Israel dwelt safely, every man under his vine and under his fig tree.' This idyllic formula (Mic 4:4; Zech 3:10) presents the Solomonic peace as a typological foretaste of the eschatological kingdom. The shalom under Solomon is not yet the final shalom but a glimpse, soon to be lost. Reformed eschatology sees this verse fulfilled ultimately in Christ's millennial and consummate reign, when all redeemed humanity shall enjoy unbroken peace under the true Prince of Peace. The garden imagery recalls Eden and points forward to the New Jerusalem.
1Kgs.4.29: God gave Solomon 'wisdom and understanding exceeding much, and largeness of heart, even as the sand that is on the sea shore.' The simile, normally used of Abraham's seed (Gen 22:17), is here applied to wisdom, suggesting that the covenant promises blossom not only in numerical seed but in spiritual fruitfulness. Solomon's wisdom encompasses creation itself (v.33), foreshadowing the dominion mandate restored in Christ. Yet the sand-like multitude of his thoughts will eventually be drawn to many wives and many gods, warning that gifts unmoored from grace become snares.
1Kgs.5.5: Solomon's intent to build a house for the name of YHWH grounds the temple project in the prior promise of 2 Samuel 7:13. The temple is not Solomon's invention but YHWH's design. The 'name' (shem) theology indicates God's covenantal presence and self-revelation, not localized confinement. Reformed theology emphasizes that the temple was always typological, pointing to Christ's body (John 2:19-21) and to the church indwelt by the Spirit (Eph 2:21). Solomon thus serves the greater Son who builds the true house.
1Kgs.6.7: 'There was neither hammer nor axe nor any tool of iron heard in the house, while it was in building.' The stones were prepared at the quarry. This sacred silence reflects Exodus 20:25 and signifies that God's house is built without the violence of human craft. Reformed theology sees here a picture of sanctification: stones are shaped elsewhere (in providence and grace) and fitted noiselessly into the spiritual temple. Christ, the chief cornerstone, builds His church not with carnal weapons but by Word and Spirit (1 Pet 2:5).
1Kgs.6.12: While the temple is rising, the LORD interposes a word: 'Concerning this house... if thou wilt walk in my statutes... then will I perform my word.' The conditional reaffirms that magnificent buildings cannot substitute for covenantal obedience. Calvin warns that ceremony without holiness is abomination. The Davidic promise is sure in Christ, but the historical Solomonic enjoyment of the promise hinges on faithful walking. The verse anticipates the temple's eventual destruction precisely because the conditions were broken.
1Kgs.7.51: 'So was ended all the work that king Solomon made for the house of the LORD.' The completion notice (kallah ha-melakah) deliberately echoes Genesis 2:1-2 (the completion of creation) and Exodus 39:32 (the completion of the tabernacle). The temple is presented as a new creation and the climax of redemptive architecture. Reformed biblical theology recognizes this triple completion pattern (creation, tabernacle, temple) as ultimately fulfilled in the finished work of Christ ('It is finished,' John 19:30) and the consummate temple of Revelation 21.
1Kgs.8.10: When the priests came out of the holy place, the cloud (anan) filled the house of the LORD. This is the Shekinah, the visible manifestation of divine presence first encountered at Sinai (Exod 19), the tabernacle (Exod 40:34), and ultimately in the incarnation (John 1:14, 'tabernacled among us'). Reformed theology insists that God's presence is gracious condescension, not local confinement. The cloud signals covenantal acceptance of the temple. The same Shekinah-glory rests upon Christ on the Mount of Transfiguration, demonstrating Him as the true and greater temple.
1Kgs.8.30: 'Hearken thou to the supplication of thy servant... and when thou hearest, forgive.' Solomon's repeated petition (vv.30, 34, 36, 39, 50) makes forgiveness the heart of temple worship. The Hebrew salachta ('thou shalt forgive') is the verb reserved exclusively for divine pardon. Reformed soteriology rejoices in this: even the typological temple was a place of mercy. The greater temple, Christ crucified, is the eternal mercy seat (hilasterion, Rom 3:25). All true prayer turns toward Him as the Israelite turned toward this house.
1Kgs.8.46: 'For there is no man that sinneth not.' This anthropological realism in the very prayer of dedication acknowledges total depravity. Solomon, in his prime, knows that exile for sin is possible, even probable. Reformed theology reads this as a confession of universal sinfulness (cf. Eccl 7:20; Rom 3:23) and a petition for restorative grace. The provision for repentance 'in the land of them that carried them captives' anticipates Daniel's prayer (Dan 9) and the gospel call to all nations. Even from afar, prayer toward Christ secures forgiveness.
1Kgs.8.56: 'Blessed be the LORD, that hath given rest unto his people Israel, according to all that he promised.' Solomon's blessing affirms God's perfect faithfulness: 'There hath not failed one word of all his good promise.' Yet Hebrews 4 will later argue that this rest was not the final rest. The Reformed canonical reading sees Solomonic rest as a true but provisional pledge of the eternal Sabbath rest that 'remaineth therefore to the people of God,' obtained only through Christ. Every promise of God finds its yea in Him (2 Cor 1:20).
1Kgs.9.4: The LORD's second appearance to Solomon repeats the conditional structure: 'If thou wilt walk before me, as David thy father walked, in integrity of heart.' The standard is David, not as a sinless man, but as one who walked in covenantal sincerity (tom-levav). Reformed theology distinguishes between perfect obedience (required of Christ as covenant Head) and evangelical obedience (the believer's grateful walk). Solomon is here exhorted to the latter; his subsequent failure exposes the necessity of the former, fulfilled only in Christ.
1Kgs.10.1: The Queen of Sheba came to prove Solomon with hard questions. Her pilgrimage from a distant land foreshadows the eschatological in-gathering of the nations to Zion (Isa 60; Ps 72:10). Christ Himself cites her as a witness against unbelieving Israel (Matt 12:42). Reformed missiology sees the gospel mission as the antitype: Gentiles drawn by report of a greater than Solomon, finding wisdom and salvation. Her admission 'the half was not told me' anticipates the eternal increase of Christ's known glory.
1Kgs.11.1: 'But king Solomon loved many strange women.' The adversative 'but' (waw) marks the tragic pivot. The Torah explicitly forbade such alliances (Deut 7:3-4; 17:17). The verb 'loved' (ahav) signals covenantal allegiance misdirected. Reformed theology sees here that even unparalleled wisdom apart from sustained reliance on grace cannot preserve the soul. The king who built the temple becomes the king who builds high places. The gifted vessel proves his need of the keeping power of the greater King who alone never turns aside.
1Kgs.11.4: 'When Solomon was old... his wives turned away his heart after other gods: and his heart was not perfect with the LORD his God, as was the heart of David his father.' The contrast with David is striking: David sinned grievously yet maintained covenantal repentance. Solomon's heart was divided (lo shalem). The progression from compromise to apostasy illustrates the slippery slope of unequally yoked alliances. Calvin warns that no degree of past gifting secures present perseverance apart from continual dependence. The throne thus splits because the king's heart split first.
1Kgs.11.11: 'Forasmuch as this is done of thee... I will surely rend the kingdom from thee.' Divine judgment on covenant infidelity yet retains covenant faithfulness ('howbeit I will not rend away all... for David my servant's sake,' v.13). Reformed covenant theology sees here both retributive justice and electing grace. The kingdom is divided historically but the Davidic line preserved typologically, awaiting the Son of David who will reunite Israel and Judah and the nations under His scepter (Ezek 37:24-28).
1Kgs.12.16: 'What portion have we in David?' The northern tribes' rejection of Davidic rule echoes the cry of Sheba (2 Sam 20:1). Their phrase becomes ironically inverted in the gospel: those who reject David's Son have no portion in salvation. The schism is judgment for Solomon's sin (11:31-33) yet also the responsibility of Rehoboam's folly. Reformed theology recognizes both divine sovereignty and human accountability operating concurrently. The fragmentation of the kingdom illustrates the consequences of departing from covenant wisdom and anticipates the gospel reunion in Christ.
1Kgs.12.28: Jeroboam's golden calves at Bethel and Dan, with the words 'Behold thy gods, O Israel, which brought thee up out of the land of Egypt,' deliberately echo Aaron's apostasy (Exod 32:4). His political pragmatism births the great national sin that defines the Northern Kingdom for two centuries (called 'the sin of Jeroboam' repeatedly in Kings). Reformed worship principles condemn such inventions: any worship not commanded is idolatry, however convenient. The regulative principle stands behind the prophetic critique. State-engineered religion always corrupts.
1Kgs.13.2: An unnamed man of God prophesies against Jeroboam's altar: 'O altar, altar, thus saith the LORD; Behold, a child shall be born unto the house of David, Josiah by name.' This astonishing prediction names Josiah three centuries before his birth (cf. 2 Kgs 23:15-16). Reformed theology sees here unequivocal evidence of detailed divine foreknowledge and superintending sovereignty over history. The Davidic remedy stands ready before the apostasy is consummated. God writes future names into present judgments.
1Kgs.14.10: 'I will bring evil upon the house of Jeroboam, and will cut off from Jeroboam every male.' The covenantal curses for idolatry (Deut 28) fall first on the apostate northern dynasty. The 'sin of Jeroboam' becomes the template for every subsequent northern king. Reformed historical theology recognizes that institutional sin metastasizes through generations unless decisively repented. The promise of an unbroken Davidic dynasty (in Judah, by grace) stands in stark contrast to the recurring extinction of northern dynasties.
1Kgs.15.3: Of Abijam: 'his heart was not perfect with the LORD his God, as the heart of David his father.' Yet the very next verse declares, 'Nevertheless for David's sake did the LORD his God give him a lamp in Jerusalem.' The 'lamp' (nir) is a Davidic emblem (2 Sam 21:17; Ps 132:17), denoting the perpetuation of the dynasty for the sake of the covenant. Reformed theology rejoices in such grace: God preserves the line not by the worth of its bearers but by His covenantal commitment, ultimately for Christ's sake.
1Kgs.16.31: Ahab took Jezebel, daughter of Ethbaal king of the Zidonians, to wife, 'and went and served Baal, and worshipped him.' This intermarriage marks Israel's deepest descent. Baal-worship, fertility religion par excellence, is now the state cult. The narrative deliberately escalates: 'as if it had been a light thing to walk in the sins of Jeroboam.' Reformed exegesis recognizes apostasy as cumulative; sin grows when unchecked. The stage is set for Elijah, the great prophetic counter to Baalism, in whom the Reformed see a paradigm of bold, Word-ministry against state idolatry.
1Kgs.17.1: Elijah the Tishbite bursts upon the narrative without genealogy: 'As the LORD God of Israel liveth, before whom I stand, there shall not be dew nor rain these years, but according to my word.' The drought directly attacks Baal's claimed domain (storm and fertility). Elijah's posture, 'before whom I stand' (lifne asher amadti), defines prophetic ministry as sustained communion with the living God. Reformed pastors find here the pattern: the preacher's authority derives wholly from his standing in God's presence and faithfulness to His Word. James calls Elijah 'a man subject to like passions' (5:17), encouraging persevering prayer.
1Kgs.17.16: 'The barrel of meal wasted not, neither did the cruse of oil fail, according to the word of the LORD.' The Sidonian widow, a Gentile in Baal's homeland, becomes the recipient of covenant provision. Jesus cites this miracle (Luke 4:25-26) to indicate God's sovereign extension of mercy beyond ethnic Israel. Reformed soteriology sees here electing grace operating in the days of greatest apostasy: God preserves a remnant from the most unlikely places. The unfailing meal prefigures Christ, the Bread of Life, whose supply never exhausts.
1Kgs.17.21: Elijah stretched himself upon the child three times and cried, 'O LORD my God, I pray thee, let this child's soul come into him again.' The first explicit resurrection in canonical narrative occurs through prophetic intercession. The Hebrew nephesh ('soul') here denotes life-principle, distinct from but united with the body. Reformed theology sees in this restoration a typological pledge of Christ's resurrection power. The prophet who calls the dead to life prefigures the Son who is Himself the Resurrection and the Life.
1Kgs.18.24: 'The God that answereth by fire, let him be God.' The proposed test reaches Israel's foundational moment: YHWH revealed Himself by fire at Sinai (Exod 19:18) and consumed the first sacrifices (Lev 9:24). Elijah is calling Israel back to her covenantal origin. The Reformed reader perceives that true religion is determined by divine self-revelation, not human consensus. The contest is not which god is more useful, but which is real. The same fire will descend on Calvary in judgment-bearing form upon the true Sacrifice.
1Kgs.18.36: Elijah's prayer is brief, biblical, and Christ-honoring: 'LORD God of Abraham, Isaac, and of Israel, let it be known this day that thou art God in Israel, and that I am thy servant, and that I have done all these things at thy word.' Three concerns: God's glory, the prophet's commission, and the Word's authority. Reformed homiletics holds this as the model for ministry: the preacher does nothing of himself, only at the Word. Effective prayer rests in covenantal naming and submission.
1Kgs.18.38: 'Then the fire of the LORD fell, and consumed the burnt sacrifice, and the wood, and the stones, and the dust, and licked up the water that was in the trench.' The total consumption demonstrates absolute sovereignty. Calvin observes that water cannot quench what God determines to ignite. The Reformed reader sees in the consumed sacrifice a foreshadowing of Christ, on whom the consuming fire of divine wrath fell completely, that we might be spared. 'Our God is a consuming fire' (Heb 12:29); but in Christ the fire purifies rather than destroys.
1Kgs.19.4: Elijah, fleeing Jezebel, requests for himself that he might die: 'It is enough; now, O LORD, take away my life; for I am not better than my fathers.' The triumphant prophet of chapter 18 becomes the despondent fugitive of chapter 19. Reformed pastoral theology takes this realistic portrait seriously: the saints, even at the heights of usefulness, may sink into despair. Spurgeon famously preached on the minister's fainting fits from this text. God's response is not rebuke but provision of food, sleep, and renewed encounter.
1Kgs.20.28: 'Because the Syrians have said, The LORD is God of the hills, but he is not God of the valleys, therefore will I deliver all this great multitude into thine hand.' YHWH's universal sovereignty is vindicated against pagan localism. Reformed theology insists upon God's omnipresence and absolute lordship over every domain. Pagan religion always seeks to circumscribe deity; biblical religion confesses 'the earth is the LORD's, and the fulness thereof.' Even an ungodly Ahab benefits from this lesson, as God uses victories to glorify His name among the nations.
1Kgs.21.3: Naboth's reply to Ahab: 'The LORD forbid it me, that I should give the inheritance of my fathers unto thee.' The Hebrew chalilah li meYHWH ('a profanation to me from YHWH') reflects Levitical land theology (Lev 25:23-28; Num 36:7). The land is YHWH's, distributed by inheritance to be retained in tribal lines until Jubilee. Naboth is no obstinate landowner but a faithful covenantal steward. Reformed ethics affirms that biblical law transcends royal preference; Naboth's resistance is principled obedience. He becomes a martyr-figure, foreshadowing all who suffer for refusing to surrender what God has bestowed.
1Kgs.21.13: 'Naboth did blaspheme God and the king.' False witnesses, suborned by Jezebel, manipulate the very Torah they violate (Deut 17:6). The judicial murder of an innocent man, accomplished through perverted religious procedure, foreshadows the trial of Christ Himself, where false witnesses would again be marshaled (Matt 26:60-61). Reformed theology recognizes that institutional injustice often cloaks itself in religious legitimacy. Yet God sees, and the prophet comes.
1Kgs.21.19: 'In the place where dogs licked the blood of Naboth shall dogs lick thy blood, even thine.' Lex talionis is invoked through prophetic word. Elijah confronts Ahab in the very vineyard, demonstrating that prophetic ministry calls power to account. Reformed theology of prophecy recognizes the courageous pastoral office to declare God's judgment without partiality, even at risk of life. Ahab's later death (22:38) precisely fulfills this word, though delayed by repentance (vv.27-29), showing both the certainty and the patience of divine justice.
1Kgs.21.27: Ahab's response to the prophetic word is striking: 'he rent his clothes, and put sackcloth upon his flesh, and fasted, and lay in sackcloth, and went softly.' His humbling, though not regenerative, postpones full judgment ('I will not bring the evil in his days'). Reformed theology distinguishes between common contrition (which restrains temporal judgment) and saving repentance (which secures eternal life). God's responsiveness even to partial humbling shows His patience and the principled justice of His ways. None can charge Him with arbitrary harshness.
1Kgs.22.22: The vision of the heavenly council, where a 'lying spirit' is permitted to deceive Ahab through false prophets, is foundational for Reformed doctrine of providence and concurrence. God remains absolutely holy, yet sovereignly governs even the deceitful intentions of fallen spirits to accomplish judgment on those who have rejected truth (cf. 2 Thess 2:11). Calvin treats this passage carefully: God ordains the deception as just retribution upon Ahab, who has loved lies. The episode demonstrates that no creature acts independently of divine permission and ordinance.
1Kgs.22.34: 'And a certain man drew a bow at a venture, and smote the king of Israel between the joints of the harness.' The 'random' arrow guided to its precise target embodies Reformed doctrine of providence: there are no accidents in the moral universe. Proverbs 16:33 summarizes: 'The lot is cast into the lap; but the whole disposing thereof is of the LORD.' Ahab's disguise (v.30) cannot evade omniscient justice. The fulfillment of Elijah's word in 21:19 demonstrates that prophetic threat is not idle. Even history's stray arrows serve sovereign purpose.
1Pet.1.1: Peter addresses 'elect exiles' (eklektois parepidemois) of the diaspora—a profound theological identity. The believers are simultaneously chosen by God's sovereign decree and resident aliens in the world. Calvin notes that election is not a private comfort but a missionary identity: those marked out by God are necessarily strangers in a world that does not share their citizenship. The geographical sweep (Pontus, Galatia, Cappadocia, Asia, Bithynia) shows the gospel's penetration into Roman Asia Minor. This dual status—elect yet exiled—frames the entire letter, supplying the lens through which suffering, holiness, and submission must be understood. The Christian's primary identity is heavenly; earthly displacement is normal, not anomalous.
1Pet.1.4: The inheritance is described by three negated adjectives: imperishable (aphtharton), undefiled (amianton), and unfading (amaranton)—an alliterative triad in Greek. Unlike Israel's earthly inheritance in Canaan, which could be defiled, lost, or fade with apostasy, the believer's inheritance is 'kept in heaven' (tetereeenen)—a perfect passive participle indicating an ongoing state of divine preservation. The contrast with Roman culture is sharp: imperial inheritances were perishable, defiled by intrigue, and fading with dynasties. Peter pastorally redirects suffering exiles from the visible to the invisible, from the temporal to the eternal, grounding present endurance in future certainty guaranteed by God Himself.
1Pet.1.10: The prophets 'searched and inquired carefully' (exezetesan kai exeraunesan)—double compound verbs emphasizing diligent investigation. They prophesied of 'the grace that was to come,' the gospel itself. This verse establishes the unity of redemptive history: OT prophets and NT believers participate in the same gospel, though the prophets saw it dimly. Reformed covenant theology grounds itself partly here: there is one people of God, one salvation, one Christ, progressively revealed. The prophets did not understand a different religion; they served a future generation. This refutes dispensational severance of OT and NT salvation. The gospel was preached beforehand to Abraham (Gal. 3:8) and prophesied throughout Israel's history.
1Pet.1.12: Even angels 'long to look' (epithumousin parakypsai) into these things—the verb suggests bending over to peer into a wonder. The salvation revealed to the church is so glorious that unfallen angels stoop in fascination. This emphasizes the unique honor of the human gospel: redemption is not for angels (Heb. 2:16) but for sinners. Angels behold but do not partake. Reformed exposition uses this to humble the church's complacency: what dazzles the seraphim should not bore the saints. The Spirit's preached gospel is the very treasury into which heaven peers, and yet the church often treats it as commonplace.
1Pet.1.17: 'Conduct yourselves with fear (en phobo) throughout the time of your exile.' The fear of God is not servile dread but reverent awe before an impartial Judge who is also Father. Peter calls God 'Father' yet immediately invokes His judging impartiality—a paradox Reformed theology embraces. Adoption does not eliminate accountability; rather, it sets accountability in the context of love. 'Time of your exile' (paroikia)—the same root as 'sojourners' in 2:11—reinforces the letter's controlling metaphor. Believers are pilgrims under the gaze of a Father-Judge. This guards against both legalistic terror and antinomian presumption.
1Pet.1.25: 'The word of the Lord remains forever' (menei eis ton aiona). Peter quotes Isaiah 40:6-8 to ground the durability of the regenerative seed. Human flesh withers like grass, but the Word abides. Then Peter identifies this Word: 'this word is the good news that was preached to you' (to rema to euangelisthen eis humas). The OT prophetic word and the apostolic gospel are not two things but one—the abiding Word of the Lord. Reformed theology treasures this identification: Scripture's authority and durability rest on its divine origin. The same Word that created light in Genesis creates new life in regeneration, and it shall outlast every empire.
1Pet.2.4: Christ is 'a living stone' (lithon zonta)—a startling oxymoron combining stability with vitality. He was 'rejected by men' (apodedokimasmenon)—the same verb used in the LXX of Psalm 118:22—'but in the sight of God chosen and precious.' Peter establishes the great contrast between human estimation and divine valuation. The world's rejection of Christ is paralleled by the world's rejection of Christians, but God's election overrides both. Reformed exposition draws comfort here: the church is built of stones the world discards. Coming to Christ (proserchomenoi)—a present participle—is continuous, not merely initial; we keep coming.
1Pet.2.7: 'The honor is for you who believe' (humin oun he time tois pisteuousin). The same Stone that is precious to believers is 'a stone of stumbling and a rock of offense' to unbelievers (v. 8). The Reformed doctrine of double predestination is reflected here: the gospel produces opposite effects on opposite peoples, according to God's appointment. Christ is unavoidable—either foundation or stumbling block. The neutrality some claim is illusion. Peter is unflinching: rejection of the cornerstone is not merely bad judgment but appointed (etethesan) by God to expose the disobedient. The same sun melts wax and hardens clay.
1Pet.2.13: 'Be subject for the Lord's sake to every human institution' (pase anthropine ktisei). The phrase 'human institution' (literally 'human creation') is significant: Peter does not deify the state but locates it among created orders. Yet submission is commanded 'for the Lord's sake'—the motive is theological, not pragmatic. Reformed political theology distinguishes between the institution (ordained by God) and the officeholder (often flawed). Submission is not absolute; Acts 5:29 ('we must obey God rather than men') sets the limit. But the default disposition of the believer is submissive, peaceable, and orderly—a powerful apologetic in a Roman context where Christians were accused of sedition.
1Pet.2.21: 'For to this you have been called, because Christ also suffered for you, leaving you an example (hupogrammon), so that you might follow in his steps.' Hupogrammos referred to a writing-pattern over which a child traced letters. Christ's suffering is the template for Christian endurance. Reformed exposition is careful: Christ is example only after He is Substitute. Peter holds both together—the cross is exemplary because it is first redemptive. The believer cannot trace what has not first been written. This text undergirds the entire Petrine theology of suffering: Christians suffer not despite but because of their union with the suffering Christ.
1Pet.3.21: 'Baptism, which corresponds to this, now saves you' (baptisma)—a notoriously debated verse. Peter immediately qualifies: 'not as a removal of dirt from the body but as an appeal to God for a good conscience' (suneideeseos agathees eperotema). The salvific efficacy is not in the water but in the union with Christ's resurrection ('through the resurrection of Jesus Christ'). Reformed sacramentology treats baptism as a sign and seal of an inward reality, not as ex opere operato. The flood typology shows baptism marking the transition from old world to new, the wicked perishing while the elect are preserved through the waters.
1Pet.4.10: 'As each has received a gift (charisma), use it to serve one another, as good stewards (oikonomoi) of God's varied grace.' Every believer has a charisma; gifts are not for an elite. The image of stewardship implies accountability—gifts are entrusted, not owned. 'Varied grace' (poikiles charitos)—the same adjective as 'various trials' in 1:6—suggests that grace is multifaceted, fitting every situation. Reformed ecclesiology grounds the universal priesthood of believers here: gifts distributed by the Spirit equip the whole body for service. Self-serving use of gifts is theft from the household of God.
1Pet.4.17: 'It is time for judgment to begin at the household of God.' Peter draws on Ezekiel 9:6, where judgment began at the sanctuary. The church's sufferings are part of God's purifying judgment, not punitive condemnation but refining discipline. 'And if it begins with us, what will be the outcome for those who do not obey the gospel?' Reformed theology distinguishes between the temporal chastening of God's family and the eschatological wrath upon the disobedient. The argument is a fortiori: if the elect are tested by fire, the reprobate face an immeasurably worse end. Suffering, then, is mercy compared to judgment.
1Pet.5.2: 'Shepherd the flock of God that is among you' (poimanate to en humin poimnion)—the verb poimaino is the same Christ used to commission Peter (John 21:16). 'Exercising oversight (episkopountes), not under compulsion but willingly, as God would have you; not for shameful gain but eagerly.' Reformed ecclesiology finds here the marriage of two offices: poimen (shepherd/pastor) and episkopos (overseer/bishop)—the same office, two functions. The motive must be voluntary, the heart eager, the gain spiritual not financial. Peter, who once denied the Shepherd, now charges undershepherds. Pastoral ministry is dangerous when motivated by power, money, or coercion.
1Sam.1.10: Hannah's bitterness of soul (marat nephesh) drives her to the sanctuary at Shiloh, where she pours out her anguish before the LORD of hosts. Reformed exposition recognizes here the doctrine of prayer as the soul's recourse to covenant grace: barrenness, the recurring motif from Sarah and Rebekah, signals Israel's own spiritual sterility under the failed priesthood of Eli. Hannah does not merely seek a child; she seeks a deliverer. Her weeping anticipates the cry of every elect soul who knows that fruitfulness must come by sovereign mercy, not human contrivance. The Spirit moves Hannah to vow her son back to Yahweh, foreshadowing the pattern of substitutionary dedication fulfilled in Christ.
1Sam.1.11: Hannah's vow invokes the Nazirite pattern (no razor on his head), binding the unborn child to lifelong consecration. The threefold address (LORD of hosts, your servant, remember me) rehearses covenant language reaching back to Genesis. To 'remember' (zakar) is not mere recall but covenantal action; God remembers Noah, Abraham, and now Hannah. Reformed theology sees in this the unbreakable link between God's electing remembrance and the salvation of his people. The hesed of Yahweh moves him to act on behalf of the lowly, overturning human strength. Samuel becomes the first answer to a prayer that ultimately culminates in the Greater Samuel, the Anointed One who establishes God's house forever.
1Sam.1.13: Eli misjudges Hannah as drunken because her lips moved without sound. The episode rebukes superficial spiritual diagnosis. Reformed pastoral theology learns from Eli's failure: the inner life of prayer cannot be evaluated by appearances. Hannah's silent intercession testifies that true prayer is heart-traffic with God, not performance for clergy. The high priest's blindness here also exposes the corruption of the whole Elide ministry, which has lost the discernment necessary for shepherding souls.
1Sam.1.20: The naming of Samuel (Shemu'el, 'heard of God') memorializes Yahweh's responsiveness to covenant prayer. Reformed exegetes note the providential precision: God ordains both the means (Hannah's petition) and the end (a prophet to anoint kings). Samuel will stand at the hinge of redemptive history, transitioning Israel from the chaos of judges to the monarchy that finds its telos in David's greater Son. Every birth narrative in Scripture, from Isaac to John the Baptist to Christ, testifies that the kingdom advances by divine intrusion into human impossibility. Hannah's son is the firstfruits of that pattern, a prophet conceived not by flesh but by promise.
1Sam.1.27: 'For this child I prayed, and the LORD has granted me my petition.' The verb sha'al ('asked') puns repeatedly through the Saul/Samuel narrative; ironically, Hannah asks rightly, while Israel later asks (sha'al) for a king like the nations. The juxtaposition is theological: petitions offered in faith and submission yield covenant blessing; demands made in unbelief yield judgment. Hannah models the elect's posture, receiving life as gift, not entitlement. She immediately surrenders the answer back to the Giver, illustrating the regenerate heart that holds creaturely goods loosely because it has tasted the Creator's hesed.
1Sam.2.1: Hannah's song erupts in covenantal praise that becomes the template for Mary's Magnificat. 'My horn is exalted in the LORD' invokes the qeren imagery of royal strength, the same horn that will fill Samuel's flask to anoint David and ultimately points to the 'horn of salvation' raised in the house of David (Luke 1:69). Reformed theology hears here the great reversal: Yahweh humbles the proud and exalts the meek. Hannah is no mere pious mother; she is a prophetess whose song defines the trajectory of the entire book and the Davidic-messianic hope.
1Sam.2.2: 'There is none holy like the LORD; there is no rock (tsur) besides our God.' The metaphor of God as Rock anchors Reformed soteriology: he is the immovable refuge of his elect. Hannah confesses God's incommunicable attributes (holiness, uniqueness, immutability) against the syncretism creeping into Shiloh. Her theology rebukes Eli's sons even before Samuel does. The exclusivity of Yahweh's deity is not abstract dogma but pastoral comfort: no rival rock can crumble, no idol can save.
1Sam.2.6: 'The LORD kills and brings to life; he brings down to Sheol and raises up.' Hannah confesses divine sovereignty over life and death, a foundational tenet of Reformed theology against any synergistic notion of salvation. The pattern of death-then-resurrection prefigures the gospel: God does not merely improve sinners but raises the dead. This verse undergirds the doctrine of effectual calling. The same Lord who sovereignly opened Hannah's womb sovereignly opens the dead heart, and ultimately raised his Son from the grave on the third day, securing all his promises.
1Sam.2.8: 'He raises up the poor from the dust; he lifts the needy from the ash heap to make them sit with princes and inherit a seat of honor.' The theme of divine reversal is now sung programmatically. Reformed expositors hear here the gospel pattern of justification: the unworthy are seated with the worthy because God has acted. The throne is God's possession, set on pillars of the earth he founded; thus the king he chooses shares in cosmic rule, foreshadowing the Son who is seated at the right hand.
1Sam.2.10: 'He will give strength to his king and exalt the horn of his anointed (mashiach).' This is the first occurrence of mashiach as a royal title in 1 Samuel, and remarkably it is spoken before any king has been requested. The Spirit places messianic prophecy on Hannah's lips: God's plan from eternity is to rule the earth through his Anointed. The verse arches over the entire books of Samuel, finding partial fulfillment in David and ultimate fulfillment in Jesus Christ, the true Mashiach whose horn is exalted at the Father's right hand.
1Sam.2.12: 'The sons of Eli were worthless men; they did not know the LORD.' The verdict on Hophni and Phinehas is devastating: priests by blood who lack covenant knowledge (yada). Reformed exposition presses the point that ministerial succession means nothing without regenerate knowledge of God. The Elide priests prefigure every nominal church office filled by unconverted men. Their corruption justifies the divine ax laid to the root of their priesthood.
1Sam.2.25: 'If someone sins against a man, God will mediate for him, but if a man sins against the LORD, who can intercede?' Eli's rebuke unwittingly confesses the need for a divine-human mediator, supplied only in Christ. The verse continues: 'they would not listen to their father, for it was the will of the LORD to put them to death.' Reformed exposition embraces the hard doctrine of judicial hardening: God justly confirms the impenitent in their rebellion. Hophni and Phinehas illustrate that a corrupt priesthood ripens for judgment, paving the way for a faithful priest after God's own heart.
1Sam.2.30: 'Those who honor me I will honor, and those who despise me shall be lightly esteemed.' The principle of covenant retribution is announced by the man of God. Reformed theology distinguishes this from works-righteousness: it describes the moral order God maintains within the covenant community. The Elides forfeited honor by despising the LORD's offerings; Hannah and her son will inherit dignity by honoring him. The principle culminates in Christ, who because he humbled himself was given the name above every name.
1Sam.2.35: 'I will raise up for myself a faithful priest, who shall do according to what is in my heart and in my mind.' This prophecy reaches beyond Zadok to the Lord Jesus, the eternal High Priest after the order of Melchizedek. Reformed covenant theology sees here the demolition of the Elide line and the promise of a priesthood that cannot fail. The 'sure house' (bayit ne'eman) builds vocabulary that will reappear in Nathan's oracle (2 Sam 7), where David is promised a sure house. Priest and king converge in Christ.
1Sam.3.1: 'The word of the LORD was rare in those days; there was no frequent vision.' The narrative diagnosis of Israel's spiritual famine. Reformed expositors note that the silencing of revelation is itself an act of judgment, akin to Amos 8:11. Yet God is preparing to break the silence through a child. The dim lamp of Eli (v. 2) symbolizes the failing covenant administration; the lamp of God still burning (v. 3) signals that grace has not been extinguished. Where God's people seem most barren, his sovereign purpose is most active.
1Sam.3.13: 'For I have told him that I am about to punish his house forever, for the iniquity that he knew.' Eli's sin is not active rebellion but failure to restrain his sons. Reformed pastoral theology presses this: spiritual leaders bear responsibility for those under their oversight. Tolerated sin in the household of God invites covenant judgment. The text underscores that knowledge of evil without action is itself culpable, foreshadowing the prophetic critique of negligent shepherds.
1Sam.3.19: 'The LORD was with him and let none of his words fall to the ground.' This idiom certifies prophetic inspiration: every word Samuel speaks God ratifies. Reformed bibliology grounds itself here, in the covenant promise that the Lord stands behind his prophet's utterance. Samuel becomes the standard against which later prophets, and ultimately the apostolic word, are measured. The presence of Yahweh ('immo) is the covenant signature later fulfilled in Immanuel.
1Sam.4.3: 'Why has the LORD defeated us today before the Philistines? Let us bring the ark of the covenant of the LORD here.' Israel's question is right but their remedy is superstitious. They treat the ark as a talisman rather than seeking the God of the ark. Reformed exposition warns against sacramentalism that severs the sign from the reality, the symbol from saving faith. The ark cannot save those who do not repent; the covenant is not a magical formula but a relationship of faith and obedience.
1Sam.4.11: 'And the ark of God was captured, and the two sons of Eli, Hophni and Phinehas, died.' The unthinkable has happened: Yahweh's throne-chest is in pagan hands, and the priestly line falls in a single day. Reformed exposition sees this as God himself going into self-imposed exile to expose the futility of presumptuous covenant claims. He will defend his own glory abroad rather than be associated with corrupted worship at home. The narrative anticipates Ezekiel's vision of the kavod departing from Jerusalem.
1Sam.4.21: 'She named the child Ichabod, saying, The glory has departed from Israel.' The kavod, the weighty manifest presence, has lifted. Reformed theology recognizes this as covenantal judgment: God's presence is not bound to architecture but to faithfulness. Yet Ichabod's birth coincides with the anointing-prophet Samuel's rise, and the future ascension of David. Departure of glory is never final for the elect people; it points forward to its return in greater glory in Christ, who tabernacled among us.
1Sam.5.4: Dagon falls before the ark, his head and hands cut off on the threshold. The pagan deity, helpless before Yahweh's mere presence, prefigures the cosmic triumph at the cross where Christ disarms the principalities. Reformed exposition celebrates this as God defending his own glory in exile; even when his people are unfaithful, Yahweh vindicates his name among the nations. The theme of mocked idols (Isa 46) finds vivid narrative here.
1Sam.5.11: 'Send away the ark of the God of Israel, and let it return to its own place, that it may not kill us and our people.' The Philistines confess Yahweh's terrible holiness in their own way. Reformed exposition notes the irony: pagans recognize God's power before Israel does. The ark journeys through Philistine cities, conquering without an army, foreshadowing the gospel that goes forth conquering by its own inherent power.
1Sam.6.19: The men of Beth-shemesh die for looking irreverently into the ark. The episode reinforces the holiness (qodesh) of God: even covenant Israel cannot trifle with the kapporeth, the mercy seat where atonement was made. Reformed theology insists that God is approachable only on his own terms, through appointed mediation. The ark's journey home foreshadows Christ's resurrection ascent and the principle that grace and holiness are inseparable.
1Sam.7.9: Samuel offers a nursing lamb as a whole burnt offering, and the LORD answers with thunder against the Philistines. Reformed exposition unites worship and warfare: the prayers of the saints, joined to sacrificial typology, are the means by which God overthrows his enemies. The lamb foreshadows the Lamb whose once-for-all sacrifice secures victory over all spiritual foes. The thunder of Yahweh, like Sinai, recalls covenant inauguration and divine warrior imagery.
1Sam.7.12: Samuel raised a stone and called it Ebenezer, 'the stone of help,' saying, 'Till now the LORD has helped us.' The memorial confesses cumulative covenant faithfulness. Reformed piety treasures the practice of remembering particular providences. Ebenezer is preached gospel: every past mercy guarantees future grace, because the same hesed-keeping God reigns. The hymn 'Here I raise my Ebenezer' draws this verse into the church's living devotion.
1Sam.8.5: 'Appoint for us a king to judge us like all the nations.' The fatal request. The elders' demand mimics Deuteronomy 17:14 but inverts its spirit; they want a king like the nations rather than a king under Yahweh. Reformed political theology recognizes here that the sin is not monarchy per se but the desire to be like the world, rejecting theocratic distinctiveness. The covenant community, then and now, is tempted to trade its peculiar identity for cultural respectability.
1Sam.8.7: 'They have not rejected you, but they have rejected me from being king over them.' Yahweh interprets the request as covenant apostasy. The kingship of God, established at Sinai (Exod 15:18), is being repudiated. Yet, remarkably, God grants their request, working judgment and grace simultaneously: through this very monarchy he will raise up the Davidic king who fulfills both the people's desire and his own redemptive plan. Reformed theology marvels at this providence: God overrules human sin to advance his kingdom.
1Sam.8.18: 'In that day you will cry out because of your king, whom you have chosen for yourselves, but the LORD will not answer you in that day.' The somber warning enumerates the king's tyranny, his taking (laqach) of sons, daughters, fields, and tithes. Reformed expositors see here the exposure of human kingship's idolatry. Only the King who gives himself, rather than takes, can satisfy. Christ inverts the pattern: 'the Son of Man came not to be served but to serve.'
1Sam.8.20: 'That we may also be like all the nations, and that our king may judge us and go out before us and fight our battles.' The threefold demand reveals Israel's loss of confidence in Yahweh as Judge and Warrior. Reformed exposition diagnoses unbelief: when God seems delayed, his people seek visible substitutes. The verse exposes the heart of sinful religion, demanding tangible saviors when the invisible covenant Lord is sufficient. The cure is not new structures but renewed faith in the King who has not abdicated.
1Sam.9.2: Saul is introduced as 'a handsome young man... from his shoulders upward he was taller than any of the people.' The narrator's emphasis on outward appearance signals dramatic irony; Israel will get a king who looks the part but lacks the heart.
