Reformed expositors hear the foreshadowing of 1 Sam 16:7, 'man looks on the outward appearance, but the LORD looks on the heart.' Saul is the king Israel chose; David will be the king God chooses.
1Sam.9.16: 'Tomorrow about this time I will send to you a man from the land of Benjamin, and you shall anoint him to be prince (nagid) over my people Israel.' Notice nagid, not melek; God designates Saul as a prince-leader, accommodating Israel's desire while maintaining his own sovereignty. Reformed theology finds here divine condescension: God works through, not in spite of, fallen human structures. The mashiach motif begins, though it will not find full expression until David and ultimately Christ.
1Sam.9.17: 'Behold, the man of whom I spoke to you! This one shall restrain my people.' The verb 'atsar ('restrain, rule') describes the king's curbing function. Reformed political theology has long seen civil government as God's instrument to restrain sin in a fallen world. Saul will fail at this task internally; the gospel King restrains evil ultimately by transformation, writing the law on the heart. Office is given for service, not self-aggrandizement.
1Sam.10.1: 'Then Samuel took a flask of oil and poured it on his head and kissed him and said, Has not the LORD anointed you to be prince over his heritage?' The first royal anointing in Israel's monarchy. Oil (shemen) sets apart for sacred office, and the recipient becomes mashiach Yahweh. Reformed theology grounds the threefold office (prophet, priest, king) here; all are anointed offices fulfilled supremely in Jesus the Christos. Saul's anointing is genuine but provisional, anticipating the true Anointed One.
1Sam.10.6: 'The Spirit of the LORD will rush upon you, and you will prophesy with them and be turned into another man.' The ruach of Yahweh empowers Saul for office, evidencing that Old Testament theocratic kingship requires divine enablement. Yet this Spirit-coming is not regeneration; it is charismatic equipping that can be removed (16:14). Reformed pneumatology distinguishes between the Spirit's empowering for office and his indwelling sanctification of the elect, a distinction starkly illustrated as Saul's career unravels.
1Sam.10.9: 'God gave him another heart.' The phrase is striking yet limited; God grants Saul a new disposition fit for kingship, but not the new covenant heart promised in Ezekiel 36. Reformed exposition distinguishes a temporary endowment from saving regeneration. The same God who turns hearts can also harden; Saul's later trajectory shows that without persevering grace, even royal gifting cannot save the soul.
1Sam.10.24: 'Long live the king!' Israel acclaims Saul publicly. The crowd's joy is sincere but spiritually shallow; they have what they wanted. Reformed exposition warns that getting one's idolatrous request is itself a form of judgment. Yet behind the acclamation God sets the stage for the deeper drama, in which a Bethlehemite shepherd will rise and a greater Son of David will be hailed by even larger crowds, only to be crucified.
1Sam.11.6: 'And the Spirit of God rushed upon Saul when he heard these words, and his anger was greatly kindled.' The Spirit-empowerment for war, like the judges, vindicates Saul's kingship at Jabesh-gilead. Reformed exposition notes that even faithless rulers are used providentially when God wills to deliver his people. The episode is a high point in Saul's reign, soon to be eclipsed by his self-willed disobedience. The pattern shows that office without heart cannot endure.
1Sam.11.13: 'Not a man shall be put to death this day, for today the LORD has worked salvation in Israel.' Saul, in his best moment, recognizes God as Savior and exercises clemency. Reformed expositors mourn that this gracious posture does not last. The early Saul illustrates that natural gifts and even Spirit-empowerment for office cannot substitute for the renewed heart. Mercy rooted in the new covenant alone perseveres.
1Sam.12.22: 'For the LORD will not forsake his people, for his great name's sake, because it has pleased the LORD to make you a people for himself.' The doctrine of perseverance grounded in election: God's covenant fidelity rests not on Israel's merit but on his own name and good pleasure. Reformed theology rests every saint's assurance here. The hesed of Yahweh is invincible because his glory is bound to the salvation of his people. Romans 11 echoes this verse for the church.
1Sam.12.23: 'Far be it from me that I should sin against the LORD by ceasing to pray for you.' Samuel models intercessory ministry as a covenant duty, not optional piety. To withhold intercession is sin. Reformed pastoral theology treasures this verse as a charter for prayerful watchcare. Samuel is a type of the Greater Intercessor, Christ, who 'always lives to make intercession' for those who draw near to God through him.
1Sam.12.24: 'Only fear the LORD and serve him faithfully with all your heart. For consider what great things he has done for you.' Reformed piety knits fear and faithful service to the recollection of redemption. Gratitude for past grace fuels present obedience. The verse becomes a covenantal sermon-ending: remember what God has done, and let that fuel a life of holy fear. This is not legalism but the third use of the law, sanctifying the redeemed heart.
1Sam.13.13: 'You have done foolishly. You have not kept the command of the LORD your God.' Saul's unauthorized sacrifice at Gilgal exposes the deepest fault of his kingship: presumption upon priestly office. Reformed exposition sees the king-priest distinction guarded here until the Melchizedekian Christ unites them. Saul's two-year reign ends not because his sin was uniquely heinous but because it manifests a heart that will not submit to revealed word. Partial obedience is full disobedience.
1Sam.13.14: 'The LORD has sought out a man after his own heart, and the LORD has commanded him to be prince over his people.' The first announcement of David, though unnamed. 'After his own heart' (kilvavo) does not mean morally perfect but covenantally responsive, the kind of heart that delights in God's law. Reformed theology sees here a hint of regeneration: David, the type of Christ, embodies the heart of the new covenant promised in Jeremiah 31.
1Sam.14.6: 'Nothing can hinder the LORD from saving by many or by few.' Jonathan's confession of God's sovereign sufficiency is one of the great theological lights in 1 Samuel. Reformed soteriology echoes this principle: salvation does not depend on numerical or material strength, but on the omnipotent will of God. Jonathan's faith stands in stark contrast to his father's fearful disobedience and prefigures the courage of David, and ultimately of Christ, who saves by the seeming weakness of the cross.
1Sam.14.24: 'Saul had laid an oath on the people, saying, Cursed be the man who eats food until it is evening and I am avenged on my enemies.' The rash oath weakens his army and exposes Saul's tyrannical confusion of personal vengeance with covenant obedience. Reformed exposition contrasts this with David, who refuses personal vengeance against Saul. The kingdom marred by Saul cannot endure; only a king who curbs himself for his people's sake will found a lasting house.
1Sam.14.45: 'Shall Jonathan die, who has worked this great salvation in Israel? Far from it!' The people's intervention rescues Jonathan from Saul's rash oath. Reformed expositors observe how Saul's reign devolves into impulsive vows and disordered zeal, while Jonathan's deliverance points forward to the truer salvation accomplished by another, where the Father does not spare his Son but gives him up for the people who deserved death.
1Sam.15.3: 'Now go and strike Amalek and devote to destruction (haram) all that they have.' The herem command against Amalek fulfills Exodus 17:14. Reformed exposition treats this not as ethnic cleansing but as eschatological judgment delegated to the covenant king. Amalek typifies persistent enmity against God's people; the destruction prefigures the final destruction of all who refuse to bow to the Anointed. Saul's failure here is failure to execute God's holy war at the level of total obedience.
1Sam.15.29: 'The Glory of Israel will not lie or have regret, for he is not a man, that he should have regret.' The doctrine of divine immutability is asserted in the same chapter that twice describes God 'regretting' Saul's kingship (vv. 11, 35). Reformed theologians resolve the apparent tension by distinguishing God's eternal counsel from his covenantal interaction with creatures. The Netzach Yisrael, the Eternal One, does not change his mind in the human sense; his unchanging nature secures every promise to the elect.
1Sam.16.1: 'Fill your horn with oil, and go. I will send you to Jesse the Bethlehemite, for I have provided for myself a king among his sons.' The verb ra'ah ('to see, provide') puns on the chapter's theme of seeing rightly. God himself selects the king. Reformed exposition delights in the doctrine of unconditional election here personified: Jesse's youngest, unconsidered by his own father, is the chosen one. Bethlehem rises into salvation-historical view; the city that will birth the Savior begins receiving prophetic notice.
1Sam.16.13: 'Then Samuel took the horn of oil and anointed him in the midst of his brothers. And the Spirit of the LORD rushed upon David from that day forward.' The horn (qeren), full and overflowing, contrasts with the small flask used for Saul. The Spirit's permanent abiding (mei-hayom hahu) marks David as the true mashiach who will not lose the Spirit's empowerment. Reformed theology sees in this enduring Spirit-anointing a typological anticipation of Christ, who is anointed without measure (John 3:34) and pours that Spirit on his elect.
1Sam.16.14: 'Now the Spirit of the LORD departed from Saul, and a harmful spirit from the LORD tormented him.' The Spirit's withdrawal from Saul is contemporaneous with his descent on David. The kingdom shifts. Reformed exposition handles the 'harmful spirit from the LORD' as a permitted instrument under sovereign providence, akin to the deceiving spirit in 1 Kings 22. God is not the author of evil but uses evil agents within his decree to discipline the rebellious.
1Sam.17.26: 'Who is this uncircumcised Philistine, that he should defy the armies of the living God?' David's question reframes the conflict theologically. The 'living God' (elohim chayyim) confronts the dead idols of Philistia. Reformed exposition emphasizes that David's confidence is not in his sling but in covenant identity. The uncircumcised stand outside God's covenant; the armies of the living God cannot finally lose. The verse trains the elect to view all spiritual conflict in covenant terms.
1Sam.17.37: 'The LORD who delivered me from the paw of the lion and from the paw of the bear will deliver me from the hand of this Philistine.' David reasons from past providence to present faith, the very logic Samuel modeled with Ebenezer. Reformed theology treasures this experiential argument: every deliverance becomes a foundation for the next. The shepherd's faith is the king's faith and ultimately becomes the church's faith, which sings, 'He who has delivered us from so great a death will deliver us.'
1Sam.17.50: 'So David prevailed over the Philistine with a sling and with a stone, and struck the Philistine and killed him. There was no sword in the hand of David.' The narrator stresses the absence of conventional weaponry. Reformed exposition celebrates the gospel pattern: God overcomes the strong by the weak (1 Cor 1:27). The shepherd's stone foreshadows the stone the builders rejected which became the cornerstone, by whom the great enemy was crushed at Calvary.
1Sam.18.1: 'The soul of Jonathan was knit to the soul of David, and Jonathan loved him as his own soul.' The covenant friendship of Jonathan and David is one of Scripture's most beautiful illustrations of self-giving hesed. Jonathan, the natural heir, recognizes God's choice and submits, surrendering crown rights in love. Reformed exposition sees here a foretaste of the renunciation of self for Christ's kingdom; Jonathan loves David in a way that anticipates the believer's love for the greater David.
1Sam.18.4: Jonathan strips off his robe and gives it to David along with his armor, sword, and bow. The transfer of royal regalia symbolically passes the kingdom. Reformed exposition sees a profound type of conversion: the believer surrenders his own credentials to be clothed with the righteousness of the true King. Jonathan's voluntary divestment models John the Baptist's posture, 'He must increase, but I must decrease,' and the saint's exchange of self-righteousness for Christ.
1Sam.18.7: 'Saul has struck down his thousands, and David his ten thousands.' The women's song ignites Saul's envy. The contrast between the rejected and the rising king sharpens. Reformed exposition observes how God's elective decree often produces hostility from the formerly favored; the natural Saul cannot tolerate the chosen David, just as the natural man hates the elect of God. Yet the providence is irreversible, the kingdom belongs to David.
1Sam.18.14: 'David had success in all his undertakings, for the LORD was with him.' The threefold refrain of this chapter, 'the LORD was with David,' tracks the covenant presence that defines Davidic kingship. Reformed theology sees the same idiom applied to Joseph and ultimately to Christ ('Immanu-El'). The success is not native ability but covenant accompaniment. The believer too prospers in proportion to the abiding presence of the Spirit.
1Sam.19.10: Saul's spear hurled at David, again, manifests the murderous hatred of the rejected king. Reformed exposition sees the unmasked enmity: those who rebel against God's anointing inevitably rage against God's anointed. The pattern climaxes when the Sanhedrin and Pilate unite to murder the true David, the greater Anointed. David's escape, like Christ slipping through hostile crowds in the Gospels, is providential preservation of the messianic line. The hand that flings the spear cannot reach the man God has chosen, for the LORD himself shields the future king. Every assault on the elect of God is restrained by divine purpose, until the appointed hour.
1Sam.20.14: 'If I am still alive, show me the steadfast love (hesed) of the LORD, that I may not die.' Jonathan invokes covenant hesed not only for himself but his descendants, foreseeing his own death and trusting David's fidelity. Reformed exposition sees a noble model of dying faith. The covenant transcends individuals; it secures the next generation. David's later kindness to Mephibosheth fulfills this oath and pictures the gospel's reach to Adam's lame children.
1Sam.20.42: 'Go in peace, because we have sworn both of us in the name of the LORD.' The covenant between David and Jonathan is sealed with divine oath. Reformed exposition treasures this as a model of covenantal friendship grounded not in sentiment but in shared submission to Yahweh. The oath outlives Jonathan's death and binds David to bless his house in the person of Mephibosheth.
1Sam.21.6: David eats the bread of the Presence, the showbread reserved for priests. Jesus appeals to this episode (Matt 12) to defend Sabbath mercy and to reveal his greater authority. Reformed exposition sees in David's necessity a foreshadowing of Christ's kingdom, in which the Son of Man is Lord of the sacred and dispenses mercy to the hungry. Ceremonial law yields to greater redemptive realities embodied in the true King.
1Sam.22.2: 'Everyone who was in distress, in debt, or bitter in soul gathered to him, and he became commander over them.' David's mighty men begin as society's rejects. Reformed expositors hear the gospel pattern: the King gathers the despised and unworthy and forms them into a kingdom. Christ likewise calls not the righteous but sinners; his church is the assembly of the indebted and bitter who find sufficiency in the Anointed.
1Sam.22.18: Doeg the Edomite slaughters eighty-five priests at Saul's command. The judgment of Eli's house (3:11-14) is now executed through Saul's wickedness, illustrating that prophetic word is fulfilled even by ungodly instruments. Reformed exposition holds this providence in reverent tension: God ordains the end and the means, yet men remain culpable. The lone survivor, Abiathar, escapes to David, signaling the transfer of legitimate priesthood toward the Davidic king.
1Sam.24.6: 'The LORD forbid that I should do this thing to my lord, the LORD's anointed, to put out my hand against him, seeing he is the LORD's anointed.' David refuses to lift hand against Saul. The mashiach Yahweh is sacrosanct because of office, not personal merit. Reformed political ethics finds here a foundational principle: God's appointed authorities, however flawed, are not removed by private vengeance but by divine providence. The greater David likewise refused to vindicate himself before earthly powers.
1Sam.24.12: 'May the LORD judge between me and you, may the LORD avenge me against you, but my hand shall not be against you.' David's plea models the saint's renunciation of self-vindication. Reformed exposition links this to Romans 12, 'Vengeance is mine, I will repay.' The believer entrusts justice to the only righteous Judge. David's refusal to lift his hand is a foretaste of Christ's submission to the Father's vindication, refusing to call legions of angels.
1Sam.25.10: Nabal scornfully asks, 'Who is David? Who is the son of Jesse?' His contempt mirrors Saul's earlier dismissal. Reformed exposition observes that the world's fool (and Nabal's name means 'fool') always despises the LORD's anointed. The pattern holds: the rich and self-secure cannot recognize true royalty. Christ too was despised as the carpenter's son, and his greater kingdom likewise hidden from the wise.
1Sam.25.32: 'Blessed be the LORD, the God of Israel, who sent you this day to meet me!' David recognizes Abigail as God's providential intervention preventing his bloodguilt. Reformed theology delights in such providences: God restrains his servants from rash sin through unexpected means. Abigail's wisdom, eloquence, and humility model the prudent woman of Proverbs and prefigure the Bride of Christ who counsels her Lord's mercy.
1Sam.25.39: 'Blessed be the LORD who has avenged the insult I received at the hand of Nabal.' Nabal's death is direct divine judgment, vindicating David's restraint. Reformed exposition observes that vengeance belongs to the Lord; the believer who refuses to take it sees God act in his own time. The Nabal narrative trains David and the reader to wait for the Lord's recompense rather than seizing it.
1Sam.26.9: 'Do not destroy him, for who can put out his hand against the LORD's anointed and be guiltless?' A second chance to kill Saul, a second refusal. The repetition cements David's character: he will not seize the kingdom by violence but receive it from God's hand. Reformed expositors compare this to Christ refusing to call legions of angels in Gethsemane; the kingdom comes by submission, not coup.
1Sam.26.23: 'The LORD rewards every man for his righteousness and his faithfulness.' David appeals to divine retributive justice. Reformed exposition reads this not as merit-based salvation but as the moral order God maintains within his covenant economy. David's faithfulness is itself a gift of grace, and its reward is itself gracious. The principle finds eschatological fulfillment when the King returns to render to each according to his works.
1Sam.27.1: 'Now I shall perish one day by the hand of Saul.' David's despair, despite repeated deliverances, exposes the saint's susceptibility to fear. Reformed exposition gently rebukes the temptation to trust circumstances over promises. David seeks refuge among Philistines, contradicting his earlier confessions; yet God preserves him even in the lapse, demonstrating that perseverance ultimately rests on divine fidelity, not human consistency.
1Sam.28.6: 'When Saul inquired of the LORD, the LORD did not answer him, either by dreams, or by Urim, or by prophets.' The threefold means of revelation is closed against Saul. Reformed exposition warns against the deafness God grants to those who finally despise his word. Saul, abandoned, turns to the witch of Endor, illustrating that suppression of truth ends in occult bondage. The forsaken king is the dark mirror of the King who would cry, 'My God, why have you forsaken me?' yet was vindicated.
1Sam.28.19: Samuel's spirit declares to Saul, 'Tomorrow you and your sons shall be with me.' The doom is sealed and immediate. Reformed exposition handles this passage soberly: God may permit a true visitation, sovereignly overriding the medium's craft, to confirm judgment already announced. The episode underscores the irrevocability of covenant rejection for the impenitent. It also contrasts pointedly with David, whose grievous sins are met not with abandonment but with chastening grace and fatherly discipline. Saul gets the answer he refused to hear when prophets and Urim were available, and it is the answer of death. The chapter is a warning to all who would treat divine silence as freedom for self-rule.
1Sam.29.6: Achish testifies of David, 'As the LORD lives, you have been honest, and your going out and your coming in with me in the army is good in my sight.' Even pagan kings are providentially turned to praise David, foreshadowing the nations confessing the greater David. Reformed exposition sees God's hand keeping David from a battle that would have been catastrophic to fight against his own people. Providence preserves the elect from sins they would otherwise commit.
1Sam.30.8: 'Pursue, for you shall surely overtake and shall surely rescue.' David inquires of the LORD through the ephod. Reformed exposition contrasts this with Saul, who had no oracle. The man after God's own heart consults God before action; the rejected king consults witches. Guidance is a covenant blessing for those who walk in faith, and David's victory belongs to his obedience to the divine word.
1Sam.30.24: 'For as his share is who goes down into the battle, so shall his share be who stays by the baggage; they shall share alike.' David's policy of equal portions becomes a permanent statute. Reformed exposition hears gospel grace: the kingdom shares its spoils not by merit of work but by union with the King. Every believer, frontline or rearguard, inherits equally with the Anointed.
1Sam.31.4: Saul falls on his own sword. The first king of Israel dies in self-destruction, the inevitable end of the man who sought his own way. Reformed exposition closes the book on Saul not with mockery but with sober warning: the unregenerate heart, granted what it asked for, ends in ruin. The narrative now opens for the rise of David, the king after God's heart, whose throne and Son endure forever.
1Sam.31.6: 'Saul and his three sons and his armor-bearer and all his men died together that same day.' The total collapse of Saul's house is judicial. The kingdom prophesied torn from him is now bodily severed. Reformed exposition observes the contrast with David, whose house God will establish forever. Two trees, two trajectories: the proud one falls, the humble is raised. The narrative ends in funeral darkness so that the dawn of David's reign may break with greater glory in 2 Samuel.
1Sam.1.3: 'This man used to go up year by year from his city to worship and to sacrifice to the LORD of hosts at Shiloh.' Elkanah's regular pilgrimage marks him as a devout Israelite even amid widespread apostasy. Reformed exposition values such ordinary covenant fidelity: the means of grace pursued in routine eventually birth the extraordinary. The introduction of 'LORD of hosts' (YHWH tseva'ot), first appearing here, sets the militant-cosmic tone. Israel needs a Warrior-King, and the prayer of one humble worshipper at the appointed feast becomes the seed of monarchy itself.
1Sam.1.17: 'Go in peace, and the God of Israel grant your petition that you have made to him.' Eli, after misjudging Hannah, finally pronounces priestly blessing. The faded priesthood still mediates a true word, illustrating that God works through flawed offices. Reformed ecclesiology insists the validity of ministerial pronouncement rests on the Word, not the worth of the minister. Hannah departs in faith, eating again with restored countenance: a model of the soul that hears the absolution of God and rises in peace.
1Sam.2.3: 'Talk no more so very proudly, let not arrogance come from your mouth; for the LORD is a God of knowledge, and by him actions are weighed.' Hannah turns her song into prophetic rebuke against Peninnah, Eli's sons, and every proud accuser. Reformed theology delights in the El Deot (God of knowledge) who weighs (takan) every action in perfect scale. Final judgment is theocentric, not subjective. The verse anticipates Hebrews 4:13: nothing is hid from the eyes of him to whom we must give account.
1Sam.2.18: 'Samuel was ministering before the LORD, a boy clothed with a linen ephod.' The child priest contrasts sharply with the corrupt adult priests. Reformed pastoral exposition often draws from this image of consecrated youth, dressed in priestly linen, serving while Eli's sons defraud. God prepares his servants in obscurity. The narrative subtly transfers genuine priestly function from the Elide line to the prophetically called Samuel, foreshadowing the displacement of unfaithful office-holders by God's chosen.
1Sam.2.26: 'Now the boy Samuel continued to grow both in stature and in favor with the LORD and also with man.' The phrase is echoed of the boy Jesus in Luke 2:52. Reformed exposition sees the typological link: Samuel, like the greater Prophet to come, advances in covenant favor and human esteem. Yet the parallel highlights the contrast: Samuel's growth gives way to the perfect growth of the incarnate Son, who alone fulfills covenant maturity without sin or shortfall.
1Sam.3.7: 'Now Samuel did not yet know the LORD, and the word of the LORD had not yet been revealed to him.' Even the future prophet must be brought to personal knowledge. Reformed exposition guards against presumption of inherited faith; the children of believers must themselves be effectually called. Knowledge of God (yada) is more than information; it is covenant intimacy granted by sovereign initiative. Samuel's awakening is the prototype of every conversion that turns hearers into hearers indeed.
1Sam.3.20: 'And all Israel from Dan to Beersheba knew that Samuel was established as a prophet of the LORD.' The phrase 'from Dan to Beersheba' is covenant-geographic shorthand for the whole nation. The prophetic office is publicly authenticated. Reformed bibliology stands here: God provides authoritative spokesmen whose words bind the conscience. Samuel's vindication anticipates the apostolic accreditation of the New Testament; God always validates the messengers through whom Scripture comes to the church.
1Sam.5.7: 'The ark of the God of Israel must not remain with us, for his hand is heavy against us and against Dagon our god.' The Philistines confess Yahweh's superiority. Reformed exposition relishes the irony: pagans gain clearer theology under God's hand than the Israelites who carried the ark presumptuously. The God who exiles himself among the Philistines reveals that no land or people can contain or domesticate him. He is sovereign even when his people are scattered.
1Sam.6.20: 'Who is able to stand before the LORD, this holy God?' The men of Beth-shemesh, terrified by the ark's lethal holiness, voice the great existential question of Old Testament religion. Reformed theology answers: only the High Priest sprinkling blood, and ultimately Christ, the holy and innocent Son who alone stands before the Father on behalf of his people. The fearful question prepares for the gospel answer in Hebrews: we have boldness to enter through the blood of Jesus.
1Sam.7.10: 'But the LORD thundered with a mighty sound that day against the Philistines and threw them into confusion.' The divine warrior fights for repentant Israel. Reformed theology sees the answer of the heavens to the cry of the earth: when God's people return, the LORD himself contends. Mizpah becomes a place of victory through prayer rather than military force, anticipating the spiritual battles of the church, won not by carnal weapons but by the prayer of the saints united with the prevailing Mediator.
1Sam.8.3: 'His sons did not walk in his ways but turned aside after gain. They took bribes and perverted justice.' Even Samuel's house produces unworthy successors. Reformed exposition refuses idealism about godly families: only the grace of regeneration secures faithful generations, not merely good upbringing. The sons' corruption provides the occasion for Israel's demand for a king, illustrating that human leadership cannot escape the entail of sin. Only the Son who is incorruptible solves the dynastic problem.
1Sam.9.21: 'Am I not a Benjaminite, from the least of the tribes of Israel? And is not my clan the humblest of all the clans of the tribe of Benjamin?' Saul's modesty is initially genuine. Reformed exposition notes that humility at the start does not guarantee humility at the finish. The verse warns against equating early piety with persevering grace. Only the Son who 'humbled himself even to death' fulfills the pattern of self-effacement throughout the kingdom's establishment.
1Sam.10.7: 'When these signs meet you, do what your hand finds to do, for God is with you.' The threefold sign confirms Saul's anointing. The promise 'God is with you' is the covenant formula soon to mark David and ultimately Immanuel. Reformed theology sees here both grace given and responsibility imposed: the Spirit's empowerment includes the call to faithful action. Saul will tragically squander what is freely granted, demonstrating that gifts without graces fail to save.
1Sam.10.27: 'But some worthless fellows said, How can this man save us? And they despised him and brought him no present.' Even the people's chosen king is despised by some. Reformed exposition observes the perennial pattern: every God-appointed ruler faces opposition. The pattern climaxes at the cross, where the true King is despised by his own. Saul will later judge these foes harshly; David will leave them to the LORD; Christ prays, 'Father, forgive them.'
1Sam.12.13: 'Behold the king whom you have chosen, for whom you have asked; behold, the LORD has set a king over you.' Samuel's sermon encapsulates divine accommodation to human demand. The king is both 'chosen' by Israel and 'set' by Yahweh, an instructive paradox. Reformed compatibilism finds expression here: human responsibility and divine sovereignty intersect without contradiction. The pattern reappears at Calvary, where lawless men crucify Christ according to God's definite plan.
1Sam.13.8: 'He waited seven days, the time appointed by Samuel.' The narrative carefully notes Saul's compliance, then his collapse on the eighth day. Reformed exposition notes that obedience to the eleventh hour is not obedience; perseverance is the test. Saul's failure on the very last hour exposes a kingdom built on appearance, not faith. Christ, by contrast, endured to the end, despising the shame, securing the kingdom that Saul forfeited.
1Sam.14.15: 'There was a panic in the camp, in the field, and among all the people. The garrison and even the raiders trembled, the earth quaked, and it became a very great panic.' The narrator stacks panic upon panic; this is divinely caused terror. Reformed exposition sees the holy war motif: God himself fights through Jonathan's faith, vindicating the principle that the LORD saves by many or few. The same God strikes terror in the hearts of his enemies at the cross, where the rulers of darkness flee.
1Sam.15.11: 'I regret that I have made Saul king, for he has turned back from following me.' The divine 'regret' (nicham) is anthropopathic, accommodating God's covenantal response to human action. Reformed theology, especially as later articulated in the same chapter (v. 29), holds these in concert: God's eternal counsel is unchangeable, yet his covenantal interaction with creatures is genuine and dynamic. The Lord's 'regret' is a profoundly real expression of covenant grief over apostasy.
1Sam.15.35: 'And Samuel did not see Saul again until the day of his death, but Samuel grieved over Saul. And the LORD regretted that he had made Saul king over Israel.' The chapter ends with a double sorrow, prophetic and divine. Reformed exposition reads pastoral sympathy here: faithful ministry weeps over apostasy. The Father grieves over his unfaithful steward, foreshadowing Christ weeping over Jerusalem. God's holiness does not preclude tender lament over the lost.
1Sam.16.18: 'I have seen a son of Jesse the Bethlehemite, who is skillful in playing, a man of valor, a man of war, prudent in speech, and a man of good presence, and the LORD is with him.' The sixfold commendation, climaxing in Yahweh's accompaniment, sketches David's complete equipping for kingship. Reformed exposition sees the pattern of God preparing his servants holistically: musical, martial, verbal, and most importantly, covenantal. Each gift will serve the consolidation of the kingdom that ultimately comes through David's greater Son.
1Sam.16.23: 'And whenever the harmful spirit from God was upon Saul, David took the lyre and played it with his hand. So Saul was refreshed and was well, and the harmful spirit departed from him.' David, the Spirit-anointed shepherd, brings relief to the Spirit-deserted king. Reformed exposition reads the typology: the music of the true David soothes spiritual torment. The Psalms born from David's harp continue to minister to the troubled hearts of the elect, as the Spirit speaks through inspired song.
1Sam.17.4: 'And there came out from the camp of the Philistines a champion named Goliath of Gath, whose height was six cubits and a span.' The 'champion' (ish habbenayim, 'man between') stands as a representative warrior. Reformed exposition finds here a perfect federal-headship setting: one combatant fights for the whole army. Goliath represents the Adamic curse, towering and seemingly invincible; David represents the Last Adam who alone can vanquish the enemy whose defeat secures the people's salvation.
1Sam.17.32: 'Let no man's heart fail because of him; your servant will go and fight with this Philistine.' David volunteers as substitute. Reformed Christology reads the typology: where Israel cowers, the Anointed steps forward to fight on their behalf. Christ likewise enters the field of battle alone and triumphs, that the saints might receive the spoils of his victory. The young shepherd is a faint shadow of the Good Shepherd who lays down his life for the sheep.
1Sam.18.30: 'Whenever the commanders of the Philistines came out to battle, David had more success than all the servants of Saul, so that his name was highly esteemed.' The exaltation of David's name is a covenantal sign. Reformed exposition observes how God elevates the chosen one in the eyes of friend and foe. The pattern fulfills in Christ: 'God highly exalted him and bestowed on him the name that is above every name.' Davidic esteem foreshadows the universal honor due to the Son.
1Sam.19.24: Saul lies stripped before Samuel, prophesying involuntarily. The proverb 'Is Saul also among the prophets?' is repeated with bitter irony. Reformed exposition reads judicial humiliation: the rejected king is exposed in spiritual nakedness. Office without holiness ends in shame. The episode reinforces that Spirit-empowerment apart from regeneration produces strange fruit. True prophecy belongs to renewed hearts who serve the King.
1Sam.20.30: Saul curses Jonathan in shocking words, exposing his rage at Jonathan's loyalty to David. Reformed exposition sees how covenant fidelity divides households: 'a man's enemies will be those of his own household.' Jonathan must choose between earthly father and the LORD's anointed. He chooses David, modeling Christ's saying that whoever loves father more than him is not worthy. Covenant loyalty transcends bloodlines.
1Sam.22.5: 'Then the prophet Gad said to David, Do not remain in the stronghold; depart, and go into the land of Judah.' The introduction of Gad signals that David has prophetic accompaniment in his wilderness years. Reformed theology values such guidance: the rising king listens to the prophetic word, in contrast to Saul who has silenced the prophets. The pattern continues in the church, which lives by the apostolic-prophetic word now inscripturated.
1Sam.23.4: 'David inquired of the LORD again. And the LORD answered him, Arise, go down to Keilah, for I will give the Philistines into your hand.' David's repeated inquiry models humble dependence. Reformed exposition prizes such persistence: the godly do not act on first impressions but seek the LORD until clarity comes. The contrast with Saul, who acts on impulse and silence, sharpens. David's habit of inquiry becomes the foundation of psalm-prayer that guides all saints.
1Sam.24.20: 'Now I know that you shall surely be king, and that the kingdom of Israel shall be established in your hand.' Saul's confession in the cave illustrates a knowledge that cannot save: he knows the truth without submitting to it. Reformed theology distinguishes notitia from saving fiducia. Demons believe and tremble. Saul confesses David yet returns to persecute him, testifying that knowledge without surrender is condemnation, not salvation.
1Sam.25.28: Abigail prays, 'For the LORD will certainly make my lord a sure house, because my lord is fighting the battles of the LORD.' The bayit ne'eman (sure house) anticipates Nathan's oracle in 2 Samuel 7. Reformed exposition notes how the Spirit puts dynastic prophecy on a wise woman's lips. Abigail's words confirm that David's wars are 'the battles of the LORD' (milhamot YHWH), and the LORD will reward such service with a perpetual house, fulfilled finally in Christ.
1Sam.26.21: 'Behold, I have acted foolishly, and have made a great mistake.' Saul's second confession of folly mirrors his first (15:24-25), but both are followed by relapse. Reformed exposition contrasts Saul's worldly sorrow with David's godly grief in Psalm 51. Confession without contrition produces no fruit; only the Spirit-wrought repentance changes hearts. Saul becomes a chilling portrait of religious affection without saving conversion.
1Sam.28.7: 'Saul said to his servants, Seek out for me a woman who is a medium, that I may go to her and inquire of her.' The same Saul who once expelled mediums (v. 3) now consults one. Reformed exposition warns that suppressed sin returns when the heart is unrenewed. The forms of righteousness (purging mediums) without the substance of faith collapse under pressure. Saul's hypocrisy is unmasked, and the kingdom is unmistakably forfeit.
1Sam.30.23: 'You shall not do so, my brothers, with what the LORD has given us. He has preserved us and given into our hand the band that came against us.' David refuses the harshness of certain men who would deny shares to the weary. Reformed exposition celebrates kingly mercy: David's leadership is shaped by gospel logic, not greed. The greater David rules his church not by withholding but by lavish grace, distributing his spoils equally to all who are united with him.
1Sam.31.13: The men of Jabesh-gilead bury Saul and his sons under a tamarisk tree at Jabesh, and fast seven days. The book ends with quiet covenant kindness toward the fallen king. Reformed exposition sees redemptive remnant fidelity: even at Israel's lowest point, hesed survives. Jabesh, once delivered by Saul (ch. 11), returns kindness in his death. The narrative's last act is a faint light of grace, foretelling the hesed of David's kingdom that is about to dawn.
1Sam.4.18: Eli falls backward off his seat at the news of the ark's capture, breaking his neck. The fat old priest dies with the kingdom of God seemingly in pagan hands. Reformed exposition reads the moment as the closing of an age: corrupt priesthood collapses literally and figuratively. The chair (kisse, also 'throne') falls. Office without faithful occupants cannot stand. Yet God will raise up a king, prophet, and faithful priest, all converging in the One who never falls.
1Sam.13.19: 'Now there was no blacksmith to be found throughout all the land of Israel, for the Philistines said, Lest the Hebrews make themselves swords or spears.' The disarmament of Israel highlights the absurdity of victory by human means. Reformed theology applies the principle: God often strips his people of natural resources to compel reliance on him alone. The same God who would use David's sling defeats Israel's enemies without iron, demonstrating that salvation is the LORD's, not man's.
1Sam.18.12: 'Saul was afraid of David because the LORD was with him but had departed from Saul.' The fear of the rejected toward the chosen is rehearsed throughout salvation history: Cain feared Abel, Esau resented Jacob, Saul fears David. Reformed exposition observes that the natural man perceives, even if dimly, the divine favor on the elect, and rages against it. The covenant presence is the line of demarcation; God's withdrawal and abiding mark out the kingdoms of darkness and of his Son.
1Sam.21.13: David feigns madness before Achish, scratching the doors and letting spittle run down his beard. Psalm 34 and 56 are the prayer-fruits of these episodes. Reformed exposition holds the saint's weakness honestly: even God's anointed deceives in fear. Yet the experience yields psalms that have nourished the church for millennia. Where David's faith wobbles, his pen, guided by the Spirit, still serves the people of God across ages.
1Sam.30.17: 'David struck them down from twilight until the evening of the next day.' The relentless pursuit recovers all that was taken. Reformed exposition sees a paradigm of redemption: the King retrieves his people from the enemy who took them captive. Christ, the greater David, descended into the realm of death to recover the captives, ascending on high and giving gifts to men. Nothing the enemy steals from the elect is unrecoverable in the King's pursuit.
1Sam.16.21: 'David came to Saul and entered his service. And Saul loved him greatly, and he became his armor-bearer.' Initially Saul loves David, before envy poisons that affection. Reformed exposition observes the bittersweet providence: David enters Saul's house in peace, where he will eventually marry his daughter and inherit his throne. The future king learns court life, sees firsthand the cost of disobedience, and is prepared in the very household where the rejected king sits. God prepares his servant under the eyes of the one whose throne he will inherit.
1Sam.2.21: 'Indeed the LORD visited Hannah, and she conceived and bore three sons and two daughters.' The reward of surrendered fruitfulness is multiplied fruitfulness. Reformed exposition delights in this principle: those who give back to God find themselves blessed beyond their first request. Hannah, who offered her firstborn, receives a fivefold further harvest. The pattern points to the gospel economy where the one who loses life finds it, and the seed that dies in the ground bears much fruit.
1Sam.3.18: 'It is the LORD. Let him do what seems good to him.' Eli's response to the harsh prophecy is a model of submissive resignation, even though it does not produce repentance. Reformed exposition distinguishes between fatalistic acceptance and faith-filled submission. Eli bows but does not return; pious words without contrite hearts cannot avert judgment. The verse is a sober reminder that the language of submission must be matched by the substance of repentance and reformation.
1Sam.7.5: 'Then Samuel said, Gather all Israel at Mizpah, and I will pray to the LORD for you.' The prophet calls a national assembly for repentance and intercession. Reformed exposition treasures the gathered means of grace: corporate confession under prophetic leadership precipitates national deliverance. The pattern undergirds the church's calls for solemn assemblies. Samuel's intercession typifies the Greater Mediator whose prayers for his people prevail with the Father against every enemy.
1Sam.10.22: 'And the LORD said, Behold, he has hidden himself among the baggage.' Saul, on his coronation day, must be drawn out from hiding. Reformed exposition reads providential foreshadowing: the man who hides his heart from God will eventually be exposed. What begins as modesty hardens into evasion. Genuine humility serves boldly; false humility shrinks from accountability. The contrast with David, who steps forward to face Goliath, is striking.
1Sam.17.55: 'Whose son is this youth?' Saul's question, though seemingly forgetful, foregrounds the dynastic theme: Whose son? David is Jesse's son, of Bethlehem of Judah, of the line that will produce Messiah. Reformed exposition reads the question as the great question of Scripture: 'Whose son is the Christ?' (Matt 22:42). The answer, hidden here, is unfolded in Nathan's oracle and finally in the gospel: he is David's son, and yet David's Lord, the Son of God incarnate.
1Sam.20.16: 'And Jonathan made a covenant with the house of David, saying, May the LORD take vengeance on David's enemies.' Jonathan binds his descendants to David's house. The covenant is solemnized between two men but reaches generations. Reformed covenant theology values such intergenerational vows: God's promises and our oaths often bind beyond our lifetime. The verse anticipates Mephibosheth, whom David will treat with covenant kindness for Jonathan's sake.
1Sam.23.14: 'David remained in the strongholds in the wilderness, in the hill country of the Wilderness of Ziph. And Saul sought him every day, but God did not give him into his hand.' The narrator's theology shines: behind every escape stands divine restraint of the persecutor. Reformed exposition rests on this providence: the elect are kept by the power of God. No weapon formed against the LORD's anointed prospers, until the appointed hour. The wilderness is no place of abandonment but of preserving grace.
1Sam.27.6: 'So that day Achish gave him Ziklag. Therefore Ziklag has belonged to the kings of Judah to this day.' Even in compromise, God grants David territorial inheritance among Judah's kings. Reformed exposition observes that the wandering will receive land, the despised will receive dominion. The greater David, who had no place to lay his head, now possesses all things and reigns over a kingdom that cannot be shaken. Ziklag is a small foretaste of the inheritance prepared for the saints.
1Sam.13.22: 'So on the day of the battle there was neither sword nor spear found in the hand of any of the people with Saul and Jonathan; but Saul and Jonathan his son had them.' Israel goes to war essentially unarmed. Reformed exposition draws the lesson: God's people, often outwardly weakened, must rely entirely on him. Christ sends his disciples as sheep among wolves, without staff or money, that the kingdom may advance by his power, not theirs.
1Thess.1.3: Paul's triad—work of faith, labor of love, steadfastness of hope (ergou tes pisteos, kopou tes agapes, hypomones tes elpidos)—becomes a Pauline signature (cf. 1 Cor 13:13). Faith is not a passive mental assent but produces ergon; love expends itself in kopos, costly toil; hope endures under affliction with hypomone. Reformed theology has long noted that these three are evidences of regeneration, not the cause of it. The genitives are subjective: faith works, love labors, hope endures. The Thessalonians' Christianity was visible, sweat-stained, and forward-leaning. That Paul remembers these 'before our God and Father' grounds Christian assurance not in introspective certainty but in fruit observable to the apostolic community.
1Thess.1.4: 'Knowing, brothers beloved by God, your election (ten eklogen hymon)'—Paul knows their election by its evidences in v. 5–10. Election is here not a speculative doctrine but a pastoral certainty grounded in the gospel's effectual operation among them. The perfect participle egapemenoi marks them as standing objects of divine love. Calvin notes that the gospel's reception in power is the mirror in which election is read; we do not climb into the secret counsel of God, but we descend to the fruits. The doctrine gives stability under persecution: those whom God chose before the foundation of the world cannot be unmade by Roman magistrates or Jewish opponents.
1Thess.1.5: The gospel came 'not in word only but also in power and in the Holy Spirit and with full conviction (en plerophoria polle).' Reformed homiletics has anchored itself here: preaching becomes effectual not by rhetorical artifice but by the Spirit's accompaniment. Plerophoria denotes a settled, full-sailed assurance produced in the hearers. The same word that goes forth as logos becomes dynamis when the Spirit attends it. This verse rebukes both rationalist preaching that depends on argument alone and emotionalist preaching that manipulates affections. The instrumentality of the word and the agency of the Spirit are inseparable but not identical—a distinction Owen and Edwards exploited richly.
1Thess.1.9: 'You turned to God from idols (epestrepsate pros ton theon apo ton eidolon) to serve a living and true God.' Conversion is described in classic two-fold motion—a turning from and a turning to—corresponding to mortification and vivification. The idols of Thessalonica were not abstract; they were civic, economic, and familial loyalties. Reformed pastors note that idolatry is never merely cognitive error but misplaced worship, and conversion accordingly is not bare opinion change but a reorientation of love. The contrast 'living and true' (zonti kai alethino) exposes idols as dead and false—they cannot hear, save, or judge.
1Thess.1.10: To 'wait for his Son from heaven (anamenein ton huion autou ek ton ouranon)' marks the Christian as eschatologically postured. The verb anameno occurs only here in the NT and connotes patient, expectant waiting. Jesus 'delivers us from the wrath to come (tes orges tes erchomenes)'—Paul presupposes a future, retributive divine wrath, not merely natural consequence. The gospel rescues sinners from God's own judgment, not from impersonal forces. Reformed theology has resisted attempts to dilute orge into pedagogical correction; the cross only makes sense if wrath is real. The Thessalonians' hope was not escape from history but rescue at history's climax.
1Thess.2.4: 'We have been approved by God to be entrusted with the gospel (dedokimasmetha hypo tou theou pisteuthenai to euangelion)'—the perfect tense indicates a tested and abiding commission. Paul's accountability is vertical, not horizontal: 'not to please man, but God who tests our hearts.' Reformed ministry doctrine grounds itself here. The minister is a steward (oikonomos), not an entrepreneur; his audience is ultimately One. The verb dokimazo (test, approve) recurs deliberately—God who tested Paul still tests his motives. Pastoral ministry that bends to congregational preference has forgotten whose mail it carries.
1Thess.2.7: Paul becomes 'gentle among you, like a nursing mother (trophos) cherishing her own children.' The textual variant nepioi/epioi need not detain us; either way the maternal image dominates. Pauline authority is exercised not magisterially but tenderly. Reformed pastoral theology, often caricatured as severe, finds here its corrective: the same apostle who anathematized false gospels (Gal 1) cradled his converts. The image refuses any dichotomy between doctrinal vigilance and affective warmth. A nursing mother is fiercely protective and intimately self-giving simultaneously—both rooted in love for the child.
1Thess.2.19: 'For what is our hope or joy or crown of boasting (stephanos kauchéseos) before our Lord Jesus at his coming (parousia)?' First mention of parousia in the letter—Paul's converts are his eschatological reward. The stephanos is the victor's wreath, not the royal diadem. Reformed pastors have read this verse as both motivation and warning: ministry's true assessment is at the parousia, and the souls won are the only durable trophies. The verse also dignifies particular congregations—Paul does not speak generically but names the Thessalonians as his crown, refusing abstraction in pastoral love.
1Thess.3.3: 'That no one be moved (sainesthai) by these afflictions. For you yourselves know that we are destined (keimetha) for this.' The verb sainesthai literally means to wag the tail, hence to be flattered or beguiled, here to be shaken or seduced. Paul's eschatology is not prosperity theology; tribulation is appointed (keimetha—we are placed) for believers. Suffering is not a sign of divine displeasure but of conformity to Christ. Reformed catechesis has historically rooted persecution-readiness here: God ordains affliction as the path of his people. The doctrine of providence here turns from comfort to cruciformity.
1Thess.3.5: 'For fear that somehow the tempter (ho peirazon) had tempted you and our labor would be in vain.' Paul names Satan with a participial title—he is characterized by his activity. Yet the apostle's fear shows pastoral realism: even genuine converts can be assaulted, and the minister's labor (kopos) is bound up with their endurance. This is not works-righteousness but covenantal solidarity. Reformed theology distinguishes between the perseverance of the saints (which is certain in God) and the believer's experience of perseverance (which is a fight). Paul's anxiety is not for the secret decree but for visible faithfulness.
1Thess.3.10: 'Praying earnestly night and day that we may see your face and supply what is lacking (ta hysteremata) in your faith.' Faith has measurable deficits that further teaching can supply. This refutes any anti-intellectual pietism that treats catechesis as optional once 'faith' is present. The Greek hyperekperissou ('most earnestly,' lit. 'beyond exceedingly abundantly') is a Pauline superlative stack. Pastoral burden is here exposed: Paul's prayer life was structured by his people's doctrinal needs. Reformed ministry holds that growth in grace is growth in knowledge of doctrine concretely embraced.
1Thess.3.13: 'So that he may establish your hearts blameless in holiness before our God and Father at the coming (parousia) of our Lord Jesus with all his saints.' Establishment (sterixai) in holiness is God's work, not the believer's autonomous achievement. The eschatological audit at the parousia is what every pastoral concern points toward. 'With all his saints (hagion)' likely refers to the glorified believers (cf. 4:14), arriving in retinue with Christ. Sanctification's telos is not personal flourishing but standing unblameably before the Father in the day of the Son. Reformed theology refuses to detach ethics from eschatology.
1Thess.4.4: 'That each one of you know how to control his own body (skeuos) in holiness and honor.' The translation of skeuos (vessel) is debated—either 'body' or 'wife.' Either way, the call is to stewarded sanctity. The Reformed reading prefers 'body,' aligning with v. 3 and the Pauline use of skeuos (2 Cor 4:7). Self-mastery (ktasthai) is learned (eidenai), implying catechetical formation. Sanctification is not spontaneous; it is taught, practiced, and matured. Honor (time) connects sexual integrity to dignity—both the believer's own and the spouse's. The body is not a neutral instrument but a temple under stewardship.
1Thess.4.7: 'For God has not called us for impurity (akatharsia) but in holiness (en hagiasmo).' The call (ekalesen) is purposive: God's effectual summons is unto a definite mode of life. Reformed soteriology insists that calling and sanctification are unbreakably linked—the kletos is set apart for hagiasmos. To live impurely is to deny the very telos of one's calling. Calvin notes that the dignity of the call elevates the obligation; the same divine voice that summoned us to himself summons us from impurity. Antinomianism cannot stand here: the call itself contains the ethic.
1Thess.4.13: 'We do not want you to be uninformed (agnoein) about those who are asleep (kekoimemenon).' Christian grief is reshaped, not abolished. 'That you may not grieve as others do who have no hope (elpida)'—the contrast is not no grief versus grief but hopeless grief versus hopeful grief. The verb koimao (to sleep) implies awakening; pagan epitaphs lacked this. Reformed funeral theology has insisted on weeping with full Christian honesty while resisting despair. Death is the last enemy, not a friend; yet for those in Christ it is decisively defanged.
1Thess.4.14: 'For since we believe that Jesus died and rose again, even so, through Jesus, God will bring with him those who have fallen asleep.' The logic is christological: the believer's resurrection is grounded in Christ's. The phrase dia tou Iesou ('through Jesus') likely modifies 'will bring,' meaning Jesus is the agent of the saints' eschatological transport. Reformed eschatology sees in this verse the unbreakable union between Christ and his people: as he was raised, they shall be raised; as he is glorified, they shall be brought with him. The resurrection is not an isolated miracle but the firstfruits of a corporate harvest.
1Thess.4.16: 'For the Lord himself will descend from heaven with a cry of command, with the voice of an archangel, and with the sound of the trumpet of God. And the dead in Christ will rise first.' The triple sonic announcement—keleusma, phone, salpingi—signals royal arrival. The Lord himself (autos) descends; no proxy. The dead in Christ (en Christo) rise first, refuting any notion that those who died missed the parousia. Reformed theology has read this as a single, public, bodily return, resisting dispensational schemes that bifurcate the second coming. The verse is comfort literature, not eschatological speculation.
1Thess.4.17: 'Then we who are alive, who are left, will be caught up together (harpagesometha) with them in the clouds to meet the Lord (eis apantesin tou kyriou) in the air; and so we will always be with the Lord.' Apantesis is the technical Greco-Roman term for the citizenry going out to meet a visiting dignitary and escorting him into the city. The believers do not fly off to a remote heaven; they meet the descending Lord and accompany him to earth's renewal. This reading resists rapture-as-evacuation theology. The climactic phrase 'always with the Lord' is the substance of the Christian hope—personal, eternal, communal presence.
1Thess.4.18: 'Therefore encourage (parakaleite) one another with these words.' Paul's eschatology is pastoral, not speculative. The doctrine of the parousia is given for mutual exhortation in grief and persecution, not for calendar-charting. Reformed homiletics has long emphasized this: doctrine is for doxology and discipleship. The middle/passive parakaleite is reciprocal—believers encourage one another. The verse implies that eschatological teaching belongs to the regular discourse of the Christian community, not the speculative margin. To withhold these doctrines is to withhold consolation.
1Thess.5.2: 'For you yourselves are fully aware that the day of the Lord (hemera kyriou) will come like a thief in the night.' Paul applies the OT 'Day of the LORD' (yom YHWH) directly to the coming of Jesus—a high christological move. The thief simile (cf. Mt 24:43) emphasizes unexpectedness, not stealth in the sense of evil intent. Reformed eschatology stresses that date-setting is foreclosed by this image; vigilance, not calculation, is the proper response. The believer's posture is preparedness, the unbeliever's posture is sleep.
1Thess.5.5: 'For you are all children of light (huioi photos), children of the day. We are not of the night or of the darkness.' The Semitic idiom 'children of' denotes character belonging. Believers possess an ontological identity in light, not merely a moral aspiration. Reformed sanctification reasons from indicative to imperative: because you are light, walk as light. The contrast is total—night and day are mutually exclusive. The pluralized address (panteshymeis) locates the church corporately in the day. This anticipates the eschatological full-day still to come, but the dawn has already broken.
1Thess.5.8: 'But since we belong to the day, let us be sober, having put on the breastplate of faith and love, and for a helmet the hope of salvation.' The triadic faith-love-hope reappears as armor (cf. Eph 6). Sobriety (nephomen) is the eschatological alertness opposite to spiritual drunkenness. Reformed ethics treats virtues not as private dispositions but as defensive equipment for warfare. The 'hope of salvation' (elpida soterias) is the helmet—what protects the head, the seat of perception. To lose hope is to be vulnerable to fatal blows of despair and apostasy.
1Thess.5.9: 'For God has not destined us for wrath, but to obtain salvation (eis peripoiesin soterias) through our Lord Jesus Christ.' The verb etheto (placed, destined) is Paul's strongest predestinarian vocabulary. The destination is positive: not orge but soteria. Peripoiesis means acquisition, possession. Reformed theology reads here a clear statement of the double aspect of God's eternal purpose—not that he predestined some to wrath in the same manner as some to salvation, but that the believer's destiny is fixed in Christ unto salvation. The instrumental dia tou kyriou hemon Iesou Christou is unbreakable.
1Thess.5.19: 'Do not quench (sbennute) the Spirit.' The metaphor is fire (cf. Acts 2). The Spirit's operations in the gathered church—prophecy, exhortation, gifts—can be smothered by either skeptical disregard or undisciplined excess. Reformed cessationism and continuationism debate the modes, but both must heed the prohibition. Calvin warns against rationalist disdain that snuffs out the Spirit's gracious workings under pretense of order. The next verses balance this: do not despise prophecies, but test everything. The Spirit is honored both by openness and by discernment.
1Thess.5.21: 'But test everything (panta de dokimazete); hold fast what is good.' The middle voice and present imperative urge ongoing discernment. Reformed epistemology of the Christian life denies any uncritical reception even of apparent prophetic words. Dokimazo is the assayer's verb—testing metals against the standard. The standard is Scripture and apostolic doctrine. To 'hold fast' (katechete) implies a grip on the good amid swirling claims. This verse undergirds the Protestant principle of testing all teachings, including ecclesial ones, by the inscripturated word.
1Tim.1.3: Paul's opening charge establishes the pastoral mandate: Timothy is to remain at Ephesus to silence those teaching a 'different doctrine' (heterodidaskalein, a Pauline coinage). The verb implies systematic deviation, not minor disagreement. Reformed exegesis notes that doctrinal fidelity is not optional refinement but the heart of pastoral office. Paul does not merely suggest dialogue with errorists; he commands their cessation. The pastor's authority derives from apostolic deposit, not personal charisma. This verse anchors the entire epistle: the church's health depends upon doctrinal purity guarded by faithful officers. Heterodoxy is treated as a fire to be quenched, not a viewpoint to be platformed.
1Tim.1.9: The law is not laid down for the just but for the lawless. Paul does not deny the law's third use (guide for sanctification) but refutes the false teachers' misuse of it. The Reformed tradition affirms the law's pedagogical function (driving sinners to Christ) and normative function (directing the regenerate). Here Paul addresses the second use, the political/civil restraint of evil. The catalog that follows roughly mirrors the Decalogue's second table. The just (dikaios) are those declared righteous by faith; the law no longer condemns them, though it still instructs. Antinomianism and legalism both miss the law's covenantal place.
1Tim.1.13: Paul's autobiographical confession grounds gospel hope: 'I was formerly a blasphemer, persecutor, and insolent opponent.' The Greek hubristen denotes violent arrogance. Yet he received mercy because he acted 'ignorantly in unbelief.' This is not exoneration but explanation; Reformed soteriology sees here the doctrine of effectual calling overriding even militant rebellion. Mercy (eleethen, passive) emphasizes divine initiative. Paul's chief-of-sinners self-understanding becomes the pattern (hupotuposin, v. 16) for all conversions. No sinner is beyond sovereign grace. The persecutor became preacher only by mercy, not merit, foreshadowing Romans 9's potter and clay.
1Tim.1.17: The doxology erupts spontaneously: 'To the King of the ages, immortal, invisible, the only God, be honor and glory forever and ever.' 'King of the ages' (basilei ton aionon) declares God's sovereignty over all epochs. 'Immortal' (aphtharto), 'invisible' (aorato), 'only' (mono) emphasize the Creator-creature distinction central to Reformed theology. Such ascriptions are not abstract speculation but worship provoked by experienced mercy. True doctrine ignites doxology; cold orthodoxy is a contradiction. Paul models how every theological reflection should terminate in praise. The 'amen' is liturgical assent, anchoring the church's worship in apostolic confession.
1Tim.1.20: Hymenaeus and Alexander were 'handed over to Satan that they may learn not to blaspheme.' This severe formula echoes 1 Corinthians 5:5 and signifies excommunication, removal from the church's protective sphere into the realm of the world Satan rules. The aim is corrective, not vindictive: 'that they may learn' (hina paideuthosin). Reformed ecclesiology recognizes church discipline as the third mark of the true church (Belgic Confession 29). Discipline is medicinal, not merely punitive, designed to bring sinners to repentance. Pastoral office includes the painful duty of formal exclusion when teaching corrupts and unrepentance persists.
1Tim.2.6: Christ 'gave himself as a ransom for all' (antilutron huper panton). The compound antilutron intensifies lutron, meaning a substitutionary ransom. Reformed theology understands 'all' here in connection with v. 4's 'all kinds,' encompassing Jews and Gentiles, kings and slaves. The atonement's sufficiency is universal; its efficacy is particular to the elect. 'The testimony given at the proper time' (kairois idiois) frames redemption within God's appointed schedule. The cross was no accident or afterthought but the eternal plan now historically manifest. Pastors must proclaim this objective accomplishment as the foundation of all salvific application.
1Tim.2.12: 'I do not permit a woman to teach or to exercise authority over a man.' The verb authentein (hapax in NT) denotes authoritative office, not domineering as some translate. Paul grounds his prohibition not in cultural custom but in creation order (v. 13) and the fall (v. 14), making it transcultural. Reformed ecclesiology preserves this complementarian conviction in its restriction of the teaching/ruling office to qualified men. This does not diminish women's gifting or ministry but locates ecclesiastical authority within the created pattern. The verse must be read alongside Galatians 3:28's spiritual equality without flattening the offices.
1Tim.2.15: 'She will be saved through childbearing' is one of the most contested verses. Reformed exegetes generally take 'saved' (sothesetai) not as eternal salvation by works but as preservation through the Genesis 3:16 curse, or as referring to the Childbearing (definite article in Greek), the incarnation through the woman's seed. 'If they continue in faith and love and holiness' shifts to plural, indicating the body of Christian women. The verse honors the domestic vocation without making it salvific in a meritorious sense. Faithful women find dignity in fulfilling their creational and covenantal callings.
1Tim.3.1: 'The saying is trustworthy: if anyone aspires to the office of overseer, he desires a noble work.' The episkope (oversight) is presented as an ergou (work), not a sinecure. Reformed polity identifies episkopos and presbuteros as overlapping terms (cf. Titus 1:5-7; Acts 20:17, 28) for the same office. Aspiration (oregetai) is legitimate when combined with character (vv. 2-7); the office is not seized but pursued through qualification. The 'noble work' (kalou ergou) ennobles ministerial labor against any clericalist abstraction. Pastoral ministry is genuine work requiring both inner call and external recognition by the church.
1Tim.3.6: 'He must not be a recent convert (neophuton), or he may become puffed up with conceit and fall into the condemnation of the devil.' Spiritual immaturity combined with elevated office produces pride. Reformed ordination practices rightly demand seasoning, theological training, and proven ministry before installation. The 'condemnation of the devil' refers either to the judgment Satan incurred through pride or the snare he sets for the proud (cf. v. 7). Either way, Paul links pride with diabolical resemblance. The pastoral office is too dangerous for novices; the elevation that should serve Christ becomes the platform for self-exaltation when character lags behind position.
1Tim.3.8: Deacons (diakonoi) likewise must be dignified (semnous), not double-tongued, not addicted to much wine, not greedy for dishonest gain. The diaconate, established in Acts 6, complements the eldership without overlapping its teaching/ruling function. Reformed polity preserves this distinction: deacons handle mercy and material affairs, freeing elders for word and prayer. The character requirements parallel the elders, demonstrating that all church officers must be exemplary, though their functions differ. 'Double-tongued' (dilogous) describes one who tells different stories to different audiences, fatal for a servant who handles confidential needs across the congregation.
1Tim.4.4: 'Everything created by God is good, and nothing is to be rejected if it is received with thanksgiving.' Paul refutes the proto-gnostic asceticism that forbade marriage and certain foods. The verse echoes Genesis 1's 'very good' verdict, which neither the fall nor the Mosaic dietary laws ultimately revoked (cf. Mark 7:19; Acts 10:15). Reformed theology affirms creation's essential goodness and the legitimacy of created enjoyments sanctified by word and prayer (v. 5). False spirituality denigrates the body, marriage, and food; biblical spirituality receives all with gratitude. Holiness is not escape from creation but its sanctified use to God's glory.
1Tim.4.10: 'We toil and strive, because we have our hope set on the living God, who is the Savior of all people, especially of those who believe.' The verse poses interpretive challenges. 'Savior of all' (panton anthropon) cannot mean universal salvation without contradicting Paul elsewhere; 'especially' (malista) qualifies. Reformed exegetes see God as preserver of all (common grace) and redeemer of believers (special grace), or 'all' as 'all kinds' with believers as the actual recipients of saving benefit. Either way, gospel labor is fueled by hope in the living God whose saving purposes will not fail. Effort flows from confidence, not anxiety.
1Tim.4.13: 'Devote yourself to the public reading of Scripture, to exhortation, to teaching.' Three pillars of Reformed worship: lectio (reading), paraklesis (exhortation), didaskalia (teaching). The public reading of Scripture inherits synagogue practice and continues in the church's liturgy. Exhortation applies the text to conscience; teaching expounds it doctrinally. Reformed worship has historically prioritized this triad against ritualism and entertainment-driven services. The pastor's chief work is verbal: reading, exhorting, teaching the Word. 'Devote yourself' (proseche) implies focused application against distractions. Until Paul comes, indeed throughout the church age, this Word-centered ministry remains the irreplaceable means of grace.
1Tim.5.1: 'Do not rebuke an older man (presbutero) but encourage him as a father.' The same word that elsewhere designates the office of elder here refers to age. Cultural respect for seniors is baptized into Christian ecclesial practice. Reformed pastoral counseling recognizes the necessity of differentiated address: same truth, varied tone. The household analogy (father, brothers, mothers, sisters, v. 2) frames the church as oikos, not bureaucracy. A young pastor like Timothy must combine boldness with deference. 'Encourage' (parakalei) is the same word used for Holy Spirit's ministry, suggesting pastoral exhortation should mirror the Comforter's gentle, truth-bearing approach.
1Tim.5.8: 'If anyone does not provide for his relatives, and especially for members of his household, he has denied the faith and is worse than an unbeliever.' Family responsibility is so embedded in Christian ethics that its abandonment constitutes practical apostasy. Reformed covenantal thinking views the household as a primary sphere of sanctification. Even pagans recognized familial duty (Aristotle, Cicero); Christian failure here disgraces the gospel. The verse rebukes piety that abandons proximate duties for grand schemes. Faith without works of family care is dead. The application extends to elder care, child-rearing, and economic provision as gospel-shaped vocations.
1Tim.5.17: 'Let the elders who rule well be considered worthy of double honor, especially those who labor in preaching and teaching.' This verse distinguishes ruling elders from teaching elders, foundational to Presbyterian polity. 'Double honor' (diples times) likely combines respect and remuneration; v. 18's wage citations confirm financial support. Reformed two-office or three-office views differ on details, but all affirm pastoral compensation as biblical and necessary. Those who labor in word work hard (kopiontes, sweat-soaked toil); the church owes them dignity and provision. Underpaid pastors signal undervalued ministry; overpaid pastors risk spiritual softening.
1Tim.5.20: 'As for those who persist in sin, rebuke them in the presence of all, so that the rest may stand in fear.' Public sin merits public rebuke when private admonition fails. Reformed church discipline follows Matthew 18's escalation but recognizes that elders, given their public office, may require more visible correction (v. 19's witnesses guard against frivolous accusation). The deterrent effect on the congregation is intentional: visible discipline sobers the rest. Modern reluctance to discipline elders has produced spiritual corruption; Paul mandates uncomfortable but necessary public accountability. The church's holiness depends upon courageous elders willing to confront their own.
1Tim.6.15: 'The blessed and only Sovereign, the King of kings and Lord of lords.' This royal doxology asserts Christ's (or God's, taken broadly) absolute supremacy over all earthly powers. Reformed theology cherishes such Kingship language as the bulwark against tyranny: every Caesar bows before the true Sovereign. 'Blessed' (makarios) and 'only' (monos) exclude rivals. The verse encourages persecuted believers under Domitianic shadows or any totalitarian threat: their final allegiance is to a higher throne. Christological reading sees Christ as the King of kings; the verse fits naturally with Revelation 17:14; 19:16.
2Chr.1.1: And Solomon the son of David was strengthened in his kingdom, and the LORD his God was with him, and magnified him exceedingly. The new reign opens with the same theological keynote that ended the first volume: the LORD his God was with him. Solomon's strength is not native but bestowed. Reformed theology resists every account of leadership that locates power in the ruler himself; the Chronicler will not allow such a misreading. Even the most magnified king is a steward of the magnification.
2Chr.1.7: In that night did God appear unto Solomon, and said unto him, Ask what I shall give thee. The dream at Gibeon places the entire reign under the umbrella of divine generosity. God invites the king to ask, and what he asks reveals what he most values. The post-exilic reader, like the church, is similarly invited to ask, and what we ask of God in prayer is a diagnostic of the heart.
2Chr.1.10: Give me now wisdom and knowledge, that I may go out and come in before this people: for who can judge this thy people, that is so great? Solomon's request is consciously pastoral. He is not asking wisdom for his own private flourishing but for the government of the people. Reformed pastoral theology echoes this: the wisdom God gives ministers is given for the flock, not for self-promotion.
2Chr.1.12: Wisdom and knowledge is granted unto thee; and I will give thee riches, and wealth, and honour, such as none of the kings have had that have been before thee, neither shall there any after thee have the like. The principle of Matthew 6:33 is illustrated centuries early: seek the kingdom and its righteousness, and these other things shall be added. Solomon does not get less by asking for one good thing; he gets more.
2Chr.2.5: And the house which I build is great: for great is our God above all gods. The reason for the temple's grandeur is theological. The greatness of the building is meant to confess the greatness of the God it serves. Reformed worship preserves this principle: the form of worship must communicate the truth of who God is.
2Chr.2.6: But who is able to build him an house, seeing the heaven and heaven of heavens cannot contain him? who am I then, that I should build him an house, save only to burn sacrifice before him? Solomon confesses the inadequacy of any house for the infinite God. The temple is not God's container but the place where He has condescended to be sought. The post-exilic temple, smaller than its predecessor, would inherit the same theology: the place where God meets His people, not where He resides.
2Chr.3.1: Then Solomon began to build the house of the LORD at Jerusalem in mount Moriah, where the LORD appeared unto David his father, in the place that David had prepared in the threshingfloor of Ornan the Jebusite. Mount Moriah is the place where Abraham bound Isaac, where the LORD provided a substitute. The temple is built on the ground of substitutionary atonement. The geography preaches the gospel before a stone is laid.
2Chr.3.10: And in the most holy house he made two cherubims of image work, and overlaid them with gold. The cherubim guard the holy of holies as they once guarded Eden. The temple is a partial recovery of paradise, a place where God meets man inside the curtain. The unrent veil before Christ stood as both invitation and barrier; the rent veil at the cross declares the end of the barrier.
2Chr.5.13: It came even to pass, as the trumpeters and singers were as one, to make one sound to be heard in praising and thanking the LORD; and when they lifted up their voice with the trumpets and cymbals and instruments of musick, and praised the LORD, saying, For he is good; for his mercy endureth for ever: that then the house was filled with a cloud, even the house of the LORD. The unison of praise is met by the cloud of glory. The Chronicler insists on the connection: God dwells where His people corporately confess His mercy. The means of grace are not magic, but they are the appointed channels of divine presence.
