2Chr.5.14: So that the priests could not stand to minister by reason of the cloud: for the glory of the LORD had filled the house of God. The cloud that filled the wilderness tabernacle now fills the temple. The post-exilic community, whose second temple lacked this visible glory, must read this verse with longing and with hope, knowing that Haggai had promised an even greater glory in the latter house.
2Chr.6.14: O LORD God of Israel, there is no God like thee in the heaven, nor in the earth; which keepest covenant, and shewest mercy unto thy servants, that walk before thee with all their hearts. Solomon's prayer of dedication confesses covenant fidelity as the central attribute of God. The God of Israel is uniquely the covenant-keeping God. Reformed federal theology rests on this confession.
2Chr.6.18: But will God in very deed dwell with men on the earth? behold, heaven and the heaven of heavens cannot contain thee; how much less this house which I have built! The pivotal question of biblical theology is asked at the dedication of the temple. The answer in Christ is yes; God in the very deed of incarnation has dwelt with men on the earth. John 1:14 is the canonical answer to Solomon's question.
2Chr.6.21: Hearken therefore unto the supplications of thy servant, and of thy people Israel, which they shall make toward this place: hear thou from thy dwelling place, even from heaven; and when thou hearest, forgive. The temple is a place toward which prayer is directed; the answer comes from heaven. The pattern teaches that ordained means do not exhaust divine presence. Christ becomes the one toward whom the church now prays, and the answer still descends from the heavenly throne.
2Chr.6.36: If they sin against thee, (for there is no man which sinneth not,) and thou be angry with them, and deliver them to the enemies, and they carry them away captives unto a land far off or near. Solomon foresees exile. The Chronicler quotes the prayer in full because his readers have lived its fulfillment. The verse stands as a prophetic anticipation of Babylon, written into the founding moment of the temple.
2Chr.6.37: Yet if they bethink themselves in the land whither they are carried captive, and turn and pray unto thee in the land of their captivity, saying, We have sinned, we have done amiss, and have dealt wickedly. The path back is named in advance: bethink, turn, pray. Daniel praying toward Jerusalem with the windows open enacts this very prescription. The Chronicler invites his readers to identify with this confession.
2Chr.6.39: Then hear thou from the heavens, even from thy dwelling place, their prayer and their supplications, and maintain their cause, and forgive thy people which have sinned against thee. The post-exilic community is essentially the answer to this prayer. The fact that they are home, rebuilding the temple, is the historical embodiment of God's hearing. Their existence preaches the faithfulness of God to Solomon's ancient petition.
2Chr.7.1: Now when Solomon had made an end of praying, the fire came down from heaven, and consumed the burnt offering and the sacrifices; and the glory of the LORD filled the house. Fire from heaven seals the prayer. The God who answered Elijah on Carmel and Moses at the tabernacle answers Solomon at the temple. Reformed theology recognizes this as the visible signature of acceptance, the sacrifice received and the house consecrated.
2Chr.7.16: For now have I chosen and sanctified this house, that my name may be there for ever: and mine eyes and mine heart shall be there perpetually. The temple is chosen as the place of the divine name. Christ will later identify himself as the true temple, the place where the divine name dwells perpetually in flesh.
2Chr.7.19: But if ye turn away, and forsake my statutes and my commandments... then will I pluck them up by the roots out of my land. The warning is exact, and history confirms its precision. Exile is not divine caprice but covenant consequence. The post-exilic reader stands on the far side of this very plucking up.
2Chr.7.22: And it shall be answered, Because they forsook the LORD God of their fathers, which brought them forth out of the land of Egypt, and laid hold on other gods, and worshipped them, and served them: therefore hath he brought all this evil upon them. The verdict on exile is offered in advance. The Chronicler's audience is meant to read this and understand exactly why their parents and grandparents went to Babylon. Idolatry, not Babylonian arms, was the root cause.
2Chr.9.1: And when the queen of Sheba heard of the fame of Solomon, she came to prove Solomon with hard questions at Jerusalem. The nations come to Zion. The Chronicler highlights this Gentile encounter as a foretaste of the eschatological vision in Isaiah 60. The greater Solomon, Christ, draws all nations to Himself.
2Chr.9.8: Blessed be the LORD thy God, which delighted in thee to set thee on his throne, to be king for the LORD thy God: because thy God loved Israel, to establish them for ever, therefore made he thee king. The Queen of Sheba theologizes the throne better than many natives. The throne is the LORD's, occupied by the LORD's appointee, for the love of the LORD's people. Reformed political theology has long recognized this triadic structure.
2Chr.9.31: And Solomon slept with his fathers, and he was buried in the city of David his father: and Rehoboam his son reigned in his stead. The Chronicler omits the apostasies of Solomon's later years, recorded so painfully in 1 Kings 11. He is not whitewashing history but selecting for his post-exilic audience a portrait of the temple's founder that emphasizes the worship his successors must imitate.
2Chr.10.15: So the king hearkened not unto the people; for the cause was of God, that the LORD might perform his word, which he spake by the hand of Ahijah the Shilonite to Jeroboam the son of Nebat. The fracture of the kingdom is named as the cause was of God. The Chronicler holds together divine sovereignty and human agency. Rehoboam's folly is genuine, and the LORD's word is genuinely fulfilled through it. Concurrence is the doctrine of providence here in narrative form.
2Chr.11.16: And after them out of all the tribes of Israel such as set their hearts to seek the LORD God of Israel came to Jerusalem, to sacrifice unto the LORD God of their fathers. A remnant from the northern tribes migrates south because they will not abandon the temple. The Chronicler's vision of true Israel is not tribal but worship-based. Wherever the LORD is rightly worshipped, there is the people of God.
2Chr.12.1: And it came to pass, when Rehoboam had established the kingdom, and had strengthened himself, he forsook the law of the LORD, and all Israel with him. Strength becomes the seedbed of apostasy. The Chronicler will repeat this pattern through the kings: prosperity tempts to forgetfulness. The lesson for any generation, including the church, is to expect spiritual danger at the moments of greatest material success.
2Chr.13.10: But as for us, the LORD is our God, and we have not forsaken him; and the priests, which minister unto the LORD, are the sons of Aaron, and the Levites wait upon their business. Abijah's pre-battle speech grounds the southern kingdom's legitimacy in legitimate worship. The Chronicler is providing his post-exilic audience with the categories to evaluate competing religious claims. True religion has the right priests, the right place, and the right LORD.
2Chr.13.18: Thus the children of Israel were brought under at that time, and the children of Judah prevailed, because they relied upon the LORD God of their fathers. Victory is interpreted theologically: they relied upon the LORD. The military details matter less to the Chronicler than the spiritual posture. The post-exilic community, militarily weak, is being taught that reliance is the decisive variable.
2Chr.14.11: And Asa cried unto the LORD his God, and said, LORD, it is nothing with thee to help, whether with many, or with them that have no power: help us, O LORD our God; for we rest on thee, and in thy name we go against this multitude. Asa's prayer captures the theology of the remnant. Whether with many or with few, the LORD's hand is the variable. The church in any era of weakness has always returned to this verse.
2Chr.16.12: And Asa in the thirty and ninth year of his reign was diseased in his feet, until his disease was exceeding great: yet in his disease he sought not to the LORD, but to the physicians. The Chronicler's critique is not of medicine as such but of a heart that does not seek the LORD even in sickness. The earlier praying Asa has become the autonomous Asa. Sanctification is not a guaranteed trajectory; even reformers can fall.
2Chr.17.9: And they taught in Judah, and had the book of the law of the LORD with them, and went about throughout all the cities of Judah, and taught the people. Jehoshaphat dispatches a teaching mission. The Word of God is the engine of national renewal. The post-exilic community under Ezra would adopt this same model of intensive teaching of the law.
2Chr.18.7: And the king of Israel said unto Jehoshaphat, There is yet one man, by whom we may enquire of the LORD: but I hate him; because he never prophesied good unto me, but always evil: the same is Micaiah the son of Imla. The honest prophet is hated by the apostate king. The pattern recurs throughout church history: faithful preaching is rarely welcomed where it is most needed. Reformed pulpits inherit Micaiah's mantle.
2Chr.19.6: And said to the judges, Take heed what ye do: for ye judge not for man, but for the LORD, who is with you in the judgment. Civil judges judge for the LORD. Jehoshaphat's charge to the magistrates places earthly justice under heavenly accountability. Reformed theology has always insisted that the magistrate is God's minister.
2Chr.20.20: Believe in the LORD your God, so shall ye be established; believe his prophets, so shall ye prosper. The double imperative joins faith in God to faith in His prophets. There is no faith in God that bypasses His Word. Reformed theology grounds saving faith in the gospel preached and read.
2Chr.20.21: And when he had consulted with the people, he appointed singers unto the LORD, and that should praise the beauty of holiness, as they went out before the army, saying, Praise the LORD; for his mercy endureth for ever. The army is led by singers. Worship goes before warfare. The Chronicler's portrait of holy war is one in which the LORD's people sing the LORD's mercy and the LORD Himself fights.
2Chr.20.22: And when they began to sing and to praise, the LORD set ambushments against the children of Ammon, Moab, and mount Seir, which were come against Judah; and they were smitten. The praise is the catalyst for the LORD's intervention. The post-exilic community, often beleaguered, is taught that worship is not retreat from the world but engagement with it on the deepest level.
2Chr.21.7: Howbeit the LORD would not destroy the house of David, because of the covenant that he had made with David, and as he promised to give a light to him and to his sons for ever. The covenant with David endures despite Jehoram's wickedness. The Chronicler reassures his post-exilic readers: the line that produces Messiah does not depend on the merits of any individual king. The light promised is finally Christ Himself.
2Chr.22.10: But when Athaliah the mother of Ahaziah saw that her son was dead, she arose and destroyed all the seed royal of the house of Judah. The Davidic line is reduced to the brink of extinction. The hostility against the seed of the woman, traceable from Cain to Pharaoh to Athaliah, is finally directed at the boy Joash. Sovereign providence will preserve the line by a hairbreadth.
2Chr.22.11: But Jehoshabeath, the daughter of the king, took Joash the son of Ahaziah, and stole him from among the king's sons that were slain... so Jehoshabeath, the daughter of king Jehoram, the wife of Jehoiada the priest... hid him from Athaliah, so that she slew him not. A priest's wife saves the Davidic line. The unsung courage of one woman preserves the covenant. Reformed theology recognizes the often hidden providence by which God secures His promises through ordinary fidelity.
2Chr.23.16: And Jehoiada made a covenant between him, and between all the people, and between the king, that they should be the LORD's people. The restoration of Joash includes a covenant renewal. True political reformation is grounded in covenantal commitment. The post-exilic community under Ezra and Nehemiah will follow the same pattern.
2Chr.24.2: And Joash did that which was right in the sight of the LORD all the days of Jehoiada the priest. Joash's faithfulness is conditioned on Jehoiada's mentorship. When the mentor dies, the king collapses. The lesson for the church is sober: borrowed piety does not survive the death of the lender.
2Chr.24.20: And the Spirit of God came upon Zechariah the son of Jehoiada the priest, which stood above the people, and said unto them, Thus saith God, Why transgress ye the commandments of the LORD, that ye cannot prosper? because ye have forsaken the LORD, he hath also forsaken you. The Spirit empowers prophetic speech. Zechariah delivers the covenantal warning that Jehoiada had once embodied. The pattern of forsaking and being forsaken is at the heart of covenant theology.
2Chr.25.2: And he did that which was right in the sight of the LORD, but not with a perfect heart. The Chronicler's verdict on Amaziah introduces the category of half-hearted obedience. Outward conformity without inward devotion is judged insufficient. Reformed pietism has always pressed this distinction.
2Chr.26.5: And he sought God in the days of Zechariah, who had understanding in the visions of God: and as long as he sought the LORD, God made him to prosper. Uzziah's prosperity correlates with his seeking. The conjunction is the Chronicler's recurring formula. As long as he sought, God made him prosper.
2Chr.26.16: But when he was strong, his heart was lifted up to his destruction: for he transgressed against the LORD his God, and went into the temple of the LORD to burn incense upon the altar of incense. Strength becomes pride; pride becomes presumption. Uzziah crosses the line between king and priest, and is judged with leprosy. Reformed theology insists on the distinction of offices, and the verse stands as warning against any conflation of civil and ecclesial authority.
2Chr.28.22: And in the time of his distress did he trespass yet more against the LORD: this is that king Ahaz. The verse contains the bitter parenthesis: this is that king Ahaz. The Chronicler holds him up as the paradigm of inverted repentance: the more God chastens, the more Ahaz rebels. Sin not repented of becomes a fixed posture.
2Chr.29.3: He in the first year of his reign, in the first month, opened the doors of the house of the LORD, and repaired them. Hezekiah's reform begins on day one. The reopening of the temple is the first political act. Reformed exposition has long noted this pattern: true reformation begins with the recovery of right worship, not with social or military reform.
2Chr.29.6: For our fathers have trespassed, and done that which was evil in the eyes of the LORD our God, and have forsaken him, and have turned away their faces from the habitation of the LORD, and turned their backs. Hezekiah confesses corporate sin. The body posture metaphor is exact: faces away, backs turned. The post-exilic community recognizes the same indictment of the previous generations.
2Chr.29.10: Now it is in mine heart to make a covenant with the LORD God of Israel, that his fierce wrath may turn away from us. Covenant renewal is the means of averting wrath. Hezekiah's instinct is theological: the relationship is not first administrative but covenantal.
2Chr.29.27: And Hezekiah commanded to offer the burnt offering upon the altar. And when the burnt offering began, the song of the LORD began also with the trumpets, and with the instruments ordained by David king of Israel. Sacrifice and song begin together. The Chronicler emphasizes the inseparability of atonement and praise. The church's liturgy preserves this same union: the gospel preached and the gospel sung.
2Chr.29.36: And Hezekiah rejoiced, and all the people, that God had prepared the people: for the thing was done suddenly. The reformation is attributed not to the king's effort but to God's preparation. Reformed soteriology recognizes here the doctrine of preparatory grace: God prepares the heart and then accepts the work.
2Chr.30.6: So the posts went with the letters from the king and his princes throughout all Israel and Judah, and according to the commandment of the king, saying, Ye children of Israel, turn again unto the LORD God of Abraham, Isaac, and Israel, and he will return to the remnant of you, that are escaped out of the hand of the kings of Assyria. Hezekiah summons the remnant of the northern tribes to Passover. The category of remnant becomes operative. The God who keeps a remnant is the God who will keep one through Babylon also. The post-exilic readers see themselves in this remnant.
2Chr.30.9: For if ye turn again unto the LORD, your brethren and your children shall find compassion before them that lead them captive, so that they shall come again into this land: for the LORD your God is gracious and merciful, and will not turn away his face from you, if ye return unto him. The promise of return after captivity is given before captivity even falls. The covenant of grace built into the warnings is itself the foundation of post-exilic hope.
2Chr.30.18: For a multitude of the people, even many of Ephraim, and Manasseh, Issachar, and Zebulun, had not cleansed themselves, yet did they eat the passover otherwise than it was written. But Hezekiah prayed for them, saying, The good LORD pardon every one. Pardon is sought for ritual irregularity. The Chronicler shows the king interceding, and the LORD hearing. Mercy outweighs minutiae when the heart is set to seek God.
2Chr.32.7: Be strong and courageous, be not afraid nor dismayed for the king of Assyria, nor for all the multitude that is with him: for there be more with us than with him. Hezekiah's exhortation reframes the visible balance of power. The unseen forces of the LORD outweigh the seen forces of Assyria. Elisha's earlier vision is the theological backdrop.
2Chr.32.8: With him is an arm of flesh; but with us is the LORD our God to help us, and to fight our battles. The contrast between arm of flesh and the LORD our God is the central antithesis of biblical faith. Reformed theology presses this antithesis at every point: human strength versus divine grace.
2Chr.32.21: And the LORD sent an angel, which cut off all the mighty men of valour, and the leaders and captains in the camp of the king of Assyria. So he returned with shame of face to his own land. The deliverance of Jerusalem is wrought without Israelite swords. The post-exilic readers, knowing how Sennacherib's empire had eventually collapsed entirely, would read this as a historical foretaste of Babylon's later fall.
2Chr.32.31: Howbeit in the business of the ambassadors of the princes of Babylon, who sent unto him to enquire of the wonder that was done in the land, God left him, to try him, that he might know all that was in his heart. The doctrine of testing is articulated explicitly. God leaves Hezekiah momentarily to expose what is in his heart. Reformed sanctification recognizes this as a regular feature of Christian experience: the providential exposure of remaining indwelling sin.
2Chr.33.12: And when he was in affliction, he besought the LORD his God, and humbled himself greatly before the God of his fathers. Manasseh, the worst of kings, repents in Babylonian captivity. The Chronicler includes this story (omitted in 2 Kings) as a paradigm: even the chief of sinners can be brought to repentance. The post-exilic community, returned from captivity, is the larger embodiment of Manasseh's individual story.
2Chr.33.13: And prayed unto him: and he was intreated of him, and heard his supplication, and brought him again to Jerusalem into his kingdom. Then Manasseh knew that the LORD he was God. The phrase Manasseh knew that the LORD he was God captures the goal of all chastening. The post-exilic community, brought back to Jerusalem, also now knows.
2Chr.34.1: Josiah was eight years old when he began to reign, and he reigned in Jerusalem one and thirty years. The young king inherits a deeply corrupted kingdom. Reformed theology has often celebrated Josiah as a paradigm of God raising up reformers in the most unlikely circumstances. Sovereignty governs the timing of reformation.
2Chr.34.3: For in the eighth year of his reign, while he was yet young, he began to seek after the God of David his father: and in the twelfth year he began to purge Judah and Jerusalem from the high places. Personal seeking precedes public reform. The pattern is theologically vital: the reformer must first be reformed himself. The church renews itself only through individuals first renewed by grace.
2Chr.34.14: And when they brought out the money that was brought into the house of the LORD, Hilkiah the priest found a book of the law of the LORD given by Moses. The recovery of the Word of God is the heart of all true reformation. The book had been there all along, lost under the rubble of neglect. The post-exilic community under Ezra would replicate this rediscovery on a national scale.
2Chr.34.27: Because thine heart was tender, and thou didst humble thyself before God, when thou heardest his words against this place, and against the inhabitants thereof, and humbledst thyself before me, and didst rend thy clothes, and weep before me; I have even heard thee also, saith the LORD. The tender heart is named as the precondition of mercy. Reformed pastoral theology cherishes this verse as a portrait of true repentance: hearing the Word, humbling, rending, weeping.
2Chr.35.6: So kill the passover, and sanctify yourselves, and prepare your brethren, that they may do according to the word of the LORD by the hand of Moses. Josiah's Passover is the largest since Samuel's day. The post-exilic community would similarly recover the Passover after their return. Continuity of worship across catastrophe is one of the Chronicler's chief themes.
2Chr.36.13: And he also rebelled against king Nebuchadnezzar, who had made him swear by God: but he stiffened his neck, and hardened his heart from turning unto the LORD God of Israel. Zedekiah's final crime is the breach of an oath sworn in the name of the LORD. Reformed political theology has always treated oaths with the utmost seriousness, since they invoke the divine name.
2Chr.36.15: And the LORD God of their fathers sent to them by his messengers, rising up betimes, and sending; because he had compassion on his people, and on his dwelling place. The patience of God is the backdrop of every chastening. He sent and sent again before He at last sent to Babylon. The mercy of God is not the absence of judgment but the long delay of judgment in the hope of repentance.
2Chr.36.16: But they mocked the messengers of God, and despised his words, and misused his prophets, until the wrath of the LORD arose against his people, till there was no remedy. The phrase till there was no remedy marks the end of patience. The post-exilic readers recognize this verse as the theological diagnosis of why their parents went into captivity.
2Chr.36.21: To fulfil the word of the LORD by the mouth of Jeremiah, until the land had enjoyed her sabbaths: for as long as she lay desolate she kept sabbath, to fulfil threescore and ten years. The seventy years of exile are interpreted as Sabbath rest for the land. Even judgment serves a purpose; the land is healing while the people repent.
2Chr.36.22: Now in the first year of Cyrus king of Persia, that the word of the LORD spoken by the mouth of Jeremiah might be accomplished, the LORD stirred up the spirit of Cyrus king of Persia, that he made a proclamation throughout all his kingdom, and put it also in writing, saying. The Chronicler ends his work with the decree of Cyrus, the same point at which the book of Ezra begins. The historical pivot is interpreted theologically: the LORD stirred up the spirit of Cyrus.
2Chr.36.23: Thus saith Cyrus king of Persia, All the kingdoms of the earth hath the LORD God of heaven given me; and he hath charged me to build him an house in Jerusalem, which is in Judah. Who is there among you of all his people? The LORD his God be with him, and let him go up. The book ends not with a period but with an open invitation: let him go up. The Chronicler concludes his entire history with a summons to ascent. The post-exilic community is invited to enact the very meaning of the book by going up to worship.
2Chr.1.5: Moreover the brasen altar, that Bezaleel the son of Uri, the son of Hur, had made, he put before the tabernacle of the LORD: and Solomon and the congregation sought unto it. The continuity of the sacrificial altar from Bezaleel to Solomon is theologically rich. The same God who furnished the wilderness tabernacle furnishes the temple. Worship is one continuous stream from Sinai to Zion.
2Chr.2.12: Hiram said moreover, Blessed be the LORD God of Israel, that made heaven and earth, who hath given to David the king a wise son, endued with prudence and understanding, that might build an house for the LORD, and an house for his kingdom. Even a Tyrian king confesses the LORD God of Israel. The temple is being built with materials and labor that confess what they construct. Reformed missiology has always seen here a foretaste of the gathering of nations.
2Chr.4.20: Moreover the candlesticks with their lamps, that they should burn after the manner before the oracle, of pure gold. The lamps before the oracle prefigure the seven Spirits before the throne in Revelation 4. The temple is a model of heavenly realities, and the Chronicler is preparing the eschatological imagination of his audience.
2Chr.5.1: Thus all the work that Solomon made for the house of the LORD was finished: and Solomon brought in all the things that David his father had dedicated. Father and son cooperate across the divide of death. David prepared, Solomon built. Reformed theology has long pressed the importance of generational labor. We work for what we will not see, trusting our children to complete what we have only prepared.
2Chr.7.3: And when all the children of Israel saw how the fire came down, and the glory of the LORD upon the house, they bowed themselves with their faces to the ground upon the pavement, and worshipped, and praised the LORD, saying, For he is good; for his mercy endureth for ever. The proper response to manifest glory is prostration. The Chronicler shows the people on the ground confessing covenant mercy. Reformed worship preserves the bodily logic of reverence.
2Chr.8.13: Even after a certain rate every day, offering according to the commandment of Moses, on the sabbaths, and on the new moons, and on the solemn feasts, three times in the year. Worship is calendared. The Chronicler grounds the rhythms of Israelite life in Mosaic command. The Christian liturgical year, in its various forms, has always traced its instinct back to such verses.
2Chr.9.22: And king Solomon passed all the kings of the earth in riches and wisdom. Solomon's preeminence is presented not for personal honor but as testimony to the God who chose him. The greater Solomon, Christ, possesses all wisdom not by gift but by nature.
2Chr.11.4: Thus saith the LORD, Ye shall not go up, nor fight against your brethren: return every man to his house: for this thing is done of me. And they obeyed the words of the LORD, and returned from going against Jeroboam. Civil war is restrained by the Word of God. The fracture is a divine judgment, not to be reversed by human force. The post-exilic community is taught that political realities, even painful ones, are sometimes ordained by the LORD.
2Chr.13.5: Ought ye not to know that the LORD God of Israel gave the kingdom over Israel to David for ever, even to him and to his sons by a covenant of salt? The covenant of salt signals indissoluble permanence. Abijah's appeal to this covenant is a confession that the kingdom belongs irrevocably to David's line. The Messianic anticipation built into the verse is unmistakable.
2Chr.14.7: Therefore he said unto Judah, Let us build these cities, and make about them walls, and towers, gates, and bars, while the land is yet before us; because we have sought the LORD our God, we have sought him, and he hath given us rest on every side. Building proceeds because rest has been granted. Asa's pattern is reformation followed by construction. The post-exilic builders Zerubbabel and Nehemiah will follow the same logic.
2Chr.16.7: And at that time Hanani the seer came to Asa king of Judah, and said unto him, Because thou hast relied on the king of Syria, and not relied on the LORD thy God, therefore is the host of the king of Syria escaped out of thine hand. Misplaced reliance is the recurring sin of the kings. Asa, who once trusted in God against Zerah's million, now trusts in Syrian arms. Reformed theology recognizes the perpetual temptation to substitute pragmatism for faith.
2Chr.17.6: And his heart was lifted up in the ways of the LORD: moreover he took away the high places and groves out of Judah. Heart and reform are linked. Jehoshaphat's outward purification follows from his inward elevation. The order matters: outward reform without inward devotion is hypocrisy.
2Chr.18.13: And Micaiah said, As the LORD liveth, even what my God saith, that will I speak. The prophet's oath of faithfulness to the divine word is the charter of every faithful preacher. Reformed homiletics returns again and again to this verse: the preacher speaks not his own opinions but what God has said.
2Chr.19.7: Wherefore now let the fear of the LORD be upon you; take heed and do it: for there is no iniquity with the LORD our God, nor respect of persons, nor taking of gifts. The character of God is the standard for the character of judges. No iniquity, no partiality, no bribery in God means none in His magistrates either. Reformed political theology has always argued for impartial justice on this ground.
2Chr.20.7: Art not thou our God, who didst drive out the inhabitants of this land before thy people Israel, and gavest it to the seed of Abraham thy friend for ever? Abraham is named the friend of God. The covenant is appealed to as the foundation of present prayer. James 2:23 will pick up this title to ground his teaching on faith and works.
2Chr.21.12: And there came a writing to him from Elijah the prophet, saying, Thus saith the LORD God of David thy father, Because thou hast not walked in the ways of Jehoshaphat thy father, nor in the ways of Asa king of Judah. The northern prophet Elijah corresponds with the southern king. The unity of God's prophetic ministry across the divided kingdom is reaffirmed. The Word of God knows no political border.
2Chr.23.11: Then they brought out the king's son, and put upon him the crown, and gave him the testimony, and made him king. And Jehoiada and his sons anointed him, and said, God save the king. The boy Joash receives both crown and testimony. Civil rule is bound to the Word of God. Reformed coronation oaths have always required the king to govern under the Bible.
2Chr.24.13: So the workmen wrought, and the work was perfected by them, and they set the house of God in his state, and strengthened it. The temple is repaired under Joash. The pattern of restoration through faithful labor anticipates the post-exilic rebuilding under Zerubbabel. God's house is restored generation by generation.
2Chr.25.7: But there came a man of God to him, saying, O king, let not the army of Israel go with thee; for the LORD is not with Israel, to wit, with all the children of Ephraim. Amaziah is told to dismiss the mercenaries because the LORD is not with them. Reformed theology has long pressed the principle that no advantage gained without God is truly an advantage.
2Chr.27.6: So Jotham became mighty, because he prepared his ways before the LORD his God. Preparation of the heart precedes might. Jotham is one of the Chronicler's positive case studies, demonstrating that quiet faithfulness is rewarded with steady strength.
2Chr.28.5: Wherefore the LORD his God delivered him into the hand of the king of Syria; and they smote him, and carried away a great multitude of them captives, and brought them to Damascus. And he was also delivered into the hand of the king of Israel, who smote him with a great slaughter. Ahaz's defeats are interpreted as divine deliverance into enemy hands. The active voice is striking: the LORD delivered him. Judgment is not absence of God but presence of God in wrath.
2Chr.29.11: My sons, be not now negligent: for the LORD hath chosen you to stand before him, to serve him, and that ye should minister unto him, and burn incense. Hezekiah charges the Levites with their high vocation. Election is for service. The Reformed doctrine of the priesthood of believers is rooted in this Old Covenant pattern, fulfilled and democratized in Christ.
2Chr.30.27: Then the priests the Levites arose and blessed the people: and their voice was heard, and their prayer came up to his holy dwelling place, even unto heaven. The benediction of the priests rises to heaven. Reformed worship retains this priestly element in the spoken benediction at the close of the service.
2Chr.31.21: And in every work that he began in the service of the house of God, and in the law, and in the commandments, to seek his God, he did it with all his heart, and prospered. The all-hearted seeker prospers. The Chronicler's recurring formula receives one of its clearest expressions. The condition of prosperity, theologically defined, is wholeness of devotion.
2Chr.32.20: And for this cause Hezekiah the king, and the prophet Isaiah the son of Amoz, prayed and cried to heaven. King and prophet pray together against Sennacherib. Reformed ecclesiology has long valued this cooperation of civil and prophetic offices. The prayer of the church and the prayer of the magistrate together rise to heaven.
2Chr.34.21: Go, enquire of the LORD for me, and for them that are left in Israel and in Judah, concerning the words of the book that is found: for great is the wrath of the LORD that is poured out upon us, because our fathers have not kept the word of the LORD, to do after all that is written in this book. Josiah commissions an inquiry on the basis of the rediscovered Scripture. The Word read precipitates the Word obeyed. Reformed worship insists on this priority of Scripture over practice.
2Chr.34.31: And the king stood in his place, and made a covenant before the LORD, to walk after the LORD, and to keep his commandments, and his testimonies, and his statutes, with all his heart, and with all his soul, to perform the words of the covenant which are written in this book. Josiah's covenant renewal sets the pattern for Ezra and Nehemiah's later renewals. Reformed church history likewise traces a long line of covenant renewals from Geneva to Westminster.
2Chr.35.18: And there was no passover like to that kept in Israel from the days of Samuel the prophet; neither did all the kings of Israel keep such a passover as Josiah kept. Josiah's Passover surpasses all in Israel since Samuel. The Chronicler's hyperbole signals the epochal nature of the moment. The post-exilic Passover under Ezra (Ezra 6) consciously imitates this great moment.
2Chr.36.10: And when the year was expired, king Nebuchadnezzar sent, and brought him to Babylon, with the goodly vessels of the house of the LORD, and made Zedekiah his brother king over him. The vessels of the temple are carried to Babylon. The Chronicler will not lose sight of these vessels; they will return in Ezra 1, and their return is the symbol of the restored worship.
2Chr.36.20: And them that had escaped from the sword carried he away to Babylon; where they were servants to him and his sons until the reign of the kingdom of Persia. The phrase until the reign of the kingdom of Persia signals the historical horizon of redemption. The dating presupposes the Cyrus decree that follows. Even in the darkest verse, the dawn is anticipated.
2Chr.6.32: Moreover concerning the stranger, which is not of thy people Israel, but is come from a far country for thy great name's sake, and thy mighty hand, and thy stretched out arm; if they come and pray in this house. Solomon's prayer extends to Gentile worshippers. The temple is a house of prayer for all nations, a phrase Christ Himself will quote. Reformed missiology grounds itself in such Old Testament universals.
2Chr.6.41: Now therefore arise, O LORD God, into thy resting place, thou, and the ark of thy strength: let thy priests, O LORD God, be clothed with salvation, and let thy saints rejoice in goodness. The closing of Solomon's prayer prefigures Psalm 132 and points to the rest of the Messianic age. Christ enters His resting place at the right hand of the Father, having clothed His saints with salvation.
2Chr.13.12: And, behold, God himself is with us for our captain, and his priests with sounding trumpets to cry alarm against you. O children of Israel, fight ye not against the LORD God of your fathers; for ye shall not prosper. Abijah identifies God Himself as the captain of His people. The post-exilic readers, and the church, find here the assurance that the LORD personally leads His people in their conflicts.
2Chr.30.8: Now be ye not stiffnecked, as your fathers were, but yield yourselves unto the LORD, and enter into his sanctuary, which he hath sanctified for ever: and serve the LORD your God, that the fierceness of his wrath may turn away from you. The post-exilic community is also called to yield. The pattern of yielding to God, entering His sanctuary, and serving Him is the substance of the Christian life as well.
2Chr.32.26: Notwithstanding Hezekiah humbled himself for the pride of his heart, both he and the inhabitants of Jerusalem, so that the wrath of the LORD came not upon them in the days of Hezekiah. Even Hezekiah needs to humble himself. Reformed sanctification refuses any account of the believer that exempts him from ongoing repentance. The best of saints continue to humble themselves before the LORD.
2Chr.33.6: And he caused his children to pass through the fire in the valley of the son of Hinnom: also he observed times, and used enchantments, and used witchcraft, and dealt with a familiar spirit, and with wizards: he wrought much evil in the sight of the LORD, to provoke him to anger. Manasseh's catalog of sins is complete and horrifying. The Chronicler's purpose in including the list is not to titillate but to magnify the mercy of God in the repentance that follows.
2Chr.34.33: And Josiah took away all the abominations out of all the countries that pertained to the children of Israel, and made all that were present in Israel to serve, even to serve the LORD their God. And all his days they departed not from following the LORD, the God of their fathers. The reformation under Josiah holds throughout his life. The verse stands as testimony that even in a doomed generation, individual faithfulness produces sustained obedience.
2Chr.35.22: Nevertheless Josiah would not turn his face from him, but disguised himself, that he might fight with him, and hearkened not unto the words of Necho from the mouth of God, and came to fight in the valley of Megiddo. Even good Josiah falls when he stops listening. The Chronicler is honest about the failures of the best kings. The lesson is sober: every saint must continue to listen for the Word of the LORD, even from unexpected mouths.
2Chr.36.14: Moreover all the chief of the priests, and the people, transgressed very much after all the abominations of the heathen; and polluted the house of the LORD which he had hallowed in Jerusalem. Even priests fall into apostasy. The corruption of the best is the worst corruption. The post-exilic priesthood under Joshua and Ezra inherits both the warning and the call to recover what had been polluted.
2Chr.6.7: Now it was in the heart of David my father to build an house for the name of the LORD God of Israel. The desire of David is honored even though the work fell to Solomon. The Chronicler preserves the dignity of unfulfilled holy desires. Reformed theology has always treated holy intentions as themselves a form of obedience.
2Chr.20.34: Now the rest of the acts of Jehoshaphat, first and last, behold, they are written in the book of Jehu the son of Hanani, who is mentioned in the book of the kings of Israel. The Chronicler sources his history with care. The historiography is not random but rooted in earlier inspired records. Scripture itself testifies to the careful preservation of Israel's memory.
2Chr.29.30: Moreover Hezekiah the king and the princes commanded the Levites to sing praise unto the LORD with the words of David, and of Asaph the seer. And they sang praises with gladness, and they bowed their heads and worshipped. The Psalter is the songbook of restoration. Reformed worship has always recognized the propriety of singing the inspired Psalms. The post-exilic community will pick up the same hymnal.
2Chr.31.5: And as soon as the commandment came abroad, the children of Israel brought in abundance the firstfruits of corn, wine, and oil, and honey, and of all the increase of the field; and the tithe of all things brought they in abundantly. The reform of giving accompanies the reform of worship. Generosity is a fruit of true religion. The post-exilic community under Nehemiah will face the same challenge of recovering the tithe.
2Chr.36.17: Therefore he brought upon them the king of the Chaldees, who slew their young men with the sword in the house of their sanctuary, and had no compassion upon young man or maiden, old man, or him that stooped for age: he gave them all into his hand. The judgment is total and indiscriminate within the covenant community. The horror of exile is preserved in the canonical record so that the post-exilic readers cannot forget the price of unbelief.
2John.1.1: 'The elder (ho presbyteros) to the elect lady (eklekte kyria) and her children.' John writes either to a particular Christian woman and her household or, more likely, to a sister congregation personified as a lady (cf. v. 13, 'your elect sister'). 'Elect' (eklekte) signals the covenantal, sovereign choosing of God which constitutes the church. John loves them en aletheia, 'in truth,' a phrase that will dominate the letter: truth is not merely doctrine held but the sphere in which Christian love operates. Reformed exposition stresses that election grounds Christian identity, and truth grounds Christian affection; love divorced from truth is sentimentality, truth divorced from love is cold orthodoxy.
2John.1.2: John loves them 'because of the truth (dia ten aletheian) which abides (menousan) in us and will be with us forever (eis ton aiona).' The truth here is no abstract proposition but the gospel of the incarnate Christ indwelling believers by the Spirit (cf. John 14:17). Its permanence ('forever') guarantees the perseverance of saints: what the Spirit has implanted, the Spirit preserves. Reformed theology hears the doctrine of the indwelling Word and Spirit, and the eternal security of those in covenant. The truth that abides is the same truth that John insists must shape every reception of teachers in this letter.
2John.1.3: 'Grace, mercy, and peace will be with us, from God the Father and from Jesus Christ the Father's Son, in truth and love.' Unusually, John adds eleos (mercy) to the typical charis kai eirene. He pairs the Father with the Son using parallel construction (para theou patros kai para Iesou Christou), affirming the full deity of Christ. The benediction is also a promise: estai, 'will be.' Truth and love together form the dual sphere of covenant blessing. Reformed readers note the high Christology and Trinitarian shape: blessing flows equally from Father and Son, in the Spirit who is truth.
2John.1.4: 'I rejoiced greatly (echaren lian) to find some of your children walking in truth (peripatountas en aletheia).' The walking metaphor (peripateo) recalls the Hebraic halakh, life as covenantal pilgrimage. To walk in truth is to live coram Deo, in the open before God, conformed to the gospel. John's joy reveals the pastor's heart: his greatest happiness is the obedience of his spiritual children. The qualifier 'some of your children' may hint that not all are walking faithfully, lending urgency to what follows. Reformed pastors learn that orthodoxy must issue in orthopraxy; doctrine without walking is dead.
2John.1.5: John appeals for the commandment 'we have had from the beginning (ap' arches),' that we love one another. The 'beginning' here is the apostolic deposit, the inaugural gospel teaching of Christ Himself (John 13:34). It is 'no new commandment' yet ever fresh. Love is covenantal obligation, not optional sentiment. Reformed exposition resists every novelty that detaches love from its biblical and Christological source. The command is not invented by the elder; it is delivered, traditioned, received. Love within the church is the sign that the same gospel still abides.
2John.1.6: 'And this is love: that we walk according to His commandments.' John defines agape concretely: obedience. He refuses the false dichotomy that pits love against law. Christ's commands are the contour of love, and walking (peripatomen) in them is love's actual shape. The verse echoes John 14:15: 'If you love me, you will keep my commandments.' Reformed theology rejoices here in the third use of the law: God's commands as the path of grateful love for the redeemed. Antinomianism cannot survive Johannine ethics; love is law-shaped, and law is love-fueled.
2John.1.7: 'For many deceivers (planoi) have gone out into the world, those who do not confess Jesus Christ coming in the flesh (erchomenon en sarki). Such a one is the deceiver and the antichrist.' The present participle erchomenon may stress the abiding incarnation: Christ remains true man. The proto-Gnostic and docetic denial of real incarnation is the antichrist spirit. Reformed Christology stands on the unimpaired humanity of the eternal Son, without which there is no atonement, no mediator, no resurrection hope. The verse warns that doctrinal error is not academic; it is satanic and damning.
2John.1.8: 'Watch yourselves (blepete heautous), so that you may not lose what we have worked for, but may win a full reward (misthon plere).' The textual variant aside, John exhorts perseverance: faith must be guarded amid deception. The 'reward' is not merit-earned but grace-given, the consummated inheritance of the saints. Reformed theology holds together perseverance and assurance: the elect will be kept, yet they are kept through diligent watchfulness, means of grace, and adherence to truth. The pastoral 'we have worked for' shows that ministers labor toward their flock's full eschatological reward.
2John.1.9: 'Everyone who goes ahead (proagon) and does not abide (menon) in the teaching of Christ does not have God.' Proagon, 'running ahead,' is the false teacher's progressivism, claiming advance beyond apostolic doctrine. To abide is to remain within received truth. The one who 'progresses past' Christ has neither Father nor Son. Reformed exposition rebuts every theological innovation that leaves behind the gospel of the apostles. Confessionalism is not stagnation but faithful abiding. Conversely, those who abide have both Father and Son, the full Trinitarian fellowship.
2John.1.10: 'If anyone comes to you and does not bring this teaching, do not receive him into the house (eis oikian) or give him a greeting (chairein).' In the first-century house-church context, hospitality functioned as endorsement and platforming. John forbids ecclesial reception of false teachers, not generic civility. The greeting (chairein) was a public solidarity. The verse is no warrant for personal rudeness but for ecclesial discernment. Reformed church polity learns here that there is a duty to refuse fellowship to those who deny Christ's incarnation. Love for the flock entails closing doors against wolves.
2John.1.11: 'For whoever greets him shares (koinonei) in his evil works.' Koinonein, to share or partake, is heavy covenantal language: to greet a heretical teacher is to participate in his evil. John assumes a moral solidarity that modern individualism resists. Endorsement is complicity. Reformed church discipline takes this seriously: harboring false teachers stains the whole body. The verse undergirds the duty of confessional fences and the sin of indifference to error. Love for Christ's name and for souls demands a clear refusal of doctrinal complicity.
2John.1.12: 'Though I have many things to write to you, I would rather not use paper (chartou) and ink (melanos), but I hope to come to you and speak face to face (stoma pros stoma), so that our joy may be complete.' Letters are gifts, but presence is fuller. The Hebraism 'mouth to mouth' recalls Num. 12:8 and bespeaks the personal, embodied character of Christian fellowship. Reformed ecclesiology values the ordinary means of grace within gathered community; even the inspired apostle prefers presence to ink. The fullness of joy (chara peplerōmenē) is communal, anticipating the eschatological joy of Christ with His bride.
2John.1.13: 'The children of your elect sister (adelphes sou tes eklektes) greet you.' Likely the congregation from which John writes sends greetings to the sister congregation addressed. The closing reaffirms election as the church's identity and shows the catholicity of true churches: each local body is bound to others by shared election in Christ. Reformed ecclesiology rejoices in this horizontal communion of the elect across distances, united by one Lord, one faith, one baptism. The letter ends as it began, in the warm air of sovereign grace and sisterly love.
2Kgs.1.10: 'Let fire come down from heaven, and consume thee and thy fifty.' Elijah's fire from heaven against Ahaziah's troops vindicates prophetic authority against royal arrogance. Jesus rebukes James and John for invoking this episode (Luke 9:54-55), not because the original judgment was unjust, but because the redemptive era has shifted from theocratic enforcement to gospel patience. Reformed theology distinguishes the prophetic-typological office from the new covenant ministry while affirming both as God's righteous administration. The same fire of judgment will yet fall in the eschaton on all who despise Christ.
2Kgs.1.16: 'Forasmuch as thou hast sent messengers to enquire of Baalzebub the god of Ekron... thou shalt not come down off that bed.' Ahaziah's consultation of a foreign deity functionally denies the existence of YHWH ('is it not because there is not a God in Israel?'). Reformed theology identifies idolatry as covenant breach and practical atheism. The repeated indictment underscores that the king's primary duty is to inquire of YHWH alone. Ahaziah's death by his own injury, certified by prophetic word, demonstrates the futility of seeking life apart from the living God.
2Kgs.2.9: 'Let a double portion of thy spirit be upon me.' Elisha requests the inheritance portion of the firstborn son (Deut 21:17). The phrase pi-shenayim is not 'twice as much' but the elder son's share, marking Elisha as Elijah's true heir in prophetic ministry. Reformed theology recognizes succession in office without confusion of person. Elisha will perform approximately twice the recorded miracles of Elijah, but the spirit is the same Spirit of God. The pattern foreshadows the apostolic succession in gospel ministry, where the Spirit is poured out without measure on Christ's body.
2Kgs.2.14: 'Where is the LORD God of Elijah?' Elisha strikes the Jordan with the mantle and the waters part. The question is not skeptical but confessional: Elisha invokes the covenant God whose presence is bound to His covenant Word, not to a single prophet. Reformed theology learns here that the saints come and go, but the LORD remains. Every faithful pastor must learn to cry, 'Where is the LORD God of my predecessors?' and find the same God responsive to faith. The mantle is given; the Spirit abides.
2Kgs.2.23: The mauling of the mocking youths (na'arim) by bears, often misread as petty vengeance, reveals serious covenantal reality: these were likely older youths from Bethel (the very seat of calf-worship) ridiculing the prophetic office and Elijah's translation ('go up, thou bald head'). Reformed theology recognizes prophetic honor as bound up with God's honor. The judgment is severe but not capricious: Bethel's idolatry has produced contempt for God's messengers. The bears come from God (cf. Deut 32:24).
2Kgs.4.1: The widow of one of the sons of the prophets faces the loss of her sons to debt-slavery. Elisha's response, multiplying her oil, demonstrates covenant compassion for the prophet's household. Reformed pastoral theology values this episode as evidence that ministerial families are particularly vulnerable and particularly under God's care. The miraculous oil (shemen, also the word for anointing oil) signals the Spirit's provision. The pots are filled until they cease, illustrating that divine supply is limited only by the vessels of faith we present.
2Kgs.4.32: Elisha raises the Shunammite's son, lying upon the child until 'the flesh of the child waxed warm.' The intimate, embodied identification of the prophet with the dead child prefigures Christ's incarnational solidarity with sinners dead in trespasses. Reformed theology sees here the gospel pattern: life flows from the Mediator who unites Himself to those He saves. The doubled stretching matches Elijah's pattern (1 Kgs 17), and the Shunammite's earlier hospitality (vv.8-10) is rewarded with covenant grace. God remembers His servants.
2Kgs.5.10: Elisha's terse instruction, delivered through a messenger, 'Go and wash in Jordan seven times,' offends Naaman's expectations of personal attention and dramatic ritual. Reformed soteriology embraces this offense: the gospel commands simple obedience to the appointed means, not heroic religious works. The seven-fold immersion in the humble Jordan typologically prefigures baptism, completed cleansing, and the Sabbath rest of the gospel. The means are despised by human pride but appointed by sovereign wisdom.
2Kgs.5.27: 'The leprosy therefore of Naaman shall cleave unto thee, and unto thy seed for ever.' Gehazi's covetousness, lying to obtain what Elisha refused, brings the very leprosy Naaman left behind onto the prophet's servant. Reformed theology sees here a sober warning: the ministry can be a means of grace and a means of judgment to the same individuals. Gehazi's heart was wrong even amid prophetic privilege, anticipating Judas. Outward proximity to gospel ministry does not save; only true faith does.
2Kgs.6.31: King Jehoram's vow, 'God do so and more also to me, if the head of Elisha the son of Shaphat shall stand on him this day,' reveals that he blames the prophet rather than repenting of national sin. The siege of Samaria, with its horrors of cannibalism (vv.28-29), is covenantal curse (Deut 28:53-57). Reformed theology recognizes that hostility to faithful preaching often intensifies precisely when judgment ought to drive men to repentance. The unconverted heart hates the messenger of its disease.
2Kgs.7.9: 'This day is a day of good tidings, and we hold our peace.' The four lepers, having discovered the abandoned Syrian camp, recognize the moral imperative of proclamation. Reformed missiology has long employed this verse: those who have tasted gospel deliverance cannot remain silent. The lepers, social outcasts, become heralds of salvation, illustrating that God uses unlikely instruments. The same logic drives the Great Commission: the bread of life cannot be hoarded while others perish.
2Kgs.8.13: Hazael's protest, 'But what, is thy servant a dog, that he should do this great thing?' is followed by the very deeds Elisha foretold. Reformed anthropology grasps this with sober realism: the human heart is capable of evils it cannot presently imagine when given opportunity and power. None can confidently say, 'I would never.' The depravity that lurks in unredeemed hearts emerges when restraints are removed. Elisha's tears (v.11) reveal pastoral grief over coming devastation, prefiguring Christ weeping over Jerusalem.
2Kgs.9.6: Jehu is anointed king over Israel by a young prophet at Elisha's command. The anointing carries the divine commission to execute judgment on the house of Ahab, fulfilling Elijah's word (1 Kgs 21:21-29). Reformed theology recognizes the legitimacy of this prophetic intervention while observing that Jehu's zeal will become its own corruption (Hos 1:4). Even an instrument of judgment must himself walk in covenantal faithfulness, which Jehu fails to do (10:31).
2Kgs.9.33: 'Throw her down. So they threw her down.' Jezebel, the great patroness of Baalism, meets the precise end Elijah prophesied: dogs eat her flesh, and no remains are gathered for burial. Reformed theology recognizes the principle: 'Be not deceived; God is not mocked' (Gal 6:7). Decades of patient sowing in evil reaps a corresponding harvest. The completeness of judgment matches the completeness of her wickedness. Yet even this is not God's final word; the cross alone shows the depth of mercy that meets justice.
2Kgs.10.31: 'But Jehu took no heed to walk in the law of the LORD God of Israel with all his heart: for he departed not from the sins of Jeroboam.' Jehu eradicated Baal but retained the calves. Reformed theology applies the principle: partial reformation is not true repentance. The reformer who removes the more obvious idol while preserving the politically useful one has not turned to God. Many ministries fall here, congratulating themselves for slaying foreign idols while domesticating native ones.
2Kgs.11.12: Joash is presented to the people, the testimony (edut, the law) is placed in his hand, and he is anointed king. Athaliah's bloody usurpation is overthrown by Jehoiada the priest, preserving the Davidic line through one infant heir. Reformed theology rejoices that the covenant promise hangs by a thread but does not break, because God upholds it. The lamp of David (1 Kgs 11:36) flickers but cannot be extinguished, for the covenant aims at Christ. The combination of priestly action with royal anointing prefigures the priest-king Messiah.
2Kgs.12.2: 'Jehoash did that which was right in the sight of the LORD all his days wherein Jehoiada the priest instructed him.' The qualifying clause is fatal: his righteousness was borrowed from his mentor. When Jehoiada died, Jehoash apostatized and even murdered Zechariah (2 Chr 24:17-22). Reformed sanctification stresses that true godliness is not transferable but must be personally rooted in regeneration. Outward conformity under godly influence is not the same as inward grace. Many fall when their spiritual scaffolding is removed.
2Kgs.13.21: When a corpse touches Elisha's bones, 'he revived, and stood up on his feet.' Even posthumously, the prophet's ministry attests resurrection power. Reformed theology resists relic-veneration here: the miracle attests not Elisha's bones but YHWH's promise. The episode functions as a literary preview of resurrection, anticipating Christ, whose own dead body became the source of life for all who are buried with Him in baptism (Rom 6:4-5). The wonder of Old Testament resurrection points to the climactic resurrection in the New.
2Kgs.14.25: Jeroboam II restored Israel's borders 'according to the word of the LORD God of Israel, which he spake by the hand of his servant Jonah.' This unexpected mercy upon a wicked king illustrates God's patience. Reformed theology recognizes that political and territorial restoration are not infallible signs of divine approval; God may bless temporally those whom He will judge eternally. Jonah's brief mention here introduces a prophet whose own mission to Nineveh will reveal God's sovereign mercy beyond Israel.
2Kgs.17.7: 'For so it was, that the children of Israel had sinned against the LORD their God.' The narrator's theological summary of the northern exile lists the cumulative covenantal violations. Reformed historiography sees here the providential interpretation of national catastrophe: Assyrian conquest is divine judgment, not random geopolitics. The chapter rehearses centuries of warnings ignored. The principle stands: 'the wages of sin is death,' for nations as for individuals. Yet within the judgment, the Davidic line in Judah remains, awaiting Messiah.
2Kgs.17.13: 'Yet the LORD testified against Israel, and against Judah, by all the prophets, and by all the seers, saying, Turn ye from your evil ways.' The exile cannot be charged to divine silence. The Reformed doctrine of the sufficiency and ministry of the Word emphasizes that God always sends prophets before judgment. The means of grace are present even in apostate generations; the responsibility of refusal lies wholly with the hearers. The unbroken stream of prophetic ministry vindicates God's justice in judgment.
2Kgs.17.33: 'They feared the LORD, and served their own gods.' The syncretism of the resettled Samaritans precisely captures the perennial human religious project: maintaining nominal allegiance to the true God while serving real allegiances. Reformed worship principles exclude this hybridization absolutely; the first commandment requires exclusive devotion. Jesus' encounter with the Samaritan woman (John 4) addresses the lingering effects of this very settlement, calling for worship in spirit and truth.
2Kgs.18.5: Of Hezekiah: 'He trusted in the LORD God of Israel; so that after him was none like him among all the kings of Judah, nor any that were before him.' This astonishing commendation, virtually paralleling Josiah's later praise (23:25), centers on trust (batach), not works. Reformed soteriology recognizes faith as the sine qua non of covenantal kingship. Hezekiah's reforms (vv.4, 6) flow from his trust, not vice versa. The order is significant: faith first, then obedience.
2Kgs.18.6: 'For he clave to the LORD, and departed not from following him, but kept his commandments.' The verb davaq ('clave') is the marriage-language of Genesis 2:24. Reformed covenantal theology sees the king-YHWH relationship as analogous to marital fidelity. Hezekiah's removal of the bronze serpent (v.4) shows discernment: even God-given symbols become idols when worshipped. The principle of semper reformanda applies: every generation must distinguish abiding truth from accreted superstition.
2Kgs.19.19: 'Now therefore, O LORD our God, I beseech thee, save thou us out of his hand, that all the kingdoms of the earth may know that thou art the LORD God, even thou only.' The deepest ground of prayer is God's glory among the nations. Reformed prayer theology, deriving from texts like this, makes God's name and renown the chief end. Salvation is petitioned for the sake of universal acknowledgment of YHWH's exclusive deity. This is the missiology of true prayer.
2Kgs.19.35: 'And it came to pass that night, that the angel of the LORD went out, and smote in the camp of the Assyrians an hundred fourscore and five thousand.' The single-night decimation of the Assyrian army demonstrates absolute divine sovereignty over the most powerful empire. Reformed theology celebrates this as evidence that God needs no human armies to accomplish His purposes. The promise of v.34, 'I will defend this city, to save it, for mine own sake, and for my servant David's sake,' shows the protection grounded in covenant. The Davidic city is preserved because the Davidic covenant cannot fail.
2Kgs.20.1: 'Set thine house in order; for thou shalt die, and not live.' The prophetic word to Hezekiah seems absolute, yet the king's prayer occasions a fifteen-year extension. Reformed theology distinguishes between God's secret will (decretive) and His revealed will (preceptive); prophetic warnings often invite repentant prayer. The case is parallel to Jonah's word to Nineveh. The God who decrees the end ordinarily decrees the means of prayer. Hezekiah's tears (v.5) demonstrate that prayer is no mere ritual but the soul's wrestling with God.
2Kgs.20.13: Hezekiah's foolish display of his treasures to the Babylonian envoys exposes the seed of Judah's eventual exile. Reformed sanctification recognizes that even great saints have besetting sins; pride often follows deliverance. The man who trusted YHWH against Sennacherib courts Babylon's friendship to feed his vanity. Isaiah's prediction of Babylonian captivity (v.17) follows directly. The principle: present compromise sows future judgment, even across generations.
2Kgs.21.6: Manasseh 'made his son pass through the fire, and observed times, and used enchantments, and dealt with familiar spirits and wizards.' The catalog of abominations (Deut 18:9-12 reversed) makes Manasseh the anti-Josiah. Reformed theology sees here the depth of human depravity manifested even in the Davidic line. Yet 2 Chronicles 33 records Manasseh's eventual repentance, demonstrating sovereign grace can reach the worst sinners. The contrast between the prolonged reign of the wicked and the early death of the righteous remains a mystery answered only at the consummation.
2Kgs.22.8: 'I have found the book of the law in the house of the LORD.' Hilkiah's discovery of the law during temple repairs (likely Deuteronomy or the entire Pentateuch) marks the beginning of true reformation. Reformed theology celebrates this moment as paradigmatic: every renewal of the church flows from rediscovery of the Word. The Reformation itself was such a finding. The book had been there all along; the people had departed from it. The remedy is always to return to Scripture.
2Kgs.22.11: 'And it came to pass, when the king had heard the words of the book of the law, that he rent his clothes.' Josiah's response is the proper one to Scripture: trembling. Reformed theology of the Word emphasizes that the truly converted hearer does not master the text but is mastered by it. Josiah does not relativize the demands or dismiss the threats; he weeps. This is what 'the fear of the LORD' looks like in operation. Such reverence is the beginning of all true reform.
2Kgs.22.19: Huldah's word: 'Because thine heart was tender, and thou hast humbled thyself before the LORD... I also have heard thee, saith the LORD.' Notably, the great reformation arises through a female prophet's mediation of God's word. Reformed theology of revelation recognizes God's freedom to use whom He will. The promise of personal mercy ('thou shalt be gathered into thy grave in peace,' v.20) does not remove the corporate judgment, illustrating that individual salvation can coexist with national catastrophe.
2Kgs.23.3: 'And the king stood by a pillar, and made a covenant before the LORD, to walk after the LORD, and to keep his commandments... with all their heart and all their soul.' Josiah's covenantal renewal follows the Deuteronomic pattern (Deut 29-30). Reformed covenant theology recognizes such renewals as the proper response to rediscovered truth. The king mediates the covenant for the people, prefiguring Christ, the true covenant-mediator who not only renews but secures the covenant by His own blood.
2Kgs.23.10: Josiah 'defiled Topheth, which is in the valley of the children of Hinnom, that no man might make his son or his daughter to pass through the fire to Molech.' The valley of Hinnom (Ge-Hinnom, hence Gehenna) becomes the scriptural symbol of final judgment precisely because of these abominations. Reformed theology recognizes Josiah's reform as a faint type of the final purgation when Christ shall cast every vestige of evil into the lake of fire. The reformer who removes idols anticipates the Lord who removes all sin.
2Kgs.23.15: 'Moreover the altar that was at Bethel, and the high place which Jeroboam the son of Nebat... had made, both that altar and the high place he brake down.' The fulfillment of the unnamed prophet's word three centuries earlier (1 Kgs 13:2) is the climax of providential exactness. Reformed theology stresses that no prophetic word falls to the ground unfulfilled. Josiah's name was written into the indictment three hundred years before his birth. This is the God who declares the end from the beginning (Isa 46:10).
2Kgs.23.25: 'Like unto him was there no king before him, that turned to the LORD with all his heart, and with all his soul, and with all his might, according to all the law of Moses.' The wording quotes the Shema (Deut 6:5). Yet this very Shema-keeping cannot avert the coming exile (v.26), for the people's apostasy under Manasseh remains uncancelled. Reformed theology recognizes that even the most exemplary individual cannot redeem a corporate guilt. Only Christ, the Shema-keeping Son, fulfills what no Davidide could.
2Kgs.23.29: Josiah's death at Megiddo at the hand of Pharaoh Necho is the most tragic note in the chapter. The greatest reformer dies young, in seemingly avoidable circumstances. Reformed theology must hold this with Huldah's promise that he would die 'in peace' (v.20): the peace was personal salvation, sparing him from witnessing the fall of Jerusalem. The God who keeps His promises sometimes does so in ways that confound expectations but never breach faithfulness.
2Kgs.24.3: 'Surely at the commandment of the LORD came this upon Judah, to remove them out of his sight, for the sins of Manasseh, according to all that he did.' The exile is theologically interpreted as covenantal judgment. Reformed historiography sees no events as merely political; behind Babylon stands the LORD. The cumulative weight of Manasseh's sin (despite his personal repentance) bears corporate consequences. The doctrine of corporate solidarity, as in Adam, finds painful application here, awaiting its only resolution in Christ, the second Adam.
2Kgs.24.14: Nebuchadnezzar 'carried away all Jerusalem, and all the princes, and all the mighty men of valour, even ten thousand captives... none remained, save the poorest sort of the people of the land.' The first deportation strips the kingdom of its leadership. Reformed eschatology has often noted the strange providence by which the exile preserves and purifies a remnant; from this exilic community will come Daniel, Ezekiel, Ezra, Zerubbabel, and ultimately the genealogy of Christ. God's judgments are ever pregnant with redemption.
2Kgs.25.7: 'They slew the sons of Zedekiah before his eyes, and put out the eyes of Zedekiah, and bound him with fetters of brass, and carried him to Babylon.' The horror of this scene fulfills both Jeremiah's prophecy (Jer 32:4) and Ezekiel's seemingly contradictory word (Ezek 12:13, that he would not see Babylon, though carried there). Both are precisely fulfilled. Reformed theology of prophecy delights in such intricate, exact fulfillment. The blinded king symbolizes the spiritual condition of unfaithful Davidic rule, awaiting the Servant who would 'open the eyes of the blind' (Isa 42:7).
2Kgs.25.9: 'And he burnt the house of the LORD, and the king's house, and all the houses of Jerusalem.' The temple, where the Shekinah had descended, where Solomon had prayed for forgiveness, where Hezekiah had spread the Assyrian letter, is reduced to ashes. Reformed theology sees here the typological lesson: external religious structures, however God-given, are not the ultimate dwelling place of God. The greater temple, the body of Christ (John 2:19-21), stands forever. The destruction of the first temple prepares for the inauguration of the eternal one.
2Kgs.25.21: 'So Judah was carried away out of their land.' The flat narrative tone bears unbearable weight. The exodus is reversed; the land of promise is emptied of its covenant people. Reformed covenant theology recognizes that the Sinai covenant's curses have fallen in full (Lev 26; Deut 28). Yet the Davidic covenant, though wounded, is not destroyed. Within the apparent end lies the seed of restoration, for the Lord cannot deny His unconditional promise to David.
2Kgs.25.27: 'In the seven and thirtieth year of the captivity of Jehoiachin king of Judah... did Evilmerodach king of Babylon... lift up the head of Jehoiachin king of Judah out of prison.' The book closes not with restoration to the land but with a Davidide raised from prison and seated at a foreign king's table. Reformed theology recognizes this as a fragile but real sign of hope: the Davidic line is preserved in exile. The lamp of David flickers in Babylon, awaiting Cyrus, awaiting Zerubbabel, ultimately awaiting the Son of David who Himself, after a captivity in death, would be raised and exalted to the throne. The closing verses of Kings are not despair but the inscrutable patience of covenant faithfulness, and the open door to the gospel.
2Pet.1.1: 'Simeon Peter, a servant and apostle of Jesus Christ, to those who have obtained a faith of equal standing (isotimon pistin) with ours by the righteousness of our God and Savior Jesus Christ.' The Semitic 'Simeon' authenticates the Petrine voice. Isotimon is unique here in the NT—'equally precious' or 'equally honored.' Whether the second-generation believers are equated with the apostles, or Gentile believers with Jewish ones, the leveling effect is profound. Reformed soteriology underscores that faith's value lies in its object, not its possessor. The grammatical construction theou hemon kai soteros Iesou Christou by Granville Sharp's rule identifies Jesus directly as God and Savior.
2Pet.1.4: 'By which he has granted to us his precious and very great promises, so that through them you may become partakers of the divine nature (theias koinonoi physeos), having escaped the corruption that is in the world because of sinful desire.' This verse anchors the patristic and Reformed-adjacent doctrine of theosis or union with God. The Reformed reading carefully distinguishes: believers share in divine moral attributes (holiness, righteousness) by participation, never in divine essence. We become partakers, not portions, of his nature. Calvin reads this as participation in the Spirit's work, conforming us to Christ. Escape from cosmic corruption is decisive (apophygontes, aorist).
2Pet.1.10: 'Therefore, brothers, be all the more diligent to confirm your calling and election (bebaian hymon ten klesin kai eklogen poieisthai). For if you practice these qualities you will never fall.' Election is objectively certain in God's decree but subjectively confirmed by perseverance in virtue. Reformed assurance theology, especially in the Puritan tradition, is rooted here: practical syllogism—the fruits of regeneration confirm the root of election. We do not make our election certain to God (it already is) but to ourselves. The promise 'you will never fall (ou me ptaisete pote)' is conditional in form but securing in function: the diligence proves the calling, and the calling is irrevocable.
2Pet.1.11: 'For in this way there will be richly provided (epichoregethesetai) for you an entrance into the eternal kingdom of our Lord and Savior Jesus Christ.' The same verb epichoregeo from v. 5 returns—what we lavishly supplied to faith God lavishly supplies in our entrance to glory. The eschatological harvest matches the agricultural sowing. Reformed eschatology preserves the symmetry: not works-merit, but works-evidence and works-shaped reception. The 'eternal kingdom' suggests an everlasting reign, not merely an episode. Christ as 'Lord and Savior' (kyriou kai soteros) here returns the title from v. 1 with christological emphasis.
2Pet.1.16: 'For we did not follow cleverly devised myths (sesophismenois mythois) when we made known to you the power and coming (parousian) of our Lord Jesus Christ, but we were eyewitnesses of his majesty.' Peter contrasts apostolic testimony with mythological invention. Sesophismenois implies sophisticated cunning—the sophistry of fabricated tales. Eyewitness (epoptai) is technical religious language for those admitted to the highest mystery rites. Reformed apologetics has emphasized this verse as foundational: the gospel rests on historical, sense-perceived events, not allegorical inventions. Modern demythologization is here preempted; Peter's epistemology is empirical and testimonial.
2Pet.1.20: 'Knowing this first of all, that no prophecy of Scripture comes from someone's own interpretation (idias epilyseos).' The phrase has been read as forbidding private interpretation by readers, but the context (v. 21) indicates the origin of prophecy, not its reception. The prophets did not generate their messages from their own initiative or insight. Reformed bibliology reads this as concerning the inspiration's source, not the laity's right to read. Idia epilysis is the prophet's own unloosing—Peter denies that prophets autonomously produced their oracles.
2Pet.1.21: 'For no prophecy was ever produced by the will of man, but men spoke from God as they were carried along (pheromenoi) by the Holy Spirit.' The verb phero is used of a ship borne by wind (Acts 27:15). The prophets were not passive automata but Spirit-driven authors. Reformed bibliology, alongside 2 Tim 3:16's theopneustos, anchors the doctrine of plenary, verbal, organic inspiration here. Human authorial particularity (vocabulary, style, situation) is preserved while the Spirit superintends so that what is written is, without remainder, God's word. The dual agency is a mystery analogous to christological two-natures.
2Pet.2.4: 'For if God did not spare angels when they sinned, but cast them into hell (tartarosas) and committed them to chains of gloomy darkness to be kept until the judgment.' Tartarosas is unique here in the NT—Tartarus, the deepest pit in Hellenistic cosmology, is appropriated for fallen angels. Reformed angelology accepts both the original angelic fall and a custodial confinement awaiting final judgment. The argument is a fortiori: if God spared not angels, surely he will not spare false teachers. Divine impartiality in judgment is the doctrinal weight.
2Pet.2.14: 'They have eyes full of adultery, insatiable for sin (akatapaustous hamartias). They entice (deleazontes) unsteady souls.' The picture of false teachers is unsparing—their desire is unfillable. Deleazo is the angler's verb, baiting a hook. Reformed pastoral diagnosis treats heresy as predatory, not merely intellectual. The unsteady (asteriktous) are particular targets; this is why catechesis and establishment in doctrine matter as anti-predator armor. The phrase 'trained in greed' (gegymnasmenen pleonexias echontes—lit. having a heart trained in covetousness) suggests sustained corrupt formation.
2Pet.2.19: 'They promise them freedom, but they themselves are slaves of corruption (douloi tes phthoras). For whatever overcomes a person, to that he is enslaved.' False teachers offer license under the banner of liberty—a perennial heresy. The principle is universal: dominion belongs to whatever masters us. Reformed anthropology takes seriously the bondage of the will to whatever the heart loves. True freedom is service to Christ, not autonomy. Augustine's non posse non peccare (the fallen will cannot but sin) and the gospel's libertas Christiana are both anchored in such Pauline-Petrine logic.
