Reformed theology values such institutional memory; the church is a remembering community, gathering weekly to recall the greater deliverance accomplished by the greater Esther, Christ Jesus. The Lord's Supper is our Purim, our perpetual remembrance of the day when the decree of death was overthrown. Just as Purim was not a Mosaic feast but a covenantally appropriate response to redemption, so the church's calendar of remembrance celebrates the unfolding of God's redemptive acts in space and time, refusing to forget what grace has wrought.
Esth.10.3: The book closes with Mordecai exalted as second to the king, seeking the welfare of his people and speaking peace to all his kindred. He has become a type of Christ enthroned at the right hand of God, advocating for His people, securing their welfare, and bringing peace. Reformed Christology reads this final scene as a prophetic glimpse of the ascended Lord, who, having defeated the great Haman, our adversary the devil, now reigns to bless His brethren. The book that began with the splendor of a pagan king ends with the glory of a Jewish exile, demonstrating that the kingdom of God advances even within the kingdoms of this world. The hidden God, never named, is everywhere praised, for the entire narrative is a doxology to His unfailing covenant love.
Esth.1.22: The decree that every man should be master in his own house, dispatched in every province's script and language, betrays the brittle insecurity beneath imperial bravado. A king who must legislate his wife's submission has already lost it. Yet for the Reformed reader, this absurd edict is providentially preparatory: the Persian postal system, the multilingual reach, the legal apparatus, all are being demonstrated as ready tools for the deliverance to come. God exercises His sovereignty not by suspending human institutions but by bending them to His covenant purposes. The very machinery of pagan empire will soon become the instrument of Israel's salvation.
Esth.2.5: The deliberate notice of Mordecai's lineage (son of Jair, son of Shimei, son of Kish, a Benjamite) is no idle genealogy. Kish was Saul's father, marking Mordecai as kin to Israel's first king. The Reformed reader sees the covenantal symmetry: a son of Kish will undo what Saul left undone with the Amalekite Agag. Genealogy in Scripture is theology in disguise; it traces the unbroken line of covenant promise through generations of exile. Even in Persia, even nameless before the world, the LORD has not forgotten His chosen tribes. The hidden God preserves hidden lineages for unhidden purposes.
Esth.3.6: Haman's escalation from personal grievance to genocidal intent reveals the demonic logic of antisemitism: the existence of God's people is itself an offense to those who hate God. Reformed theology recognizes the spiritual warfare here. To destroy the Jews would be to falsify God's covenant promises, an attack not merely on a people but on the faithfulness of the LORD Himself. Every persecution of the church throughout history follows this same pattern, an assault on the seed of promise. Yet every such assault is doomed, for the gates of hell shall not prevail against the people God has covenanted to preserve.
Esth.4.3: Throughout every province, the Jews respond to the decree with fasting, weeping, and lamentation in sackcloth and ashes. This corporate response, while not naming God, is unmistakably directed toward Him. Reformed worship recognizes lament as a primary mode of covenant prayer; the people who cannot save themselves cry out to the One who can. The fasting is not mere mourning but a confession of dependence, a relinquishment of self-sufficiency. This communal grief, simultaneous across the empire, weaves a tapestry of intercession that the silent God of the text hears clearly. Faith expresses itself even when words fail.
Esth.4.13: Mordecai's warning to Esther, that she will not escape simply by remaining silent in the palace, dismantles every illusion of safe neutrality. The Reformed doctrine of vocation insists that God places His servants in particular stations precisely so they may serve in costly faithfulness. Privilege is never given merely for personal preservation; it is stewardship for the kingdom. Mordecai's words echo through every age to every Christian tempted to hide behind comfort while God's people suffer. To refuse the call of providence is to forfeit one's part in the deliverance, but it cannot stop the deliverance, for the LORD will raise up help from another quarter.
Esth.5.4: Esther's invitation to the banquet, rather than an immediate accusation, displays remarkable wisdom. She does not blurt out her petition but uses the providential opportunity to disarm Haman through hospitality and to ripen the king's favor through patience. Reformed prudence sees this as faith expressing itself in wise tactics; courage need not be reckless. The Spirit who emboldens also gives discernment for timing. Esther's two-banquet strategy, prolonging suspense, draws Haman further into the trap of his own pride. Providence often works through delay, ripening situations for the precise moment when truth can land with maximum effect.
Esth.6.6: Haman's assumption, 'whom would the king delight to honor more than me?' is a textbook study in pride preceding destruction. Self-deception always overestimates one's standing in the eyes of others. The Reformed doctrine of total depravity teaches that the unregenerate heart inherently distorts reality around the self. Haman's lavish honors, recommended for his own glorification, become his public humiliation when redirected to Mordecai. God laughs at the proud (Psalm 2:4), and here we hear that divine laughter in the very mechanism of Haman's downfall. The honors he designed for himself crown his enemy.
Esth.7.7: The king storms into the garden in fury, leaving Haman to plead for his life from Esther. The narrative tension is exquisite: a Persian queen sits in judgment upon the empire's second man, and his fate is sealed by his own desperate gesture. Reformed providence delights in such moments where human plotting unravels with no apparent intervention from heaven, yet every detail bears the fingerprints of God. The king who could not sleep, the queen who timed her petition perfectly, the courtier who falls precisely when accused, all combine in a sequence too intricate for chance. The hidden God is the master playwright.
Esth.8.8: Mordecai is empowered to write a counter-decree in the king's name, sealed with the royal signet. The first decree could not be revoked, but it could be answered. This legal mechanism is theologically rich: the law of death is not abolished but overcome by a greater law of life. Reformed soteriology hears here a faint echo of the gospel logic. The sentence of death pronounced in Eden could not be revoked, for God's word does not return void. Yet a greater Word answers it: the Son enters under the curse and writes, in His own blood, a counter-decree of salvation that empowers the condemned to live.
Esth.8.16: 'The Jews had light and gladness and joy and honor.' This fourfold description of post-deliverance experience captures the comprehensive nature of redemption. Light replaces the darkness of fear; gladness replaces mourning; joy replaces lamentation; honor replaces shame. Reformed theology insists that salvation is never merely the removal of threat but the bestowal of positive blessing. The Jews are not just spared, they are exalted. So too in Christ, the believer is not merely acquitted but adopted, not merely forgiven but glorified. The reversal is total because the Redeemer is total in His sufficiency and grace.
Esth.9.16: Throughout the provinces, the Jews defended their lives, gained relief from their enemies, and slew seventy-five thousand who hated them, but did not lay hands on the plunder. The repeated insistence on restraint from plunder is striking. Reformed ethics distinguishes between just defense and greedy aggression. The Jews fight to live, not to enrich themselves. This holy restraint marks them as a people set apart, contrasting sharply with Saul's failure regarding Amalekite spoil (1 Samuel 15). The covenant community demonstrates that vindication need not corrupt the vindicated; God's people may receive deliverance without losing their integrity.
Esth.9.20: Mordecai writes down these events and sends letters to all the Jews, establishing the feast as a perpetual ordinance. The act of writing canonizes memory, embedding the deliverance in the textual life of the covenant community. Reformed bibliology sees here a principle: God's mighty acts must be recorded, read, and remembered, lest forgetfulness erode faith. Just as the church preserves the apostolic Scriptures, so the Jewish community preserves Esther. The written word becomes the vehicle of communal identity across generations. What providence accomplishes once must be retold a thousand times until faith is fully formed in every successor.
Esth.10.1: The closing notice that Ahasuerus laid tribute on the land and the islands seems an anticlimactic detail, yet it reasserts the broad sweep of imperial reality within which God's deliverance occurred. The Persian Empire continues; world history rolls on; taxes are levied. Reformed providence teaches that God's redemptive acts unfold within, not apart from, the ordinary structures of human governance. The deliverance of the Jews did not topple Persia; it preserved God's people within Persia until the appointed time of return. Salvation in this age is salvation for pilgrim life within a continuing fallen world.
Esth.10.2: The reference to the Book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Media and Persia for further details about Mordecai's greatness reminds us that the inspired narrative selects and shapes its material toward theological purpose. Other records exist; this one is canonical. Reformed bibliology affirms that Scripture, while historically grounded, is not exhaustive history but redemptive history. The Holy Spirit superintends the selection of what must be remembered for the people of God. Mordecai's full administrative career is mentioned but not detailed, because the canonical interest lies elsewhere: in the providential preservation of the covenant people through the hidden hand of God.
Esth.2.10: Mordecai's instruction that Esther should not reveal her people or kindred reflects prudent realism about diaspora life under foreign rulers. Yet there is providential design even in this concealment, for her hidden Jewish identity becomes the very element that makes her later disclosure devastating to Haman. Reformed theology recognizes that wisdom often requires reticence; not every truth must be declared at every moment. The hidden queen mirrors the hidden God: both are most effective precisely because their identity remains veiled until the appointed revelation. Concealment is not deception when it serves the deeper unfolding of covenant purpose.
Esth.3.15: While the king and Haman sat down to drink, the city of Susa was thrown into confusion. The contrast is searing: the architects of genocide toast each other while the city reels at the cruelty. Reformed theology notes the moral disorder embedded in unjust power; even pagan Susa senses something is wrong. The conscience implanted by the Creator stirs uneasily under wickedness, even when wickedness is legal. The drunken complacency of Haman foreshadows the drunken self-confidence of every persecutor of the church, who imagines his triumph secure while heaven prepares his overthrow.
Esth.6.12: After being publicly honored by Mordecai, Haman hurries home with his head covered in mourning. The reversal of postures is striking: the man who could not bear Mordecai's refusal to bow now walks beside Mordecai's horse and returns home in shame. Reformed providence delights in such precise inversions, where each element of the proud man's plot is replayed against him. The covered head, traditionally a sign of grief and disgrace, becomes the public marker of Haman's collapse. Pride goes before destruction, and the haughty spirit before a fall, exactly as Proverbs 16:18 declares.
Esth.9.17: The thirteenth day was a day of slaughter, and on the fourteenth they rested and made it a day of feasting and gladness. The pattern of work followed by rest, conflict followed by celebration, mirrors the biblical rhythm from creation onward. Reformed theology sees here the eschatological shape of redemption: the labor of warfare gives way to sabbath rest. Just as the people of God will one day rest from all their enemies, so Purim anticipates the final feast of the redeemed. Every Christian gathering on the Lord's Day participates in this same rhythm: the warfare of the week dissolved into the joy of resurrection celebration.
Exod.1.7: The fruitfulness of Israel in Egypt is no accident; it is the visible outworking of the covenant promise to Abraham (Gen 12:2; 17:6). The Hebrew piles up verbs of multiplication, parah and rabah, the same language used at creation (Gen 1:28) and after the flood (Gen 9:1). Reformed exegesis sees here the sovereign God advancing redemptive history despite hostile circumstances. Even before the deliverer is born, Yahweh is making good His word. The land is filled, prefiguring the eschatological filling of the earth with the seed of the woman, ultimately fulfilled in the multiplication of the church (Acts 6:7), the new Israel gathered in Christ.
Exod.1.8: The new king who knew not Joseph marks a decisive narrative pivot from grace to oppression. Knowledge here is covenantal and relational; ignorance of Joseph is ignorance of the God who used Joseph to save Egypt. This is the pattern of every age: the world forgets the kindness of God's people and turns to persecution. Calvin notes the ingratitude of princes who forget benefits received. Pharaoh's amnesia exposes the autonomy of fallen rulers who recognize no debt to God's providence. The verse foreshadows every Herod, every Caesar, every tyrant who will rise against the Lord and His Anointed (Ps 2:2).
Exod.1.14: Their lives were made bitter, mar in Hebrew, the same root that will reappear in the bitter herbs (merorim) of the Passover meal (Exod 12:8). The bondage is total: bricks, mortar, all manner of service in the field, all with rigor (perek, harshness). Reformed theology sees Egypt as a type of sin's tyranny over the unregenerate; bondage is not merely external but existential. The groaning of Israel under the lash anticipates Romans 8:22, the whole creation groaning until redemption. Only a greater Exodus accomplished by a greater Moses can break such bondage.
Exod.1.17: But the midwives feared God. The fear of God, yirat elohim, is the root of true ethics in Reformed thought (Prov 1:7). Shiphrah and Puah resist Pharaoh's genocidal command not by arms but by quiet covenantal faithfulness. Their civil disobedience is sanctioned because the higher law of God forbids murder. This is the foundational principle later articulated by Peter: we must obey God rather than men (Acts 5:29). God blesses them with houses, an inversion: those who preserve life are themselves built up. The episode shows that God advances His purposes through humble, faithful image-bearers, often women whom the world overlooks.
Exod.1.22: Pharaoh's edict to drown every Hebrew son in the Nile is satanic in its scope, an attempt to extinguish the seed of promise. Yet the Nile, weaponized for death, will become the cradle of the deliverer (2:3) and ultimately the first plague's site of judgment (7:20). The dragon-pharaoh foreshadows Herod's slaughter of the innocents (Matt 2:16), both attempting to kill the redeemer in his infancy, both thwarted by sovereign providence. The serpent's seed wars against the woman's seed, but God writes salvation through the very means of attempted destruction.
Exod.2.10: She named him Moshe, saying, I drew him out of the water. The Hebrew mashah (to draw out) plays on his name, foreshadowing his role as the one who draws Israel out of Egypt through the waters of the Sea. Moses is preserved by an Egyptian princess, raised in Pharaoh's own house, the very household that sought his death. Reformed theology marvels at the irony of providence: the deliverer is trained in the wisdom and weakness of Egypt before God uses him. He is a type of Christ, who likewise comes out of Egypt (Matt 2:15; Hos 11:1) and draws His people through the waters of baptism.
Exod.2.23: The king of Egypt died; and the children of Israel sighed by reason of the bondage. Four verbs pile up: they sighed, cried, their cry came up, God heard. The covenant God is not deaf to the groaning of His people. The verb shama (heard) anticipates the great covenantal Shema. Their cry is not yet repentance but raw suffering, and even this God hears. Reformed theology emphasizes that prayer arises from need, and God is moved not by merit but by His own faithfulness to covenant. The cry of the oppressed reaches the throne of heaven.
Exod.2.24: God remembered His covenant with Abraham, with Isaac, and with Jacob. Divine remembrance (zakar) is never mere recollection but covenantal action. God does not forget; the verb signals the moment when promise begins to issue in deliverance. The exodus is not improvised mercy but the keeping of an ancient oath (Gen 15:13-14). This is the bedrock of Reformed soteriology: salvation flows from the eternal covenant, not from changing circumstances. As God remembered the patriarchs, so in Christ He remembers His elect from before the foundation of the world (Eph 1:4).
Exod.3.2: The angel of the LORD appeared in a flame of fire from the midst of a bush. The bush burned but was not consumed, picturing the holiness of God dwelling among His people without destroying them. The malak Yahweh here is identified with God Himself in the next verse, a Christophany in the judgment of Reformed exegetes from Calvin to Owen. The unconsumed bush is Israel itself: oppressed in the furnace of Egypt yet not annihilated, because Yahweh dwells in her midst. It also foreshadows the incarnation, the divine fire dwelling in the bush of human flesh without destroying it.
Exod.3.5: Put off thy shoes from off thy feet, for the place whereon thou standest is holy ground (qodesh). The first occurrence of holiness in Exodus establishes the central theme: the gulf between the holy God and sinful man, a gulf which only God can bridge. Removing sandals signals creaturely lowliness and ceremonial preparation. Calvin observes that even the ground becomes holy by association with God's presence. Holiness is not in the bush or soil but in the presence of the Holy One. Sinners cannot stand on holy ground without mediation, and Moses here is granted access by sheer grace.
Exod.3.14: I AM THAT I AM, ehyeh asher ehyeh. The covenantal name Yahweh is rooted in pure being, self-existence, aseity. God is not defined by anything outside Himself; He simply IS. This is the theological foundation for divine simplicity, immutability, and independence. Reformed theology has long anchored the doctrine of God in this self-revelation. Christ takes this name to Himself in the seven I AM sayings of John, and supremely in John 8:58: before Abraham was, I AM. The God who delivered Israel is the same I AM who became flesh to deliver His people from sin.
Exod.3.15: This is my name forever, my memorial unto all generations. Yahweh binds His name to the patriarchs: the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob. The name is given for remembrance (zikkaron), to be invoked in worship across the ages. Reformed worship is therefore covenantal and historically rooted, never abstract. The name distinguishes the true God from the gods of Egypt and all idols. When Christ teaches us to pray Hallowed be thy name, He stands in this tradition: the revealed name of the covenant God, now climactically revealed in the name above every name (Phil 2:9).
Exod.4.11: Who hath made man's mouth? have not I the LORD? The doctrine of God's exhaustive sovereignty over creation, including disability, is here unflinchingly stated. He makes the dumb, the deaf, the seeing, the blind. This text undergirds the Reformed confession that nothing falls outside God's decree (WCF 3.1). Moses' weakness is no obstacle; rather, it is the canvas for divine power. The same God who shaped Moses' mouth will give it words. This anticipates Paul's confession that God's strength is made perfect in weakness (2 Cor 12:9). Disability and limitation become occasions for God's glory.
Exod.4.22: Israel is my son, even my firstborn. This is the first time Israel is called God's son in the Pentateuch, establishing a covenantal sonship that grounds the entire exodus narrative. The firstborn (bekor) holds the privileged inheritance. Pharaoh's refusal to release God's firstborn will cost him his own. Hosea 11:1, Out of Egypt have I called my son, applies this both to Israel and ultimately to Christ (Matt 2:15). Jesus is the true and faithful Son who relives Israel's history without failure. Believers, united to Him, are adopted into this filial status (Rom 8:15).
Exod.4.24: The LORD met him, and sought to kill him. This dark and abrupt episode reveals that even the chosen deliverer is under judgment for covenant unfaithfulness, the uncircumcised son. No one approaches the holy God on the basis of office or calling; the sign of the covenant must be in place. Zipporah's swift action with the flint knife and her cryptic bridegroom of blood points forward to the bloody covenant by which God's people are spared. Reformed exegetes note that Moses cannot mediate redemption until his own house is in covenantal order. The shadow of substitutionary blood already falls across the narrative.
Exod.5.2: Who is the LORD, that I should obey his voice? Pharaoh's question is the question of every unregenerate heart. The verb yada (know) is covenantal: Pharaoh confesses ignorance of Yahweh and refuses His authority. The plagues that follow are designed precisely to answer this question: that thou mayest know that I am the LORD (7:5, 17; 8:22). Reformed theology sees here the noetic effect of sin: the natural man cannot acknowledge God as God. Only by sovereign disclosure, accomplished through judgment or grace, does the heart bow. Pharaoh will be made to know Yahweh, but to his destruction.
Exod.6.6: I will bring you out, I will rid you, I will redeem you, I will take you. The four I wills of redemption form a covenantal grammar. Redemption (gaal) carries the kinsman-redeemer connotation: Yahweh acts as next of kin to ransom His enslaved people. Stretched-out arm and great judgments indicate redemption is accomplished through power and judgment, not negotiation. These four verbs are commemorated in the four cups of the Passover Seder. The pattern climaxes in the gospel: Christ brings out, rids, redeems, and takes His people unto Himself, bearing the judgment that secures the deliverance.
Exod.6.7: I will take you to me for a people, and I will be to you a God. This is the covenant formula in its purest form, repeated throughout Scripture (Lev 26:12; Jer 31:33; Rev 21:3). The exodus is not merely from bondage but to fellowship. Reformed covenant theology sees the goal of redemption not as deliverance per se but as communion with God. Pharaoh's house is exchanged for Yahweh's house. The formula will reach its consummation in the New Jerusalem when God dwells with His people forever. Salvation is fundamentally relational: He shall be their God, and they shall be His people.
Exod.7.3: I will harden Pharaoh's heart. The hiphil of qashah (to harden) appears here for the first time on the divine side. Throughout the plague cycle, the text alternates: sometimes God hardens, sometimes Pharaoh hardens himself. Reformed theology holds both in tension without contradiction. Pharaoh's hardening is judicial, a giving over (Rom 1:24) to the wickedness he has chosen. God is sovereign over even the rebellion of His enemies, and uses it to multiply His signs and reveal His glory (Rom 9:17). This is the doctrine of reprobation in narrative form, executed in righteousness, never in caprice.
Exod.8.19: This is the finger of God. The Egyptian magicians, having replicated water-to-blood and frogs by demonic deception, fail at the gnats (kinnim) and confess defeat. The finger of God is the same phrase used of the tablets of the law (31:18) and invoked by Christ when casting out demons (Luke 11:20). The plagues are a cosmic war between the God of Israel and the gods of Egypt (Num 33:4). Reformed theology sees them as judicial acts that simultaneously vindicate God's people, expose idolatry as nothing, and call the nations to know Yahweh.
Exod.9.16: For this cause have I raised thee up, for to shew in thee my power. Paul cites this verse in Romans 9:17 to ground the doctrine of God's sovereign election and reprobation. Pharaoh exists at this moment, on this throne, for this purpose: to be the instrument by which Yahweh's glory is published. God owes him no salvation, only justice. The God who can make Pharaoh a vessel of wrath can also make Israel a vessel of mercy (Rom 9:23). Reformed theology denies any injustice in God's free decree; the clay has no claim against the potter.
Exod.10.21: Darkness which may be felt. The ninth plague strikes at the supreme deity of Egypt, Ra the sun god. For three days darkness covers Egypt, but the children of Israel had light in their dwellings (10:23). This anticipates the eschatological separation between light and darkness, between the city of God and the city of man. Christ's three hours of darkness on the cross (Matt 27:45) reverse the pattern: now the true Israelite, the obedient Son, dwells in darkness so that we might dwell in light. The plague is also a foretaste of outer darkness, the fate of those who reject the light.
Exod.12.3: In the tenth day of this month they shall take to them every man a lamb. The Passover (pesach) lamb is selected and inspected for four days (12:6), prefiguring Christ's entry into Jerusalem on the tenth of Nisan and His public scrutiny by the religious leaders before His sacrifice on the fourteenth. The lamb is for an house, signaling household covenantal solidarity. Reformed paedobaptists see here the principle that God deals with families in covenant. The blood applied secures not a single individual but the whole house, anticipating the Acts pattern: thou shalt be saved, and thy house (Acts 16:31).
Exod.12.5: Your lamb shall be without blemish, a male of the first year. Tamim, without blemish, is the language repeated throughout Leviticus for acceptable sacrifice. Peter explicitly applies this to Christ, a lamb without blemish and without spot (1 Pet 1:19). The unblemished requirement points beyond mere ritual cleanness to the moral perfection of the true Lamb. No imperfect sacrifice can substitute for sinners. Christ alone, as the spotless Lamb of God (John 1:29), satisfies the type. Reformed theology insists on the necessity of His active obedience: only a perfectly righteous substitute can bear the sin of His people.
Exod.12.7: They shall take of the blood, and strike it on the two side posts and on the upper door post. The blood is applied where it can be seen, marking the house as under judgment already executed in the lamb. Reformed theology has long noted the cruciform shape implied: side posts and lintel forming a shape over which the angel of death must pass. The blood is not merely shed but applied; the parallel to the gospel is exact. Christ's blood shed on Calvary must be applied to the heart by faith. Without application there is no protection; with application, the destroyer must pass over.
Exod.12.13: When I see the blood, I will pass over you. The verb pasach gives the feast its name. Some Reformed exegetes (Owen, Ainsworth) argue the verb means not merely to skip but to hover protectively over. Either way, the blood is the basis: when I see the blood. God's eye is fixed on the substitute, not on the worthiness of those inside. This is propitiation in seed form: the wrath that would justly fall on the firstborn is averted by the blood of another. The same wrath is exhausted at the cross, where God set forth Christ as a propitiation through faith in his blood (Rom 3:25).
Exod.12.14: This day shall be unto you for a memorial; and ye shall keep it a feast. The Passover is instituted as a perpetual ordinance, marking Israel's identity in time. Reformed theology recognizes the role of memorial signs (zikkaron) in covenant life: they preach the gospel through ritual rehearsal. Christ transforms this very meal into the Lord's Supper (Luke 22:19), saying, This do in remembrance of me. The new memorial does not abolish the old but fulfills it. Every Lord's Supper is a Christian Passover, proclaiming the Lord's death till he come (1 Cor 11:26).
Exod.12.23: The LORD will pass through to smite the Egyptians; and when he seeth the blood... he will not suffer the destroyer to come in unto your houses. Two agents are distinguished: Yahweh and the destroyer (mashchit). Reformed exegetes have variously identified the destroyer as an angel under God's command. The blood-marked door is an inviolable boundary. The image is sober: the same night is salvation for some and judgment for others. The line is drawn by blood. So at the great judgment, only those covered by Christ's blood will be passed over; the rest meet the destroyer.
Exod.12.46: Neither shall ye break a bone thereof. The lamb is to be eaten roasted whole; no bone broken. John 19:36 applies this directly to Christ: For these things were done, that the scripture should be fulfilled, A bone of him shall not be broken. The unbroken bones of the Passover lamb are providentially preserved in the unbroken bones of Christ on the cross. This typological precision is, for Reformed exegesis, evidence of the unity of redemptive history and the sovereign authorship of Scripture. Every detail of the type points with precision to the antitype.
Exod.13.2: Sanctify unto me all the firstborn... it is mine. The death of Egypt's firstborn purchases the firstborn of Israel; therefore they belong to Yahweh. The principle of redemption-by-substitution is now extended into the ordinary life of Israel through the redemption of the firstborn. This anticipates the doctrine that all believers, having been bought with a price, are not their own (1 Cor 6:19-20). Christ Himself is the firstborn of all creation (Col 1:15) and the firstborn from the dead (Col 1:18); in Him the firstborn principle is consummated. The church is the assembly of the firstborn (Heb 12:23).
Exod.13.21: The LORD went before them by day in a pillar of a cloud, to lead them the way; and by night in a pillar of fire. The visible glory of God (the Shekinah) accompanies His pilgrim people. The pillar is at once protection, guidance, and presence. Reformed theology sees in the cloud a foreshadowing of the indwelling Spirit who guides the church. Paul applies the imagery: our fathers were under the cloud (1 Cor 10:1), making it a baptism into Moses, a type of Christian baptism. The cloud rests over the tabernacle (40:38), and ultimately the glory dwells in Christ (John 1:14, eskenosen, tabernacled).
Exod.14.13: Stand still, and see the salvation of the LORD. The imperative captures the heart of biblical salvation: it is the LORD's, not ours. Yeshuat Yahweh, the salvation of Yahweh, is precisely what the name Jesus (Yeshua) means. At the Red Sea, Israel can do nothing; Yahweh fights, Israel sees. This is monergism in narrative form. Reformed soteriology denies any contribution of the creature to redemption; the work is wholly God's. The same God who fought for Israel at the Sea fights for His elect at the cross, where Christ alone bears the battle and wins the victory.
Exod.14.21: The LORD caused the sea to go back by a strong east wind. God uses ordinary providence (the wind) to accomplish extraordinary deliverance. Reformed theology embraces both miracle and means; God is not less sovereign when He uses secondary causes. The waters part, becoming a wall on the right hand and on the left (14:22). The same waters that bear Israel through to safety drown Egypt: the means of salvation for the elect is the means of judgment for the reprobate. Paul reads the Sea as baptism (1 Cor 10:2): Christ is the new Moses leading the church through judgment waters into resurrection life.
Exod.14.30: Thus the LORD saved Israel that day out of the hand of the Egyptians. Yasha, to save, is the great verb of redemption. Notice the sequence: Israel saw the dead Egyptians upon the sea shore and feared the LORD and believed (14:31). Sight produces faith here, and faith fixes on the saving work of God. The judgment of the enemy and the salvation of the people are one event. So at the cross, Christ judges the principalities and powers (Col 2:15) while saving His own. The Red Sea pictures the great divide between the world and the church, judgment and grace.
Exod.15.2: The LORD is my strength and song, and he is become my salvation. The Song of the Sea is the first recorded hymn of redemption in Scripture. Notice the trinity of attributes: strength, song, salvation. The verse is later quoted by Isaiah (12:2) and the psalmist (118:14), each time at moments of redemption. Reformed worship is fundamentally redemptive song, the church singing what God has done. The phrase he is become my salvation anticipates the personal naming of the Savior: Yeshua. In Christ, God Himself has become our salvation. Worship arises naturally on the far shore of redemption.
Exod.15.13: Thou in thy mercy hast led forth the people which thou hast redeemed. Mercy here is chesed, covenant loyalty, steadfast love. Redemption is rooted not in Israel's worth but in God's freely binding love. The verb led (nahah) anticipates the shepherding language of Psalm 23. Reformed theology insists redemption flows from God's eternal love, not from human merit or foreseen faith. He guides those He has redeemed; redemption and providence are unified in covenant grace. The same chesed that delivered Israel is the chesed sealed in Christ's blood, never to be removed from His people (Rom 8:38-39).
Exod.16.4: I will rain bread from heaven for you. Manna (man hu, what is it?) is the daily provision of the wilderness, gathered each morning, doubled on the sixth day, none on the seventh. The lesson is dependence: man doth not live by bread only, but by every word that proceedeth out of the mouth of the LORD (Deut 8:3). Christ identifies Himself as the true bread from heaven (John 6:32-35), the antitype of manna. The fathers ate manna and died; the bread which Christ gives is His flesh, which gives eternal life. Daily dependence on the Word is the pattern of Christian life.
Exod.17.6: I will stand before thee there upon the rock in Horeb; and thou shalt smite the rock, and there shall come water out of it. Yahweh stands upon the rock and is, in a sense, smitten in it. Paul's astonishing identification: that Rock was Christ (1 Cor 10:4). The smiting of the rock by Moses' rod, the same rod that judged Egypt, prefigures the smiting of Christ under the rod of God's wrath. From the smitten Rock flows living water for thirsty pilgrims. Christ smitten once (Heb 9:28) provides Spirit-water for all who believe (John 7:37-39).
Exod.19.6: Ye shall be unto me a kingdom of priests, and an holy nation. The priestly kingdom envisioned at Sinai becomes the church in Christ (1 Pet 2:9; Rev 1:6). Every member of God's people is set apart, qadosh, holy, a mediating presence to the nations. Reformed theology resists clericalism by recovering this priesthood of all believers, while affirming the special office of pastor. Israel was to be a missional, priestly nation; she largely failed; Christ is the true priest-king, and in Him the church now fulfills the calling. We offer up spiritual sacrifices and proclaim His excellencies.
Exod.19.18: Mount Sinai was altogether on a smoke, because the LORD descended upon it in fire. The theophany combines fire, smoke, earthquake, trumpet. Sinai is terror, a holiness that cannot be approached. Hebrews 12:18-21 contrasts Sinai with Zion: ye are not come unto the mount that might be touched. The terror of Sinai is real and abiding for those outside Christ; for the believer, the same God is met in the new and living way through the blood of the better covenant. The fire that warns becomes the fire that sanctifies; the trumpet that warns becomes the trumpet of resurrection.
Exod.20.2: I am the LORD thy God, which have brought thee out of the land of Egypt. The Decalogue is grounded not on creaturely obligation alone but on covenantal redemption. The preface establishes the relational and salvific foundation: indicative before imperative. Reformed theology insists obedience flows from grace, never the reverse. Israel is not commanded to obey in order to be redeemed but because she has been redeemed. The same logic governs the Christian life: Be ye holy, for I am holy (1 Pet 1:16) follows the great deliverance accomplished by Christ. The law is the rule of grateful obedience for the redeemed.
Exod.20.3: Thou shalt have no other gods before me. The first commandment establishes the exclusive worship of Yahweh. Before me (al-panai) means in my presence or against my face: idolatry is provocation. Calvin called the human heart a perpetual factory of idols. Reformed theology sees idolatry not merely in carved images but in any rival ultimate loyalty: money, self, family, nation. The first commandment is the soul of all the others; if God is supreme, all else falls into place. Christ's summary, Thou shalt love the Lord thy God with all thy heart (Matt 22:37), distills this command.
Exod.20.4: Thou shalt not make unto thee any graven image. The second commandment forbids not merely idolatry of false gods but improper worship of the true God by means of images. The Reformed tradition has been particularly careful here, distinguishing the regulative principle: God may only be worshiped as He has commanded. He is invisible spirit; any image limits and lies about Him. The incarnation is no exception: Christ is the image of the invisible God (Col 1:15), but we have no warrant to manufacture images of Him. Worship is by Word and Spirit, not by visual representation.
Exod.20.7: Thou shalt not take the name of the LORD thy God in vain. To take in vain (la-shav) is to lift up emptily, to use without weight or reverence. The third commandment governs all speech about God: oaths, prayer, preaching, casual speech. Reformed catechesis (Heidelberg 99-102) extends this commandment to silence in the face of blasphemy and to the careful invocation of God's name. The hallowing of the name is the first petition of the Lord's Prayer, showing how central it remains in Christian piety. The Word made flesh, who bears the name above every name (Phil 2:9), trains us to say Father with reverence.
Exod.20.8: Remember the sabbath day, to keep it holy. The Sabbath is rooted in creation (20:11) and redemption (Deut 5:15). Reformed theology has historically held to a continuing fourth commandment now applied on the Lord's Day, the day of resurrection. The Sabbath is a creation ordinance, predating the Mosaic covenant, and therefore moral and perpetual. It is also typological: a weekly foretaste of the eternal rest that remains (Heb 4:9). Christ is Lord of the Sabbath (Mark 2:28); in Him we cease from our works and rest in His finished work, even as we await consummate rest.
Exod.20.13: Thou shalt not kill. The sixth commandment, ratsach, forbids unjust killing, that is, murder. It does not forbid all taking of life (capital punishment, just war, accidental killing are addressed elsewhere in Torah). Christ extends the command to anger and contempt (Matt 5:21-22), revealing its full depth. Reformed ethics derives from this command the sanctity of human life, image-of-God anthropology applied: every human bears the imago Dei (Gen 9:6) and is therefore inviolable. Abortion, euthanasia, and unjust war all stand condemned. Positively, the command requires the preservation and promotion of human life.
Exod.20.14: Thou shalt not commit adultery. The seventh commandment safeguards marriage as a creation ordinance and a covenant. Christ interprets it to forbid lust as well as the act (Matt 5:27-28). Marriage, for Reformed theology, is a one-flesh covenant between one man and one woman, reflecting the covenantal love of Christ for His church (Eph 5:22-33). Sexual sin is therefore not merely private but covenantally treasonous, idolatrous in its substitution of false love for true. Hosea takes Israel's idolatry as adultery against Yahweh. Faithful marriage preaches the gospel; adultery is a public sermon against it.
Exod.20.15: Thou shalt not steal. The eighth commandment establishes the legitimacy of private property and forbids its violation. It assumes that God has ordained means by which men may possess and use the earth's goods. Reformed catechesis (WLC 141-142) applies this commandment positively, requiring lawful labor, generosity, contentment, and care for the poor. To steal is to deny providence and to assault one's neighbor. Christ's ministry releases the captives but does so by legitimate means. The new heart, formed by the gospel, says with Paul, Let him that stole steal no more, but rather let him labour... that he may have to give (Eph 4:28).
Exod.20.17: Thou shalt not covet. The tenth commandment turns inward to the heart's desires, exposing what no human court can judge. Paul testifies that this commandment alone broke him: I had not known sin, but by the law: for I had not known lust, except the law had said, Thou shalt not covet (Rom 7:7). The commandment proves that the law is spiritual, demanding inward conformity. No mere outward righteousness suffices. Coveting reveals the depth of original sin and drives sinners to Christ, in whom alone the heart is renewed. The Spirit produces contentment, the antidote to coveting.
Exod.20.20: Fear not, for God is come to prove you, and that his fear may be before your faces, that ye sin not. A studied paradox: do not fear, but let His fear be before you. The fear of slavish dread is forbidden; the filial fear of reverent obedience is commanded. Reformed piety distinguishes these carefully. The same God who terrifies on Sinai woos at Calvary, yet remains awesome in holiness throughout. The fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom (Prov 9:10), the keeping power against sin. Believers approach with reverence and godly fear, for our God is a consuming fire (Heb 12:29).
Exod.21.1: Now these are the judgments (mishpatim) which thou shalt set before them. The Book of the Covenant (chapters 21-23) follows the Decalogue, applying its principles to the case law of Israel. Mishpatim are not new commandments but the working out of the moral law in Israel's particular circumstances. Reformed theology has long distinguished moral, ceremonial, and judicial law, with the moral law abiding, the ceremonial fulfilled in Christ, and the judicial expired with the nation, though instructive in its general equity (WCF 19.4). The mishpatim train God's people in righteous judgment.
Exod.21.6: His master shall bore his ear through with an awl; and he shall serve him for ever. The willing slave, who loves his master and refuses freedom, has his ear pierced as a sign of perpetual service. Psalm 40:6 picks up this image: mine ears hast thou opened, applied by Hebrews 10:5-7 to Christ. The Son becomes a willing servant, giving His ear to the Father's will, saying, Lo, I come to do thy will, O God. The pierced ear of Exodus prefigures the willing servanthood of Christ, the perfect Son who chooses obedience even unto death.
Exod.23.20: Behold, I send an Angel before thee, to keep thee in the way. The Angel of the LORD here is no created messenger but bears the divine name (23:21): for my name is in him. Reformed exegetes have generally identified this Angel with the pre-incarnate Son. He guides, guards, judges, and dispenses pardon, all divine prerogatives. The same Angel who led Israel through the wilderness now leads the church through the world. The continuity of the divine guide across both testaments testifies to the unity of Christ's redemptive work from creation to consummation.
Exod.24.7: All that the LORD hath said will we do, and be obedient. Israel's confident vow at the inauguration of the Sinai covenant. They will break it within forty days at the golden calf. The history of Israel under the Mosaic covenant is the history of broken vows. Reformed covenant theology sees this not as the fundamental flaw of God's dealings but as the demonstration of the necessity of a better covenant, a new covenant in Christ's blood (Heb 8:8-13). The law cannot save those who promise to keep it; only Christ, the Yes and Amen of all God's promises, can.
Exod.24.8: Behold the blood of the covenant, which the LORD hath made with you. Moses sprinkles the people with the blood of oxen, ratifying the covenant. Christ deliberately echoes this at the Last Supper: this is my blood of the new testament (Matt 26:28). The old covenant blood pointed forward; the new covenant blood is the substance. Hebrews 9:18-22 elaborates: without shedding of blood is no remission. Reformed sacramental theology grounds the Lord's Supper in this covenantal pattern: blood-sealed communion with God, accomplished by Christ, applied by faith.
Exod.25.8: Let them make me a sanctuary; that I may dwell among them. The whole purpose of the tabernacle is divine indwelling. Mishkan, dwelling place, comes from shakan, to dwell, the root of Shekinah. The God who descended on Sinai now condescends to dwell among His people. John 1:14 climaxes this: the Word became flesh and tabernacled among us. Christ is the true tabernacle. Eventually God will dwell with men in the New Jerusalem (Rev 21:3). The whole biblical story is the story of God dwelling with His people, accomplished in Christ and the Spirit, consummated at the last day.
Exod.25.17: Thou shalt make a mercy seat (kapporet) of pure gold. The kapporet covers the ark, hides the broken law, receives the blood. Paul calls Christ a hilasterion (mercy seat) in Romans 3:25. Christ is the place where law's demand and grace's gift meet. The broken tablets within the ark are covered by the blood-sprinkled lid; so our broken obedience is covered by Christ's blood. The cherubim above gaze down on the kapporet, as the angels desire to look into the gospel (1 Pet 1:12). The whole sanctuary is built around this place of propitiation.
Exod.25.22: There I will meet with thee, and I will commune with thee from above the mercy seat. The throne of grace is precisely the blood-sprinkled mercy seat. God meets His people not in unmediated holiness but at the place of atonement. Hebrews 4:16 invites believers to come boldly unto the throne of grace. That throne, in fulfillment, is Christ Himself, in whom God speaks and meets His people. Reformed worship is therefore Christocentric: every approach to God is through the one Mediator (1 Tim 2:5). There is no communion with God outside the kapporet.
Exod.26.31: Thou shalt make a vail of blue, and purple, and scarlet. The veil separates the holy place from the most holy. It is at once an invitation (here is God's presence) and a barrier (no one may approach). Hebrews 10:19-20 reveals its meaning: the veil is His flesh. When Christ died, the temple veil was rent from top to bottom (Matt 27:51), proclaiming that access to God is now open through Christ's torn body. The cherubim woven into the veil recall the cherubim that guarded Eden; in Christ, the way back to the tree of life is reopened.
Exod.28.30: Thou shalt put in the breastplate of judgment the Urim and the Thummim. The high priest carries the names of the twelve tribes over his heart and the means of divine guidance on his breastpiece. Christ, our great High Priest, bears the names of His people over His heart (cf. Song 8:6) and Himself is the perfect embodiment of light (urim) and perfection (thummim). He is the wisdom of God to His church (1 Cor 1:30). What was symbolic in Aaron is substantive in Christ: He carries us before the Father in unbroken intercession (Heb 7:25).
Exod.29.45: I will dwell among the children of Israel, and will be their God. The covenant formula reappears at the climax of the priestly ordination chapter. The whole point of the priestly system is communion: God dwelling with His people. Without atonement, no dwelling; without dwelling, no covenant; without covenant, no people. Reformed theology sees the entire Levitical apparatus as preparatory and pedagogical, training Israel in the holiness required for fellowship and pointing relentlessly to the priest-mediator who would actually achieve it. In Christ, the dwelling promised in Exodus 29:45 reaches its consummation.
Exod.31.13: My sabbaths ye shall keep: for it is a sign between me and you. The Sabbath is a covenantal sign, marking Israel as Yahweh's people. It points to the rest that remains (Heb 4:9), the ceasing of works in fellowship with God. The Sabbath rhythm is rooted in creation, refreshed in redemption (Deut 5:15), and consummated in resurrection. Reformed theology holds that the moral substance of the fourth commandment continues, while the day shifts to the first day in light of Christ's resurrection. The Lord's Day weekly rehearses the new creation.
Exod.32.4: These be thy gods, O Israel, which brought thee up out of the land of Egypt. The golden calf is not (primarily) the worship of a foreign deity but a violation of the second commandment: an attempt to worship Yahweh through an image. This is far more dangerous than crude polytheism, because it dresses idolatry in orthodox language. Calvin called this the constant temptation of fallen worship: making God in our own image. The Reformed regulative principle is the antidote: God may only be worshiped as He has commanded. The calf is what every age produces when piety is cut loose from Word.
Exod.32.32: Yet now, if thou wilt forgive their sin; and if not, blot me, I pray thee, out of thy book. Moses' intercession reaches its climax in this stunning offer of substitution. He cannot actually substitute for Israel, but in spirit he prefigures the one who can: Christ, who actually was blotted out for the sake of His people, bearing the wrath their sin deserved. Paul echoes this passion in Romans 9:3, wishing himself accursed for his brethren, while also acknowledging only Christ can be the curse (Gal 3:13). The mediator's heart of self-sacrificing love is the ground of all true ministry.
Exod.33.14: My presence shall go with thee, and I will give thee rest. The presence (panim, face) of God is the marrow of the covenant. Without it, Israel is just another nation. With it, she is God's distinct people. Augustine paraphrased: Thou hast made us for Thyself, and our hearts are restless till they find their rest in Thee. Christ is Immanuel, God with us (Matt 1:23), and gives the rest Moses sought (Matt 11:28). The presence is no longer in cloud and pillar but in the Spirit indwelling believers, the earnest of the face-to-face vision to come.
Exod.33.20: Thou canst not see my face: for there shall no man see me, and live. The unmediated vision of God is fatal to sinful creatures. God conceals His face but reveals His back, His glory in passing. Reformed theology emphasizes the necessity of mediation: God is known only as He chooses to be known. John 1:18 declares: No man hath seen God at any time; the only begotten Son... hath declared him. Christ is the place where the invisible God becomes visible without destroying. In Him we behold the glory we could not otherwise survive.
Exod.34.29: The skin of his face shone while he talked with him. Moses' face reflects the glory he has beheld; Israel cannot bear it and must veil it. Paul makes much of this in 2 Corinthians 3: the glory of the old covenant fading, the glory of the new covenant unveiled in Christ. Believers, with unveiled face, behold the glory of the Lord and are transformed (2 Cor 3:18). The lesser glory of Moses gives way to the greater glory of Christ, mediated by the Spirit, written not on stones but on hearts. Sanctification is glory-reflection.
Exod.40.34: Then a cloud covered the tent of the congregation, and the glory of the LORD filled the tabernacle. The book of Exodus ends as it began: with God present among His people. Where Genesis ended with Joseph in a coffin in Egypt, Exodus ends with the glory descending in Israel. The same glory will fill Solomon's temple (1 Kgs 8:10-11), depart in Ezekiel (10:18), and return climactically in Christ (John 1:14). The end of Exodus is the beginning of God's dwelling with man, a story that runs through the whole Bible to Revelation 21:3, where the tabernacle of God is with men.
Exod.40.38: For the cloud of the LORD was upon the tabernacle by day, and fire was on it by night, in the sight of all the house of Israel. The closing verse seals the wilderness pilgrimage with the visible presence of God. The cloud is mercy and guidance; the fire is holiness and protection. The same God who delivered from Egypt now travels with His people. For the church, the cloud is the Spirit poured out at Pentecost, leading and indwelling. We journey not by sight, but the visible faith of the saints across the ages testifies that the LORD goes before us still, in the sight of all the house of Israel.
Exod.6.3: But by my name JEHOVAH was I not known to them. The patriarchs knew the name Yahweh in some sense (Gen 4:26; 15:7), but they did not know its full covenantal weight as the redemption-keeping God. The verb yada (know) is experiential, not merely cognitive. Now, in delivering Israel, God will be known as Yahweh in a deeper way. Reformed theology recognizes progressive revelation: God is the same throughout, but He discloses Himself more fully across redemptive history, climaxing in Christ. We today know the name fuller still: the LORD is Jesus, who saves His people from their sins.
Exod.7.5: The Egyptians shall know that I am the LORD. The plagues serve a missiological purpose: even the Egyptians, in judgment, will be confronted with the truth of who Yahweh is. Some will harden, but others (a mixed multitude, 12:38) will go up with Israel, foreshadowing the gathering of the nations to Christ. Reformed theology embraces the cosmic scope of redemption: God's glory among the nations is His ultimate aim. The plagues anticipate the final judgments of Revelation, which similarly serve to vindicate God's name and gather His elect from every tribe.
Exod.10.2: That thou mayest tell in the ears of thy son, and of thy son's son, what things I have wrought in Egypt. The exodus is a story to be retold across generations, the archetype of catechesis. Reformed theology has always emphasized covenantal nurture: parents teaching children the works of God. The Passover liturgy itself enshrines the question, What mean ye by this service? (12:26). Children of the covenant grow up hearing the gospel of redemption. So in the new covenant, we instruct children diligently (Deut 6:7) and bring them up in the nurture and admonition of the Lord (Eph 6:4).
Exod.13.8: And thou shalt shew thy son in that day, saying, This is done because of that which the LORD did unto me when I came forth out of Egypt. The Passover liturgy includes generational identification: not merely what God did for ancestors but what He did for me. Each Israelite enters into the redemption as if personally delivered. So in Christian worship, the believer says with Paul, the Son of God loved me, and gave himself for me (Gal 2:20). Redemption is corporate and individual at once. The Lord's Supper similarly invites personal participation in the once-for-all event of the cross.
Exod.15.18: The LORD shall reign for ever and ever. The Song of the Sea ends with cosmic kingship. The defeat of Pharaoh is the establishment of Yahweh's reign. The kingdom of God is the central theme that runs from this declaration to the consummation in Revelation 11:15: the kingdoms of this world are become the kingdoms of our Lord, and of his Christ. Reformed theology embraces the present-and-future reality of the kingdom: Christ reigns now from heaven, and His reign will be visibly consummated. The exodus is the down payment; the new heavens and new earth, the full payment.
Exod.16.15: It is manna: for they wist not what it was. The name itself, man hu (what is it?), preserves the mystery. The manna is daily, sufficient, miraculous, and gathered with no surplus except for the sabbath day. It teaches dependence and faith. Christ's claim to be the bread of life (John 6) builds directly on this: the manna sustained mortal life for a generation; Christ sustains eternal life forever. Reformed theology emphasizes that we feed on Christ daily by faith, gathering fresh from His Word, not hoarding or substituting our own provision.
Exod.17.11: When Moses held up his hand, that Israel prevailed. The battle with Amalek is fought below, but won above, by the lifted hands of intercession. Aaron and Hur hold up Moses' hands; the picture is of mediation sustained by community. Christ, our true intercessor, lifts His pierced hands eternally before the Father (Heb 7:25). The church does not merely fight earthly battles but supports those who lift up holy hands (1 Tim 2:8) in prayer. Spiritual warfare is won by intercession, and the war ultimately belongs to the Lord (1 Sam 17:47).
Exod.21.24: Eye for eye, tooth for tooth. The lex talionis is not a charter for personal vengeance but a principle of judicial proportion: punishment must fit the crime. It restrains escalation. Christ does not abolish this in Matthew 5:38-39 but applies a different principle to personal ethics: do not retaliate. The civil magistrate still bears the sword (Rom 13:4); the disciple turns the cheek. Reformed ethics distinguishes the spheres carefully. At Calvary, the perfect lex talionis is satisfied: Christ bears in full what our sin deserved, freeing us from retributive condemnation.
Exod.23.19: Thou shalt not seethe a kid in his mother's milk. This curious command, repeated three times in Torah, has occasioned much exegesis. It likely forbids participation in a Canaanite fertility ritual. More broadly, it sets a tone of compassion in covenant life: the means of nurture should not become the means of death. Mother and offspring are not to be confused. Reformed exegesis sees in such laws the formation of a tender, ordered, distinct people. The God of Israel is not a deity of cruelty or chaos; His law trains the heart in compassionate categories.
Exod.25.30: Thou shalt set upon the table shewbread before me alway. The bread of the presence (lechem ha-panim, bread of the face) testifies to perpetual covenant fellowship. Twelve loaves represent the twelve tribes; God dines with His people in symbolic communion. Christ, the true bread, fulfills both this and the manna. He sets a table before us in the Lord's Supper, where the presence of God meets His covenant people. Reformed sacramentology sees the Supper as more than memorial: by the Spirit, we feed on Christ and enjoy real communion with our exalted Head.
Exod.26.33: The vail shall divide unto you between the holy place and the most holy. The structured holiness of the tabernacle teaches that God dwells in unapproachable light. Levels of holiness mark the sanctuary: outer court, holy place, holy of holies. Only the high priest enters the inmost sanctuary, only once a year, only with blood. Hebrews shows that Christ has entered the true holy of holies, heaven itself, with His own blood (Heb 9:12). The veil of His flesh is rent, and the way is opened. The architecture of the tabernacle preaches the gospel.
Exod.27.20: Pure oil olive beaten for the light, to cause the lamp to burn always. The menorah burned continually in the holy place. Olive trees stand for fruitful, anointed life; the oil prefigures the Spirit. Zechariah 4 develops the picture: the lampstand fed by olive trees represents the Spirit-empowered work of God. Christ is the Light of the world (John 8:12) and pours out the Spirit on His church, who is then the light of the world by derivation (Matt 5:14). The lampstand burns by the Spirit's oil; the church shines only as Christ supplies her.
Exod.28.36: Thou shalt make a plate of pure gold, and grave upon it... HOLINESS TO THE LORD. The high priest wears the inscription qodesh la-Yahweh on his forehead. Holiness is the theme of his ministry. The plate covers iniquities of the holy things (28:38), so that even Israel's worship requires atonement. Christ, our true high priest, wears the inscription not on metal but in His own being. He is intrinsically holy, and bears the iniquities of our sanctified offerings before the Father. Reformed theology recognizes that even our best deeds need cleansing through His mediation.
Exod.29.18: It is a burnt offering unto the LORD: it is a sweet savour. The whole burnt offering (olah, ascending offering) ascends entirely in smoke; nothing is reserved for the worshiper. It signifies total consecration. The sweet savor (rei'ach nichoach) language is applied to Christ in Ephesians 5:2: Christ also hath loved us, and hath given himself for us an offering and a sacrifice to God for a sweetsmelling savour. Christ's whole life and death ascends to the Father as wholly acceptable. In Him, our half-hearted offerings are received as sweet because they ascend with His.
Exod.30.10: Once in the year shall he make atonement upon it... it is most holy unto the LORD. The annual Day of Atonement on the horns of the incense altar testifies that even the place of prayer needs cleansing. Hebrews 9-10 contrasts the once-a-year repetition with Christ's once-for-all offering: by one offering he hath perfected for ever them that are sanctified (Heb 10:14). The annual rhythm preached the inadequacy of bull and goat blood; the cross declared the sufficiency of Christ. Reformed theology insists no further sacrifice can be offered; the Lord's Supper is memorial and communion, not re-sacrifice.
Exod.32.10: Now therefore let me alone, that my wrath may wax hot against them. God's invitation to Moses to step aside is, paradoxically, an invitation to intercede. The Hebrew implies, do not stand in the breach, that I may consume them. Moses recognizes the implicit invitation and pleads. The pattern is profoundly instructive: God ordains intercession as the means by which He shows mercy. Christ stands in the ultimate breach (Ezek 22:30), interposing Himself between God's wrath and the elect. Reformed theology marvels that the ordained intercessor is also the One whose blood is the price of mercy.
Exod.33.11: The LORD spake unto Moses face to face, as a man speaketh unto his friend. The intimacy of Moses with Yahweh is unique in Old Testament history (cf. Num 12:8). Yet later (33:20), even Moses cannot see God's face directly. The face-to-face is figurative for unparalleled friendship within creaturely limits. Christ surpasses Moses (Heb 3:3): in Him we have the friend of sinners (Matt 11:19) who is also the eternal Son. Believers are called His friends (John 15:15). The intimacy denied even to Moses is granted to the lowliest believer through union with Christ.
Exod.34.7: Forgiving iniquity and transgression and sin, and that will by no means clear the guilty. The two halves of God's character meet in this verse. Mercy is abundant, but justice is uncompromised. How can He both forgive and not clear the guilty? Only at the cross. Christ bears the guilt (the not-clearing) so that forgiveness may be lavished without compromising righteousness (Rom 3:25-26). Reformed theology, especially through Anselm and the Reformers, has built its doctrine of substitutionary atonement on this very tension. The God who proclaims His name in Exodus 34 displays it climactically at Calvary.
Exod.36.1: Then wrought Bezaleel and Aholiab, and every wise hearted man, in whom the LORD put wisdom and understanding to know how to work all manner of work for the service of the sanctuary. The work of God's house is done by Spirit-empowered men. Reformed theology embraces vocational diversity: artists, builders, craftsmen, all serving God. The unity of the work and the diversity of gifts prefigure the body of Christ (1 Cor 12). Every member contributes; all are filled with the Spirit for service. The tabernacle is the church's anticipation.
Exod.39.43: Moses did look upon all the work, and, behold, they had done it as the LORD had commanded, even so had they done it: and Moses blessed them. The repetition recalls Genesis 1: God saw all that he had made... it was very good. The tabernacle is a microcosm, a new creation in miniature. Moses' inspection-and-blessing pattern parallels God's creation week. Reformed biblical theology sees the Mosaic sanctuary as a recapitulation of Eden, a place of communion lost in the fall and reopened by grace. Christ is the true tabernacle, the new creation in person, and His finished work is approved by the Father with resurrection.
Exod.7.17: In this thou shalt know that I am the LORD: behold, I will smite with the rod that is in mine hand upon the waters which are in the river, and they shall be turned to blood. The first plague targets the Nile, the source of Egyptian life and a deity in itself. Yahweh demonstrates absolute lordship over creation. The plagues form a deliberate pattern of de-creation: bringing chaos upon Egypt as a reverse of Genesis 1, while Israel is preserved. Reformed theology sees creation and judgment as two acts of the same sovereign hand. The Lord who set the waters at creation can turn them to blood at His word.
Exod.8.10: Tomorrow. He said, Be it according to thy word: that thou mayest know that there is none like unto the LORD our God. Pharaoh's strange answer tomorrow when asked when to remove the frogs reveals stubborn pride: even one more night with frogs is preferable to bowing immediately. Yet Moses turns it to a vehicle of divine vindication: God will show His incomparability by removing them on Pharaoh's chosen day. Reformed theology delights in such moments where human folly becomes the canvas of divine glory. The tomorrow of human delay becomes the appointed today of God's display.
Exod.9.27: I have sinned this time: the LORD is righteous, and I and my people are wicked. Pharaoh's confession during the hailstorm is verbal but not heart-deep. He acknowledges sin under duress, then hardens again when the pressure lifts. Reformed theology distinguishes natural conviction from saving repentance. Even Pharaoh can mouth the words; only the regenerate heart truly turns. Judas confessed, I have sinned (Matt 27:4), and went to his death; David said the same words (2 Sam 12:13) and went on in covenant. The same words can mark utterly different hearts.
Exod.11.7: But against any of the children of Israel shall not a dog move his tongue, against man or beast: that ye may know how that the LORD doth put a difference between the Egyptians and Israel. The verb hiphil of palah (to make a distinction) marks the great divide. The plagues from the fourth onward distinguish Goshen from Egypt. Reformed theology sees here the doctrine of election in narrative form: God draws a line, and the line is sovereign mercy, not Israel's worth. The same God puts a difference at the final judgment between the sheep and the goats (Matt 25:32). The line is drawn by the blood.
Exod.12.36: The LORD gave the people favour in the sight of the Egyptians, so that they lent unto them. The plundering of the Egyptians (12:35-36) fulfills Genesis 15:14: they shall come out with great substance. The reparations are not theft but back-wages and divine compensation. Reformed theology sees here the principle that God ultimately vindicates His oppressed people, often using the very means of their oppressors' wealth. The plundering anticipates the great gathering of the wealth of the nations (Isa 60:5) into the kingdom of God. The redeemed leave Egypt rich.
Exod.14.4: I will be honoured upon Pharaoh, and upon all his host. God deliberately leads Israel into a position where escape seems impossible, drawing Pharaoh after them. The aim is glory. Reformed theology sees this as a model of providence: God sometimes brings His people into seemingly hopeless straits precisely to magnify His deliverance. The cross is the supreme example: Christ trapped by Roman power and Jewish enmity, only to triumph by apparent defeat. The honor of God upon Pharaoh anticipates the honor of God upon every adversary at the last day.
Exod.16.31: The taste of it was like wafers made with honey. The manna is sweet, free, sufficient. Sweet because it is grace; free because it falls from heaven; sufficient because each gathers as much as he needs. Christ is sweeter than manna (Ps 19:10), the bread of life given freely. Reformed devotional theology has long savored the sweetness of Christ in such typological reading. To taste and see that the Lord is good (Ps 34:8) is the experiential dimension of faith. The wilderness journey is sustained on heavenly bread, and so is the Christian life.
Exod.19.10: Sanctify them today and tomorrow, and let them wash their clothes. Preparation for theophany requires consecration: washing, abstaining, attending. The lesson is enduring: those who would meet with God do not approach casually. Hebrews 12:14 echoes: holiness, without which no man shall see the Lord. The washing prefigures the washing of regeneration (Titus 3:5) and the cleansing of the bride for her husband (Eph 5:26). Reformed worship retains a sense of preparation: confession, prayer, the call to assembly, lest we approach unworthily and incur judgment.
Exod.19.16: There were thunders and lightnings, and a thick cloud upon the mount, and the voice of the trumpet exceeding loud. Sinai is sensory overwhelm: God meets Israel in unmistakable terror. The trumpet (shofar) here connects to the eschatological trumpet of resurrection (1 Cor 15:52). Mount Sinai is contrasted in Hebrews 12:18-24 with Mount Zion, the heavenly assembly. Reformed theology holds both: the law remains weighty and binding, but the believer comes to the better mountain through Christ. The thunders of Sinai prepare us to appreciate the still small voice of the gospel.
Exod.20.18: The people saw the thunderings, and the lightnings... and they removed, and stood afar off. Israel's reaction at Sinai is to flee from God's presence. Sin produces distance. The sinner cannot bear unmediated holiness. They beg Moses, Speak thou with us, and we will hear: but let not God speak with us, lest we die (20:19). Reformed theology recognizes the necessity of mediation: a fact built into Israel's first encounter with the law. Christ is the better mediator who draws us near (Heb 12:24). The same God who terrified at Sinai is, in Christ, the God of all comfort.
Exod.24.10: They saw the God of Israel: and there was under his feet as it were a paved work of a sapphire stone. The seventy elders ascend with Moses and behold something of God's glory and live, an extraordinary mercy. Sapphire pavement language recurs in Ezekiel 1:26 and Revelation 4:6: the throne of God. The covenant meal that follows (24:11, they saw God, and did eat and drink) prefigures the marriage supper of the Lamb (Rev 19:9). Reformed theology has seen such moments as foretastes of beatific vision, partial now, full when we see Him as He is.
Exod.31.18: He gave unto Moses... two tables of testimony, tables of stone, written with the finger of God. The law is divinely authored. The two tables likely each contained the full Decalogue, ancient covenant practice giving each party a copy. Both copies are kept in the ark, sealed in God's house: an enduring covenant document. Reformed theology grounds the moral authority of the Ten Commandments in this divine writing. Later, the new covenant promise is that God will write the law on the heart (Jer 31:33; 2 Cor 3:3). The same finger that wrote on stone now writes by the Spirit on the fleshy tablets of believers' hearts.
Exod.32.14: The LORD repented of the evil which he thought to do unto his people. Anthropopathic language: God speaks as if changing His mind, but in covenant terms, He is being utterly consistent with His revealed character. He had told Moses to step aside; Moses interceded; God responds. The interplay is real, not pretended, but does not impugn God's immutability. Reformed theology sees prayer as ordained means within God's eternal counsel. He decrees the end and the means; intercession truly works because God truly works through it. Christ's intercession is similarly real and effective.
Exod.33.18: I beseech thee, shew me thy glory. Moses' bold petition expresses the deepest cry of every saint. To see the glory of God is the heart of human longing, the fulfillment of being made in His image. God grants it, but only as much as Moses can bear. Reformed theology calls this the visio Dei, the beatific vision, partially given here through Christ (2 Cor 4:6: the light of the knowledge of the glory of God in the face of Jesus Christ), fully given at glorification (1 John 3:2: we shall see him as he is).
Exod.35.31: He hath filled him with the spirit of God, in wisdom, in understanding, and in knowledge. Bezaleel is the first explicit Spirit-filled man named in Scripture, and he is filled for craftsmanship. The Spirit's work is not limited to ecstatic phenomena but extends to skilled labor. Reformed theology embraces this breadth. The Spirit who hovered over creation now hovers over the construction of the tabernacle, a new creation in microcosm. Every gift, manual or intellectual, exercised in service to God, is the Spirit's outflow. There is no work too humble for the Spirit's anointing when it builds God's house.
Exod.2.3: When she could not longer hide him, she took for him an ark (tevah) of bulrushes. The Hebrew word for the basket is the same word used for Noah's ark. Both are vessels of preservation amid waters of judgment, carrying the seed through which God will save the world. Reformed biblical theology delights in such patterns: the Noahic ark, the basket-ark of Moses, and ultimately the womb of Mary, all bear God's chosen instruments through threatening waters. The pitch (zephet) seals the ark; so Christ is sealed by the Spirit, kept safe by the Father in His mission to redeem.
Exod.3.6: I am the God of thy father, the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob. Christ Himself cites this verse to prove the resurrection (Matt 22:32): God is not the God of the dead, but of the living. The patriarchs still live, awaiting bodily resurrection, because their God remains their God beyond the grave. Reformed eschatology grounds the resurrection hope here, not on philosophical immortality but on the covenant faithfulness of the living God. He who began the covenant will keep it past death itself, the last enemy.
Exod.3.7: I have surely seen the affliction of my people. The infinitive absolute construction (raoh raiti) intensifies the verb: I have seeing seen. God's omniscient compassion is not detached observation but covenantal involvement. The verb yada (know) in 3:7 has the same intensity: I know their sorrows. Reformed theology emphasizes that God's knowledge is never bare cognition but personal, covenantal, redemptive knowledge. The same God who sees Israel sees His elect today (Heb 4:13), not in indifference but in saving compassion through Christ.
Exod.4.2: What is that in thine hand? And he said, A rod. The rod of a shepherd, ordinary in itself, becomes the rod of God (4:20) by which Egypt is judged and the sea is parted. God uses what we have, sanctifying common means for sacred ends. Reformed theology sees here a paradigm: the gospel is preached through ordinary words, the sacraments administered with common bread and water, all becoming powerful by divine appointment. The shepherd's staff prefigures Christ the Good Shepherd, who wields the rod and staff of comfort over His sheep (Ps 23:4).
Exod.7.1: I have made thee a god (elohim) to Pharaoh. Moses is set in a representative capacity: he speaks and acts for God before Pharaoh, with Aaron as his prophet. The lesser elohim language (cf. Ps 82:6) does not deify Moses but designates official authority. Reformed theology sees here the pattern of office: God works through ordained representatives. Christ is the supreme prophet, sent by the Father (John 17:18). Ministers stand in this prophetic line, bearing God's word with authority that is borrowed but real.
Exod.9.12: The LORD hardened the heart of Pharaoh. After Pharaoh's repeated self-hardening, God's judicial hardening is now explicit. Romans 9:18 cites this pattern. Reformed theology distinguishes God's permissive hardening (giving over to chosen sin) from any active introduction of evil. The hardening is just because the sin is real and freely embraced. Pharaoh is not punished for what he did not do; he is punished for what he persistently chose, and the divine hardening confirms his rebellion. This is sober theology, but it is biblical.
Exod.10.7: How long shall this man be a snare unto us? Pharaoh's own servants now plead with him to relent. The plagues have broken Egypt economically and socially. Yet Pharaoh persists, an emblem of how sin blinds even kings to obvious ruin. Reformed theology recognizes the noetic effect of sin: the unregenerate cannot see clearly even when their interests demand it. Only sovereign grace opens eyes. Pharaoh's stubbornness is also typological: every rebel against God ultimately ruins his own kingdom.
Exod.11.5: All the firstborn in the land of Egypt shall die. The tenth plague is judicial reckoning: Pharaoh refused to release Yahweh's firstborn (4:22-23), so Pharaoh's firstborn dies. The principle of measure-for-measure runs through the plague cycle. The death of the firstborn anticipates the death of the only-begotten Son, who alone, by bearing the firstborn judgment, can spare His people. The Lamb is slain so that the firstborn need not die. Christ, the firstborn from the dead (Col 1:18), reverses the judgment by absorbing it.
Exod.12.11: Thus shall ye eat it; with your loins girded, your shoes on your feet, and your staff in your hand. The Passover is eaten in haste, in pilgrim posture. Believers live as those redeemed but not yet at home. Peter applies this exact imagery: gird up the loins of your mind (1 Pet 1:13). The Christian life is sojourning, not settled, awaiting the consummate exodus into the promised land. Reformed theology embraces this pilgrim ethos: the believer dwells in the world but is not of it (John 17:16).
Exod.12.15: Seven days shall ye eat unleavened bread. The Feast of Unleavened Bread (matzot) follows immediately on Passover. Leaven (chametz) becomes a symbol of corruption to be purged. Paul applies this directly: purge out therefore the old leaven, that ye may be a new lump... let us keep the feast, not with old leaven, neither with the leaven of malice and wickedness; but with the unleavened bread of sincerity and truth (1 Cor 5:7-8). Reformed sanctification is the ongoing purgation of sin from a redeemed people, who are already unleavened in Christ.
Exod.12.27: It is the sacrifice of the LORD's passover. The slaughtered lamb is called a sacrifice (zevach), grounding the meal in atonement, not mere remembrance. The blood is propitiatory. Reformed theology insists Passover is fundamentally a sacrificial meal, prefiguring Christ our Passover sacrificed for us (1 Cor 5:7).
