The meal is the covenant communion that follows the atoning blood. So the Lord's Supper presupposes Christ's sacrifice and is the meal of those reconciled by His blood.
Exod.12.48: When a stranger shall sojourn with thee, and will keep the passover... let all his males be circumcised, and then let him come near and keep it. The Passover is open to the proselyte who joins the covenant. The redemption is not ethnically restricted; the door is open to those who embrace the God of Israel by covenant sign. This anticipates the inclusion of the nations in the new covenant (Gal 3:8). Reformed theology emphasizes both the priority of covenantal Israel and the universal scope of God's saving purpose, climaxing in one new humanity in Christ (Eph 2:15).
Exod.13.13: Every firstling of an ass thou shalt redeem with a lamb; and if thou wilt not redeem it, then thou shalt break his neck. The unclean firstborn donkey, unfit for sacrifice, is redeemed by the substitution of a clean lamb. The picture is striking: an unclean creature spared by the death of a clean substitute. Every sinner is the unclean donkey; Christ is the spotless Lamb whose death redeems. Reformed substitutionary atonement finds vivid expression here. The penalty stands; only the substitute lamb provides escape.
Exod.14.31: Israel saw that great work which the LORD did upon the Egyptians: and the people feared the LORD, and believed the LORD. The trinity of saving response: seeing (in faith), fearing (with reverence), believing (in trust). Faith is grounded in God's mighty acts, supremely in Christ's death and resurrection. Reformed theology grounds saving faith in the historic act of God; it is not mere mysticism but trust in God's revealed redemptive deeds. Israel's faith at the Sea is paradigmatic for every believer: we look back on accomplished redemption and believe.
Exod.15.1: Then sang Moses and the children of Israel this song unto the LORD. The Song of the Sea is the first national worship of redeemed Israel. Salvation produces song. The pattern persists: Mary sings (Luke 1:46), Zechariah sings (1:67), the redeemed in heaven sing the song of Moses and the song of the Lamb (Rev 15:3). Reformed worship is shaped by this pattern: theology is doxology, redemption produces praise. The exodus is Israel's first hymnal, and the church inherits and amplifies the song.
Exod.15.6: Thy right hand, O LORD, is become glorious in power: thy right hand, O LORD, hath dashed in pieces the enemy. The right hand of Yahweh is His covenant strength. Christ ascends to the right hand of the Father (Mark 16:19), the place of power. The right hand that dashed Egypt now extends mercy through Christ and will dash all enemies at the consummation (Ps 110:5). Reformed theology sees the same God working consistently across redemptive history, the right hand revealing both judgment and grace at every climax.
Exod.16.3: Would to God we had died by the hand of the LORD in the land of Egypt, when we sat by the flesh pots. Israel romanticizes Egypt within weeks of deliverance. The pattern of unbelief idealizes the bondage from which God has redeemed. Reformed theology recognizes the indwelling sin of even the redeemed: the journey to glory passes through wilderness, and the temptation is always to return. Hebrews 3 warns us by Israel's example. The flesh pots of Egypt are every former sin we are tempted to remember fondly. Christ's better food calls us forward.
Exod.17.7: He called the name of the place Massah, and Meribah, because of the chiding of the children of Israel, and because they tempted the LORD, saying, Is the LORD among us, or not? Massah (testing) and Meribah (strife) become bywords for unbelief (Ps 95:8-9; Heb 3:8-9). Israel tests God; God tests Israel. Reformed theology sees in this episode the perpetual struggle of faith. The question is the question: is the LORD among us? Christ is Immanuel, God with us; faith answers the Massah question with confidence rooted in the cross.
Exod.18.21: Provide out of all the people able men, such as fear God, men of truth, hating covetousness; and place such over them. Jethro's counsel establishes a hierarchy of judges. The qualifications are moral and spiritual, not merely administrative: fear of God, truthfulness, freedom from greed. Reformed ecclesiology builds office on similar lines (1 Tim 3; Titus 1). Christ delegates authority through gifts, but always with reference to character. Civil and ecclesiastical office both require these qualities. The lesson endures: God ordains structure, but structure without holy men is hollow.
Exod.19.4: Ye have seen what I did unto the Egyptians, and how I bare you on eagles' wings, and brought you unto myself. Eagle imagery captures the tenderness and power of God's deliverance: the eagle that bears its young on its wings, sweeping low to lift them when they falter (Deut 32:11). Redemption is not merely deliverance from Egypt but bringing unto God Himself. The goal is communion. Reformed theology insists the chief end of redemption is fellowship with the Triune God, not personal benefit alone. We are saved unto God, through Christ.
Exod.20.6: Shewing mercy unto thousands of them that love me, and keep my commandments. The vast disparity between three or four generations of judgment and thousands of generations of mercy reveals God's character: He delights to show mercy. Love and obedience are inseparable: those who love God keep His commandments (John 14:15). Reformed theology denies any antinomianism: gospel grace produces holy obedience as its necessary fruit. The covenant blessing extends across generations, anchoring the principle of household covenantalism that runs through Scripture.
Exod.20.10: But the seventh day is the sabbath of the LORD thy God: in it thou shalt not do any work, thou, nor thy son, nor thy daughter. The Sabbath is comprehensive: family, servants, even animals rest. Reformed theology sees in this scope the dignity of all creatures and the inclusiveness of God's mercy. The Sabbath is not merely cessation but consecration; it directs the heart and the household to worship and rest in God. Christ, Lord of the Sabbath, gives true rest (Matt 11:28). The Lord's Day continues this rhythm in light of resurrection.
Exod.20.16: Thou shalt not bear false witness against thy neighbour. The ninth commandment safeguards truth in human community. False witness destroys justice, reputation, and trust. Christ is the Truth (John 14:6), and the devil is the father of lies (John 8:44). Reformed catechesis (WLC 145) extends this commandment broadly: gossip, slander, flattery, all violate it. Positively, the command requires honest speech, fair judgment, and the protection of one's neighbor's good name. The new heart speaks truth in love (Eph 4:15).
Exod.21.12: He that smiteth a man, so that he die, shall be surely put to death. Capital punishment for murder is rooted in Genesis 9:6 (image of God) and reaffirmed here. Reformed theology has historically affirmed the legitimate authority of the magistrate to execute justice (Rom 13:4). The mishpat is rooted in the sanctity of human life, not in vengeance. Christ does not abolish civil justice; He addresses personal ethics. The cross does not nullify capital justice but reveals that the ultimate Judge has Himself born the capital penalty for His people.
Exod.22.22: Ye shall not afflict any widow, or fatherless child. The covenant law's special concern for the vulnerable: widow, orphan, sojourner. God Himself is their advocate (22:23). Reformed social ethics flows from this covenantal concern. James echoes it (Jas 1:27): pure religion includes visiting the fatherless and widows. The God who delivered slaves loves the powerless. Christian ethics is not merely private piety but covenantal justice. Christ embodies and intensifies this concern, ministering supremely to the broken.
Exod.23.12: Six days thou shalt do thy work, and on the seventh day thou shalt rest: that thine ox and thine ass may rest, and the son of thy handmaid, and the stranger, may be refreshed. The Sabbath is reasserted with social and creational scope. Even animals rest. The motive is humanitarian and creational, fitting Genesis 2's pattern. Reformed theology sees the moral law of Sabbath as enduring, providing rest for body, restoration for soul, and proclamation of redemption. The new covenant Lord's Day brings these benefits in light of Christ's finished work.
Exod.24.11: Upon the nobles of the children of Israel he laid not his hand: also they saw God, and did eat and drink. The covenant meal: seventy elders eating in God's presence and surviving. The mercy is staggering, given the warnings of Sinai. The meal anticipates the Lord's Supper, where sinners eat in God's presence at the table of His Son. Reformed sacramentology sees the Supper as covenant meal, communion with the risen Christ, foretaste of the marriage supper of the Lamb (Rev 19:9). What was reserved for elders is opened to every believer through Christ.
Exod.25.10: They shall make an ark of shittim wood. The ark of the covenant is the central piece of tabernacle furniture, containing the testimony (the tablets), Aaron's budded rod, and a pot of manna. It is the throne of God among His people. Acacia wood overlaid with gold pictures the union of incorruptible and corruptible, prefiguring the union of divine and human in Christ. The ark goes before Israel into battle and across the Jordan, picturing Christ leading His people through every barrier. The ark's loss is exile; its return is restoration.
Exod.25.40: Look that thou make them after their pattern, which was shewed thee in the mount. The earthly tabernacle is a copy of a heavenly reality. Hebrews 8:5 cites this verse: serving the example and shadow of heavenly things. Reformed biblical theology sees the tabernacle as preaching the gospel by typology, all pointing to Christ and the heavenly sanctuary He has entered. Worship below mirrors worship above. The principle of the regulative principle is hinted at: God has revealed how He is to be worshiped, and we may not improvise.
Exod.27.1: Thou shalt make an altar of shittim wood. The altar of burnt offering stands in the courtyard, the first thing one encounters approaching the tabernacle. There is no access to God except through sacrifice. The altar is large, four-cornered, with horns at each corner. Reformed theology sees here the universal need for atonement: every approach to God passes the altar. Christ is our altar (Heb 13:10), the place where the substitutionary fire consumes our sin. The court altar precedes the holy place; so atonement precedes communion.
Exod.28.1: Take thou unto thee Aaron thy brother, and his sons with him, from among the children of Israel, that he may minister unto me in the priest's office. The priesthood is divinely instituted, not chosen by men. Hebrews 5:4 cites this principle for Christ's priesthood: no man taketh this honour unto himself, but he that is called of God, as was Aaron. Reformed theology emphasizes the divine call to ministry; office is not self-assumed. Christ is the called one, the Father's appointed High Priest. Aaron's line ends; Christ's priesthood is forever after the order of Melchizedek.
Exod.28.21: The stones shall be with the names of the children of Israel, twelve, according to their names. The high priest carries the names of the tribes on his shoulders (28:12) and over his heart (28:29) when he enters the holy place. Christ, our great High Priest, bears the names of His elect on His shoulders (strength) and over His heart (love) before the Father continually. The intercession of Christ is personal, named, particular. Reformed theology rejects abstract or potential atonement: He bears the names of His own.
Exod.29.36: Every day shalt thou offer a bullock for a sin offering for atonement: and thou shalt cleanse the altar... and anoint it. Even the altar requires atonement. The priests need atonement before they can mediate atonement. The whole system is steeped in the recognition of pervasive uncleanness. Reformed theology sees this as a divinely engineered impossibility: Levitical sacrifice can never finally cleanse, only repeatedly cover. Hebrews 10:1-4 spells this out: the blood of bulls and goats cannot take away sins. Christ alone, the unblemished and perfect priest, accomplishes what the system signified.
Exod.30.6: Thou shalt put it before the vail that is by the ark of the testimony. The altar of incense stands just before the veil, as close to God's presence as a piece of furniture in the holy place can be. Incense rises continually, a picture of unceasing prayer. Revelation 5:8 makes the connection explicit: the prayers of the saints rise as incense. Reformed devotional theology sees prayer as priestly: through Christ, our prayers ascend with His incense, never alone, always perfumed by His mediation.
Exod.30.30: Thou shalt anoint Aaron and his sons, and consecrate them, that they may minister unto me in the priest's office. Anointing with oil sets apart for sacred service; the oil prefigures the Spirit. Christ is the Anointed One (Mashiach, Christos), anointed without measure (John 3:34) by the Spirit at His baptism. Believers, in turn, share in this anointing (1 John 2:20, 27). Reformed theology sees the priesthood of all believers as a gracious extension of Christ's anointed office, with no human mediator standing between Christ and the believer.
Exod.31.3: I have filled him with the spirit of God, in wisdom, and in understanding, and in knowledge, and in all manner of workmanship. Bezaleel's filling with the Spirit for craftsmanship is the first such Old Testament filling. The Spirit equips for the building of God's house. Reformed pneumatology sees the Spirit's work in the ordinary as well as the extraordinary: every faithful labor for the kingdom, every Spirit-given skill, contributes to building the temple of God. The continuity from Bezaleel to the building of the church (1 Cor 12) is the pattern of God's work.
Exod.32.6: The people sat down to eat and to drink, and rose up to play. Paul cites this verse (1 Cor 10:7) as a warning against idolatry. Idolatrous worship typically combines false worship with sensual indulgence. The pattern endures: every age's idols feed the appetites. Reformed theology sees idolatry as fundamentally a heart problem before it is a worship problem. The remedy is not better behavior but a new heart, given by the Spirit, that rises up to worship the true God in spirit and in truth (John 4:24).
Exod.32.26: Then Moses stood in the gate of the camp, and said, Who is on the LORD's side? let him come unto me. The dividing question of every age. Levi rallies, executing judgment on idolaters. Christ likewise demands decisive allegiance: he that is not with me is against me (Matt 12:30). Reformed theology recognizes no neutrality before God. The question Moses asks is the question every soul must answer. The Levites' faithfulness in this crisis becomes the basis for their priestly election (Deut 33:8-9).
Exod.32.31: Oh, this people have sinned a great sin, and have made them gods of gold. Moses returns to God after dealing with Israel and pleads. The intercessor's burden is the great sin, named without minimization. Reformed theology insists confession be honest: sin must be called sin. Yet the same intercessor pleads. Christ does both perfectly: He knows our sin in full, and yet He intercedes (Heb 7:25; 1 John 2:1). The Mosaic mediator points beyond himself to the only fully effective Mediator.
Exod.33.16: Wherein shall it be known here that I and thy people have found grace in thy sight? is it not in that thou goest with us? The presence of God among His people is what distinguishes them from all other nations. Apart from God's presence, Israel is just another tribe. Reformed ecclesiology echoes this: the church is the church only as God dwells in her by His Spirit and Word. Where Christ is not present by His Spirit, the assembly is no church regardless of name. The presence of God is the church's ultimate distinctive.
Exod.34.1: Hew thee two tables of stone like unto the first: and I will write upon them the words that were in the first tables, which thou brakest. The covenant is renewed after the breach of the golden calf. Moses must hew the tables; God will write. Grace re-establishes covenant after sin's rupture, a foreshadowing of the new covenant in Christ's blood, written not on stone but on hearts (Jer 31:33). Reformed theology celebrates the durability of the covenant of grace: human failure is real, but God's covenant is unbreakable, secured in the obedience of Christ.
Exod.34.14: Thou shalt worship no other god: for the LORD, whose name is Jealous, is a jealous God. Yahweh's name is Jealous (Qanna). Divine jealousy is not petty envy but covenantal zeal: He will not share His glory with another (Isa 42:8). Reformed theology embraces this attribute as a corollary of God's holiness and love. The husband-image grounds the imagery: the covenant is a marriage; idolatry is adultery. Christ jealously loves His bride (2 Cor 11:2) and will not share her with another. His jealousy is our protection.
Exod.34.28: He wrote upon the tables the words of the covenant, the ten commandments. The Ten Words (aseret ha-devarim) are explicitly identified as the words of the covenant. Reformed theology has long held the Decalogue as the moral law in summary form, written by God's own finger, abiding for all generations. While ceremonial and judicial particulars expire, the moral substance continues, fulfilled and intensified in Christ. The Ten Commandments remain in catechism and confession because God Himself preserved them in the ark.
Exod.35.5: Take ye from among you an offering unto the LORD: whosoever is of a willing heart, let him bring it, an offering of the LORD. Tabernacle construction is funded by willing hearts. The principle of willing, joyful giving is foundational. Paul echoes it: God loveth a cheerful giver (2 Cor 9:7). Reformed stewardship theology insists giving is grace-driven, not coerced or grudging. The ark's gold and the altar's bronze come from hearts moved by the Spirit. So in the new covenant, the church is built up by Spirit-given gifts and offerings.
Exod.36.6: The people were restrained from bringing. So abundant was the giving that Moses had to command Israel to stop. The contrast with the church's frequent stinginess is sobering. When hearts are truly engaged with God's work, generosity overflows. Reformed theology sees this as a model: not a fixed tithe under compulsion but Spirit-prompted, joyful abundance. The early church mirrors this in Acts 2:44-45 and 4:32-37. Where the gospel takes root, generosity follows as fruit of the Spirit.
Exod.40.2: On the first day of the first month shalt thou set up the tabernacle. New Year's day, the day of new beginnings, becomes the day of God's house being set up. The first month, Nisan, is also the Passover month. The tabernacle is set up on the anniversary of redemption: a fitting calendar. Reformed biblical theology sees here the inseparability of redemption and worship. God redeems in order to dwell with His people; He dwells with them by means of redemption. The two are one in His purpose.
Exod.40.33: So Moses finished the work. The verb echoes Genesis 2:1 (God ended his work) and anticipates John 19:30 (it is finished). The tabernacle is a new creation in miniature, finished by the appointed mediator. Christ finishes the work of redemption on the cross, the true tabernacle of God's dwelling with man. Reformed theology delights in these literary echoes: God works, finishes, and rests. Each finished work is a deposit toward the consummate finishing at the renewal of all things (Rev 21:6).
Exod.13.18: God led the people about, through the way of the wilderness of the Red sea. God's chosen route is not the shortest but the formative one. The wilderness is where Israel learns dependence, faith, and identity. Reformed theology sees the wilderness as paradigmatic for Christian sanctification: God leads us not by the easiest path but by the path that forms us. The way to glory passes through tribulation (Acts 14:22). Hebrews 11 lists the heroes of faith who walked by faith through wilderness paths.
Exod.16.10: They looked toward the wilderness, and, behold, the glory of the LORD appeared in the cloud. Even Israel's grumbling does not silence God's glory. He answers complaint with manna and quail and self-disclosure. Reformed theology sees God's patience as covenantal: He bears with His people because of the covenant of grace, not because of their merit. The glory in the cloud is the same glory that will fill the tabernacle (40:34) and ultimately rest in Christ (John 1:14). The wilderness is glory's stage.
Exod.18.11: Now I know that the LORD is greater than all gods. Jethro's confession, after hearing the exodus story. The Gentile priest of Midian recognizes Yahweh's supremacy. Reformed missiology sees this as a foreshadowing of the gathering of the nations to Israel's God. Even now, the gospel of redemption brings Jethros from every corner to confess Yahweh. The exodus narrative is itself missionary: the great deeds of God draw outsiders to faith. Christ's resurrection has the same drawing power: he draws all peoples unto Himself (John 12:32).
Exod.19.9: Lo, I come unto thee in a thick cloud, that the people may hear when I speak with thee, and believe thee for ever. The cloud at Sinai is mediating darkness: God is present but veiled, lest Israel be consumed. The veil also protects Moses' authority: Israel will hear God speak to him, and trust him. Reformed theology sees here the divine condescension: God accommodates Himself to what creatures can bear. Christ is the supreme accommodation, God veiled in flesh, that we might draw near and not be destroyed.
Exod.19.12: Set bounds unto the people round about, saying, Take heed to yourselves, that ye go not up into the mount, or touch the border of it. The mountain is fenced; encroachment means death. Holiness has perimeters. Reformed worship has long honored this principle of access: not all may approach without mediation. The new covenant opens access through Christ (Heb 10:19), but does not abolish the principle of holiness. We come boldly, but with reverence and godly fear (Heb 12:28-29).
Exod.21.30: If there be laid on him a sum of money, then he shall give for the ransom of his life. The principle of ransom (kopher) appears in the case law: a life under capital sentence may be redeemed by payment, in cases not involving murder. The principle is foundational for atonement theology: redemption requires a price. Christ gave Himself a ransom for many (Mark 10:45). Reformed atonement theology emphasizes the actual payment of a real price: not a symbolic gesture but the precious blood of the Son.
Exod.22.29: The firstborn of thy sons shalt thou give unto me. The firstborn principle is reaffirmed: every firstborn son belongs to God by the Passover purchase. The redemption money commutes the obligation. Reformed covenant theology sees in the firstborn principle the seeds of substitutionary atonement: the firstborn dies (or is ransomed), so the rest may live. Christ, the firstborn of all creation and the firstborn from the dead, is the one in whom this principle is supremely fulfilled.
Exod.23.16: The feast of harvest, the firstfruits of thy labours... the feast of ingathering, which is in the end of the year. The annual feasts (Passover, Weeks/Pentecost, Tabernacles) structure the agricultural and redemptive year. Each feast is fulfilled in Christ: Passover in His death, Pentecost in the Spirit's outpouring, Tabernacles in the consummation when God dwells with His people. Reformed biblical theology sees the feasts as a redemptive calendar pointing forward to Christ. The pattern of firstfruits anticipates Christ as the firstfruits of resurrection (1 Cor 15:20).
Exod.24.4: Moses wrote all the words of the LORD, and rose up early in the morning, and builded an altar under the hill, and twelve pillars, according to the twelve tribes. Twelve pillars represent the twelve tribes; the altar represents God. The covenant ceremony brings God and people into relationship, but blood mediates between altar and pillars. Reformed covenantal theology sees the covenant always mediated by blood. The twelve tribes will reappear as twelve apostles, twelve foundations of the New Jerusalem (Rev 21:14), the consummate covenant community.
Exod.24.16: The glory of the LORD abode upon mount Sinai, and the cloud covered it six days: and the seventh day he called unto Moses out of the midst of the cloud. The pattern of six days plus the seventh recalls creation. Sinai is a new creation event, the giving of covenant to a new humanity. The seventh day call is the call of glory. Reformed theology sees here a pattern that runs through Scripture: God works through six and reveals on the seventh. The Lord's Day, the eighth day of new creation, fulfills this pattern in Christ's resurrection.
Exod.25.21: Thou shalt put the mercy seat above upon the ark; and in the ark thou shalt put the testimony that I shall give thee. The structure preaches the gospel: the law within, the mercy seat above. The broken law cries out for judgment; the blood-sprinkled mercy seat covers it. So at the cross, the law's demands are not abolished but covered by Christ's atoning blood. Reformed soteriology insists that justice is fully satisfied (the law remains in the ark) and mercy is fully extended (the kapporet covers it). Both stand together in Christ.
Exod.27.21: Aaron and his sons shall order it from evening to morning before the LORD. The lampstand is tended continually. Light in the holy place never goes out. Reformed worship recognizes the necessity of continuing means of grace: the Word preached, the sacraments administered, prayer offered. The light does not maintain itself; it must be tended. Christ, the Light of the world, gives the Spirit who keeps the church's light burning across the ages, until the day breaks and shadows flee.
Exod.28.41: Thou shalt put them upon Aaron thy brother, and his sons with him; and shalt anoint them, and consecrate them, and sanctify them, that they may minister unto me in the priest's office. The triple act of anointing, consecrating, sanctifying establishes the priest. Each verb has distinct nuance: anoint (set apart by oil), consecrate (fill the hand, mille yad), sanctify (make holy). Reformed theology sees here the comprehensive separation of the priest unto God. Christ is anointed by the Spirit, His hands filled by the Father with all the offerings, and He is the Holy One of God (Mark 1:24).
Exod.29.42: This shall be a continual burnt offering throughout your generations at the door of the tabernacle of the congregation before the LORD. The morning and evening tamid sacrifices framed Israel's daily life. Worship was structured by sacrifice. Reformed daily piety reflects this rhythm: morning and evening prayer, the household devotional pattern. Christ is the once-for-all offering (Heb 10:10), but the rhythm of daily approach to God in His name continues. The tamid trains us to live before God always.
Exod.30.18: Thou shalt also make a laver of brass... to wash withal. The bronze laver, made from the mirrors of the women (38:8), stands between the altar and the tent. Priests must wash before ministering. Reformed theology sees in this washing a picture of cleansing applied to those already redeemed: the disciples are clean already (John 13:10) but still need their feet washed. Christ washes us in His Word (Eph 5:26), continually applying the cleansing of His blood by the Spirit. Sanctification follows justification.
Exod.32.19: Moses' anger waxed hot, and he cast the tables out of his hands, and brake them beneath the mount. The breaking of the tables is symbolic: the covenant has been broken by Israel before Moses even reaches them. The shattering at the foot of Sinai mirrors the shattered covenant at the foot of the mountain. Reformed theology sees this as proof that no human can keep the covenant of works. Yet covenant grace renews: new tables are written, prefiguring the new covenant in Christ's blood, written not on stone but on hearts forever.
Exod.32.30: Ye have sinned a great sin: and now I will go up unto the LORD; peradventure I shall make an atonement for your sin. Moses' atonement is intercessory and aspirational. He cannot truly atone, but he goes as if to try. The peradventure is humble: Moses does not presume to dictate to God. Reformed theology sees here the limit of any merely human mediator: only the God-man can actually accomplish atonement. Christ goes up to the Father with no peradventure but with His own blood, securing eternal redemption (Heb 9:12).
Exod.33.13: If I have found grace in thy sight, shew me now thy way, that I may know thee. Moses' great prayer: not for blessing, not for ease, but to know God. This is the heart of Reformed piety. Calvin's Institutes opens with the inseparability of knowing God and knowing oneself. The deepest desire of the regenerate is theological in this experiential sense: to know God truly and personally. Christ defines eternal life as just this: that they might know thee the only true God, and Jesus Christ, whom thou hast sent (John 17:3).
Exod.34.5: The LORD descended in the cloud, and stood with him there, and proclaimed the name of the LORD. God Himself proclaims His own name. There is no higher authority on God than God. Reformed theology grounds theology in revelation: we know God only as He has revealed Himself. The proclamation that follows (34:6-7) is the most important self-disclosure in the Old Testament. Christ likewise reveals the Father (John 14:9): he who has seen me has seen the Father. The God of Exodus 34 is the God revealed in the face of Christ.
Exod.34.10: Behold, I make a covenant: before all thy people I will do marvels, such as have not been done in all the earth. The covenant renewal includes the promise of marvels. The God of covenant is the God of wonder. The conquest of Canaan, the rise of David, the building of the temple, all flow from this renewed covenant. Reformed theology sees God's covenant fidelity confirmed by mighty acts in history. The supreme marvel is the resurrection of Christ, the wonder by which all the promises are confirmed yes and amen (2 Cor 1:20).
Exod.35.21: They came, every one whose heart stirred him up, and every one whom his spirit made willing. The Spirit moves hearts to give. There is no coercion in the construction of God's house. Reformed theology celebrates this: the Spirit produces willing service in His people. The new covenant promise is a heart of flesh (Ezek 36:26) that delights to obey. Tabernacle giving foreshadows kingdom service: every gift truly God-pleasing arises from Spirit-prompted willingness. The widow's mite (Mark 12:42) and the spices of the women at the tomb (Mark 16:1) flow from the same Spirit.
Exod.39.32: Thus was all the work of the tabernacle of the tent of the congregation finished: and the children of Israel did according to all that the LORD commanded Moses, so did they. Three times the chapter emphasizes obedience: as the LORD commanded Moses (39:1, 5, 7, 21, 26, 29, 31, 32). The pattern is set: God commands, Israel obeys, the work is finished. This is covenantal religion. Reformed theology esteems detailed obedience to God's revealed will. Christ likewise does all that the Father commands (John 17:4), accomplishing the true tabernacle work.
Ezek.1.1: The book opens with a date, a place, and an opened heaven. Ezekiel, exiled by the Chebar canal in Babylon, receives 'visions of God' (mar'oth Elohim) on foreign soil. Reformed exegesis insists this is sovereign condescension: the Lord pursues His covenant people into captivity, refusing to be domesticated within the geography of Jerusalem. The opened heavens anticipate the heavens torn at Christ's baptism (Mark 1:10) and Stephen's gaze in Acts 7. Where the throne is, there is the Holy Land. God's presence is not bound by temple walls, a foretaste of John 4:21-24.
Ezek.1.4: The stormy wind (ruach se'arah) from the north and the brightness round about announce theophany. The 'north' was the conventional direction of Babylon's invasion, yet here the Lord comes from that quarter, claiming sovereign authority over the very direction of judgment. The fire enfolding itself recalls Sinai (Exod 19) and Isaiah's burning seraphim. Calvin notes that God uses terrifying imagery because exile dulls the soul; spiritual sight must be jolted awake. The chashmal (amber/gleaming metal) refuses simple definition, restraining all idolatrous attempts to picture God.
Ezek.1.28: The kabod (glory) of the LORD, framed by the rainbow of Genesis 9, moves the prophet from speech to prostration. The covenant bow, sign of mercy after judgment, surrounds the throne so that wrath never escapes covenant faithfulness. 'The likeness of the appearance' (demuth ke-mar'eh) is fourfold removed from direct description, guarding divine transcendence. Reformed theology grounds preaching here: the prophet falls before he speaks. Revelation 4 borrows wholesale from this scene, with the slain Lamb at the center. Christ is the consummation of this glory (John 1:14, Heb 1:3). True ministry begins in the dust.
Ezek.2.1: 'Son of man' (ben-adam) is used over ninety times, marking Ezekiel as a representative human standing under the towering glory just seen. The title underscores creaturely smallness even as the Lord lifts him to his feet by the Spirit. Jesus claims this title (Mark 2:10), gathering Ezekiel's role into His own messianic office. Reformed preaching learns here that the man God uses is the man God first humbles, then animates. Standing is not by self-effort but by ruach. The minister is a son of dust who speaks for the Ancient of Days.
Ezek.2.3: The commission targets a 'rebellious nation' (goy ha-mordim), the covenant people described with a Gentile word, exposing how far they have fallen. Israel is no longer treated as 'am' (people) but as another 'goy.' This is the theology of covenant breach: privilege does not exempt from indictment. The faithful pastor today must address even church members as potential rebels needing repentance. The Lord's persistence ('to this very day') displays patience that funds judgment. Ezekiel's audience is hard, but God still sends the word, anticipating the gospel's promiscuous proclamation despite predictable rejection.
Ezek.2.7: Success is not measured by reception. 'Whether they hear or refuse' Ezekiel must speak. This is liberating doctrine for the discouraged minister: faithfulness, not fruitfulness, is the immediate command. Calvin: the prophet must scatter seed knowing the soil is largely stone. The rebellion of the hearer never excuses the silence of the herald. This anticipates Paul's 'aroma of Christ' which is life or death depending on the recipient (2 Cor 2:15-16). Ezekiel models a doctrine of preaching that survives every generation's cultural hostility.
Ezek.3.3: The scroll, full of lamentation, mourning, and woe, must first be eaten. The minister must internalize judgment before declaring it. Sweetness in the mouth despite bitter content shows that obedience to God's word, even hard word, satisfies the soul. Jeremiah ate the words and rejoiced (Jer 15:16); John ate the scroll bitter in the belly (Rev 10:9). The pattern: God's truth digested becomes the preacher's substance. Reformed homiletics rejects the dispenser model; the herald is first a hearer who has chewed the text until it tastes like manna.
Ezek.3.17: The watchman (tsofeh) image governs Ezekiel's ministry and provides the template for pastoral accountability. Blood guilt for unwarned sinners falls on the silent watchman. This passage, with 33:1-9, undergirds the seriousness of preaching every counsel of God (Acts 20:26-27). Reformed elders are charged to watch souls 'as those who must give account' (Heb 13:17). The doctrine is not legalistic but covenantal: the watchman represents the covenant Lord who refuses to let sinners perish without warning. Common grace warning is a means of saving grace.
Ezek.4.1: The sign-act of besieging a brick depicting Jerusalem turns the prophet's body into a sermon. Word and sacrament-like enactment combine; the dramatic action makes the abstract concrete. This anticipates the incarnational fullness of preaching: not merely concepts but visible signs (cf. baptism and Supper). The 390 days of left-side lying picture Israel's iniquity, the 40 days the right side, Judah's. Ezekiel becomes a living parable, prefiguring how Christ Himself will become the embodied Word, bearing on His body the sins of His people.
Ezek.5.11: Because Israel defiled the sanctuary, the Lord will Himself diminish them. The threefold 'I will' marks divine action that will not be withheld. The pollution of worship is the cardinal offense; idolatry in the temple corrupts the very heart of the covenant. Reformed worship is jealous worship; what the Lord prescribes is precise. The promise here, fearful as it is, ultimately drives toward the cross where Christ bears the diminishment, dispersing of life so His people might be gathered.
Ezek.6.9: Even amid scattering, 'they that escape shall remember me.' Judgment is not the final word; it is the chastening rod that produces self-loathing repentance. The remnant doctrine emerges: a people preserved through fire who turn from idols to the living God. Calvin sees here the fruit of effectual grace; remembrance is not natural but granted. The shattered idolatrous heart finally sees the broken heart of God ('I am broken with their whorish heart'). The new covenant will provide the grace to remember and turn (Jer 31, Ezek 36).
Ezek.7.19: Silver and gold cannot deliver in the day of wrath. Mammon, idolized as security, becomes refuse. This anticipates 1 Peter 1:18 ('not redeemed with corruptible things'). The fall of trust in wealth is itself a mercy, exposing the soul's true poverty. Reformed economics: every treasure not Christ becomes ash on judgment day. The text also drives toward the gospel where redemption is by precious blood. The dross of the world's wealth makes the gold tried by fire (Rev 3:18) the only worthy purchase.
Ezek.8.6: The Spirit transports Ezekiel to Jerusalem to expose hidden idolatry. 'Greater abominations' progressively unveil: image of jealousy, secret chambered idolatry, women weeping for Tammuz, sun worship at the temple gate. The chapter's cumulative force is that the temple itself is profaned. The Lord sees what darkness conceals (Heb 4:13). Reformed pastoral theology reads chambers of the heart in the same light: there is no secret sin God does not see. Christ's atonement reaches into those very chambers, cleansing what no human eye perceived.
Ezek.9.4: The mark (taw, the last Hebrew letter, originally a cross-shape) on the foreheads of those who sigh and groan over abominations is a sign of preservation amid judgment. The doctrine of distinguishing grace stands forth: God knows His own, and judgment passes over the marked. This anticipates Passover blood, baptismal sign, and the sealing of Revelation 7. Sighing over corporate sin is not weakness but evidence of regeneration. The cross-shape of the taw is providentially suggestive: only those marked by the cross of Christ are spared the wrath their sins deserve.
Ezek.10.18: The kabod departs from the threshold, a movement begun in chapter 9 and completed in chapter 11. Ichabod (1 Sam 4:21) writ large. The departure is gradual, indicating divine reluctance; God leaves the temple as one tearing Himself from a beloved place. This is the theological catastrophe of the exile: not just political fall but the loss of the manifest presence. The recovery of glory awaits the incarnation, when 'the Word became flesh and tabernacled' (John 1:14), the kabod returning in the person of Christ.
Ezek.11.23: The glory rests on the Mount of Olives east of the city, the same mount from which Christ ascended (Acts 1) and to which He will return (Zech 14:4). The departure is paused over this very location, a theological and topographical foreshadowing. The Lord does not abandon utterly; He withdraws to a vantage from which He will, in due time, return in the flesh of His Son. The Mount of Olives binds Old and New Testaments in a single redemptive geography.
Ezek.12.2: The rebellious house has eyes that do not see and ears that do not hear, the same indictment Jesus quotes (Matt 13:13). Spiritual blindness is moral, not merely intellectual; it is the natural state of the unregenerate heart. Only the new heart of 11:19 and 36:26 unstops ears and opens eyes. Reformed doctrine of total depravity is no abstraction; it is exegesis of texts like this. The remedy is the Spirit's sovereign work, not louder preaching alone.
Ezek.13.10: False prophets daub the wall with untempered mortar, crying 'Peace' where there is no peace. Such ministry collapses under the storm of judgment. The chapter exposes ministerial malpractice: comforting where God condemns, smoothing what God exposes. Reformed preaching refuses cheap peace, knowing only the peace through the blood of the cross is real (Col 1:20). Faithful ministers must rebuild walls with the lime of truth, not the whitewash of pleasantries.
Ezek.14.14: Even Noah, Daniel, and Job, paragons of righteousness, could deliver only their own souls in such a corrupted generation. The doctrine of personal accountability is sharpened: covenant family does not save covenant rebels. Yet by contrast, the greater than Noah-Daniel-Job, Christ Jesus, delivers many by His one righteousness (Rom 5:19). The very inability of these heroes to save others highlights the unique mediation of the Son.
Ezek.15.2: The vine of Israel has no use except to bear fruit; if barren, it is fit only for fire. The image anticipates John 15: Christ is the true vine in whom His people abide and bear fruit. Apart from Him, branches wither (John 15:6). Reformed soteriology reads this as the necessity of union with Christ. National Israel as vine fails; the messianic Vine succeeds, and engrafting into Him by faith is the only salvation.
Ezek.16.6: 'And when I passed by thee, and saw thee polluted in thine own blood, I said unto thee... Live.' Sovereign electing love seizes the abandoned infant. The verb 'live' (chayi) is the divine fiat that creates spiritual life from nothing. This is the imagery of unconditional election: not because Israel was lovable, but because God loved. Augustine and Calvin both treasured this verse. The Father's word over a soul dead in sin is the same: live. By the same word, Lazarus came forth (John 11:43-44).
Ezek.16.60: Despite Israel's harlotry, the Lord remembers His covenant 'in the days of thy youth' and establishes an 'everlasting covenant' (berith olam). Covenant memory is divine, not merely human, and it grounds the persistence of grace. The everlasting covenant is fulfilled in Christ's blood (Heb 13:20). Ezekiel's logic is unsparing about sin yet stunningly tenacious about grace: precisely because the wife is unworthy, the husband's faithfulness magnifies the gospel.
Ezek.17.22: The Lord will plant a tender twig from the high cedar on a high mountain, and it will become a noble cedar sheltering every kind of bird. Messianic prophecy: the tender shoot is the Branch (Isa 11:1, Jer 23:5). The kingdom grows from a single sprout into a tree of refuge for all nations. Jesus' parables of the mustard seed (Matt 13:31-32) draw directly from this Ezekielian image. The Davidic line, seemingly lopped, will produce the cosmic kingdom.
Ezek.18.4: 'The soul that sinneth, it shall die.' The categorical statement asserts personal responsibility against the proverb that fathers' sour grapes set children's teeth on edge. Yet the apostle does not abolish federal headship (Rom 5); rather Ezekiel rebukes fatalistic deflection. Each soul is accountable, and the gospel offers each soul the imputed righteousness of the Last Adam. Sin's wage is death (Rom 6:23); the answer is not blame-shifting but turning to the Mediator who alone can pay.
Ezek.18.20: The chapter's central principle: 'The son shall not bear the iniquity of the father.' This is forensic individualism within covenant. It defends divine justice against accusation and prepares for the gospel paradox: while no son rightly bears another's guilt, the sinless Son willingly bears ours (Isa 53:6, 2 Cor 5:21). What is not legally transferable becomes lovingly substituted in Christ. The chapter does not deny corporate sin but denies fatalistic excuse.
Ezek.18.32: 'Wherefore turn yourselves, and live ye.' The Lord 'has no pleasure in the death of him that dieth.' This expresses God's revealed will (voluntas signi) to all hearers: He commands repentance and offers life. Reformed theology distinguishes secret and revealed will without compromising sincerity of the call. The same God who decrees the end uses the means of free offer. Calvin: God speaks here as a physician, urging the patient to take the cure. The verse is a warm gospel summons inside a stern legal chapter.
Ezek.19.14: The lament for the princes ends with no scepter to rule. The Davidic line appears extinguished. Yet the lament is itself prophetic: only when the human dynasty is reduced to nothing does the divine Davidic king arise. The seemingly dead stump of Jesse will yield the Branch (Isa 11). Ezekiel's funeral song over the kings is, in covenantal logic, an Advent anticipation.
Ezek.20.12: 'I gave them my sabbaths, to be a sign between me and them.' The Sabbath is a covenant marker, anticipating the eternal rest of Hebrews 4. Israel's Sabbath-breaking is not mere ritual failure but rejection of covenant identity. The new covenant fulfills sabbath rest in Christ, who is Lord of the Sabbath (Mark 2:28) and gives rest to the weary (Matt 11:28). Reformed sabbatarianism reads this as enduring witness to covenant grace, now centered on the resurrection day.
Ezek.20.33: 'As I live, surely with a mighty hand... will I rule over you.' Even rebellion will not unseat divine sovereignty. The Lord will purge the rebels (v. 38) and bring His covenant people through wilderness judgment to face His face. This is sanctifying severity. Reformed soteriology: the Shepherd separates and refines. The remnant brought through is the church purchased and led by Christ.
Ezek.21.27: 'I will overturn, overturn, overturn it: and it shall be no more, until he come whose right it is.' Threefold overturning marks total dynastic collapse. Yet the messianic clause is decisive: the throne will remain vacant until the rightful King comes. Genesis 49:10 echoes here. The verse points forward to Christ, the true heir of David, whose kingdom shall not be shaken (Heb 12:28). The triple overturning of human pretensions clears the stage for the Son of David.
Ezek.22.30: 'I sought for a man among them, that should make up the hedge, and stand in the gap before me for the land, that I should not destroy it; but I found none.' The need for a mediator is exposed. Where Moses interceded (Ps 106:23), now no one stands. The verse drives the reader to long for the only true gap-stander, Jesus Christ, the one Mediator (1 Tim 2:5), who 'ever liveth to make intercession' (Heb 7:25). The desolation of human mediation is the necessity of divine.
Ezek.23.49: After the wrenching parable of Oholah and Oholibah, Ezekiel concludes: 'Ye shall know that I am the Lord God.' The recurring formula crowns chapter after chapter. Spiritual adultery does not erase divine identity; it occasions its public vindication. Reformed theology: God's name is finally sanctified through judgment as well as through mercy. The cross is where both meet: there sin is judged and the Lord is known.
Ezek.24.16: 'Behold, I take away from thee the desire of thine eyes with a stroke.' The death of Ezekiel's wife becomes a sign-act for the loss of the temple, the desire of Israel's eyes. The prophet's personal grief is conscripted for prophetic ministry. Pastoral cost is real. The verse anticipates the Father's giving of His own beloved Son, the desire of His eyes, that His people might live. No grief in ministry is purposeless when grafted into Christ's grief.
Ezek.25.14: 'I will lay my vengeance upon Edom by the hand of my people Israel.' The oracles against the nations (chs. 25-32) declare the Lord's universal lordship; He judges Ammon, Moab, Edom, Philistia, Tyre, Sidon, Egypt. Reformed theology insists no nation is autonomous. The vengeance promised here finds ultimate execution at the cross (where wrath is borne) and the parousia (where wrath is finally administered). Christ is Lord of all peoples.
Ezek.26.14: 'I will make thee like the top of a rock... thou shalt be built no more.' Tyre's pride collapses. The detailed prophecy, fulfilled progressively through Nebuchadnezzar and Alexander, attests divine omniscience. Reformed apologetics highlights such fulfilled prophecy as evidence of inspiration. Tyre's commercial idolatry mirrors modern Babylon (Rev 18). The downfall of every economic Babel makes way for the city whose builder is God.
Ezek.27.36: Tyre, the merchant city of song, becomes a horror. The dirge of chapter 27 anticipates the fall of Babylon the Great in Revelation 18, with merchants weeping over fallen wealth. Reformed economic ethics: trade unmoored from righteousness invites judgment. Cities built on commerce alone end as 'horrors,' a sobering word in any commercial age. The believer trades for the pearl of great price (Matt 13:46).
Ezek.28.14: The lament over the king of Tyre, set in Eden as 'the anointed cherub that covereth,' has long been read by many Reformers as glimpsing satanic pride behind earthly tyrants. Whether read literally of the Tyrian king or typologically, the trajectory is from glory to fire. Pride is the root sin (1 Tim 3:6). The anointed cherub fell; the anointed Christ humbled Himself and was exalted (Phil 2). Two trajectories, two destinies.
Ezek.29.21: 'In that day will I cause the horn of the house of Israel to bud forth.' Amid Egypt's downfall, a budding horn for Israel: a small but messianic sign. Horn-language anchors at Luke 1:69 ('a horn of salvation in the house of his servant David'). The fall of Egypt and the rise of the horn are one redemptive pattern: pagan power humbled, Davidic strength raised in the Christ.
Ezek.30.3: 'The day is near, even the day of the Lord is near.' The yom Yahweh extends from local Egypt-judgment to cosmic eschaton. The prophets telescope: every historical day of the Lord rehearses the final one. 1 Thessalonians 5 picks up the same vocabulary. Reformed eschatology reads history sacramentally; each judgment is a foretaste of the Last.
Ezek.31.3: Assyria likened to a cedar of Lebanon, beautiful and exalted, fell because of pride. Empire after empire receives the same indictment. Daniel 4 will retell it of Babylon. The lesson: every Babel-tower of human glory is felled. The only cedar that endures is the Branch from Jesse's stump, the Servant who emptied Himself.
Ezek.32.7: 'I will cover the heaven, and make the stars thereof dark.' Cosmic imagery for political collapse appears throughout the prophets and is reused by Jesus in Matthew 24. Eschatological language is ordinarily metaphorical for kingdom transitions, but it telescopes finally into actual cosmic judgment. The end of Pharaoh prefigures the end of every Pharaoh-figure, until the Lamb sits on the throne and there is no need of sun or moon (Rev 21:23).
Ezek.33.11: 'As I live, saith the Lord God, I have no pleasure in the death of the wicked; but that the wicked turn from his way and live: turn ye, turn ye from your evil ways; for why will ye die, O house of Israel?' This is the clearest gospel summons in Ezekiel. The triple 'turn' is urgent and tender. Reformed theology distinguishes God's secret decree from His revealed will (Deut 29:29); here is the revealed will, sincere and pressing. Calvin treats the verse as the heart of God preached. Sinners are commanded to repent, and the command itself, accompanied by the Spirit, is the means of life.
Ezek.33.7: The watchman commission is reissued, doubled in solemnity. Pastoral ministry must warn even when warning seems futile. The blood-guilt clause makes preaching a matter of life and death. Acts 20:26-27 echoes deliberately. The faithful watchman declares the whole counsel of God; silence is treason against souls.
Ezek.34.4: The shepherds of Israel feed themselves while neglecting the sick, weak, and lost. Pastoral malpractice condemned. Reformed eldership accountability flows from this passage. The chapter is the great prophetic charter for Christ's pastoral self-disclosure in John 10. Where shepherds fail, the Shepherd-King will come Himself.
Ezek.34.16: 'I will seek that which was lost.' Luke 19:10 quotes this Ezekielian voice in Jesus' mouth: 'The Son of Man came to seek and to save the lost.' The Lord Himself becomes shepherd. The verbs are sovereign: I will seek, bring back, bind up, strengthen. Effectual grace, not mere offer. The Shepherd's initiative is the gospel.
Ezek.35.15: Mount Seir's desolation is permanent because of perpetual hatred toward Jacob. Edom's enmity stands as a type of unending opposition to God's people, ultimately judged. By contrast, Christ has loved 'with an everlasting love' (Jer 31:3). Edom and Israel become two communities, two destinies, two trajectories of grace and rejection.
Ezek.36.22: 'Not for your sakes do I this... but for mine holy name's sake.' The motive of restoration is doxological, not anthropocentric. Augustine and Calvin draw deeply here: salvation is finally about God's glory. The unconditional election is unconditional precisely because it terminates on God's name. This guards grace against being confused with merit.
Ezek.36.27: 'And I will put my Spirit (ruchi) within you, and cause you to walk in my statutes.' Indwelling Spirit produces obedience. The new covenant pairs new heart with the abiding Paraclete (John 14:17). Sanctification is not bootstrap morality but Spirit-empowered walking. Romans 8:4 echoes the cause-and-effect: the righteous requirement of the law is fulfilled in those who walk by the Spirit.
Ezek.36.32: Repeated assertion: 'Not for your sakes do I this.' Shame is the proper response to grace; not despair, but the humility that magnifies the giver. Reformed piety cultivates this holy shame. Where merit is excluded, worship begins.
Ezek.37.3: 'Son of man, can these bones live?' The valley of dry bones poses the soteriological question. Ezekiel's answer, 'O Lord God, thou knowest,' is faith refusing both presumption and despair. The bones, very dry, are the church-in-exile and the human heart in death. Only divine power answers the question. The chapter is the OT's clearest picture of resurrection.
Ezek.37.5: 'Behold, I will cause breath (ruach) to enter into you, and ye shall live.' The ruach pun is rich: spirit, breath, wind. Genesis 2:7 echoes; new creation parallels first creation. The same ruach that hovered over the deep raises the dead. John 20:22, where the risen Christ breathes on the disciples, fulfills Ezekiel's pattern. Resurrection is by the Spirit's pneumatic work.
Ezek.37.10: 'And the breath came into them, and they lived, and stood up upon their feet, an exceeding great army.' Resurrection is corporate. The army is the church gathered out of death. Reformed eschatology: the bodily resurrection of the saints is the consummation of regeneration (Rom 8:11). Even now, the Spirit raises souls dead in trespasses (Eph 2:5).
Ezek.37.14: 'I shall put my spirit in you, and ye shall live, and I shall place you in your own land... I have spoken it, and performed it.' Promise and performance. The new covenant's effectual word brings resurrection-life and inheritance. Hebrews 11 reads inheritance ultimately as the better country. The bones live, the people return, and ultimately the church inherits the new earth.
Ezek.37.24: 'And David my servant shall be king over them; and they all shall have one shepherd.' One shepherd, one flock, one Davidic king: the messianic kingdom is unified, not fragmented. The Christ as the Son of David rules an undivided people, Jew and Gentile (Eph 2:14-16). Ecclesial unity is rooted not in human consensus but in the headship of Christ.
Ezek.37.26: 'I will make a covenant of peace with them; it shall be an everlasting covenant.' Berith shalom, berith olam. Peace and permanence. The new covenant in Christ's blood (Luke 22:20) is the realization. Reformed covenant theology sees one covenant of grace progressively administered, finally ratified in Christ. The 'everlasting' anchors assurance: what God establishes, He sustains.
Ezek.38.2: Gog of the land of Magog, prince of Rosh, Meshech, and Tubal, gathers a coalition from the far north. Interpretations vary, but Reformed exegesis (e.g., Calvin, the Westminster divines) typically reads Gog symbolically as the eschatological assemblage of God-opposing nations, fulfilled in the final assault before Christ's return (Rev 20:8). The text is more theological than geopolitical: the gathered foe is the climactic enemy of the people of God.
Ezek.38.16: 'I will be sanctified in thee, O Gog, before their eyes.' The very assault of Gog becomes the stage for divine vindication. God uses the nations' rage to display His holiness. Psalm 2 logic. The wrath of man becomes the praise of God (Ps 76:10). Eschatology is doxology.
Ezek.38.23: 'Thus will I magnify myself, and sanctify myself; and I will be known in the eyes of many nations, and they shall know that I am the Lord.' The recognition formula reaches its eschatological zenith. The end of history is the universal acknowledgment of the covenant Lord. Philippians 2:10-11 fulfills: every knee bows, every tongue confesses Jesus as Lord.
Ezek.39.7: 'So will I make my holy name known in the midst of my people Israel; and I will not let them pollute my holy name any more.' Permanent sanctification of the name. The new covenant church will never again profane the divine name as the old Israel did, because the Spirit indwells and Christ has consecrated us. Hebrews 10:14 anchors: 'by one offering he hath perfected for ever them that are sanctified.'
Ezek.39.25: 'Now will I bring again the captivity of Jacob, and have mercy upon the whole house of Israel, and will be jealous for my holy name.' Mercy and jealousy meet. Restoration is full-house, comprehensive. Romans 11 reads the future of ethnic Israel along these lines. Reformed expositors differ on details, but the trajectory is grace triumphant.
Ezek.39.29: 'Neither will I hide my face any more from them: for I have poured out my Spirit upon the house of Israel.' Pentecost language. Joel 2 echoes. The outpouring of the Spirit is the sign of consummated covenant fellowship. Acts 2 fulfills typologically; the new heavens and new earth fulfill finally. The hidden face becomes the beatific vision (1 Cor 13:12, Rev 22:4).
Ezek.40.2: 'In the visions of God brought he me into the land of Israel, and set me upon a very high mountain, by which was as the frame of a city.' The temple vision begins from a high mountain, recalling Sinai and anticipating Revelation 21:10 ('the holy Jerusalem... from a great and high mountain'). Reformed interpretation generally reads chapters 40-48 not as a literal millennial blueprint but as a typological vision of the eschatological dwelling of God with His people, fulfilled in Christ and consummated in the new creation.
Ezek.40.4: 'Declare all that thou seest to the house of Israel.' The visionary is also herald. Apocalyptic vision becomes pastoral comfort. Calvin: the symbolic temple is given to sustain hope in exile, picturing what God will do. The function is pastoral; the content is christological in fulfillment. The minister conveys vision to the dispersed.
Ezek.41.4: The most holy place, a perfect cube, prefigures Revelation 21:16, where the New Jerusalem is itself a cube. The whole city becomes the holy of holies because the Lord and the Lamb are its temple (Rev 21:22). Ezekiel's geometry is theological: God's dwelling expands until creation is the sanctuary. Reformed eschatology embraces this temple-cosmos trajectory.
Ezek.42.13: 'They are holy chambers, where the priests... shall eat the most holy things.' Priestly provision is preserved in the vision. In the new covenant, all believers are priests (1 Pet 2:9), and the most holy things are the body and blood of Christ received in faith. The visionary chambers anticipate the church's communion at the Lord's table.
Ezek.43.2: 'Behold, the glory of the God of Israel came from the way of the east.' The kabod returns from the very direction it departed (10:18-19, 11:23). What was lost is restored. Christ enters Jerusalem from the east at the triumphal entry, ascends from the eastern Mount of Olives, and will return from the east (Matt 24:27). The compass rose of redemption.
Ezek.43.7: 'The place of my throne, and the place of the soles of my feet, where I will dwell in the midst of the children of Israel for ever.' Permanent dwelling. The new covenant promise of Immanuel is anticipated. Revelation 21:3 fulfills: 'the tabernacle of God is with men.' Eternal indwelling is the goal of all redemptive history.
Ezek.43.10: 'Shew the house to the house of Israel, that they may be ashamed of their iniquities.' The vision of holiness is intended to produce repentance. Beholding the glory of God exposes sin. Isaiah 6 follows the same pattern. The purpose of the temple vision is not architectural curiosity but ecclesial sanctification.
Ezek.44.2: 'This gate shall be shut... because the Lord, the God of Israel, hath entered in by it.' The eastern gate is sealed because the King has passed through. Some early Christian interpreters saw a Marian or christological figure here. More soundly: the entrance of the kabod sanctifies the gate; what God has filled, no creature trifles with. Reformed worship: the Lord's coming into His sanctuary marks it off as holy.
Ezek.44.23: 'And they shall teach my people the difference between the holy and profane, and cause them to discern between the unclean and the clean.' Pastoral ministry's didactic core: distinguishing categories. Reformed worship and ethics insist on the categories the world dissolves. The minister discerns and teaches discernment. Hebrews 5:14 picks up the theme.
Ezek.45.17: The prince offers sacrifices for the people, picturing royal-priestly mediation. The prince in Ezekiel's vision is not a perfect Messiah-figure (he offers for himself, 45:22), yet the office foreshadows the King-Priest after Melchizedek's order. Christ alone perfectly fulfills both offices, offering Himself once for all (Heb 7:27).
Ezek.46.9: 'He that entereth in by the way of the north gate to worship shall go forth by the way of the south gate.' Worship has direction and order. Disciplined movement, not chaos. Reformed regulative worship treasures structure. The pattern of orderly procession anticipates the church's liturgical life and the great procession of Revelation 7.
Ezek.47.1: 'Behold, waters issued out from under the threshold of the house eastward.' The river of life flows from the temple, deepening as it goes: ankle, knee, loins, swimming-deep. John 7:38 ('out of his belly shall flow rivers of living water') and Revelation 22:1 ('a pure river of water of life... proceeding out of the throne of God and of the Lamb') fulfill. Christ Himself is the source; the Spirit is the river. The deepening is the believer's progressive immersion in grace.
Ezek.47.9: 'Every thing shall live whither the river cometh.' Wherever the gospel-river flows, life. Even the Dead Sea is healed. Reformed missiology and eschatology: the church's mission carries this river to the nations, and the consummation will heal all that the curse has poisoned (Rev 22:2-3).
Ezek.47.12: 'The leaf thereof for medicine.' Trees beside the river bear monthly fruit and healing leaves. Revelation 22:2 takes the image directly: 'the leaves of the tree were for the healing of the nations.' Eden restored, surpassed. The garden-temple-city converge. Christ, the true tree of life, supplies both fruit and healing.
Ezek.48.20: The holy district, square and consecrated, anchors the new land. Geometry once again expresses theology: order, holiness, centrality of the Lord's portion. The land itself is reorganized around worship. Reformed sociology reads this as worship's centripetal force in the new creation: all life ordered to God's presence.
Ezek.48.35: 'And the name of the city from that day shall be, The Lord is there (Yahweh Shammah).' The book ends not with a temple but with a city named for divine presence. Ichabod is reversed forever. Revelation 21-22 fulfills: the New Jerusalem has no temple because God and the Lamb are its temple. The whole biblical story arcs from God walking in the garden to God dwelling in the city. Yahweh Shammah is the gospel's last word: He is there, and where He is, His people are home.
Ezra.1.1: Now in the first year of Cyrus king of Persia, that the word of the LORD by the mouth of Jeremiah might be fulfilled, the LORD stirred up the spirit of Cyrus king of Persia, that he made a proclamation throughout all his kingdom. The book opens by attributing world history to the fulfillment of prophecy. The seventy years of Jeremiah have been counted in heaven and unfolded in earth. The same divine sovereignty that stirred up Pul the Assyrian to chasten now stirs up Cyrus the Persian to redeem. Reformed theology rejoices in this verse: God is Lord of empires and editor of decrees, and His Word does not return void.
Ezra.1.2: Thus saith Cyrus king of Persia, The LORD God of heaven hath given me all the kingdoms of the earth; and he hath charged me to build him an house at Jerusalem, which is in Judah. The pagan king confesses that the LORD has charged him with the building. Whether Cyrus understood this confession in monotheistic terms or syncretistically, the inspired historian records his words at face value. Isaiah 45 had named Cyrus a century and a half earlier as the LORD's anointed; the prophecy is now embodied.
Ezra.1.3: Who is there among you of all his people? his God be with him, and let him go up to Jerusalem, which is in Judah, and build the house of the LORD God of Israel, (he is the God,) the apposition (he is the God) is striking from a Persian king. The decree is essentially a missionary call: who will go up? The post-exilic community is constituted by those who answered the call.
Ezra.1.5: Then rose up the chief of the fathers of Judah and Benjamin, and the priests, and the Levites, with all them whose spirit God had raised, to go up to build the house of the LORD which is in Jerusalem. The same Spirit who stirred Cyrus stirs the returnees. Reformed soteriology recognizes here the doctrine of effectual call: God's stirring of the spirit produces a willing people in the day of His power. Not all Jews returned; only those whose spirits God had raised.
Ezra.1.7: Also Cyrus the king brought forth the vessels of the house of the LORD, which Nebuchadnezzar had brought forth out of Jerusalem, and had put them in the house of his gods. The temple vessels return. Daniel had witnessed Belshazzar profane these very vessels (Daniel 5); now they are restored. The Chronicler-Ezra historian has been tracking these vessels since 2 Chronicles 36, and their return is the visible sign of restored worship.
Ezra.2.1: Now these are the children of the province that went up out of the captivity, of those which had been carried away, whom Nebuchadnezzar the king of Babylon had carried away unto Babylon, and came again unto Jerusalem and Judah, every one unto his city. The list of returnees is preserved with the same care as the genealogies of Chronicles. Each name is a victory of grace, each family a flag planted in the land of promise. The list itself is theology.
Ezra.2.2: Which came with Zerubbabel: Jeshua, Nehemiah, Seraiah, Reelaiah, Mordecai, Bilshan, Mispar, Bigvai, Rehum, Baanah. The number of the men of the people of Israel. Zerubbabel and Jeshua, prince and priest, lead together. The pattern of paired civil and religious leadership prefigures the union of offices in Christ.
Ezra.2.59: And these were they which went up from Telmelah, Telharsa, Cherub, Addan, and Immer: but they could not shew their father's house, and their seed, whether they were of Israel. Some could not prove their lineage. The Chronicler-Ezra historian preserves their uncertainty without resolving it. The visible church of the Old Testament had always contained ambiguity at its margins, as does the visible church today.
Ezra.2.62: These sought their register among those that were reckoned by genealogy, but they were not found: therefore were they, as polluted, put from the priesthood. The priesthood requires documented descent. The Reformed principle of regulated worship insists that ministers be properly called and credentialed. Self-appointed priests are excluded.
Ezra.2.68: And some of the chief of the fathers, when they came to the house of the LORD which is at Jerusalem, offered freely for the house of God to set it up in his place. Voluntary giving builds the house. Reformed stewardship is grounded in the gladness of free offering, not the compulsion of taxation. The post-exilic temple, like the wilderness tabernacle, is built from willing hearts.
Ezra.3.2: Then stood up Jeshua the son of Jozadak, and his brethren the priests, and Zerubbabel the son of Shealtiel, and his brethren, and builded the altar of the God of Israel, to offer burnt offerings thereon, as it is written in the law of Moses the man of God. The altar is built before the temple. Sacrifice is restored even before the structure. The pattern is gospel-shaped: atonement first, then dwelling.
Ezra.3.3: And they set the altar upon his bases; for fear was upon them because of the people of those countries: and they offered burnt offerings thereon unto the LORD, even burnt offerings morning and evening. They sacrifice in fear. The presence of hostile neighbors does not delay the worship. The post-exilic community models a courage of liturgy that the church in every persecuted era has imitated.
Ezra.3.10: And when the builders laid the foundation of the temple of the LORD, they set the priests in their apparel with trumpets, and the Levites the sons of Asaph with cymbals, to praise the LORD, after the ordinance of David king of Israel. The Davidic order of worship is reinstituted. The continuity with David's pattern is theologically essential. The post-exilic temple is not a new religion but a recovery of the ancient one.
Ezra.3.11: And they sang together by course in praising and giving thanks unto the LORD; because he is good, for his mercy endureth for ever toward Israel. And all the people shouted with a great shout, when they praised the LORD, because the foundation of the house of the LORD was laid. The same refrain that opened Solomon's temple opens the second temple. The mercy that endures forever has indeed endured forever, through Babylon and back.
Ezra.3.12: But many of the priests and Levites and chief of the fathers, who were ancient men, that had seen the first house, when the foundation of this house was laid before their eyes, wept with a loud voice; and many shouted aloud for joy. Tears and shouts mingle. The older generation mourns what was lost; the younger rejoices in what is begun. Reformed pastoral theology cherishes this verse for its honesty about the complex emotions of restoration.
Ezra.3.13: So that the people could not discern the noise of the shout of joy from the noise of the weeping of the people: for the people shouted with a loud shout, and the noise was heard afar off. The mingled sound is the soundtrack of every true work of God in a fallen world. Joy at what is restored is never separable from grief at what was lost.
Ezra.4.1: Now when the adversaries of Judah and Benjamin heard that the children of the captivity builded the temple unto the LORD God of Israel. The opposition appears immediately. Reformed church history has documented the same pattern: the moment the church begins to build, opposition arises. Spiritual conflict is not a sign that one is off mission but often that one is on it.
Ezra.4.3: But Zerubbabel, and Jeshua, and the rest of the chief of the fathers of Israel, said unto them, Ye have nothing to do with us to build an house unto our God; but we ourselves together will build unto the LORD God of Israel, as king Cyrus the king of Persia hath commanded us. The refusal to admit syncretistic helpers is not exclusivism but covenantal fidelity. The temple cannot be built by those who do not confess the LORD alone. Reformed theology has always insisted on this distinction in the work of the church.
Ezra.4.4: Then the people of the land weakened the hands of the people of Judah, and troubled them in building. The opposition turns to discouragement. The same psychological warfare faces every generation of God's builders. Reformed pastors learn to recognize this pattern and exhort their flocks to persist.
Ezra.4.24: Then ceased the work of the house of God which is at Jerusalem. So it ceased unto the second year of the reign of Darius king of Persia. The work stops for sixteen years. The Chronicler-Ezra historian does not hide this failure. The post-exilic community knows itself to be a community of starts and stops, sustained only by divine perseverance.
Ezra.5.1: Then the prophets, Haggai the prophet, and Zechariah the son of Iddo, prophesied unto the Jews that were in Judah and Jerusalem in the name of the God of Israel, even unto them. The prophetic Word reignites the work. Haggai 1 and Zechariah 1 contain the very preaching that this verse summarizes. Reformed theology insists that nothing else but the Word preached can rouse a stalled people of God.
Ezra.5.2: Then rose up Zerubbabel the son of Shealtiel, and Jeshua the son of Jozadak, and began to build the house of God which is at Jerusalem: and with them were the prophets of God helping them. Civil ruler, priest, and prophets together rebuild the house. The triple cord of office is the visible structure of the post-exilic community.
Ezra.5.5: But the eye of their God was upon the elders of the Jews, that they could not cause them to cease, till the matter came to Darius: and then they returned answer by letter concerning this matter. The eye of God upon the elders is the providential canopy under which the work proceeds. Reformed pastoral encouragement returns to this image: the eye of the LORD is on His people, and no opposition can extinguish what He is doing.
Ezra.5.11: And thus they returned us answer, saying, We are the servants of the God of heaven and earth, and build the house that was builded these many years ago, which a great king of Israel builded and set up. The confession before the Persian governor is bold and theological. We are the servants of the God of heaven and earth. The post-exilic community knows itself first by its allegiance, not by its ethnicity or politics.
Ezra.5.12: But after that our fathers had provoked the God of heaven unto wrath, he gave them into the hand of Nebuchadnezzar the king of Babylon, the Chaldean, who destroyed this house, and carried the people away into Babylon. The honest confession of the cause of exile is offered to a foreign court. The community owns its history of judgment. Reformed corporate confession is grounded in this kind of honesty.
Ezra.6.3: In the first year of Cyrus the king the same Cyrus the king made a decree concerning the house of God at Jerusalem, Let the house be builded, the place where they offered sacrifices, and let the foundations thereof be strongly laid. The original decree of Cyrus is rediscovered in the Persian archives. Sovereign providence has preserved the very document needed to authorize the work. Reformed theology delights in such providential preservation: the right paper at the right moment in the right hand.
Ezra.6.10: That they may offer sacrifices of sweet savours unto the God of heaven, and pray for the life of the king, and of his sons. Darius commands that the Jews pray for him. Even pagan kings recognize, however dimly, the value of intercession by the people of God. The principle of 1 Timothy 2 is anticipated.
Ezra.6.14: And the elders of the Jews builded, and they prospered through the prophesying of Haggai the prophet and Zechariah the son of Iddo. And they builded, and finished it, according to the commandment of the God of Israel, and according to the commandment of Cyrus, and Darius, and Artaxerxes king of Persia. Three Persian kings are named alongside the commandment of God. The Chronicler-Ezra historian sees the divine command operating through the imperial decrees. Reformed political theology recognizes the providential use of pagan magistrates for the good of the church.
Ezra.6.15: And this house was finished on the third day of the month Adar, which was in the sixth year of the reign of Darius the king. The dedication date is preserved. The temple is completed in 516 BC, exactly seventy years after its destruction. The mathematics of judgment yield to the mathematics of restoration.
Ezra.6.16: And the children of Israel, the priests, and the Levites, and the rest of the children of the captivity, kept the dedication of this house of God with joy. Joy is the dominant note. Despite the smallness of the second temple compared to Solomon's, the dedication is marked by genuine gladness. Reformed worship learns to find joy in modest beginnings, knowing that God is not measured by the size of His house.
Ezra.6.21: And the children of Israel, which were come again out of captivity, and all such as had separated themselves unto them from the filthiness of the heathen of the land, to seek the LORD God of Israel, did eat. Gentile proselytes are included in the Passover. The post-exilic community is not racially closed but covenantally defined. Whoever separates from idolatry to seek the LORD is welcome at the table.
Ezra.6.22: And kept the feast of unleavened bread seven days with joy: for the LORD had made them joyful, and turned the heart of the king of Assyria unto them, to strengthen their hands in the work of the house of God. The LORD turned the heart of the king. The same divine action that stirred up Cyrus has continued to operate through Darius. Proverbs 21:1 is here in narrative form: the king's heart is in the hand of the LORD.
Ezra.7.6: This Ezra went up from Babylon; and he was a ready scribe in the law of Moses, which the LORD God of Israel had given: and the king granted him all his request, according to the hand of the LORD his God upon him. Ezra is introduced as a ready scribe. The hand of the LORD upon him is the recurring motif. Reformed exposition has long held up Ezra as the pattern of the faithful pastor-scholar: ready in the law, dependent on God's hand.
Ezra.7.10: For Ezra had prepared his heart to seek the law of the LORD, and to do it, and to teach in Israel statutes and judgments. The triple commitment is sequential and inseparable: seek, do, teach. Reformed homiletics has long pressed this order. The minister cannot teach what he has not done, and he cannot do what he has not first sought.
Ezra.7.13: I make a decree, that all they of the people of Israel, and of his priests and Levites, in my realm, which are minded of their own freewill to go up to Jerusalem, go with thee.
