Judg.3.10: The Spirit of the LORD (ruach YHWH) came upon him, and he judged Israel. He went out to war, and the LORD gave Cushan-rishathaim king of Mesopotamia into his hand. The first occurrence of ruach YHWH coming upon a judge sets the pattern: shophet-ship is pneumatic, not merely political. Othniel does not judge in his own strength but as Spirit-anointed deliverer. Reformed Christology recognizes here the seminal pattern fulfilled in the One on whom the Spirit rests without measure (Isaiah 11:2, John 3:34). Every subsequent judge participates partially in what Christ alone perfectly embodies, the Spirit-anointed Servant who executes covenant justice in the earth.
Judg.3.15: Ehud son of Gera, the Benjaminite, a man bound of his right hand (itter yad-yemino). The phrase is often rendered left-handed but literally describes a restricted right hand, possibly congenital. Benjamin means son of the right hand, making Ehud's condition a deliberate irony. God chooses the weak and the unlikely to shame the strong (1 Corinthians 1:27). The eighteen-inch sword strapped to his right thigh evades inspection precisely because of the assumed orientation of warriors. Reformed providence delights in such reversals; God's deliverance does not depend on human strength or expectation.
Judg.3.20: I have a word from God (devar Elohim) for you. Ehud's declaration to Eglon is laden with theological irony: the word of God for the obese Moabite king is judgment by the sword. Throughout Scripture devar Elohim is both creative and destructive, life-giving to the elect and death-dealing to the reprobate (Hebrews 4:12). Eglon rises in respect, exposing himself to the very judgment he honored. The narrative crudely mocks pagan power; the king who oppressed God's people is undone by a word and a blade, prefiguring the eschatological judgment when the Word made flesh strikes the nations with the sword of His mouth.
Judg.3.31: After him was Shamgar son of Anath, who killed six hundred Philistines with an oxgoad. The brevity of Shamgar's notice belies its theological weight. His matronymic (son of Anath, a Canaanite war goddess) suggests mixed pedigree, yet God uses him to deliver Israel. The oxgoad, an agrarian implement, becomes a weapon, anticipating the eschatological reversal where plowshares and pruning hooks reorder creation. Reformed providence delights to use the unlikely instrument; the same God who saved by an ass's jawbone (Samson) and a sling stone (David) prefigures the cross, where wood and nails defeat the principalities.
Judg.4.4: Deborah, a prophetess (nevi'ah), the wife of Lappidoth, was judging Israel at that time. Deborah is the only judge explicitly called a prophet, uniting the offices of revelation and rule. Her name means bee, evocative both of sweetness and sting. In a male-led covenantal economy, her elevation rebukes the male leadership vacuum, particularly Barak's hesitancy. Reformed exegesis avoids egalitarian or patriarchal extremes here; God raises whom He wills for extraordinary deliverance, while the ordinary patterns of household and ecclesial order remain. Deborah's role is providential and exceptional, illustrating divine sovereignty over instrumentality.
Judg.4.9: The road on which you are going will not lead to your glory (tif'artekha), for the LORD will sell Sisera into the hand of a woman. Barak's faith is real (Hebrews 11:32) but compromised, and divine sovereignty turns his hesitation into providential humiliation. The honor of victory is transferred from the male warrior to a tent-dwelling Kenite woman, Jael. The theology of glory is inverted: God will not share His glory, and He often distributes it according to faith, not flesh. The Reformed doctrine of soli Deo gloria is dramatized in narrative form.
Judg.4.14: Up! For this is the day in which the LORD has given Sisera into your hand. Does not the LORD go out before you? Deborah's command employs the perfect tense (natan, has given) for an event still future, the prophetic perfect that treats divine promise as accomplished fact. The going-out of YHWH before His people echoes the holy-war language of Deuteronomy 20:4. Reformed exegesis recognizes here the pattern of faith: the believer acts on the basis of God's promised victory, not present circumstance. The certainty of providence is the warrant for obedience, and Christ's resurrection is the great prophetic perfect that grounds all Christian courage.
Judg.4.21: Jael drove the tent peg through his temple. The brutality is shocking but theologically deliberate. Sisera, the supposedly mighty, is undone by domestic implements: milk in a bowl, a hammer, a peg. The Canaanite general who raped and pillaged is ironically destroyed by a woman in a tent, a reversal of the violations he routinely committed. The seed of the woman crushes the head of the serpent's agent (Genesis 3:15 echoes). Reformed typology hears the protoevangelium in the splintering skull of God's enemy.
Judg.5.7: The villagers ceased in Israel; they ceased until I, Deborah, arose, a mother in Israel (em be-Yisrael). Deborah's self-designation as mother is not biological but covenantal; she is matriarchal protector of the covenant community. The title parallels the prophetic father (cf. 2 Kings 2:12) and confirms her role as nurturer of Israel's faith and freedom. Reformed ecclesiology sees the church herself as our mother (Calvin, Institutes 4.1), and Deborah typifies that maternal office of nurture and defense. Her arising marks divine intervention in covenantal collapse, foreshadowing the church's perpetual need of Spirit-given leadership when ordinary structures fail.
Judg.5.20: From the heavens the stars fought, from their courses they fought against Sisera. Deborah's song depicts cosmic warfare; the heavenly host wars against Israel's enemies. This is not poetic decoration but covenantal theology: creation itself is enlisted in the battles of YHWH's kingdom. The stars in their courses recall Genesis 1's ordered cosmos serving the Creator's purposes. Reformed theology affirms that history is theatre of divine sovereignty in which heaven and earth are not autonomous spheres. Christ, by whom and for whom all things were created, commands the same hosts in the spiritual warfare of the church.
Judg.5.31: So may all your enemies perish, O LORD; but may those who love Him be like the sun rising in its strength. The song concludes with imprecation and benediction held together. Reformed exegesis does not sanitize the imprecatory: the perishing of God's enemies is the negative side of the salvation of His people. Yet love (the participle ohavav, those loving Him) is the positive identifier of the covenant remnant. The image of the rising sun in its gevurah (strength) is both eschatological and Christological; Malachi 4:2 will name this Sun the Sun of Righteousness who arises with healing in His wings.
Judg.6.1: The sons of Israel did evil in the eyes of the LORD, and the LORD gave them into the hand of Midian seven years. The cycle restarts with grim regularity. Midian, descendants of Abraham through Keturah, are kindred enemies; the oppression is fratricidal. The number seven is sabbatical, suggesting that Israel's failure to keep the covenant rest results in covenantal un-rest. The narrative pattern reinforces the doctrine of the covenant of works in the typological land grant: obedience yields rest, disobedience yields exile and oppression, anticipating the greater Sabbath in Christ (Hebrews 4).
Judg.6.10: I said to you, I am the LORD your God; you shall not fear the gods of the Amorites in whose land you dwell. But you have not obeyed my voice. The unnamed prophet's indictment precedes the deliverer's call, establishing that judgment must precede mercy in covenantal logic. The first commandment is rehearsed not as new revelation but as broken stipulation. Reformed exegesis recognizes that the law's accusation is the necessary preamble to the gospel's deliverance; sin must be named before it can be remitted. The pattern anticipates the prophetic ministry of the Baptist preceding the greater Deliverer.
Judg.6.12: The Angel of the LORD appeared to him and said, The LORD is with you, O mighty man of valor (gibbor he-chayil). The address is laden with theological irony, for Gideon is hiding in a winepress threshing wheat. The mal'akh YHWH speaks and is identified with YHWH (verse 14), a Christophanic appearance. Gideon is named according to what he will become by grace, not what he is by sight, a narrative instance of justification's forensic logic: God calls things that are not as though they were (Romans 4:17). The valor is not native; it is imputed and then imparted by the Spirit who clothes him in verse 34.
Judg.6.15: How can I save Israel? Behold, my clan (alpi) is the weakest in Manasseh, and I am the youngest in my father's house. Gideon's protest follows the prophetic call pattern of Moses (Exodus 3) and later Jeremiah (1:6). Reformed theology hears here not false humility but the consistent biblical principle that God chooses the weak that the power may be of God and not of man. Manasseh itself was the half-tribe of compromise (1:27), and Abiezer the smallest within it. Grace selects the least to display sovereignty; the doctrine of unconditional election is dramatized in vocational call.
Judg.6.24: Gideon built an altar there to the LORD and called it YHWH-Shalom. The Angel's word, peace to you, becomes a divine name and a memorial. Shalom is not mere absence of conflict but covenantal wholeness, the eschatological gift secured by the propitiation of divine wrath. Gideon's altar stands amid Baal worship as a confession that peace with God comes through revealed worship, not syncretism. The name anticipates the One whom Isaiah 9:6 calls Sar Shalom, the Prince of Peace, who, having made peace by the blood of His cross, is Himself our peace (Ephesians 2:14).
Judg.6.34: The Spirit of the LORD clothed (lavshah) Gideon. The verb is striking: literally, the Spirit put Gideon on like a garment. The agency reverses our usual conception; Gideon does not wield the Spirit, the Spirit wears Gideon. This Old Testament charismatic empowerment for office prefigures the fuller new covenant indwelling. Reformed pneumatology distinguishes the temporary, vocational anointing of judges and kings from the permanent, regenerating, sanctifying work of the Spirit upon all believers in Christ. The same Spirit who clothed Gideon now clothes the church with power from on high (Luke 24:49).
Judg.6.39: Let me speak just once more. Let me make a test (anaseh) just once more with the fleece. Gideon's double fleece is often misread as exemplary; the narrative tone is forbearing rather than commendatory. Gideon himself acknowledges presumption (Let not your anger burn). God condescends to weak faith, not because such testing is normative but because grace meets its objects where they are. Reformed exegesis cautions against deriving guidance methodology from this episode; the closed canon and the Spirit's illumination supersede such ad hoc signs. God's patience here magnifies His mercy, not Gideon's method.
Judg.7.2: The people with you are too many for me to give the Midianites into their hand, lest Israel boast against me, saying, My own hand has saved me. The theology of this verse is foundational. Yahweh actively orchestrates Israel's weakness to safeguard His glory. Self-salvation is the perennial heresy; the cross of Christ stands against every theology of the boast. From three hundred reduced to nothing, Reformed soteriology hears the gospel logic: God saves in such a way that no flesh shall glory in His presence (1 Corinthians 1:29). The reduction is grace's pedagogy.
Judg.7.20: The sword of the LORD and of Gideon. The torches in jars, the trumpet blasts, the shouted slogan: the battle is won by symbolic action, not military force. The treasure is in earthen vessels (2 Corinthians 4:7), light shining out of darkness. The compound name pairs divine and human agency without confusion or equivalence; YHWH is primary, Gideon instrumental. Reformed theology sees here the doctrine of concursus, divine and creaturely action in non-competitive distinction, a pattern that culminates in the hypostatic union where divine and human meet without confusion in Christ.
Judg.7.22: The LORD set every man's sword against his comrade and against all the army. The Midianite host destroys itself; God's deliverance requires no Israelite blade. The motif of the enemy's self-destruction recurs at the Red Sea (Exodus 14), Jehoshaphat's deliverance (2 Chronicles 20), and ultimately Calvary, where the powers crucified the Lord of glory and thereby disarmed themselves (Colossians 2:15). Reformed soteriology finds in such episodes a pattern of victorious passivity in the elect: the battle belongs to YHWH, and salvation is monergistic. Three hundred trumpeters watching is a parable of the gospel itself.
Judg.8.23: I will not rule over you, and my son will not rule over you; the LORD will rule over you. Gideon's verbal orthodoxy is exemplary, affirming the divine kingship that Samuel will later defend (1 Samuel 8). Yet his subsequent making of the ephod (verse 27) functionally contradicts his confession. Reformed exegesis sees here the gap between profession and practice that haunts every generation. To say YHWH reigns yet erect rival objects of devotion is to commit theological perjury. Gideon foreshadows the failure of every Israelite leader who cannot finally do what he proclaims, awaiting the king who is what He says.
Judg.8.27: Gideon made an ephod, and all Israel played the harlot (zanah) after it there. The ephod, a priestly garment, has been distorted into an idol. Even the means of grace, when severed from divine command, become snares. The verb zanah introduces the sustained metaphor of spiritual adultery that runs through Hosea and Ezekiel: covenant breach as marital betrayal. Reformed worship is regulated by Scripture precisely because well-intentioned innovation in worship slides inexorably into idolatry. Gideon's ephod becomes a snare to him and to his house, illustrating that even victors in faith must guard the second commandment.
Judg.8.33: As soon as Gideon died, the sons of Israel turned back and played the harlot after the Baalim, and made Baal-berith their god. The name Baal-berith means lord of the covenant, a deliberate parody of YHWH's covenantal lordship. Israel does not abandon covenantal language; she transfers it to a counterfeit deity. Reformed theology recognizes the perennial danger of using right vocabulary for wrong worship; orthodoxy in syllables can mask idolatry in substance. The deliverer scarcely buried, the people apostatize, demonstrating that external deliverance cannot regenerate the heart. Only the new covenant in Christ's blood writes the law within.
Judg.9.7: Jotham went and stood on the top of Mount Gerizim. Mount Gerizim was the mount of blessing in Deuteronomy 27, but Jotham now pronounces from it a parabolic curse. The bramble parable exposes Abimelech's coronation as the sterile reign of the worthless. The narrative critique is severe: Abimelech, whose name means my father is king, slaughters seventy brothers on one stone, an inverted altar of fratricide. Reformed political theology recognizes that magistracy without covenantal legitimacy becomes tyranny, and that human kingship grasped by violence cannot deliver. Only the Davidic-Christic king reigns rightly.
Judg.9.15: If in good faith you are anointing me king over you, come and take refuge in my shade. The bramble's offer of shade is comically impossible; brambles cast no shade and ignite easily. Jotham's parable indicts the Shechemites for choosing a king who can give nothing and consume everything. Reformed political theology recognizes here the bramble-pattern of every illegitimate authority that demands worship without providing protection. Christ alone is the cedar of Lebanon under whose branches the nations find true rest (Ezekiel 17:23), the King who genuinely shelters His people because He first laid down His life for them.
Judg.9.23: God sent an evil spirit (ruach ra'ah) between Abimelech and the lords of Shechem. Reformed theology must address this carefully. God is not the author of moral evil, yet He sovereignly governs and uses even malevolent spirits as instruments of just retribution. The same God who sent a lying spirit in 1 Kings 22 here sends discord as the means of judicial reckoning for the bloodguilt of seventy brothers. The doctrine of providence affirms God's holy and righteous use of secondary causes including hostile ones, without making Him the agent of the evil itself. Compatibilism is on full display.
Judg.9.53: A certain woman threw an upper millstone on Abimelech's head and crushed his skull. The death is laden with poetic justice: he killed his brothers on one stone, and one stone now crushes him. He demanded his armor-bearer kill him so it not be said a woman slew him, but the narrator preserves the truth precisely. The seed of the serpent, the would-be king, has his head crushed by an unnamed woman, another faint echo of Genesis 3:15. Reformed exegesis sees God's righteous judgment ironically administered through despised means, a recurring pattern climaxing at the cross.
Judg.10.13: You have forsaken me and served other gods; therefore I will save you no more. The divine declaration appears final, mirroring the covenant curses of Deuteronomy 28. Yet what follows is one of the most stunning verses in the book: His soul could no longer endure the misery of Israel (verse 16). The tension between divine threat and divine compassion is not contradiction but the dynamic of covenantal grace. God's no is not His final word; His hesed overrules His threat for the sake of His own name. Calvin saw here the inviolable mercy that grounds the covenant of grace despite Israel's manifest unworthiness.
Judg.10.16: They put away the foreign gods from among them and served the LORD; and His soul (nephesh) was grieved (titqatzar) over the misery of Israel. The verb qatzar with nephesh means to be shortened in soul, an idiom for impatience or distress, here translated as grief or weariness over their suffering. Anthropopathic but real: the immutable God expresses covenant compassion in language we can grasp. Reformed exegesis preserves both divine impassibility (no internal change in essence) and the genuine relational engagement of God with His people's pain, fully revealed at the incarnation when the Word becomes flesh and weeps at Lazarus's tomb.
Judg.10.6: The sons of Israel again did evil in the sight of the LORD and served the Baals and the Ashtaroth, the gods of Aram, the gods of Sidon, the gods of Moab, the gods of the Ammonites, and the gods of the Philistines. The catalogue is exhaustive; Israel is now a polytheistic supermarket. Seven nations' deities are itemized, a number of completeness signaling total apostasy. Reformed exegesis sees here the inevitable pluralism of a covenant community that has rejected exclusive devotion; tolerance of any rival inevitably tolerates all. The first commandment cannot be partially obeyed; loving God with all the heart admits no division of allegiance.
Judg.11.1: Jephthah the Gileadite was a mighty man of valor (gibbor chayil), but he was the son of a harlot (ben ishah zonah). The narrator immediately juxtaposes valor and stigma. Jephthah's social rejection by his half-brothers parallels the experience of David and ultimately of Christ, the stone rejected by the builders. God's electing grace operates beyond bloodline propriety, raising up the despised for His purposes. Hebrews 11:32 places Jephthah in the hall of faith despite the moral ambiguity of his story, a sober reminder that justification is by faith, not perfect performance, and that God uses flawed instruments without endorsing their flaws.
Judg.11.30: Jephthah made a vow (neder) to the LORD. The rash vow, whatever comes out of the doors of my house, exposes Jephthah's syncretistic theology. Pagan gods required bargaining; YHWH does not. The vow violates the explicit prohibition of human sacrifice (Deuteronomy 12:31, 18:10) and reflects the theological confusion of the era. Whether the daughter was actually slain or devoted to perpetual virginity (a longstanding interpretive debate) the tragedy is genuine. Reformed exegesis treats Jephthah as historical not exemplary: faith may be real while doctrine is malformed, and untaught zeal can lead to grievous error.
Judg.11.39: She had not known a man. The narrative leaves the precise nature of the daughter's fate ambiguous, but whether literal sacrifice or perpetual celibacy, the loss to the covenant line is total. The line of Jephthah ends. The covenant promise to Abraham of seed as numerous as the stars is again threatened by human folly, yet God's purposes will not fail. Reformed reading sees here the cumulative weight of judges-era apostasy: the covenant family is being dismembered from within. Only a faithful Son's faithful obedience (Hebrews 5:8) can finally redeem and propagate the true seed.
Judg.12.3: When I saw that you would not deliver me, I took my life in my hand and crossed over against the sons of Ammon. Jephthah's defense to the Ephraimites recalls his earlier solitary stand. The phrase took my life in my hand (sim nephesh be-kaf) denotes resolute self-offering in the face of mortal risk. Reformed typology hears a faint echo of the greater Deliverer who takes His life in His hand, not under coercion but in voluntary self-giving (John 10:18). Yet Jephthah's victory devolves into fratricide, exposing the limits of every human deliverer; only the One who takes up His life again secures lasting peace.
Judg.12.6: Then they would say to him, Say now, Shibboleth. The Ephraimites' inability to pronounce the sibilant correctly costs forty-two thousand lives. Civil war within the covenant community over a phoneme is the nadir of tribal fratricide. The episode illustrates how external markers can mask internal apostasy; pronunciation distinguished friend from foe, but the deeper question of covenant fidelity goes unaddressed by either side. Reformed ecclesiology recognizes the danger of identifying the church by accidental rather than essential marks. The true shibboleth is faith in Christ, not pedigree, dialect, or denominational affiliation.
Judg.11.27: I therefore have not sinned against you; but you do me wrong by making war against me. May the LORD, the Judge (ha-shophet), judge today between the sons of Israel and the sons of Ammon. Jephthah's diplomatic appeal climaxes by invoking YHWH as ha-shophet, the divine title from which the book takes its name. The judges of Israel are merely instruments; God Himself is the true Judge. Reformed theology affirms that all earthly judicature is delegated, derivative, and accountable to the divine bench. The ultimate vindication of the righteous belongs to Him who judges justly (1 Peter 2:23), and Jephthah's appeal anticipates the eschatological assize of the Son of Man.
Judg.13.3: The Angel of the LORD appeared to the woman and said, Behold, you are barren and have not borne, but you shall conceive and bear a son. The annunciation pattern joins Sarah, Rebekah, Rachel, Hannah, and ultimately Elizabeth and Mary. Barrenness is the theological soil of covenant grace; God's redemptive interventions consistently begin where human possibility ends. The mal'akh YHWH, who is later worshipped as God Himself (verse 22), reveals Christophanically the same divine pattern that culminates in the virgin birth. The Nazirite stipulations from the womb consecrate Samson's life entirely to God before any human will is exercised, a picture of unconditional electing grace.
Judg.13.18: Why do you ask my name, seeing it is wonderful (peli)? The Angel's self-identification uses peli, the same root that Isaiah 9:6 will employ when naming the Messiah Pele Yo'etz, Wonderful Counselor. The name is hidden because it transcends creaturely categories. Manoah and his wife realize after the fact, We have seen God (verse 22), confirming the Christophanic identity of the visitor. Reformed Christology sees here a pre-incarnate appearance of the Son, the Wonder of His name not yet displayed in flesh but already revealed in promise. The mystery awaits Bethlehem for its full unveiling.
Judg.13.25: The Spirit of the LORD began to stir (le-pa'amo) him in Mahaneh-dan. The verb pa'am denotes pulsing or impelling motion, a forceful divine prompting. The Spirit's work on Samson is sporadic and instrumental rather than sanctifying. This is a crucial Reformed distinction: charismata for office can operate without genuine sanctification of the man. Samson is empowered but not transformed. The contrast with the new covenant ministry of the Spirit, who indwells permanently and conforms to Christ, is sharp. Samson serves his function while perishing personally, a sobering warning that gifts without grace are no salvation.
Judg.13.5: He shall be a Nazirite (nazir) to God from the womb, and he shall begin to save Israel from the hand of the Philistines. Samson is the only Nazirite-from-the-womb in Scripture, paralleled by John the Baptist (Luke 1:15) and contrasted with the voluntary vows of Numbers 6. The verb yachel (begin) is theologically loaded; Samson begins what David and ultimately Christ complete. No deliverer in Judges accomplishes total salvation. The Nazirite consecration, with its abstentions and uncut hair, is bodily testimony to set-apart status, prefiguring the perfect consecration of the holy One of God who fulfills every typological office.
Judg.14.4: His father and mother did not know that it was from the LORD, for He was seeking an opportunity (to'anah) against the Philistines. The narrator opens a window onto divine sovereignty: even Samson's improper desire for a Philistine woman is woven into providence. This does not justify the desire; the moral evil remains imputable to Samson, but God uses it teleologically. Reformed compatibilism holds together human responsibility and divine ordination without contradiction. Joseph's brothers meant evil, God meant it for good (Genesis 50:20); Samson sins, God arranges. The cross is the ultimate example of evil intentions accomplishing the eternal purpose of redemption.
Judg.14.14: Out of the eater came something to eat, and out of the strong came something sweet. Samson's riddle, framed around the lion's carcass and bee's honey, becomes accidentally prophetic. The pattern of life from death, sweetness from strength, anticipates the gospel logic itself: out of the strong One slain comes the bread of life. Reformed typology, while cautious not to over-allegorize, recognizes that the Spirit superintends even the riddles of compromised judges to foreshadow the cross. The lion of Judah slain becomes the source of honeyed grace; the strong Man bound provides liberty for the captives.
Judg.15.18: I die of thirst, and now shall I fall into the hand of the uncircumcised? Samson's prayer is the cry of a man at the end of himself. The slaughter at Lehi was followed not by triumph but by depletion. God's deliverer must learn his own dependence. The water from the hollow place (En-haqore, the spring of him who calls) parallels Moses striking the rock; both prefigure Christ from whom living water flows (1 Corinthians 10:4; John 7:38). Reformed exegesis sees God's care for His flawed servant despite covenant infidelity, and points beyond Samson to the One who, also thirsty unto death, secures eternal refreshment for His people.
Judg.16.17: He told her all his heart, and said to her, A razor has never come upon my head, for I have been a Nazirite to God from my mother's womb. Samson's confession is theologically devastating: he commodifies his consecration. The Nazirite vow was never a magic formula but a covenantal sign, and Samson treats the sign as if it were the substance. Reformed sacramental theology distinguishes signum from res significata; the outward sign without the inward grace is empty. Samson's fall illustrates the danger of clinging to external religion while the heart has long since defected. The hair was not the strength; the consecration was.
Judg.16.20: He did not know that the LORD had departed from him. The most chilling sentence in the book. Samson's strength was never autonomous; it was always sustained by the Spirit's presence. He plays with his vows, surrenders his secret, awakens to fight as before, and discovers absence. Reformed theology distinguishes the temporary vocational anointing (which can be withdrawn, as with Saul) from the saving indwelling of the Spirit promised in the new covenant which seals believers to the day of redemption (Ephesians 4:30). Samson's tragedy is the warning of every presumptuous saint who confuses past gifts with present grace.
Judg.16.21: The Philistines seized him, gouged out his eyes, brought him down to Gaza, and bound him with bronze fetters; and he was a grinder in the prison. The threefold humiliation (blinded, bound, grinding) reverses Samson's earlier strength. Grinding was women's work and slave labor; the strongman has become beast. Yet the narrator notes in verse 22 that his hair began to grow again, a quiet sign of returning consecration. Reformed exegesis sees in Samson's prison a parable of every fallen saint whom God still preserves; the Spirit grieved is not the Spirit forfeited where election holds. Restoration is the dark thread within judgment.
Judg.16.28: Remember me, O Lord GOD, and strengthen me only this once, O God. Samson's final prayer is the prayer of a sinner reduced to nothing but mercy. Blind, bound, mocked, he calls upon Adonai YHWH and on Elohim. The repeated divine names confess the only ground of his hope. Reformed soteriology sees here the deathbed faith of a flawed believer; salvation rests not on accumulated works but on cried-for grace. Hebrews 11:32 vindicates this prayer as faith. Samson dies grasping the pillars and grasping the covenant Lord whose mercy alone can deliver.
Judg.16.30: Let me die with the Philistines. The dying Samson kills more in his death than in his life. The pattern is unmistakably Christ-typological: a deliverer betrayed by those he loved, mocked by the enemies of God, hands outstretched between two pillars, achieving in apparent defeat the greatest victory. Yet the typology is also by contrast: Samson dies for his own vengeance; Christ for His enemies' redemption. Samson grasps wooden pillars; Christ embraces a wooden cross. Samson pulls down a temple of Dagon; Christ rebuilds the true temple in three days. The shadow falls; the substance is Christ.
Judg.17.6: In those days there was no king in Israel; every man did what was right in his own eyes (yashar be-einav). The refrain (also 18:1, 19:1, 21:25) functions as the editorial spine of the book's appendix. The Hebrew yashar (right, straight) is precisely what is to be done in the eyes of the LORD (Deuteronomy 12:25), but the relativizing be-einav transfers the standard from God to self. This is the hermeneutic of autonomy and the essence of fall: epistemological self-rule. Reformed theology identifies this as the perpetual posture of unregenerate humanity. The implicit longing for a king is also a longing for the King, the eschatological Davidide whose eyes are the proper standard.
Judg.17.13: Now I know that the LORD will prosper me, because I have a Levite as my priest. Micah's syncretism is breathtaking: he has manufactured idols, set up his own son as priest, and now upgrades to a Levite, mistaking institutional pedigree for divine sanction. The verse is biting satire. Reformed exegesis sees here the perversion of the means of grace; sacraments and ministers, divorced from God's appointed worship, become superstition. The Levite is hired (verse 10), reduced to commodity. True religion is corrupted at its most outward level while the heart remains idolatrous, a pattern as old as Cain and as current as nominal Christianity.
Judg.17.10: Dwell with me and be to me a father (av) and a priest (kohen), and I will give you ten pieces of silver a year. Micah's offer to the wandering Levite reduces the priesthood to commodity. The Levite is hired by salary, suggesting the spiritual prostitution of office for fee. Reformed ecclesiology guards against both clericalism and Erastian capture; gospel ministry is by divine call, not market transaction. The episode foreshadows Simon Magus, the Judas pricing of the Son of Man, and every subsequent simoniacal corruption. The thirty pieces and ten pieces alike testify that ordained ministry severed from divine authority becomes merchandise.
Judg.18.1: In those days there was no king in Israel. The refrain reintroduces the appendix's theme as the Danites migrate. Dan's tribal failure (cf. 1:34) prompts a campaign that abandons their inheritance for an easier conquest. The northern Dan settlement will become a center of idolatry, and Dan is conspicuously omitted from the sealed twelve tribes in Revelation 7. The geographic relocation is theological apostasy. Reformed covenant theology sees the catastrophic consequence of refusing to fight the appointed battles of faith; what the church will not contend for at her front door, she will compromise on at her back.
Judg.18.27: They came to Laish, to a people quiet and unsuspecting, and struck them with the edge of the sword. The Danites apply the herem of holy war to a people God had not designated, an act of unauthorized violence. Their relocation establishes the northern Dan, future seat of Jeroboam's golden calf (1 Kings 12:29). Reformed covenant theology sees here the seed of schism that will fracture the kingdom. When the appointed boundaries are abandoned, both moral and geographic, idolatry institutionalizes. The very tribe that failed to dispossess Canaanites at home becomes Canaanite in temperament abroad.
Judg.18.30: Jonathan, the son of Gershom, the son of Moses (Manasseh in some traditions). The text identifies the Danite priest as a direct descendant of Moses himself (the Masoretes inserted a suspended nun to read Manasseh, sparing Moses's name from explicit dishonor). The grandson of the lawgiver is now priest of an idol shrine. This is generational catastrophe: from Sinai's mediator to a Danite idol in three generations. Reformed covenant succession theology sees here the fragility of covenant transmission and the absolute necessity of regeneration in every generation; covenantal privilege does not guarantee covenantal fidelity.
Judg.19.1: In those days, when there was no king in Israel. The third occurrence of the refrain frames the most horrifying narrative in the book. The Levite-concubine episode is deliberately reminiscent of Sodom (Genesis 19), but the wickedness now occurs not in pagan cities but in Gibeah of Benjamin, the covenant tribe. Israel has become Sodom. The narrator's silence is its own indictment; no character speaks for God, no prayer, no prophet. Reformed reading sees the doctrine of total depravity laid bare in the covenant community itself. The land that was given to drive out Canaan has become Canaanite.
Judg.19.30: Such a thing has not happened or been seen from the day the sons of Israel came up from the land of Egypt until this day. The dismembered concubine sent in twelve pieces throughout Israel inverts the consecration of the kingdom. The exodus is invoked as the standard from which Israel has fallen. Reformed exegesis hears here the covenantal lament: redemption out of Egypt was for holiness, not for license. When the redeemed community produces atrocity equal to or worse than its enemies, the covenant cries out for renewed mediation, for a true Husband who will deliver His unfaithful bride and present her without spot.
Judg.19.22: Worthless men (bene beliyya'al) of the city surrounded the house. The phrase sons of Belial designates covenantal apostates, and the demand to know the man recapitulates Sodom precisely. The horror is that Gibeah, in Benjamin, the territory of God's chosen people, has become Sodom. The covenant community has assimilated to the very wickedness it was called to expel. Reformed exegesis sees here the inversion of covenantal calling; the holy nation has become indistinguishable from the nations destined for judgment. The narrator's silence about prayer, prophet, or priest is its own indictment of an apostate church.
Judg.20.16: Among all this people there were seven hundred chosen men who were left-handed; every one could sling a stone at a hair and not miss. The military note is significant: Benjamin (son of the right hand) fields elite left-handers. The civil war that follows nearly extinguishes the tribe, fulfilling no prophecy but exposing the fratricide that consumes a covenant community without a king or proper priestly mediation. Reformed political theology sees in tribal warfare the inevitable result of covenant breakdown; when the vertical relation to God collapses, horizontal relations within the covenant people likewise rupture. Only the King who reconciles to God reconciles brothers to brothers.
Judg.20.18: The sons of Israel arose, went up to Bethel, and inquired of God, Who shall go up first for us to fight against the sons of Benjamin? The chapter opens with the same question as 1:1, but now the enemy is internal. The opening question of the book reappears in tragic form; the war intended for Canaanites is now waged against brothers. Reformed exegesis observes the diagnostic value of this inversion. When the church loses the spiritual antithesis with the world, she invariably manufactures intramural antitheses. Civil war within the people of God is the bitter fruit of a covenant community that has forgotten its true enemies.
Judg.20.28: Phinehas the son of Eleazar, the son of Aaron, stood before it in those days. The naming of Phinehas pins the chronology early in the period of the judges, demonstrating that the appendix narratives (chapters 17-21) describe events from earlier rather than later in the era. Theologically, Phinehas's presence highlights the priestly mediation that should have functioned but failed. The same priest who zealously defended covenant holiness in Numbers 25 now ministers in a fragmenting covenant community. Reformed covenant theology recognizes that even a faithful priest cannot prevent covenant disintegration apart from a faithful people; the priesthood awaits its perfect fulfillment in Christ.
Judg.20.26: Then all the sons of Israel and all the people went up and came to Bethel and wept; they sat there before the LORD and fasted that day until evening. After two devastating defeats, Israel finally engages in genuine corporate lament before YHWH. The pattern is instructive: divine victory often follows humbling, and the means of grace (fasting, weeping, sacrifice) are not optional ornaments but covenantal necessities. Reformed worship recognizes that battles are won at the altar before they are won on the field. Yet even here, Israel weeps for the fracture of the tribe rather than for the sin that fractured it, a shallow contrition that the new covenant exceeds in heart-rending repentance.
Judg.21.3: O LORD, the God of Israel, why has this happened in Israel, that today there should be one tribe missing in Israel? The plaintive repetition of Israel four times in one verse exposes the irony: the covenant people are concerned about tribal completeness while displaying covenant unfaithfulness. They mourn the symptom while perpetuating the disease. The solutions they devise (slaughter at Jabesh-gilead, abduction at Shiloh) compound the wickedness. Reformed exegesis sees the bankruptcy of human casuistry attempting to resolve the consequences of sin without addressing its root. The covenant cannot be patched by human ingenuity; it requires divine intervention.
Judg.21.5: What one of all the tribes of Israel did not come up to the LORD at Mizpah? Israel binds herself with rash oaths and then employs further violence to circumvent them. The covenant community generates new sins to remedy old ones, an economy of evil compounding itself. Reformed exegesis sees here the bankruptcy of self-atonement; sin cannot expiate sin. The book's final chapters are a sustained demonstration that the covenant people, left to themselves, cannot extricate themselves from their own moral incoherence. Only the great High Priest who offers Himself once for all can resolve what tribal Israel cannot, securing eternal redemption rather than provisional patches.
Judg.21.15: The people had compassion (nicham) on Benjamin, because the LORD had made a breach (peretz) in the tribes of Israel. The narrator attributes the fracture itself to YHWH's sovereign action, even though Israel's own civil war effected it. Reformed compatibilism affirms God's ultimate causal sovereignty over events accomplished through human agency without thereby authoring the moral evil. The breach in the covenant community is divine discipline, exposing the bankruptcy of self-rule. The wordplay on peretz subtly anticipates the Davidic ancestor (Genesis 38:29, Ruth 4:18) and ultimately the Messiah, who alone can repair the breach in the people of God.
Judg.21.25: In those days there was no king in Israel; every man did what was right in his own eyes. The book closes as it has framed itself, on a verdict and a longing. The absence of a king is not merely political but theological: there is no functioning mediator, no ruler under God who shapes the people in conformity to covenant. The verse anticipates 1 Samuel and ultimately David, but reaches its full resolution only in great David's greater Son. The Reformed canonical reading sees the entire book of Judges as a sustained argument from need; only Christ the King, who Himself is the right and the eyes by which all is measured, can finally end the era of self-rule. The closing verse is not despair but expectation, opening the canon's narrative toward the King whose kingdom has no end.
Lam.1.1: 'How doth the city sit solitary, that was full of people!' The book opens with the Hebrew exclamation 'Ekah'—the funeral wail. Jerusalem, once 'great among the nations' and 'princess among the provinces,' now sits alone, a widow. The reversal is total. Reformed expositors hear the dreadful seriousness of covenant judgment in this opening line: privilege without obedience ends in desolation. Yet the very lament is itself a covenant act—Israel still speaks her grief to the Lord who chastens. The first letter of the Hebrew alphabet (aleph) begins the acrostic, signaling that grief is not chaos but ordered, bounded by God. Pain confessed within the alphabet is pain still under the rule of the covenant Lord.
Lam.1.5: 'Her adversaries are the chief, her enemies prosper; for the LORD hath afflicted her for the multitude of her transgressions.' The theology is unflinching: the calamity is from the LORD, and the cause is sin. Reformed exposition refuses to soften either clause. The Babylonians are real agents, but Yahweh is the ultimate author of the chastening (cf. Isaiah 10). 'Her children are gone into captivity before the enemy'—covenant judgment falls visibly on the next generation. This is not arbitrary cruelty but the long-foretold curse of Deuteronomy 28. The verse refuses both fatalism (the enemies are not autonomous) and victim-mentality (Judah is not innocent). True lament begins with God-centered honesty.
Lam.1.9: 'Her filthiness is in her skirts; she remembereth not her last end; therefore she came down wonderfully... O LORD, behold my affliction: for the enemy hath magnified himself.' Forgetfulness of the last end—failure to remember covenant consequences—lay beneath the moral pollution. Yet in mid-verse, lament breaks into prayer: 'O LORD, behold.' Reformed pastoral theology notes this pattern: the soul that begins with description must end with petition. Even in deepest defilement, Zion still addresses Yahweh as covenant Lord. The cry 'behold' assumes that he has not closed his eyes. To keep speaking to God in calamity is itself a fruit of grace.
Lam.1.12: 'Is it nothing to you, all ye that pass by? behold, and see if there be any sorrow like unto my sorrow, which is done unto me, wherewith the LORD hath afflicted me in the day of his fierce anger.' The most haunting verse of the chapter. Christian exposition from earliest days has heard the voice of the suffering Christ in this cry, taken up rightly because Jesus bore in his passion the covenant curse Jerusalem here endures. The verse confesses that the affliction is from the LORD and that its measure is unique. Reformed preaching has not hesitated to apply this to Calvary, where the unique sorrow of the sinless Sufferer answered for the sorrows of his people.
Lam.1.18: 'The LORD is righteous; for I have rebelled against his commandment.' This is the theological hinge of the book's opening chapter. In the midst of devastation, Zion confesses divine righteousness. There is no charge against God; the fault is wholly on the rebellious creature. Reformed expositors have long pointed to this verse as the model of right repentance: God is justified in his judgments. Such confession is itself the sign of remaining grace. The unregenerate heart accuses God; the believing heart, even under the heaviest stroke, still says, 'The LORD is righteous.' Genuine lament never softens into self-pity but hardens into self-indictment and divine vindication.
Lam.1.20: 'Behold, O LORD; for I am in distress: my bowels are troubled; mine heart is turned within me; for I have grievously rebelled.' The body bears the weight of the soul's grief—'bowels troubled,' 'heart turned.' Reformed anthropology recognizes that true repentance is not merely intellectual but visceral. Yet even the bodily anguish is owned as the just fruit of rebellion. 'Abroad the sword bereaveth, at home there is as death'—judgment is total, inside and out. Still she addresses 'O LORD.' The chapter ends with prayer for the day of vengeance against enemies (v.22), not from personal vindictiveness but from confidence that Yahweh is the righteous Judge of all.
Lam.2.1: 'How hath the LORD covered the daughter of Zion with a cloud in his anger, and cast down from heaven unto the earth the beauty of Israel.' The second 'Ekah' begins the second acrostic. The cloud, once a symbol of covenant presence, has become a cloud of wrath. The temple, the 'beauty of Israel,' is cast down. Reformed expositors note the staggering reversal: the same God who came in the cloud to bless now veils himself in the cloud to chasten. He has not forgotten his footstool, but he has not spared it. The chapter will press relentlessly the truth that Yahweh himself is the active subject of these horrors—he has 'swallowed up,' 'cast down,' 'cut off.'
Lam.2.6: 'And he hath violently taken away his tabernacle... he hath destroyed his places of the assembly: the LORD hath caused the solemn feasts and sabbaths to be forgotten in Zion.' The very sites of covenant worship have been ruined by the covenant Lord. Reformed ecclesiology must reckon soberly with this verse: external religion, however ancient and divinely instituted, can be removed when faith and obedience fail. The destruction of the temple was not the failure of God's purpose but its severe vindication. 'In the indignation of his anger' he 'despised the king and the priest'—offices and persons fall together. No institution is safe apart from covenant fidelity.
Lam.2.13: 'What thing shall I take to witness for thee? what thing shall I liken to thee, O daughter of Jerusalem?... for thy breach is great like the sea: who can heal thee?' The poet searches for comparisons and finds none. The wound is sea-wide, beyond human remedy. The question 'who can heal thee?' is not despair but doorway: only the LORD who wounded can heal. Reformed expositors hear here the principle of Hosea 6:1—'he hath torn, and he will heal.' No human comfort, no political alliance, no religious reform can mend a sea-sized breach. The verse drives the soul past every lesser hope to the only Physician of incurable wounds.
Lam.2.17: 'The LORD hath done that which he had devised; he hath fulfilled his word that he had commanded in the days of old.' Judgment is not improvisation but fulfillment of long-given warning. Deuteronomy 28-32 stands behind this verse. Reformed covenant theology insists that God's threatenings are as sure as his promises; the same faithfulness that performs blessing performs curse. 'He hath thrown down, and hath not pitied.' Yet the very fulfillment proves that his word does not return void. The same divine fidelity that made the desolation will, when the time comes, make the restoration. The God who keeps his curses keeps his promises.
Lam.2.19: 'Arise, cry out in the night... pour out thine heart like water before the face of the LORD: lift up thy hands toward him for the life of thy young children.' Even in chapter two's relentless darkness, the prophet calls Zion to prayer. The verse is a manual of lament: rise, cry, pour out, lift up. Reformed pastoral theology has often returned here to teach the language of intercession in catastrophic times. The 'face of the LORD' is still sought, even as it has been hidden. The 'life of thy young children' grounds prayer in covenant concern for the next generation. Lament is not silence; it is hot, ordered, persevering speech to God.
Lam.3.1: 'I am the man that hath seen affliction by the rod of his wrath.' The central chapter shifts from the city's voice to a representative sufferer's. The acrostic intensifies: each letter governs three verses, sixty-six in all. 'I am the man'—a representative, perhaps the prophet, but also a figure that Christian exposition has rightly read as anticipating Christ, the Man of Sorrows who bore the rod of wrath in his people's stead. Reformed christology hears in this opening the deepest expression of substitutionary suffering. The 'rod' is not random; it is in the LORD's hand, lifted in covenant discipline that, on Christ, becomes covenant atonement.
Lam.3.8: 'Also when I cry and shout, he shutteth out my prayer.' The bleakest line in scripture's prayer-life: 'he shutteth out my prayer.' The sufferer's experience is of an unanswering heaven. Reformed pastoral theology refuses to deny this experience to the saints. Real prayer is sometimes met with felt silence, and the sufferer must still pray. The verse is honest about the dark night without making darkness ultimate. It stands within an acrostic—within the order God himself imposes—and within a chapter that will rise to 'great is thy faithfulness.' Felt silence is not final silence. The God who seems to shut out prayer hears even the prayer that complains of being shut out.
Lam.3.18: 'And I said, My strength and my hope is perished from the LORD.' The lowest point. Hope itself seems gone. Reformed expositors note the precise wording: 'I said'—this was confession of feeling, not pronouncement of fact. The next verses will reverse it. The verse is permission to name despair without surrendering to it. The believer may say, 'My hope is perished,' and yet, by remembering, find hope return. The acrostic continues; the alphabet does not stop because the heart has stopped. The faithfulness of God outlasts the failure of feeling. What the sufferer says here is true to his experience but not the truth of his case.
Lam.3.21: 'This I recall to my mind, therefore have I hope.' The hinge verse of the entire book. Hope is not a feeling that drifts in but a discipline of memory. The sufferer 'recalls'—deliberately, against the current of despair. Reformed pastoral theology has prized this verse as the architecture of Christian endurance. We do not generate hope; we remember covenant truth and find that hope returns. What follows in verses 22-23 is the content of remembrance: the LORD's mercies, his compassions, his faithfulness. The verse teaches the saint to fight despair not with optimism but with theology, not with new feelings but with old truths.
Lam.3.24: 'The LORD is my portion, saith my soul; therefore will I hope in him.' The Levites had no inheritance among Israel because the LORD was their portion (Numbers 18:20). Now the entire suffering remnant lays claim to that priestly privilege. Reformed soteriology hears here justification by union with the covenant Lord himself. Possessions can be plundered, cities razed, temples burned—but the portion of the soul cannot be taken. 'Therefore will I hope in him' is the logic of grace: because he is mine, hope is reasonable. Job said it ('though he slay me, yet will I trust in him'); the psalmist sang it ('the LORD is my portion'); the lamenter here clings to it.
Lam.3.31: 'For the LORD will not cast off for ever.' The word 'forever' transforms the lament. Yes, he has cast off—but not forever. Reformed covenant theology rests heavily on this verse. The chastening is severe but bounded; the discipline is real but not final. 'Though he cause grief, yet will he have compassion according to the multitude of his mercies' (v.32). Verse 33 adds, 'For he doth not afflict willingly nor grieve the children of men.' His afflicting is not the natural overflow of his heart; his compassion is. The verse undergirds Christian patience: cast off, yes; cast off forever, no.
Lam.3.55: 'I called upon thy name, O LORD, out of the low dungeon.' The depth of place ('low dungeon') is matched by the height of address ('thy name, O LORD'). The verse teaches that no pit is too deep for prayer. Reformed pastoral theology has often paired this with Jonah 2 and Psalm 130: out of the depths, the saint cries, and is heard. 'Thou hast heard my voice' (v.56) follows: prayer ascends from dungeon to throne. The God who casts down is the God who hears. Verse 57: 'Thou drewest near in the day that I called upon thee'—divine drawing-near is the answer to human calling-upon.
Lam.4.1: 'How is the gold become dim! how is the most fine gold changed!' The fourth lament returns to 'Ekah' but now mourns the dishonor of holy things. The 'stones of the sanctuary' are 'poured out in the top of every street.' Reformed exposition has heard here a warning about every age's profanation of the sacred—when temple stones become curbstones. The dimming of gold pictures the loss of glory: what was set apart has become common. The chapter will dwell on the suffering of children and the failure of every refuge. The acrostic is shorter now (two verses per letter); grief is wearing thin, but order still holds.
Lam.4.6: 'For the punishment of the iniquity of the daughter of my people is greater than the punishment of the sin of Sodom, that was overthrown as in a moment.' Sodom perished suddenly; Judah perishes slowly, watching her own destruction unfold. The verse refuses to flatter covenant people: their punishment is greater because their privilege was greater. Reformed exposition has applied this to the visible Church: nearer to grace, severer the discipline if grace is despised. Hebrews 10 echoes the principle. The verse rebukes any complacency built on past privilege; it is no kindness to suppose that long covenant history exempts a people from the sharper edge of the covenant sword.
Lam.4.13: 'For the sins of her prophets, and the iniquities of her priests, that have shed the blood of the just in the midst of her.' The fall is laid at the feet of corrupt religious leadership. Prophets and priests, who should have guarded covenant fidelity, instead spilled innocent blood. Reformed ecclesiology takes this with utmost seriousness: the corruption of teaching offices is not a minor matter but a national catastrophe. When watchmen sin, the city falls. Verse 14 pictures them wandering blind, defiled with blood. The verse is a perpetual warning against the abuse of spiritual office, and a call for ministers to remember whose blood was justly shed and whose was unjustly shed in their charge.
Lam.4.20: 'The breath of our nostrils, the anointed of the LORD, was taken in their pits, of whom we said, Under his shadow we shall live among the heathen.' The Davidic king, Yahweh's anointed, has been captured. The hope of national life under a covenant king has collapsed. Reformed expositors have heard here a longing that points beyond Zedekiah to a greater Anointed One. No earthly king could finally be 'the breath of our nostrils.' The verse exposes the inadequacy of every human messiah and prepares the heart for Christ, in whose shadow the nations truly live. The fall of the lesser anointed teaches the indispensability of the true Anointed.
Lam.4.22: 'The punishment of thine iniquity is accomplished, O daughter of Zion; he will no more carry thee away into captivity.' A startling note of consolation in chapter four: punishment has an end. Reformed remnant theology hears the bounded character of judgment. Exile will not be perpetual. Verse 22b adds that Edom's iniquity will be visited; covenant justice does not forget the gloating enemy. The verse encourages the captives that the same God who measured out wrath measures its end. Isaiah 40:2 will pick up the same theme: 'her warfare is accomplished.' Within lament, hope inserts itself by divine sovereign decree.
Lam.5.1: 'Remember, O LORD, what is come upon us: consider, and behold our reproach.' The final chapter abandons the acrostic—22 verses but no alphabet. Grief has stripped down to the bare cry: 'Remember.' Reformed prayer takes its bearings from this opening. The covenant Lord is asked to remember, not because he forgets, but because remembrance is his act of saving intervention (cf. Genesis 8:1, Exodus 2:24). 'Behold our reproach' assumes that he sees and is moved. The chapter is a corporate confession; every clause is plural. The shape of the prayer is the shape of all Christian intercession: name the suffering, ask God to remember, expect that he will act.
Lam.5.7: 'Our fathers have sinned, and are not; and we have borne their iniquities.' The generations are bound together in covenant solidarity. The verse is not a complaint of injustice but an acknowledgment of how covenant works: blessings and curses cross generational lines. Reformed covenant theology has explored this carefully—children bear consequences of parents' sin, yet are also called to repent and turn. The verse will not be misused as fatalism (Ezekiel 18 will balance it); it is an honest accounting of present pain. The remnant does not blame fathers and stop there; it owns the inheritance and brings it to God.
Lam.5.16: 'The crown is fallen from our head: woe unto us, that we have sinned!' The double clause is theology compressed: the visible glory has fallen, and the cause is sin. Reformed exposition will not separate the two confessions. The 'crown' is covenant honor, royal calling, the dignity of being God's people. It has fallen because sin cannot retain glory. 'Woe unto us'—the grief is owned, not blamed away. Yet to confess so plainly is itself a sign of grace. Where there is honest 'woe,' there is hope of restoration. The crown will be restored only by the King who wore the crown of thorns and earned the crown of glory.
Lam.5.19: 'Thou, O LORD, remainest for ever; thy throne from generation to generation.' Against every fallen crown stands the unfallen throne. The human crown lies in the dust; Yahweh's throne stands. Reformed worship grounds itself here: God's reign is not contingent on Judah's fortunes. He remains. The verse is the answer to verse 16. Lost crowns can be borne when the eternal throne endures. From this throne every restoration will proceed. Calvin's instinct: when all visible supports collapse, the saint sees the invisible throne and worships. The verse anticipates Hebrews 12:28—the kingdom that cannot be moved.
Lam.5.21: 'Turn thou us unto thee, O LORD, and we shall be turned; renew our days as of old.' The closing prayer is monergistic. Repentance is asked as a gift: 'Turn us, and we shall be turned.' Reformed theology of conversion stands precisely here—the human turning follows divine turning. Jeremiah 31:18 echoes the same. The remnant has learned that even repentance must be granted. 'Renew our days'—not return us to a vanished past, but make our days new. The verse is the lament's high point of hope: salvation, repentance, renewal—all from the LORD, all asked as gift. The prayer is bold because it is grounded in covenant grace, not human resolve.
Lev.1.3: The 'olah (burnt offering) consumed wholly upon the altar pictures total consecration; nothing is reserved. The worshipper presents a male without blemish, tamim, anticipating the spotless Lamb of God (1 Pet. 1:19). Reformed exegetes have long noted that this sacrifice is not for specific transgressions but for the offerer's general state as a sinner before a holy God. The voluntary 'at the door of the tent of meeting' underscores that access to God is by His appointed means alone, foreshadowing Christ as the only door (John 10:9). Worship is not creativity but obedience; the burnt offering teaches that God must be approached on His terms.
Lev.1.4: The laying on of the hand (samakh) is not a casual gesture but a deliberate transfer, identifying the worshipper with the victim so that 'it shall be accepted for him to make atonement (kipper) for him.' Calvin sees here the great gospel principle: substitution. The sinner's guilt passes to the innocent; the victim's acceptance returns to the sinner. This is the vocabulary of imputation in seed form, ripening fully when Isaiah declares the Servant 'wounded for our transgressions' (Isa. 53:5). Every Israelite who pressed his hand on a bull's head was rehearsing the doctrine that would crystallize at Calvary: Christ counted as us, that we might be counted in Him.
Lev.1.9: The phrase 'a pleasing aroma (reach nichoach) to the LORD' echoes Genesis 8:21 and reveals the goal of sacrifice: not merely averting wrath but pleasing the Father. Paul applies this very phrase to Christ's self-offering in Ephesians 5:2. The Reformed reading sees in the rising smoke a picture of the Spirit ascending Christ's perfect obedience to the Father's nostrils. The fire that consumes is not the flame of judgment alone but the holy delight of God in covenant fellowship restored. Worship that pleases God is worship offered through the merit of the Mediator, never through human ingenuity.
Lev.1.17: Even the poor man's pigeon was acceptable; God scaled the cost of approach to the offerer's means. The principle, later voiced by Paul (2 Cor. 8:12), is that God measures gifts by willingness, not weight. Yet the bird, like the bullock, must die. There is no economy of approach that bypasses blood. Reformed theology insists this universality, every Israelite from prince to pauper sacrificed, prefigures the one Mediator equally accessible to all the elect. The cross levels the ground at the foot of the altar.
Lev.2.1: The minchah (grain offering) alone is bloodless, yet it follows the burnt offering, never precedes it. Fellowship and consecration of life and labor rest upon prior atonement. The fine flour represents the fruit of human industry, sanctified by oil (the Spirit) and frankincense (prayer ascending). Reformed commentators note that good works can please God only when offered upon the foundation of accepted blood. Christ Himself is the true grain offering, perfectly consecrated humanity yielded to the Father. We bring our works as minchah only because His 'olah has been received.
Lev.2.11: Leaven (se'or) is forbidden because it pictures corruption working secretly through the whole lump (1 Cor. 5:6-8). Honey, though sweet, ferments. Both speak of the natural decay of fallen things. Christ's perfect humanity contained no leaven of sin, no native sweetness that turned to corruption. The minchah teaches that consecrated service must be purged of moral pollution. The Westminster divines saw here the necessity of mortifying indwelling sin if our worship is to ascend acceptably; even our best offerings need to be cleansed by the blood of the Greater Sacrifice.
Lev.2.13: Salt, the covenant element, preserves and seasons. 'The salt of the covenant of your God' must accompany every grain offering, binding the worshipper to inviolable fellowship. Numbers 18:19 and 2 Chronicles 13:5 develop the 'covenant of salt' as a permanent, indissoluble bond. Christ tells His disciples, 'Have salt in yourselves' (Mark 9:50). Reformed exegesis sees the unconditional faithfulness of God's covenant pledge sealed in this small grain, pointing to the everlasting covenant ratified in the blood of Christ which can neither corrupt nor be broken.
Lev.3.1: The shelamim (peace or fellowship offering) celebrates restored communion. Unlike the wholly consumed burnt offering, portions are eaten by priest and worshipper, creating a sacred meal in God's presence. This is the only sacrifice in which the offerer feasts; it is therefore the climactic sacrifice, the goal toward which all atonement aims. Reformed theology delights in this picture: God Himself, the priest, and the redeemed share one table. The Lord's Supper is its New Covenant counterpart, where Christ our Peace (Eph. 2:14) is the food of the redeemed in restored fellowship with the Father.
Lev.3.17: The prohibition of fat and blood is a perpetual statute, fundamental to Israel's worship. The fat (chelev) belonged to God as the choicest portion, and the blood, as the seat of life (17:11), was reserved for atonement. To consume them was to plunder God's altar. Reformed expositors see the moral lesson: God claims the best, not the leftovers, of every life He has redeemed. The principle is universalized for the church in Romans 12:1, where bodies, not bullocks, become living sacrifices, the fat and blood of our days surrendered to Him.
Lev.4.3: The chatat (sin offering) addresses unintentional sin (bishgagah), revealing that even unwitting transgression incurs guilt requiring blood. The anointed priest's sin brings guilt on the people, a sober reminder that those in covenantal headship sin representatively. This anticipates the doctrine of imputation in reverse: in Adam, his sin became ours; in Christ, our sin became His. The high view of sin here, that ignorance does not excuse, exposes the depth of human corruption. Total depravity is not first about willful rebellion but about a nature so warped that even our unintended acts pollute.
Lev.4.13: When the whole congregation sins unintentionally, corporate guilt accrues. There is no 'private' Christianity in the Old Testament; covenant solidarity binds the people together before God. Reformed federalism finds rich soil here: the church, like Israel, sins corporately and must repent corporately. A bull is required, the same magnitude as for the high priest, showing the gravity of communal transgression. This points forward to Christ, who as the corporate head of His people made one corporate atonement adequate for all His body.
Lev.4.20: 'And the priest shall make atonement (kipper) for them, and they shall be forgiven (salach).' Here is the gospel announcement in nascent form: forgiveness flows from atonement; atonement is mediated by priest and blood. The verb kipper, traditionally rendered 'cover,' more accurately denotes 'to ransom by substitute' or 'to wipe clean.' Reformed scholarship has emphasized that atonement is not Israel achieving forgiveness but God granting it through the means He Himself provides. The passive 'they shall be forgiven' confirms divine monergism: God forgives; man receives.
Lev.5.1: Hearing a public adjuration and withholding testimony incurs guilt. Sin includes silence in the face of evil. James 4:17 echoes this: 'Whoever knows the right thing to do and fails to do it, for him it is sin.' Sins of omission are not lesser sins. The Reformed doctrine of comprehensive obedience requires not only abstention from evil but active witness to truth. Every silence in the courtroom of the world's questions, when faithfulness demands speech, is laid to the believer's account and must be brought under the cleansing blood.
Lev.5.7: 'If he cannot afford a lamb, he shall bring two turtledoves or two pigeons.' The descending scale of offerings, lamb, birds, fine flour, ensures no Israelite is excluded from atonement by poverty. Mary, mother of our Lord, brought this very offering at her purification (Luke 2:24), evidence of the Holy Family's poverty and fulfillment of the law's tender provision. Reformed soteriology celebrates this: the gospel is not for the spiritual elite but for all whom the Father gives the Son, prince and pauper alike, all approaching by the same blood.
Lev.5.17: Even unrecognized guilt requires offering. 'Though he did not know it, yet he is guilty and shall bear his iniquity.' Conscience is not the sole arbiter of sin; God's law is. This rebukes the modern reduction of morality to felt guilt. Reformed theology insists that the standard is objective, rooted in God's character, and that hidden sin is real sin. Psalm 19:12, 'Who can discern his errors? Declare me innocent from hidden faults,' arises from this truth. Christ's atonement covers not only known iniquities but the vast hinterland of sins we never noticed.
Lev.6.13: 'Fire shall be kept burning on the altar continually; it shall not go out.' The perpetual flame symbolizes the unceasing efficacy of atonement and the constant readiness of God to receive His people. Reformed expositors have applied this to the heart: zeal for the LORD must not flicker. Yet ultimately the verse points to Christ's once-for-all sacrifice whose value never diminishes; the 'fire' of His atoning merit burns eternally before the Father. Hebrews 7:25 declares He 'always lives to make intercession,' the abiding altar-fire of the new covenant.
Lev.6.27: 'Whatever touches its flesh shall be holy.' The sin offering communicates holiness by contact. This is the inverse of impurity, which spreads by contact in 11-15. In Christ, the principle is gloriously realized: the leper, the bleeding woman, the dead girl, all touched by the Holy One, contract not His defilement but His sanctity. The Reformed doctrine of union with Christ finds an Old Testament shadow here: to touch Christ is to be made holy; to be in Christ is to share His consecration before the Father.
Lev.7.11: The thanksgiving (todah) offering, a subset of the peace offering, is brought when no specific deliverance is being celebrated, simply gratitude for God's faithfulness. The rabbis said all sacrifices will cease in the messianic age except the todah. The Christian eucharist (literally 'thanksgiving') fulfills this expectation: at the Lord's Table we celebrate the great deliverance with thanks, knowing that propitiation is finished but praise is unending. Gratitude is not optional in Reformed piety; it is the chief response of the redeemed to sovereign grace.
Lev.8.6: The consecration of Aaron and his sons begins with washing, then clothing, then anointing, then blood. The order is theologically rich: cleansing precedes commissioning, righteousness precedes service. Reformed soteriology recognizes the parallel: those whom God calls He first cleanses (1 Cor. 6:11), then clothes in Christ's righteousness, then anoints with the Spirit. No man qualifies himself for priesthood; all is from above. The water bath (rachatz) prefigures regeneration, applied externally yet pointing to inner renewal accomplished by sovereign grace.
Lev.8.23: Blood is applied to the right ear, thumb, and great toe of Aaron, his hearing, his working, his walking, all consecrated by atonement. There is no compartment of priestly life untouched by the blood. The Reformed doctrine of the lordship of Christ over every faculty is here in figure: ears to hear God's word, hands to do His work, feet to walk His paths, all bought with blood. The believer-priesthood (1 Pet. 2:9) inherits this comprehensive consecration; nothing is secular in the redeemed life.
Lev.9.24: 'And fire came out from before the LORD and consumed the burnt offering.' At the inauguration of the priesthood, divine fire publicly authenticates the appointed worship. This is not magic but covenantal endorsement, repeated at Solomon's temple (2 Chr. 7:1) and Pentecost (Acts 2:3). God Himself ignites true worship; man cannot manufacture acceptance. Reformed liturgy insists on the regulative principle for this very reason: God authorizes; man does not improvise. The fire that fell here will fall fully on Christ at Calvary, where divine judgment consumes the perfect Sacrifice and divine pleasure crowns it.
Lev.10.1: Nadab and Abihu offered 'strange fire' ('esh zarah), fire God 'had not commanded.' The sin was not adding to worship from base motives only, but presuming to worship outside the divine prescription. Calvin built the regulative principle on this text: in worship, what is not commanded is forbidden. The severity of the judgment (devouring fire) reveals the gravity of presumption. God who is approachable through His own appointed means is unapproachable on any other terms. Liberty in worship is the liberty to obey, not to innovate.
Lev.10.3: 'Among those who are near me I will be sanctified, and before all the people I will be glorified.' The closer the proximity, the higher the standard. Pastors, elders, parents, all who stand near to God, must reckon with this principle. James 3:1 echoes it: 'Not many of you should become teachers, my brothers, for you know that we who teach will be judged with greater strictness.' Aaron's silence (vayyiddom Aharon) is the only proper response to the inscrutable judgments of a holy God. Job's submission and Aaron's quiet alike confess that God is just in all His works.
Lev.10.10: The priest must 'distinguish between the holy and the common, and between the unclean and the clean.' Discernment is a priestly duty. The collapse of these categories is the collapse of biblical religion. In a culture that cannot say what is sacred or what is sinful, the church's prophetic task is exactly this distinguishing work. Reformed theology has always insisted on the antithesis: light and darkness, kingdom and world, holy and profane. To blur them is not love but treason against the God who Himself separates.
Lev.11.44: 'For I am the LORD your God. Consecrate yourselves therefore, and be holy, for I am holy.' Peter cites this verse in 1 Peter 1:16, applying it directly to Christians. The dietary laws have passed away (Mark 7:19), but the principle abides: holiness is the inevitable response to a holy God's redemptive claim. The order is critical: 'I am the LORD your God' precedes 'be holy.' Indicative grounds imperative. Reformed sanctification is never the basis but always the fruit of God's claim upon His people. We do not become holy to be His; we are His, therefore we become holy.
Lev.12.6: After childbirth a woman brings a lamb for a burnt offering and a pigeon for a sin offering. Childbirth is not sinful, yet the new mother is reminded that she has brought another fallen child into a fallen world. The sin offering acknowledges the depth of original sin pervading even the most natural and joyful events. Reformed doctrine sees Adam's federal headship implicated here; every birth replicates the inherited corruption. Mary's offering of two pigeons (Luke 2:24) reflects her poverty, but her Son needed no sin offering, the only child born uncorrupted by Adam's transgression.
Lev.13.45: The leper must cry 'Unclean, unclean!' This prescribed self-stigma is not cruelty but covenantal mercy: the community is protected, and the sufferer is positioned to seek the priest's verdict and possible cleansing. Spiritually, leprosy throughout Scripture pictures sin: progressive, polluting, isolating, ultimately fatal apart from divine intervention. Christ's healing of lepers (Mark 1:41) by touch reverses the contagion: His holiness overcomes their uncleanness rather than their uncleanness defiling Him. The gospel is the news that the Holy One has reached out and the cry 'Unclean!' has been answered with 'I will; be clean.'
Lev.14.7: The cleansing of the leper involves two birds, one slain, one set free over running water sprinkled with the slain bird's blood. This is one of the most explicit typological pictures in Leviticus: death and resurrection, atonement and liberation. The released bird, dipped in blood, soars away, the cleansed sinner counted righteous and free. Reformed theology marvels at the union: the slain bird and the freed bird are presented together, like Christ crucified and risen, the basis and the announcement of justification. Romans 4:25 captures the whole: 'delivered up for our trespasses and raised for our justification.'
Lev.15.31: 'Thus you shall keep the people of Israel separate from their uncleanness, lest they die in their uncleanness by defiling my tabernacle.' The chapter's exhaustive uncleanness laws climax here: holiness is not optional because God dwells in the camp. The principle scales in the New Covenant: the church is God's temple (1 Cor. 3:16), and our individual bodies are temples of the Spirit (1 Cor. 6:19). What seems excessive in Leviticus is actually mercy, God taking pains to keep a sinful people alive in proximity to Himself.
Lev.16.2: Aaron 'shall not come at any time into the Holy Place inside the veil before the mercy seat (kapporet)... lest he die.' Access to God is restricted to one man, on one day, by one means: blood. The tearing of the temple veil at Christ's death (Matt. 27:51) is the explosion of this restriction; what Aaron approached trembling, believers now enter with confidence (Heb. 10:19-22). The very limitation here magnifies the grace of the new covenant. The mercy seat, where God promised to meet His people, is fulfilled in Christ Himself, the hilasterion (Rom. 3:25).
Lev.16.8: Two goats: one for the LORD, one for Azazel. The two together display the dual aspect of one atonement, propitiation toward God and expiation away from sinners. Reformed exegetes have rightly resisted reading 'Azazel' as a demon receiving the goat; the goat carries sin away into uninhabited wilderness, picturing iniquity removed beyond recovery. Both goats together prefigure Christ, who in one offering both satisfied divine justice and bore sin away as far as east from west (Ps. 103:12). One Mediator, two completed actions.
Lev.16.16: 'Thus he shall make atonement (kipper) for the Holy Place, because of the uncleannesses of the people of Israel.' Atonement reaches even to the sanctuary itself, polluted by the sins of those it serves. The cosmic scope of sin's defilement, and of Christ's atonement, is here in seed. Hebrews 9:23 explicitly applies this: 'the heavenly things themselves' required 'better sacrifices.' The Reformed gospel preaches a Christ whose blood cleanses persons, the church, and the cosmos itself, restoring the entire defiled creation to fellowship with God.
Lev.16.21: Aaron lays both hands on the goat and confesses 'all the iniquities of the people of Israel, and all their transgressions, all their sins.' The threefold expression, iniquities ('avon), transgressions (pesha'), sins (chatta'ah), exhausts the categories of human evil. The same triad describes Christ's burden in Isaiah 53. Confession identifies sin with the substitute; substitute carries sin away. Calvin saw here the very mechanics of imputation: every sin specified, every sin transferred. The believer's confession over Christ in faith is the New Covenant counterpart to Aaron's two hands on the head of the goat.
Lev.16.30: 'On this day shall atonement be made for you to cleanse you. You shall be clean before the LORD from all your sins.'
