Ps.76.10: 'Surely the wrath of man shall praise thee' — perhaps the most magnificent statement of providence in the Psalter. Even rebellion is conscripted into doxology; the residue (sheerit) of wrath God girds upon Himself as ornament. Joseph's brothers, Pharaoh, Sennacherib, Pilate — all meant evil, but God meant it for good (Gen 50:20; Acts 4:27-28). This verse undergirds the Westminster doctrine that God ordains whatsoever comes to pass, including sin, without being the author of it. The cross is the supreme instance: man's worst act became God's greatest praise.
Ps.77.2: 'My sore ran in the night, and ceased not' — Asaph's hand stretched out (niggerah) all night describes prayer that will not let go even when consolation is refused. The soul refuses comfort precisely because it craves God Himself, not God's gifts. Reformed piety knows this dark night: the believer wrestles, like Jacob, until the breaking of the day. The discipline of unanswered prayer is not abandonment but pedagogy. Christ Himself prayed in Gethsemane with strong crying and tears (Heb 5:7), sanctifying the believer's midnight vigil and guaranteeing that no sleepless petition is wasted.
Ps.77.10: 'This is my infirmity' — the turning point of the psalm. Asaph names his own weakness (challothi, my piercing or wound) and stops accusing God. The years of the right hand of the Most High become his anchor. Spiritual recovery in Reformed pastoral theology proceeds along this exact line: confess infirmity, then remember works. Memory is a means of grace. The believer rehearses redemption — Red Sea, Calvary, empty tomb — until faith catches fire again. The cure for the dark thought is not introspection but doxological recollection.
Ps.77.19: 'Thy way is in the sea, and thy path in the great waters, and thy footsteps are not known.' At the Red Sea God walked where no track remains. Providence often leaves no visible trail; we see the deliverance only after the waters close. The believer is shepherded (v. 20) by Moses and Aaron, types of the prophet-priest Christ, even when the divine footsteps are hidden. This is the doctrine of deus absconditus (the hidden God) wedded to deus revelatus: His ways are unsearchable, yet He has set His Shepherd over us.
Ps.78.2: 'I will open my mouth in a parable' — Matthew quotes this of Christ (Matt 13:35), making Asaph's didactic poem a prophecy of the gospel mashal. The whole psalm is catechesis: the church teaches the next generation by rehearsing redemptive history. Reformed covenant theology stands or falls on this principle — children are taught the works of God so that they might set their hope in God (v. 7). The parable mode discloses to insiders and conceals from the willfully ignorant; it is grace and judgment in one form.
Ps.78.38: 'But he, being full of compassion, forgave their iniquity, and destroyed them not.' The structural refrain of the psalm: Israel rebels, God chastens, God relents. The verb kipper (atoned, forgave) anticipates the Day of Atonement and ultimately Calvary. Why does the covenant survive Israel's repeated apostasy? Not because Israel is faithful but because God remembered that they were but flesh (v. 39). The same covenant mercy that spared the wilderness generation now keeps the saints through the blood of the better Mediator.
Ps.78.70: 'He chose David his servant' — the climactic election. The psalm narrates the rejection of Shiloh and Ephraim and the choosing of Judah, Zion, and David. Reformed theology hears here unconditional election particularized: God selects the shepherd boy from the sheepfolds to feed Jacob His people. Every Davidic king points forward to the Son of David, the Good Shepherd who lays down His life for the flock. The integrity of His heart and the skillfulness of His hands (v. 72) belong supremely to Christ.
Ps.79.9: 'Help us, O God of our salvation, for the glory of thy name; and deliver us, and purge away our sins, for thy name's sake.' The plea is theocentric: not 'because we deserve' but 'for thy name's sake.' This is the deepest argument in prayer — God's own glory bound up with the rescue of His people. The verb kipper (purge, atone) again anticipates the cross. Asaph teaches the church to pray gospel prayers: deliverance grounded in God's reputation, atonement sought in pure mercy.
Ps.80.3: 'Turn us again, O God, and cause thy face to shine; and we shall be saved' — the threefold refrain (vv. 3, 7, 19) intensifies as the psalm advances, naming God successively as Elohim, Elohim Tzevaot, and Yahweh Elohim Tzevaot. Salvation is not self-generated but received when the divine face turns. This is the priestly benediction (Num 6:25) prayed back to God in lament. Reformed soteriology: regeneration is monergistic. We do not turn ourselves; God turns us, and the shining of His face in the face of Jesus Christ (2 Cor 4:6) is our salvation.
Ps.80.17: 'Let thy hand be upon the man of thy right hand, upon the son of man whom thou madest strong for thyself.' Ish yeminkha (man of Thy right hand) and ben adam (son of man) converge messianically. Israel as collective son fails; the true Son of Man, seated at the right hand (Ps 110:1), succeeds. The vine that was burned with fire (v. 16) becomes the True Vine (John 15) only through the Son. Calvin saw here a direct prophecy of Christ as the head and surety of the church.
Ps.81.10: 'Open thy mouth wide, and I will fill it.' The covenant invitation reaches its emotional peak. God's grievance is not that Israel asked too much but that they asked too little. Reformed prayer theology embraces this: large petitions honor a large God. Yet Israel would not (v. 11), and so He gave them up to their own counsels (v. 12) — the judicial hardening that Romans 1 expounds. Grace offered and refused becomes wrath ratified. The gospel call still resounds: open wide and be filled with the bread of life.
Ps.82.6: 'I have said, Ye are gods; but ye shall die like men.' Christ cites this verse in John 10:34-36 to defend His own deity by an a fortiori argument. Human judges, called elohim because they wield delegated authority, will fall; how much more is He whom the Father sanctified and sent properly called Son of God. Reformed exegesis preserves both senses: the rebuke of unjust magistrates and the typological foreshadowing of the true Judge. Verse 8 — 'Arise, O God, judge the earth' — is fulfilled in the Son to whom all judgment is committed (John 5:22).
Ps.84.2: 'My soul longeth, yea, even fainteth for the courts of the LORD' — the verb kalethah (is consumed, exhausted) is the language of holy desire. The Korahite singer pines not for ritual but for the living God. Reformed worship cherishes this affection: the means of grace are precious because the God of the means is precious. Christ has become the true sanctuary; the believer who longs for His presence will find that blessed are they who dwell in His house, ever praising Him (v. 4).
Ps.84.5: 'Blessed is the man whose strength is in thee; in whose heart are the ways of them' — mesilloth (highways) inscribed in the heart turn pilgrimage into a state of soul. The man who passes through the Valley of Baca (weeping) makes it a well (v. 6); tears become springs because the pilgrim travels by faith. This is the Reformed doctrine of perseverance: the saints go from strength to strength (v. 7) until every one in Zion appears before God. Christ is the way; in Him the pilgrim is already arrived even while still walking.
Ps.84.10: 'A day in thy courts is better than a thousand' — the sweetest comparative in the Psalter. The doorkeeper's threshold trumps the tents of wickedness. Spurgeon: 'one hour in heaven outweighs an eternity in hell.' Reformed piety is not stoic indifference to earthly goods but a reordered loves: communion with God becomes the summum bonum. The threshold of the temple was the place where one could be near without yet being inside; in Christ the veil is rent, and we enter boldly.
Ps.85.10: 'Mercy and truth are met together; righteousness and peace have kissed each other.' The four divine attributes that seemed at war over fallen sinners embrace at the cross. Mercy without righteousness is sentimentality; righteousness without mercy is condemnation. Calvin and the Reformed tradition see here the heart of the atonement: God is just and the justifier (Rom 3:26). Truth springs from the earth (the incarnation, Bethlehem dust) and righteousness looks down from heaven (the Father's vindication of the Son in resurrection).
Ps.86.11: 'Unite my heart to fear thy name' — yached levavi, gather my divided heart into one. The petition is for a sanctified affection that no longer wavers between God and idols. Reformed sanctification is precisely this: the gradual integration of all our desires under the lordship of Christ. James 1:8 indicts the double-minded man; David here prays the cure. The undivided heart fears the name, and the name unites the heart — a beautiful circle of grace.
Ps.87.5: 'And of Zion it shall be said, This and that man was born in her.' Civic registries of the heavenly city list believers from Rahab (Egypt), Babylon, Philistia, Tyre, and Cush. Zion is the mother of all who are born again (Gal 4:26). The new birth (yulad bah) is not ethnic but spiritual; the Most High Himself shall establish her. This psalm is among the strongest Old Testament hints of Gentile inclusion in covenant grace, fulfilled when the gospel goes to the nations.
Ps.88.6: 'Thou hast laid me in the lowest pit, in darkness, in the deeps.' Heman's psalm is the darkest in the Psalter, ending without resolution: 'darkness is my only companion' (v. 18, lit.). Yet it remains canonical prayer. The Reformed pastor learns here that not every lament must end in sunlight; sometimes faith holds on by the bare fact that one is still addressing God. Christ in Gethsemane and Golgotha entered this exact pit and bore its full horror, so that no believer's darkest night is now untenanted by the Savior.
Ps.89.3: 'I have made a covenant with my chosen' — the great covenant psalm of the Davidic promise (2 Sam 7). The verb karat brit (cut a covenant) recalls blood ratification. Verse 28: 'My mercy will I keep for him for evermore, and my covenant shall stand fast with him.' The psalm then collapses into lament (vv. 38ff) as David's house lies in ruins. Yet the covenant does not fail; it is fulfilled in great David's greater Son, whose throne is established forever (Luke 1:32-33).
Ps.89.34: 'My covenant will I not break, nor alter the thing that is gone out of my lips.' This verse is the granite floor of Reformed covenant theology. God's word does not return void. Even when the Davidic kingdom appears destroyed, the divine oath remains. Hebrews 6:17-18 builds upon this immutability: two unchangeable things — God's promise and His oath — give the believer strong consolation. The covenant of grace is sealed in the blood of the eternal covenant (Heb 13:20).
Ps.90.2: 'From everlasting to everlasting, thou art God' — me'olam ad olam attah El. Moses opens the Psalter's only prayer attributed to him with eternity. The doctrine of aseity grounds the entire psalm: God is, before mountains were brought forth. The transience of human life (vv. 5-6, like grass that is cut down) is meaningful only against the backdrop of God's everlastingness. Reformed worship begins here — with the eternal I AM who alone is the dwelling place of His people.
Ps.90.12: 'So teach us to number our days, that we may apply our hearts unto wisdom.' The petition is not for longer life but for wiser life. Numbering days is a moral discipline: it shrinks vanity and enlarges eternity. The Hebrew chokmat-levav (heart-wisdom) is practical, not merely speculative. Calvin: 'we shall never arrive at true wisdom unless we seriously reflect upon the brevity of human life.' The Christian numbers his days in light of the day of Christ.
Ps.90.17: 'Let the beauty of the LORD our God be upon us: and establish thou the work of our hands.' Mortal labor terminates in dust unless the eternal God establishes it. The repetition (twice in one verse) is desperate and beautiful. Only what God makes permanent survives. In Christ, our labor is not in vain in the Lord (1 Cor 15:58); the resurrection vindicates every cup of cold water given in His name.
Ps.92.12: 'The righteous shall flourish like the palm tree: he shall grow like a cedar in Lebanon.' The Sabbath psalm pictures the saints as evergreen, fruitful in old age (v. 14). The palm bears fruit when most trees fail; the cedar is enduring strength. Planted in the house of the LORD (v. 13), the believer draws covenant nourishment. Reformed perseverance is not bare endurance but flourishing growth. The sap of grace runs through the cedar's heart until the last day.
Ps.93.1: 'The LORD reigneth' — YHWH malakh inaugurates the great enthronement psalms (93, 95-99). Calvin: this is the gospel summary in two words. The world is established and cannot be moved (tikkon tevel) because the King reigns. Mountains of providence break against the throne but never overturn it. Christ's resurrection is the historical ratification: He has been declared Son of God with power (Rom 1:4) and now reigns until every enemy is under His feet.
Ps.95.7: 'Today, if ye will hear his voice, harden not your hearts.' Hebrews 3-4 makes this verse the locus of the doctrine of perseverance and the warning against apostasy. 'Today' is the gospel's urgent now. The wilderness generation is the cautionary type: they heard but did not believe and so did not enter the rest. The Sabbath rest remains for the people of God in Christ, the true Joshua. Reformed pastors press this 'today' upon every congregation.
Ps.96.1: 'Sing unto the LORD a new song' — shir chadash recurs through the Psalter and climaxes in Revelation 5:9. The new song is sung because of new redemption. The Gentiles are summoned (v. 3) to join the chorus; this is missionary doxology. Calvin: 'the kingdom of Christ is here described.' The whole earth becomes the choir loft when the gospel goes forth.
Ps.97.10: 'Ye that love the LORD, hate evil.' Love and hatred are not opposed but conjoined: the same affection that delights in God recoils from sin. Reformed sanctification rejects sentimental love severed from moral discrimination. Christ Himself loved righteousness and hated wickedness (Ps 45:7; Heb 1:9). The believer's hatred of sin is the underside of his love for the Holy One.
Ps.98.1: 'O sing unto the LORD a new song; for he hath done marvellous things: his right hand, and his holy arm, hath gotten him the victory.' Isaac Watts caught the Christological reading in 'Joy to the World': the new song celebrates Messiah's coming. The right hand and holy arm anticipate the suffering Servant of Isaiah 53:1, the arm of the LORD revealed. Resurrection is the cosmic victory; every creature is summoned to applaud (vv. 7-9).
Ps.102.25: 'Of old hast thou laid the foundation of the earth: and the heavens are the work of thy hands.' Hebrews 1:10-12 applies vv. 25-27 directly to the Son. The eternal Creator addressed in this lament is Christ. The heavens grow old like a garment but Thou art the same (attah hu). Christological reading is not allegory imposed but apostolic exegesis received. The Mediator of redemption is the Architect of creation.
Ps.103.1: 'Bless the LORD, O my soul' — barki nafshi. David addresses his own soul, refusing passive devotion. Self-exhortation is a Reformed pattern: the believer preaches to himself. All that is within me is summoned to bless the holy name. The benefits enumerated (vv. 3-5) — forgiveness, healing, redemption, crowning, satisfying — are gospel benefits in nuce.
Ps.103.12: 'As far as the east is from the west, so far hath he removed our transgressions from us.' East and west are infinitely separated; they never meet. Unlike north and south (which converge at the poles), east and west are an unending parallel. So is the distance between the justified sinner and his sins after Calvary. Calvin: 'God absolves us so completely that no remembrance of our sins remains.' The metaphor itself preaches imputation.
Ps.104.24: 'O LORD, how manifold are thy works! in wisdom hast thou made them all.' Creation is wisdom incarnate. The psalm parallels Genesis 1, celebrating each domain. Reformed theology of creation: nature is a theater of God's glory (Calvin). Verse 30: 'Thou sendest forth thy spirit, they are created.' The Spirit who hovered at the first creation is the same Spirit who births the new creation in Christ.
Ps.104.34: 'My meditation of him shall be sweet: I will be glad in the LORD.' Meditation (siach) is not emptying but filling — pondering God's works until the heart sings. The chiastic close of the psalm (Bless the LORD, O my soul, framing v. 1 and v. 35) demonstrates that creation theology terminates in doxology, not deism.
Ps.105.8: 'He hath remembered his covenant for ever.' The historical psalm rehearses Genesis-Exodus through covenant lens. God remembers (zakar) — not because He forgot, but because He acts upon what He has spoken. The covenant with Abraham (v. 9) is the engine of redemptive history. Reformed covenant theology reads here the unity of the Old and New Testaments under one covenant of grace.
Ps.106.1: 'O give thanks unto the LORD; for he is good: for his mercy endureth for ever.' The opening hodu refrain becomes the great liturgical antiphon (Ps 107, 118, 136). Ki tov, ki le'olam chasdo — He is good, His chesed endures forever. This is the heartbeat of biblical worship: God's character (good) plus God's covenant love (chesed) extending eternally. Reformed liturgy can do no better than echo this refrain.
Ps.106.45: 'And he remembered for them his covenant, and repented according to the multitude of his mercies.' Anthropomorphic language (vayinnachem, He relented) does not mean God changes His mind metaphysically; it means He responds covenantally to repentance. Reformed theology distinguishes God's secret will (immutable) and His revealed will (relational). The covenant remembered is the same covenant that holds the church today.
Ps.107.2: 'Let the redeemed of the LORD say so' — ge'ulei YHWH. The verb ga'al carries the kinsman-redeemer theme of Boaz and ultimately Christ. The psalm gathers four pictures of trouble (lost in desert, prisoners, sick fools, sailors in storm) and four cries answered. Each receives the refrain: 'Then they cried unto the LORD in their trouble, and he saved them out of their distresses.' Reformed soteriology summarized: grace meets every variety of human ruin.
Ps.107.20: 'He sent his word, and healed them.' The personal Word that heals foreshadows the eternal Word made flesh (John 1:14). The same Word that flung galaxies into being speaks healing into our diseased souls. Calvin: 'he sets forth the wonderful efficacy of God's word.' Reformed worship is Word-centered because the Word is Christ-centered, and Christ is healing.
Ps.110.4: 'Thou art a priest for ever after the order of Melchizedek.' Hebrews 5-7 expounds this verse extensively. Melchizedek's priesthood is non-Levitical, non-genealogical, royal, and eternal — perfectly fitted to Christ. The oath (nishba YHWH velo yinnachem, the LORD has sworn and will not relent) makes this priesthood absolutely irrevocable. The Reformed doctrine of Christ's threefold office reaches its priestly apex here: He intercedes forever, our High Priest who lives to save.
Ps.110.7: 'He shall drink of the brook in the way: therefore shall he lift up the head.' The conquering King stoops to drink mid-battle and rises stronger. Some Reformed readers see Christ's incarnation and humiliation: He drank of the cup of suffering en route, and now His head is lifted up in resurrection and ascension. The brook (nachal) is the wadi of testing through which the Messiah passed, as our Savior must.
Ps.113.1: 'Praise ye the LORD. Praise, O ye servants of the LORD' — the opening of the Egyptian Hallel (Pss 113-118), sung at Passover. This is the hymn Christ likely sang with the disciples in the upper room (Matt 26:30). The fivefold hallelu in two verses centers worship on the Name. Servants (avadim) — once slaves of Pharaoh, now bondservants of YHWH — set the tone of redemption-praise.
Ps.113.7: 'He raiseth up the poor out of the dust, and lifteth the needy out of the dunghill.' Hannah's song echoes here (1 Sam 2:8), and Mary's Magnificat re-echoes it (Luke 1:52). The God of reversal lifts beggars to thrones. The cross is the supreme dunghill from which the King of kings was raised to glory. Reformed soteriology delights in this divine exchange: He came down to lift us up.
Ps.115.1: 'Not unto us, O LORD, not unto us, but unto thy name give glory.' The double negative emphatic — lo lanu, lo lanu — is the soul of Reformed doxology. Calvin made this verse a personal motto. Soli Deo gloria flows from this fountain. Idol-makers seek glory; servants of the living God flee glory back to its proper owner.
Ps.116.12: 'What shall I render unto the LORD for all his benefits toward me?' The question is rhetorical: nothing can be rendered as payment, only as response. The cup of salvation (v. 13, kos yeshu'ot) and the calling on the Name reveal the answer: the believer responds to grace with eucharist. This verse anchors Reformed sacramental theology — the Lord's Supper as covenant response, not contribution.
Ps.116.15: 'Precious in the sight of the LORD is the death of his saints.' Yakar (precious, costly) in God's eyes. The deaths of His covenant people are not random losses but treasured passages. Christ's own death is supremely precious; the saints' deaths are precious because they are united to His. Calvin: God so values His own that not one falls without His care.
Ps.117.1: 'O praise the LORD, all ye nations' — the shortest psalm and the centerpiece of the Bible by chapter count. Romans 15:11 cites it to prove Gentile inclusion in covenant praise. Two verses suffice to summarize redemptive history: nations summoned, mercy great, truth enduring. Reformed missiology lives here: the goyim are not strangers but worshipers in waiting.
Ps.118.24: 'This is the day which the LORD hath made; we will rejoice and be glad in it.' In context, the day is the day of resurrection-vindication of the rejected stone. The Christian church has long applied it to the Lord's Day. Every Sabbath the gathered saints celebrate the day God made by raising His Son. The verb asah (made) is creational; resurrection is new creation.
Ps.118.26: 'Blessed be he that cometh in the name of the LORD.' Sung by the crowds at Christ's triumphal entry (Matt 21:9), and by Christ Himself prophetically (Matt 23:39): 'Ye shall not see me henceforth, till ye shall say, Blessed is he that cometh in the name of the Lord.' The Hosanna of Palm Sunday is the eschatological cry of Israel's final return. Christ comes in the Name and as the Name.
Ps.119.18: 'Open thou mine eyes, that I may behold wondrous things out of thy law.' Niphla'ot (wonders) hidden in plain text. The Word remains a closed book without the Spirit's opening of the eyes. Reformed illumination: the same Spirit who breathed the Scripture opens it to the heart.
Ps.119.25: 'My soul cleaveth unto the dust: quicken thou me according to thy word.' Daleth: the soul stuck (davqah) to the dust requires resurrection (chayyeni). The Word that raised Lazarus quickens the depressed soul. Reformed pastoral theology: the Word is means of revival, not mere instruction.
Ps.119.32: 'I will run the way of thy commandments, when thou shalt enlarge my heart.' Heart-enlargement (tarchiv libbi) is divine work; the running is human response. Sanctification cooperates with grace but does not originate it. Augustine prayed: 'command what you will and grant what you command.'
Ps.119.50: 'This is my comfort in my affliction: for thy word hath quickened me.' Zayin stanza testifies that the Word is reviving in the midst of trouble. Reformed bibliology: Scripture functions not as escape but as revival in the suffering itself.
Ps.119.54: 'Thy statutes have been my songs in the house of my pilgrimage.' Pilgrimage (megurai) is the Christian life. Doctrine becomes melody when the pilgrim walks. Reformed psalmody: the law itself is sung, not merely recited.
Ps.119.67: 'Before I was afflicted I went astray: but now have I kept thy word.' Teth stanza. Affliction is sanctifying pedagogy. The believer testifies, with Calvin, that adversity teaches obedience. The cross precedes the crown, in the Master and the disciple alike.
Ps.119.71: 'It is good for me that I have been afflicted; that I might learn thy statutes.' The startling confession that suffering is good. Heidelberg Catechism Q.26: even adversity comes by God's fatherly hand. The Reformed doctrine of providence sweetens every wound.
Ps.119.75: 'I know, O LORD, that thy judgments are right, and that thou in faithfulness hast afflicted me.' Yodh: faithfulness afflicts. God's covenant love does not exclude pain; it includes the pain that prunes. Hebrews 12 expounds this stanza in apostolic prose.
Ps.119.89: 'For ever, O LORD, thy word is settled in heaven.' Lamedh stanza, the doctrinal mountaintop of Psalm 119. The Word is le'olam, eternal; it is nitzav (stationed, settled) in heaven. Reformed bibliology: Scripture rests on the immutable speaking of God, not on the fluctuating reception of men.
Ps.119.130: 'The entrance of thy words giveth light; it giveth understanding unto the simple.' Pe stanza. The petach (opening, doorway) of God's word floods the soul. The simple (peti, the gullible) becomes wise. Reformed perspicuity: Scripture is clear enough for the unlearned to receive saving knowledge.
Ps.119.165: 'Great peace have they which love thy law: and nothing shall offend them.' Shin stanza. Peace (shalom) flourishes in those whose loves are ordered to the Word. Stumbling-blocks lose power over law-lovers. Reformed assurance is rooted here: love of Scripture and love of God reinforce each other.
Ps.119.176: 'I have gone astray like a lost sheep; seek thy servant; for I do not forget thy commandments.' The psalm's last word is paradox: a wanderer who remembers, a servant who needs seeking. Reformed soteriology: even diligent saints must be sought by the Shepherd. The psalm of Torah-love ends in grace alone.
Ps.121.1: 'I will lift up mine eyes unto the hills, from whence cometh my help.' The first Song of Ascents in the keener sense. The hills could be threats (bandit-haunts) or false sanctuaries (high places). The pilgrim looks past them to the Maker of heaven and earth (v. 2). Reformed theology: help does not come from creation but from the Creator.
Ps.121.4: 'Behold, he that keepeth Israel shall neither slumber nor sleep.' Pagan deities like Baal must be roused (1 Kings 18:27); YHWH never tires. The keeper (shomer) imagery saturates the psalm (six occurrences). Reformed providence: God's preservation is unceasing, personal, and covenantal.
Ps.122.1: 'I was glad when they said unto me, Let us go into the house of the LORD.' Pilgrim joy, communal worship. Reformed ecclesiology rejects the lone-ranger Christian: the saints together climb to Zion. The liturgical 'Let us' is the church's constant invitation.
Ps.122.6: 'Pray for the peace of Jerusalem.' Sha'alu shelom Yerushalayim — note the wordplay between sha'alu (ask) and shalom and yeru-shalayim. Christological reading: the true Jerusalem is the city above (Gal 4:26), and Christ is its peace (Eph 2:14). To pray for Jerusalem's peace is to pray for the consummation of the kingdom.
Ps.123.2: 'Behold, as the eyes of servants look unto the hand of their masters... so our eyes wait upon the LORD our God.' The posture of expectant dependence. Reformed prayer is upward-looking, hand-watching, mercy-seeking. The slave waits for command; the maid for provision. The believer is both.
Ps.124.8: 'Our help is in the name of the LORD, who made heaven and earth.' The verse Reformed worshipers have used as call to worship for centuries. Creation undergirds redemption: the One who made all can save His own. Calvin's Geneva prayed this every Sunday.
Ps.126.5: 'They that sow in tears shall reap in joy.' Agricultural pilgrimage from sowing-tears to reaping-joy. The cross-resurrection pattern is built into creation itself. Reformed eschatology: tears precede joy, but joy is the harvest. Christ's tears at Lazarus's tomb yielded resurrection joy, prefiguring His own.
Ps.128.1: 'Blessed is every one that feareth the LORD; that walketh in his ways.' Fear and walk: orthodoxy and orthopraxy. The wisdom-psalm grants household blessing — fruitful wife, olive-shoot children. Reformed family theology blossoms here: the godly home as a means of covenant continuance.
Ps.130.3: 'If thou, LORD, shouldest mark iniquities, O Lord, who shall stand?' De Profundis. Reformation hymn par excellence — Luther's 'Aus tiefer Not.' The rhetorical question demands the gospel: justification by imputed righteousness. No one stands on their own merits.
Ps.130.4: 'But there is forgiveness with thee, that thou mayest be feared.' Forgiveness produces, not abolishes, fear. This is the paradox of grace: pardoned sinners fear more deeply, not less. Calvin: the goodness of God leads to repentance and reverent awe simultaneously.
Ps.130.7: 'Let Israel hope in the LORD: for with the LORD there is mercy, and with him is plenteous redemption.' Padut (redemption) is overflowing, abundant. Christ's atonement is not bare sufficient but superabundant. Reformed soteriology: His blood overflows the demand of justice.
Ps.131.1: 'LORD, my heart is not haughty, nor mine eyes lofty.' David disclaims pride. The psalm of weaned-child humility is one of the briefest. Reformed sanctification cultivates this littleness: the soul like a weaned child, not striving for greatness, content with the Father.
Ps.132.11: 'The LORD hath sworn in truth unto David; he will not turn from it; Of the fruit of thy body will I set upon thy throne.' Peter quotes this in Acts 2:30 to argue for Christ's resurrection enthronement. The Davidic oath finds its yes and amen in the risen Son of David seated at the right hand.
Ps.134.1: 'Behold, bless ye the LORD, all ye servants of the LORD, which by night stand in the house of the LORD.' The final Song of Ascents addresses the night-watch. Worship continues when the daytime crowd has gone home. Reformed nocturnal piety: the saints bless God in the dark hours.
Ps.135.6: 'Whatsoever the LORD pleased, that did he in heaven, and in earth, in the seas, and in all deep places.' Westminster Confession 3.1 echoes this verse: God ordains whatsoever comes to pass. Divine pleasure (chafetz) is the engine of providence. Reformed sovereignty saturates every domain.
Ps.136.1: 'O give thanks unto the LORD; for he is good: for his mercy endureth for ever.' The Great Hallel. Twenty-six refrains of ki le'olam chasdo (His covenant love is forever). The repetition is not redundancy but liturgy: every act of God in creation and redemption is rehearsed under one banner — chesed forever.
Ps.136.23: 'Who remembered us in our low estate: for his mercy endureth for ever.' The personal middle of the universal hymn. From cosmic creation to personal recollection: the same chesed that hung the stars stoops to remember slaves. The incarnation is the supreme remembering: God remembered us in our lowest estate by becoming low Himself.
Ps.137.1: 'By the rivers of Babylon, there we sat down, yea, we wept, when we remembered Zion.' The exile psalm. The harps hung on willows (v. 2) silenced by grief. Reformed theology of pilgrimage: the church in this world is in Babylonian exile, longing for the heavenly Zion. Tears are appropriate now; songs await the homecoming.
Ps.138.6: 'Though the LORD be high, yet hath he respect unto the lowly.' The transcendent God draws near to the low. The Magnificat reverberates here. Reformed theology of condescension: God's exaltedness does not preclude but enables His nearness. The proud He knows from afar; the humble He stoops to.
Ps.139.7: 'Whither shall I go from thy spirit? or whither shall I flee from thy presence?' Divine omnipresence personalized. There is no neutral space, no escape, no privacy from God. For the rebel this is terror; for the redeemed it is comfort. The Spirit who searches us in v. 1 holds us in v. 10.
Ps.139.13: 'For thou hast possessed my reins: thou hast covered me in my mother's womb.' Sanctity of life rooted in divine fashioning. The verb sakak (covered, knit together) describes weaving. Reformed bioethics: every embryo is divinely woven. Christ Himself was thus knit (Luke 1:31), sanctifying every womb.
Ps.143.2: 'Enter not into judgment with thy servant: for in thy sight shall no man living be justified.' Paul cites this in Romans 3:20 and Galatians 2:16 to establish that no flesh is justified by works of the law. The verse is Old Testament gospel: justification cannot be ex operibus. The believer pleads for non-judgment grounded in mercy alone.
Ps.143.10: 'Teach me to do thy will; for thou art my God: thy spirit is good; lead me into the land of uprightness.' The Spirit's leading is sanctifying pedagogy. Reformed pneumatology: the Spirit teaches, leads, sanctifies, and brings into the level land. Romans 8:14 echoes: those led by the Spirit are sons.
Ps.145.8: 'The LORD is gracious, and full of compassion; slow to anger, and of great mercy.' The credal echo of Exodus 34:6. The Reformed God is no capricious tyrant but the covenant God whose attributes Moses heard. This refrain pulses through the Old Testament; the gospel is its consummation.
Ps.145.16: 'Thou openest thine hand, and satisfiest the desire of every living thing.' The open hand of God is the meal table of the cosmos. Common grace flows from this verse. Reformed theology distinguishes saving grace from common grace, but both flow from the same open hand. Christ is the satisfying bread.
Ps.146.3: 'Put not your trust in princes, nor in the son of man, in whom there is no help.' The first of the final Hallel (146-150). Princes die; the Lord reigns forever. Reformed political theology: kings and presidents are not saviors. Only the Son of Man at the Father's right hand is salvation.
Ps.146.8: 'The LORD openeth the eyes of the blind: the LORD raiseth them that are bowed down: the LORD loveth the righteous.' The miracles of Christ literally fulfilled this psalm: blind eyes opened, lame raised, righteous loved. When John the Baptist asked, 'Art thou he?' Jesus pointed to fulfillment of texts like this (Matt 11:5).
Ps.148.1: 'Praise ye the LORD. Praise ye the LORD from the heavens: praise him in the heights.' Cosmic doxology summons every created thing — angels, sun, moon, sea creatures, mountains, beasts, kings, young men, old men, children. Reformed creational theology: every creature has a praise-vocation. The redeemed lead the creation choir back to its proper Conductor.
Ps.149.4: 'For the LORD taketh pleasure in his people: he will beautify the meek with salvation.' Yefa'er (will beautify, glorify) the meek. The doctrine of glorification: God adorns His humble ones. Salvation is not bare rescue but cosmetic beautification — the believer made splendid in the righteousness of Christ.
Ps.150.1: 'Praise ye the LORD. Praise God in his sanctuary: praise him in the firmament of his power.' The final psalm orchestrates an exuberant Hallelujah. Sanctuary on earth and firmament above are simultaneously praising. Reformed worship is no quiet affair when fully grasped: it is symphonic, embodied, cosmic.
Ps.150.6: 'Let every thing that hath breath praise the LORD. Praise ye the LORD.' Kol haneshamah — every breath. The Psalter ends not with petition but with universal praise. The first man received neshamah from God's mouth (Gen 2:7); the last verse returns it. Christological consummation: every knee bows, every tongue confesses, every breath praises Christ Jesus, to the glory of God the Father (Phil 2:10-11).
Ps.92.1: 'It is a good thing to give thanks unto the LORD' — tov lehodot. The Sabbath psalm declares thanksgiving morally good. Worship is not optional aesthetic but ethical fitness. Reformed Lord's Day theology: gathering to give thanks is the proper exercise of redeemed humanity.
Ps.94.19: 'In the multitude of my thoughts within me thy comforts delight my soul.' Sar'appai (anxious thoughts, branchings of the mind) are met by tanchumekha (Thy consolations). Reformed pastoral theology: anxious overthinking is countered not by suppression but by superior delights. The soul is comforted by truths, not silenced.
Ps.99.5: 'Exalt ye the LORD our God, and worship at his footstool; for he is holy.' Three times the psalm declares 'he is holy' (vv. 3, 5, 9), echoing Isaiah's seraphic vision. Reformed worship is fundamentally trinitarian holiness adoration. The footstool (Ark, temple, ultimately the earth — Isa 66:1) is the meeting place of holy God and bowing creature.
Ps.101.2: 'I will behave myself wisely in a perfect way... I will walk within my house with a perfect heart.' David's royal vow extends to private life. Public office and household walk must be congruent. Reformed integrity theology: the home is the proving ground of professed piety.
Ps.108.13: 'Through God we shall do valiantly: for he is it that shall tread down our enemies.' Compound divine-human agency. We do valiantly; He does the treading. Reformed sanctification: monergistic in regeneration, synergistic in sanctification, but always with God as primary agent.
Ps.109.4: 'For my love they are my adversaries: but I give myself unto prayer.' Reviled for love, the psalmist responds with prayer (literally, va'ani tefillah, 'and I am prayer'). Christ embodies this: hated for loving, He prayed for His enemies (Luke 23:34). Reformed imitation of Christ: weaponize prayer when the world weaponizes hatred.
Ps.111.10: 'The fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom.' Reshit chokmah. Proverbs 9:10 echoes verbatim. Reformed epistemology: knowledge does not begin in autonomous reason but in covenantal reverence. All true wisdom flows from this fountain.
Ps.112.7: 'He shall not be afraid of evil tidings: his heart is fixed, trusting in the LORD.' Heart fixed (nakhon libbo) — settled, established. Reformed assurance: the fixed heart has nothing to fear from breaking news. Trust in YHWH is the anchor in every storm of headlines.
Ps.114.7: 'Tremble, thou earth, at the presence of the Lord, at the presence of the God of Jacob.' Earth quakes at God's coming. The exodus psalm pictures sea fleeing, Jordan retreating, mountains skipping. Reformed eschatology: this trembling is rehearsal for the final shaking when Christ returns.
Ps.115.16: 'The heaven, even the heavens, are the LORD's: but the earth hath he given to the children of men.' Creation mandate echoed. Stewardship not ownership. Reformed dominion theology: humanity governs as covenant tenants under the heavenly Owner.
Ps.119.62: 'At midnight I will rise to give thanks unto thee.' Cheth stanza. Nocturnal worship interrupts sleep. Reformed devotion is not confined to convenient hours; love finds odd times for thanksgiving.
Ps.119.140: 'Thy word is very pure: therefore thy servant loveth it.' Tsade stanza. Tzerufah (refined, tested) word is loved precisely for its purity. Reformed bibliology: Scripture's perfection makes it lovable, not burdensome.
Ps.120.1: 'In my distress I cried unto the LORD, and he heard me.' Opening Song of Ascents. The pilgrim begins not in Jerusalem but in distress. Reformed pilgrimage: the journey to God begins in honest cry from far country.
Ps.122.7: 'Peace be within thy walls, and prosperity within thy palaces.' Shalom encloses Jerusalem. The triple naming — peace within walls, prosperity within palaces, peace upon you (v. 8) — saturates the city in shalom. Christ is the peace; the church is the city; eternity is the consummation.
Ps.129.4: 'The LORD is righteous: he hath cut asunder the cords of the wicked.' Israel's neck-yoke severed. Reformed liberation theology, properly so called: Christ's righteousness cuts the cords of sin and death. Easter is the cosmic cord-cutting.
Ps.139.16: 'Thine eyes did see my substance, yet being unperfect; and in thy book all my members were written.' The book of God records each prenatal day. Reformed predestination meets embryology: God's foreknowledge encompasses every chromosome and every appointed day.
Ps.142.3: 'When my spirit was overwhelmed within me, then thou knewest my path.' Overwhelm and being known coexist. Reformed providence: the believer's confusion does not preclude the Lord's clarity. He knows the path even when we do not.
Ps.144.3: 'LORD, what is man, that thou takest knowledge of him! or the son of man, that thou makest account of him!' Echo of Psalm 8. Hebrews 2 reads this Christologically: the Son of Man in whom God takes account is supremely Christ, who deigned to take human nature.
Ps.147.11: 'The LORD taketh pleasure in them that fear him, in those that hope in his mercy.' Two affections combine — fear and hope. Reformed assurance is not presumption but reverent hope in chesed. God's pleasure rests on this dual disposition.
Ps.150.3: 'Praise him with the sound of the trumpet: praise him with the psaltery and harp.' Eight instruments enumerated across vv. 3-5. Reformed worship debates aside, the psalm clearly sanctions instrumental praise. Every cultural sound is summoned. Heaven joins the orchestra; let earth tune up.
Ps.106.21: 'They forgat God their saviour, which had done great things in Egypt.' Forgetfulness as the root of idolatry. Reformed memorial worship: the Lord's Supper is given because we forget; the calendar of grace exists because the heart drifts from past mercies.
Ps.107.9: 'For he satisfieth the longing soul, and filleth the hungry soul with goodness.' Christ takes up this language in the Beatitudes (Matt 5:6) and in 'I am the bread of life' (John 6). The hungry soul has divine guarantee of satisfaction. Reformed soteriology: empty hands receive full grace.
Ps.107.43: 'Whoso is wise, and will observe these things, even they shall understand the lovingkindness of the LORD.' The psalm closes by making chesed the object of contemplation. Wisdom is the discipline of beholding God's covenant love in providence. Reformed sanctification: wisdom and chesed-recognition are inseparable.
Ps.118.17: 'I shall not die, but live, and declare the works of the LORD.' Christ's own resurrection cry hidden in the Hallel. The verse spoken by Israel becomes the testimony of the risen Messiah. Reformed Christology: the resurrection is the foundation of the believer's living and declaring.
Ps.139.10: 'Even there shall thy hand lead me, and thy right hand shall hold me.' The omnipresent God leads in the dark places. Reformed providence: hand and right hand are personal, gripping, securing. There is no flight from God's grip — for the believer, blessedly so.
Ps.143.5: 'I remember the days of old; I meditate on all thy works; I muse on the work of thy hands.' Memorial therapy. Three verbs — zakar, hagah, soach — saturate the dejected soul with God's past acts. Reformed pastoral practice: in despair, rehearse redemption history.
Ps.85.8: 'I will hear what God the LORD will speak: for he will speak peace unto his people.' The believer's posture: silent listening before speaking petition. The God who speaks shalom is gospel preacher. Reformed ministry: the Word read and preached is God speaking peace to His covenant people.
Ps.116.7: 'Return unto thy rest, O my soul; for the LORD hath dealt bountifully with thee.' Self-address commanding return to menuchah. Hebrew gamal alai (dealt fully with me) is grace language. Reformed sanctification: the soul tells itself to rest in remembered grace. Christ is our rest (Matt 11:28).
Ps.96.13: 'For he cometh, for he cometh to judge the earth: he shall judge the world with righteousness, and the people with his truth.' Double 'he cometh' — first and second advent. The Lord who came in flesh will come again as Judge. Reformed eschatology rejoices: the Judge is the Savior, and His judgment is righteous truth.
Ps.97.1: 'The LORD reigneth; let the earth rejoice; let the multitude of isles be glad thereof.' Distant coastlands summoned to the Davidic kingdom's celebration. Reformed missiology: the King's reign extends to every shore, and every shore has reason to rejoice.
Ps.105.4: 'Seek the LORD, and his strength: seek his face evermore.' Seeking God's face (paneh) is intimate communion. Reformed worship terminates not in benefits received but in the Face beheld. Moses asked, 'Show me thy glory'; Christ shows us the Father.
Ps.107.30: 'Then are they glad because they be quiet; so he bringeth them unto their desired haven.' Storm-tossed sailors brought to mechoz (haven). Reformed eschatology: every storm of providence ends in the desired haven if the saint is in Christ. The harbor lights are eternal.
Ps.96.5: 'For all the gods of the nations are idols: but the LORD made the heavens.' Elilim — nothings, vanities — over against the Creator. Reformed apologetic: idols may be elaborate culturally but are ontologically empty. Only the heaven-making God deserves worship.
Ps.87.7: 'All my springs are in thee.' Of Zion this is sung. The believer locates every fountain — joy, salvation, hope — in the city whose builder and maker is God. Reformed ecclesiology: the church, the heavenly Jerusalem, holds every spring of life because Christ Himself is the fountain.
Ps.110.3: 'Thy people shall be willing in the day of thy power.' Nedavot (free-will offerings, willingness) on yom cheilekha (the day of Thy might). Reformed soteriology resolves the apparent paradox: divine power produces human willingness. Effectual calling makes the elect freely embrace Christ in His enthronement.
Ps.118.14: 'The LORD is my strength and song, and is become my salvation.' The verse Moses sang at the Red Sea (Exod 15:2), Isaiah echoed (Isa 12:2), and the Hallel rehearses. Three exodus-points anchor the Reformed gospel: deliverance through water, prophetic recapitulation, and messianic culmination.
Ps.118.27: 'God is the LORD, which hath shewed us light: bind the sacrifice with cords, even unto the horns of the altar.' Sacrifice bound to altar — Calvary foreshadowed. Christ, the willing Lamb, was bound and offered up. Reformed atonement theology sees here the consensual binding of the Substitute.
Ps.84.7: 'They go from strength to strength, every one of them in Zion appeareth before God.' Pilgrim ascent gains rather than loses energy as it nears Zion. Reformed perseverance: grace builds, doesn't deplete. Every saint, without exception (kol echad), arrives in the divine presence.
Ps.103.13: 'Like as a father pitieth his children, so the LORD pitieth them that fear him.' Avhood as analogy for divine compassion. Reformed family theology: human fatherhood is dim icon of divine. The fear of the LORD is met with fatherly racham (womb-deep mercy).
Ps.149.1: 'Sing unto the LORD a new song, and his praise in the congregation of saints.' New song in qehal chasidim (assembly of holy ones). Reformed corporate worship: praise blossoms in covenant gathering, not solitary spirituality. Saints together sing what individuals could never muster.
Ps.150.5: 'Praise him upon the loud cymbals: praise him upon the high sounding cymbals.' Tziltzelei shema (cymbals of hearing) and tziltzelei teru'ah (cymbals of acclamation). Reformed exuberance: the Psalter's final summons is loud, multilayered, unrestrained. The Sabbath of God's people is sonic celebration.
Ruth.1.1: The narrator situates the account 'in the days when the judges ruled,' a period characterized by covenantal apostasy and the refrain that 'every man did what was right in his own eyes' (Judg. 21:25). The famine in Bethlehem (literally 'house of bread') is no mere agricultural misfortune but a covenantal sanction promised in Deuteronomy 28 for national disobedience. Elimelech ('My God is King') ironically flees the land of promise for Moab, a nation cursed in Deuteronomy 23:3-6. The journey eastward, like Lot's, foreshadows judgment. Yet the providential hand of God will use this exile to weave a redemptive thread reaching to David and ultimately to Christ.
Ruth.1.6: Naomi 'arose' upon hearing that the LORD had 'visited' (paqad) His people by giving them bread. This verb paqad signifies God's gracious covenantal intervention, the same word used of His visiting Sarah (Gen. 21:1) and ultimately fulfilled in Christ's incarnation (Luke 1:68, 78). Bread returns to the 'house of bread,' a tangible sign that covenant mercy has not been abrogated despite the chastening famine. Naomi's return is not merely geographic but covenantal repentance, a turning back toward the land of promise and the God who governs it. The text quietly affirms that the LORD's faithfulness to His people drives every providence.
Ruth.1.8: Naomi blesses her daughters-in-law: 'The LORD deal kindly (hesed) with you.' This is the first occurrence of hesed in the book and signals its dominant theme. Naomi recognizes that human hesed is rooted in and answerable to divine hesed; she invokes God to repay what she cannot. Reformed covenant theology emphasizes that all true loyal-love among creatures derives from and reflects the prior loyal-love of the covenant God. Naomi's prayer that the LORD reward her daughters-in-law for kindness shown 'to the dead, and to me' anticipates the ultimate eschatological recompense given to those who have served Christ in the least of His brethren (Matt. 25:40).
Ruth.1.13: Naomi protests, 'the hand of the LORD is gone out against me.' Her language echoes the plague narratives (Exod. 9:3) and Job's lament (Job 19:21). She rightly attributes her affliction to YHWH's sovereign hand, refusing the modern temptation to detach providence from particular sufferings. Yet her interpretation of that hand is incomplete: she sees only judgment where covenant mercy is also at work. The Reformed pastor learns here that saints in deep grief may misread providence even while affirming sound theology. Faith holds firm to God's sovereignty even when His ways are inscrutable (Rom. 11:33).
Ruth.1.16: Ruth's confession is one of the most remarkable conversions in the Old Testament. Her words 'thy people shall be my people, and thy God my God' constitute a covenant oath, paralleling the proselyte formula of later Judaism. As a Moabitess she renounces the gods of Chemosh and embraces YHWH by sovereign grace, becoming a forerunner of the Gentile ingathering prophesied in Isaiah 56. Her hesed toward Naomi mirrors and is grounded in the divine hesed she has come to know. This is not mere familial loyalty but regenerative faith expressed in covenantal cleaving (dabaq), the same verb used of marriage in Genesis 2:24.
Ruth.1.17: Ruth invokes a self-maledictory oath: 'The LORD do so to me, and more also, if aught but death part thee and me.' Her use of the covenant name YHWH demonstrates that she has personally appropriated Israel's God. This is faith reckoned for righteousness in the line of Abraham (Gen. 15:6; Rom. 4). The Westminster Confession's doctrine of saving faith is illustrated here: assent, knowledge, and trust expressed in covenantal commitment. Ruth's incorporation into Israel anticipates Ephesians 2:12-13, where those once 'strangers from the covenants of promise' are 'made nigh by the blood of Christ.'
Ruth.1.20: Naomi rejects her name (meaning 'pleasant') and renames herself Mara ('bitter'), echoing Israel's wilderness experience at Marah (Exod. 15:23). Her invocation of 'Shaddai' (the Almighty) reflects a patriarchal title used in seasons of suffering and providential testing (cf. Job's repeated use). Naomi's theology is impeccable even in grief: she attributes her affliction not to chance but to God's sovereign hand. Yet her assessment that she returned 'empty' is mistaken, for Ruth stands beside her as the instrument of redemption. This tension between felt providence and actual providence is a hallmark of Reformed pastoral theology.
Ruth.1.22: The chapter concludes with the providential note that they came to Bethlehem 'in the beginning of barley harvest.' This temporal marker is heavy with significance: the barley harvest coincided with Passover and the Feast of Firstfruits (Lev. 23:10-14). Resurrection imagery hovers over the narrative, a reversal from famine to abundance, from emptiness to fullness. The narrator, in characteristic Hebrew economy, signals that God is orchestrating the timing of redemption. Nothing in the chronology is incidental; the sovereign Lord of harvests is preparing the field where Boaz and Ruth will meet.
Ruth.2.1: The introduction of Boaz is theologically loaded. He is described as 'a mighty man of wealth' (gibbor chayil), a phrase used of valiant warriors and men of covenantal substance. He is 'of the kindred of Elimelech,' establishing the kinship requirement essential to the go'el (kinsman-redeemer) institution of Leviticus 25 and Deuteronomy 25. The narrator deliberately delays Ruth's encounter with him, building the providential drama. Boaz emerges as a type of Christ: kinsman by incarnation, mighty in wealth, willing and able to redeem. The Reformed reader sees here the careful preparation of redemptive history that culminates in Bethlehem's greater Son.
Ruth.2.3: The Hebrew text contains a delightful idiom: 'her hap was to light on a part of the field belonging unto Boaz.' Literally, 'her chance chanced upon.' The narrator employs apparent randomness to highlight providence's hidden hand. This is the doctrine of concursus: secondary causes operate genuinely while God's eternal decree governs the outcome (WCF 5.2). What appears as coincidence to Ruth is the orchestration of the Almighty. Calvin's commentary on providence finds vivid illustration here, what the world calls fortune is the disposing of God. Every step of the elect is ordered by Him who works all things after the counsel of His will (Eph. 1:11).
Ruth.2.4: Boaz arrives greeting his workers, 'The LORD be with you,' and they respond, 'The LORD bless thee.' This exchange reveals the spiritual atmosphere of his estate. Boaz is no nominal Israelite but a man whose business is conducted in covenantal speech. The greeting 'YHWH be with you' is the same blessing pronounced upon Gideon (Judg. 6:12) and ultimately fulfilled in Immanuel, 'God with us.' Reformed theology of vocation finds here a paradigm: the godly employer sanctifies labor by invoking God's presence over it. Boaz models the integrated piety the Westminster Larger Catechism describes in its exposition of the moral law in everyday life.
Ruth.2.7: The foreman reports that Ruth had requested to glean 'after the reapers among the sheaves,' and 'hath continued even from the morning until now.' The Mosaic law required that gleanings be left for the poor and the stranger (Lev. 19:9-10; Deut. 24:19). Ruth approaches as both. Her diligence and humility, gleaning rather than presuming, illustrate the posture of grace-receivers. She does not demand but seeks permission, recognizing her place as a covenant outsider made dependent on covenant mercy. The Reformed reader sees the believer's posture before God: empty hands extended, receiving what the Master of the harvest provides.
Ruth.2.10: Ruth falls on her face and asks why she has found 'grace' (chen) in Boaz's eyes, 'seeing I am a stranger' (nokriyyah). The term emphasizes her foreignness, her lack of covenantal claim. Her question anticipates the gospel's logic: grace by definition is undeserved favor extended to the alien. Ephesians 2:12-13 finds its narrative seedbed here. Boaz's favor toward this Moabitess prefigures Christ's reception of Gentile sinners. The doctrine of unconditional election shines through: there is no merit in Ruth, only need; no claim, only grace received in faith.
Ruth.2.12: Boaz pronounces a benediction: 'The LORD recompense thy work, and a full reward be given thee of the LORD God of Israel, under whose wings thou art come to trust.' The metaphor of taking refuge under YHWH's 'wings' (kanaph) recurs in Psalm 36:7 and 91:4. Strikingly, Boaz himself will become the answer to his own prayer when he later spreads his 'wing' (kanaph, same word, 3:9) over Ruth in the redemption covenant. The pastor-redeemer becomes the means by which God shelters the seeker. So Christ both prays for His people and is Himself their refuge, the Mediator who answers His own intercession.
Ruth.2.20: Naomi exclaims, 'Blessed be he of the LORD, who hath not left off his kindness (hesed) to the living and to the dead.' This is the theological centerpiece of the chapter. Hesed is covenantal loyal-love, steadfast faithfulness rooted in covenant obligation yet exceeding mere duty. Naomi recognizes that Boaz's kindness is the visible manifestation of God's hesed working through covenant means. She then reveals the term that will dominate the rest of the book: 'the man is near of kin unto us, one of our next kinsmen' (mig'aleinu, from go'el). Redemption theology now moves to center stage.
Ruth.2.23: The chapter closes with Ruth gleaning 'unto the end of barley harvest and of wheat harvest,' approximately seven weeks spanning Passover to Pentecost. This providentially controlled interval prepares the ground for the threshing-floor encounter of chapter 3. The narrator's restraint is remarkable: nothing happens between Boaz and Ruth during this season. The Reformed reader notes how God's redemptive purposes unfold according to His timetable, not ours. Patience in providence is a means of sanctification. The harvests gather both grain and the elect bride.
Ruth.3.1: Naomi seeks 'rest' (manoach) for Ruth, the same concept that frames Israel's covenantal hope (Deut. 12:9; Heb. 4). Rest in Scripture is not mere repose but settled covenantal security, a foretaste of eschatological Sabbath. Naomi's maternal concern for Ruth's marriage is, at a deeper level, a theological quest for shalom under the wings of Israel's God. The pursuit of marital rest in Boaz typifies the church's pursuit of spiritual rest in Christ, who declares, 'Come unto me... and I will give you rest' (Matt. 11:28).
Ruth.3.9: Ruth's request, 'spread therefore thy skirt (kanaph) over thine handmaid; for thou art a near kinsman (go'el),' is the pivotal moment of the book. The verb deliberately echoes Boaz's earlier prayer (2:12) and the marriage symbolism of Ezekiel 16:8. Ruth is not seducing but invoking covenantal redemption: she identifies herself by her need (handmaid) and Boaz by his office (go'el). This is faith pleading covenant promises. The Reformed reader sees the believer's posture toward Christ: appealing to His office, His willingness, His covenantal kinsmanship secured through incarnation. Christ alone is qualified, willing, and able to spread His covering over the church.
Ruth.3.10: Boaz responds, 'Blessed be thou of the LORD, my daughter: for thou hast shewed more kindness (hesed) in the latter end than at the beginning.' Ruth's hesed is identified again as the engine of the narrative, but here it is matched and exceeded by Boaz's hesed. Covenantal love begets covenantal love. The Reformed doctrine of sanctification appears: the believer's love is responsive, derivative, and ultimately Spirit-wrought, flowing from God's prior love (1 John 4:19). Boaz's commendation of Ruth's covenantal preference for him over young men foreshadows Christ's commendation of those who choose Him above worldly attractions.
Ruth.3.12: Boaz's frank admission, 'howbeit there is a kinsman nearer than I,' introduces the legal complication that will be resolved in chapter 4. The Mosaic order of redemption (Lev. 25:48-49) must be honored. Boaz refuses to short-circuit covenant law even for love's sake. This is no obstacle but a stage upon which redemptive righteousness will be displayed. Christ similarly fulfills all righteousness, satisfying every legal demand before redeeming His bride. There is no antinomian shortcut in the covenant; redemption proceeds along the channel of God's righteous order.
Ruth.3.13: Boaz's oath, 'as the LORD liveth,' invokes the covenant name as guarantor. He pledges to settle the matter by morning, displaying decisive resolve characteristic of a true redeemer. His phrase 'if he will not do the part of a kinsman to thee, then will I do the part of a kinsman to thee' demonstrates willingness, the indispensable trait of a go'el. A redeemer must be near, able, and willing; Boaz exhibits all three. So Christ, our greater Boaz, swore by Himself (Heb. 6:13) to redeem His people, and His willingness is established by the eternal counsel of the Trinity.
Ruth.3.18: Naomi's counsel, 'Sit still, my daughter... for the man will not be in rest, until he have finished the thing this day,' is a profound word of faith. Ruth's part is to wait; Boaz's part is to act. This captures the Reformed soteriological grammar: the believer rests in the finished work of the Redeemer who 'will not rest' until redemption is accomplished. Christ at Gethsemane and Calvary did not rest until He could declare, 'It is finished.' The bride's rest is grounded in the Redeemer's restless love. Sanctification flows from this prior justifying work.
Ruth.3.11: Boaz declares, 'all the city of my people doth know that thou art a virtuous woman' (eshet chayil). The phrase is the feminine counterpart to Boaz's own description as a 'mighty man of wealth' (gibbor chayil, 2:1). Significantly, the next canonical occurrence of eshet chayil is in Proverbs 31:10, immediately following the canonical position of Ruth in the Hebrew arrangement. The narrator presents Ruth as the embodied exemplar of the virtuous woman whom Solomon's mother extols. Reformed theology of the imago Dei recovered through grace finds illustration here: the foreigner once outside the covenant becomes the paradigm of covenant womanhood.
Ruth.4.1: Boaz goes up to the gate, the legal forum of the ancient Near East. The narrator's note that the nearer kinsman 'came by' uses language reminiscent of providential coincidence. God orchestrates the legal proceedings as surely as He orchestrated the field encounter. The gate is where elders sit, witnesses gather, and judgments are rendered, an apt setting for a transaction that prefigures the cosmic courtroom where Christ secures redemption. Hebrews 13:12 notes that Jesus suffered 'without the gate,' yet His redemption is publicly attested before angels and men.
Ruth.4.5: Boaz reveals the catch: redeeming the field requires also marrying Ruth the Moabitess 'to raise up the name of the dead upon his inheritance.' This combines the go'el institution (Lev. 25) with levirate marriage (Deut. 25:5-10). The redemption is comprehensive: property and posterity, land and lineage. So Christ's redemption is not merely transactional but personal, securing both the inheritance and the people. He purchases not real estate alone but a bride for Himself, raising up a name that shall not be cut off (Isa. 56:5).
Ruth.4.7: The narrator pauses to explain the ancient custom: 'a man plucked off his shoe, and gave it to his neighbour: and this was a testimony in Israel.' The shoe-removal symbolized the transfer of the right of redemption, originally connected with walking the boundaries of one's inheritance (cf. Deut. 11:24). The narrator's parenthetical aside ('Now this was the manner in former time') indicates that he writes for a later audience for whom the custom is archaic. Reformed exegetes note that the legal externals of redemption typify the deeper spiritual realities: redemption involves the transfer of rights, the public certification of the new owner, and the witnessed standing of the redeemed.
Ruth.4.9: Boaz's declaration before the elders, 'Ye are witnesses this day,' establishes the legal standing of the redemption. Witnesses are constitutive of biblical covenants (Deut. 19:15; Heb. 10:28). The transaction is comprehensive: 'all that was Elimelech's, and all that was Chilion's and Mahlon's.' Boaz assumes the entire estate of the bankrupt house. This anticipates the Pauline declaration that Christ 'was made a curse for us' (Gal. 3:13), assuming our debts in their entirety. The Heidelberg Catechism's first answer, that we 'belong... to my faithful Saviour Jesus Christ,' rests on the same logic of total covenantal acquisition that Boaz here enacts.
Ruth.4.6: The nearer kinsman declines: 'I cannot redeem it for myself, lest I mar mine own inheritance.' His self-interested calculation exposes him as unwilling, though able. The contrast with Boaz is stark and Christological. The unnamed kinsman represents the law itself, near, related, yet impotent to redeem without diminishing the redeemer's own estate (cf. Rom. 8:3, 'what the law could not do'). Only Boaz, whose name means 'in him is strength,' is willing to suffer apparent loss to gain the bride. Christ, the willing Redeemer, did not count equality with God a thing to be grasped but emptied Himself (Phil. 2:6-7).
Ruth.4.10: Boaz publicly declares, 'Moreover Ruth the Moabitess... have I purchased to be my wife.' The verb qanah ('purchase, acquire') is the language of covenantal acquisition. Ruth is redeemed by purchase, paralleling the church 'purchased with his own blood' (Acts 20:28). The public, witnessed nature of this transaction matters: covenant redemption is never private or hidden but openly proclaimed before witnesses. The gospel is heralded, not whispered. So too our redemption was effected publicly at Calvary, witnessed by heaven and earth, sealed by the Spirit.
Ruth.4.11: The elders pronounce a remarkable blessing invoking Rachel and Leah, 'which two did build the house of Israel,' and connecting this marriage to Ephrathah and Bethlehem. The blessing is prophetic, anticipating the messianic significance of Bethlehem (Mic. 5:2). The community recognizes that this marriage is no private matter but a covenantal event with national implications. Reformed covenant theology emphasizes the corporate dimension of redemption: individual marriages, families, and lineages serve the larger purposes of God's covenant of grace stretching from Eden to the eschaton.
Ruth.4.13: 'So Boaz took Ruth, and she was his wife... and the LORD gave her conception.' Ruth had been ten years barren in her first marriage (1:4-5). Now the LORD opens her womb. The text explicitly attributes conception to divine agency, joining Ruth to the matriarchs Sarah, Rebekah, Rachel, and Hannah, all of whom conceived by sovereign grace to advance redemptive history. Election operates at the level of biology, not merely sociology. The Lord who 'gives conception' is the same Lord who gives the new birth (John 1:13), and both are matters of sovereign grace.
Ruth.4.14: The women of Bethlehem bless the LORD 'which hath not left thee this day without a kinsman (go'el).' Strikingly, the title go'el is here applied not to Boaz but to the newborn child, Obed. The redemption secured by Boaz finds its forward extension in his offspring, anticipating that the ultimate Redeemer will come through this very line. Israel's hope is not in a single redeemer but in the covenant Seed (Gen. 3:15) who passes through generations until the appointed Christ appears. The cry, 'that his name may be famous in Israel,' is fulfilled supremely in Jesus.
Ruth.4.15: The women say of Obed, 'he shall be unto thee a restorer of thy life, and a nourisher of thine old age.' The Hebrew 'restorer' (meshiv nefesh) is the same idiom Psalm 23:3 uses of YHWH the Shepherd: 'he restoreth my soul.' Naomi, who returned 'empty' (1:21), is now declared to have her life restored through covenant offspring. Remarkably, the women add that Ruth 'is better to thee than seven sons,' an extraordinary commendation in a patrilineal culture. Covenant grace overturns natural expectations: a Moabitess outshines seven sons because she has been incorporated into the covenant by faith.
Ruth.4.17: The naming of the child by the neighbors and the genealogical note 'he is the father of Jesse, the father of David' is the narrative climax. The whole book has been driving toward this Davidic conclusion. The inclusion of a Moabitess in the messianic line subverts ethnic pride and anticipates the gospel's reach to the nations. Matthew 1:5 will pick up this thread, listing Ruth among the four women in Christ's genealogy, each marking a moment of grace breaking ethnic and moral boundaries. Covenant theology sees here the unbroken line from Abraham's promise to David's throne to Christ's eternal kingdom.
Ruth.4.18: The genealogy from Pharez to David that closes the book is no appendix but the theological key to the whole narrative. Ten generations are listed, with Boaz in the seventh place, the position of completion, and David in the tenth, the position of fullness. The book of Ruth is ultimately not about Ruth or Boaz but about the God who weaves a covenantal lineage through famines and harvests, deaths and births, foreigners and natives, to bring forth the King. Matthew 1 will extend this same line to Christ, the true Kinsman-Redeemer who buys His bride with His own blood, raises up a name that shall never be cut off, and reigns forever upon David's throne.
Song.1.2: The book opens not with introduction but with longing: 'Let him kiss me with the kisses of his mouth.' The bride begins in the third person and slips into direct address, the very grammar of desire. Reformed expositors from Bernard to Owen heard here the soul's cry for Christ's own communion, not merely the gifts of grace but the Giver. The 'kiss' is covenantal intimacy, the seal of love between Bridegroom and bride. That his love is 'better than wine' subordinates every earthly comfort to fellowship with Christ. The Church's worship begins where this verse begins: in dissatisfaction with mediated tokens and hunger for the immediate presence of the Lord himself.
Song.1.3: The 'savour of thy good ointments' and the 'name' poured forth as ointment together teach that Christ's person and his renown are inseparable. His name is fragrant because his work is finished; the gospel diffuses the aroma of his obedience. 'Therefore do the virgins love thee'—those espoused to him by sovereign grace are drawn by what they smell of him in the preached word. Reformed theology insists that affection follows revelation: the virgins love because the name has been made known. Where Christ is truly named, hearts are wooed. The verse anticipates 2 Corinthians 2:14-16: the savour of Christ is life to those being saved.
Song.1.4: 'Draw me, we will run after thee.' The order is monergistic: drawing precedes running, grace precedes obedience. John 6:44 stands behind this confession. Yet the drawn soul is not passive afterward—she runs, and others run with her, for grace is communal as well as personal. The 'king' has 'brought me into his chambers,' a foretaste of the marriage supper. Reformed piety has long meditated here on the secret place of fellowship into which the King admits his bride. We are glad and rejoice in him, not merely in his benefits. The remembrance of his love, more than wine, governs the whole verse: communion fuels mission.
Song.1.5: 'I am black, but comely.' The bride confesses both her sin-burned condition under the sun of affliction and the loveliness imparted to her in the Beloved. This is the paradox of the justified believer—simul iustus et peccator. Reformed expositors have read the daughters of Jerusalem as nominal professors who judge by appearance, while the bride looks to the imputed comeliness she possesses in her Husband. The tents of Kedar speak of pilgrim weariness; the curtains of Solomon, of royal beauty. The Church on earth bears both marks at once. She does not deny her blackness, but she will not let it eclipse the comeliness given her in Christ.
Song.1.7: 'Tell me, O thou whom my soul loveth, where thou feedest.' The bride seeks the Shepherd in the place of his appointed feeding. She refuses to wander 'as one that turneth aside by the flocks of thy companions'—a settled refusal of false shepherds and disordered worship. Reformed ecclesiology has heard here the soul's resolve to find Christ where he has promised to be: word, sacrament, and the company of his under-shepherds. The 'noon' is the hour of rest and danger alike; the bride will not be content with anything less than the Shepherd himself. Seeking Christ is not vague; it follows the means he has ordained.
Song.1.12: 'While the king sitteth at his table, my spikenard sendeth forth the smell thereof.' Communion with the enthroned King draws fragrance out of the bride. The order is pointed: he sits, she pours. Worship is responsive, not initiatory. The spikenard anticipates Mary of Bethany (John 12), whose extravagant devotion the Spirit had long since rehearsed in this verse. Reformed devotion has always insisted that the affections are not optional ornaments but the heart of true religion. Where the King is enthroned in the conscience, costly love is poured out without calculation. The table is his; the fragrance is hers; the glory is wholly his.
Song.2.1: 'I am the rose of Sharon, and the lily of the valleys.' Though older readers sometimes assigned these words to Christ, the Hebrew flow gives them to the bride, who modestly compares herself to common wildflowers of the plain. She is one among many, unremarkable in herself. The point is sharpened by the Beloved's reply in verse 2: among the daughters she is a lily among thorns. Grace finds the bride lowly and exalts her by the King's regard. Reformed exposition draws the line clearly: the Church's beauty is alien, derived, and reflective. She is a wildflower until the King names her, and then she is incomparable in his eyes.
Song.2.3: 'As the apple tree among the trees of the wood, so is my beloved among the sons.' The bride answers his praise with hers, exalting Christ above all rivals. The 'sons' are the best the world offers; he surpasses them all. To sit under his shadow is to find shelter from wrath; to taste his fruit is to feed on his finished work. Reformed expositors from Sibbes onward dwelt on this verse as a portrait of resting in Christ's mediation. The bride does not labor to make the tree bear; she sits, she tastes, she enjoys. Faith is fundamentally receptive, and the sweet fruit is the imputed righteousness and tender benefits of the Saviour.
Song.2.7: 'I charge you, O ye daughters of Jerusalem... that ye stir not up, nor awake my love, till he please.' This refrain (also 3:5; 8:4) protects the sovereignty of love's timing. Communion with Christ is not manufactured by spiritual technique; it is given when the Beloved pleases. Reformed piety here meets a sober word against forcing experiences. The bride enjoys present rest and refuses to disturb it by anxious seeking. The 'roes and hinds of the field' invoke the gentleness fitting to love's mysteries. The Church learns to wait on the Spirit, who blows where he wills, and to receive seasons of nearness and absence from the hand of a wise Husband.
Song.2.15: 'Take us the foxes, the little foxes, that spoil the vines.' Small sins, neglected, devour tender grace. The vines are 'in blossom'—the work of love is fragile and growing. Reformed sanctification has long pressed the doctrine of mortification from such verses: the believer must hunt the small foxes—lingering bitterness, secret pride, undisciplined speech—before they ravage the vineyard. The plural 'us' is corporate. The Church together watches over the flowering communion of her members. Owen's counsel echoes here: be killing sin or it will be killing you. The little foxes never stay little; what is not crucified will crucify the joy of fellowship with Christ.
Song.3.1: 'By night on my bed I sought him whom my soul loveth: I sought him, but I found him not.' The bride awakens to absence. Reformed experience has always reckoned with seasons of withdrawal—when the Beloved hides his face to deepen desire. She seeks where she lies, in the place of ease, and does not find him. The lesson is plain: Christ is not always met on the pillow of complacency. The verse rebukes settled comfort and pushes the soul into action (verse 2). The dark night is not absence of grace but a gracious absence, calculated to draw the bride to her feet. Hidden love is still love; sought love is true love.
Song.3.2: 'I will rise now... I will seek him whom my soul loveth.' The bride moves from bed to broadways, from privacy to public seeking. Yet still she does not find him in the streets. Reformed expositors note that even right seeking does not coerce the Beloved's appearing. The verse warns against thinking that activity itself secures presence. The 'watchmen' (v.3) ultimately give no help; the answer comes only when she passes them. Christ is sometimes sought beyond the ordinary helps before he is found. Yet the seeking itself is grace, the work of the Spirit who plants in the heart what only Christ's appearing can satisfy.
Song.3.4: 'I held him, and would not let him go, until I had brought him into my mother's house.' Found at last, she will not let him go. The grip of faith follows the visit of grace. She brings him 'home,' to the place of communal life and worship. Reformed piety has heard here the believer's longing to keep Christ central not merely in private experience but in the household of faith. Jacob's wrestling stands behind this clause—'I will not let thee go, except thou bless me.' The bride has learned the holy violence of love: to hold Christ when he draws near, and to bring his presence into every room of life.
Song.3.11: 'Behold king Solomon with the crown wherewith his mother crowned him in the day of his espousals.' The wedding procession crowns the King. Reformed typology hears the greater Solomon, crowned by his people on the day of his joy. Hebrews 2:9 sees Jesus 'crowned with glory and honour' for the suffering of death; that crowning is also our espousal. The 'day of his espousals' and 'the day of the gladness of his heart' is the day the Church becomes his. The wedding of the Lamb is the joy set before him. Calvin's instinct: Christ's marriage to his Church is not afterthought but the climactic delight of redemption.
Song.4.1: 'Behold, thou art fair, my love; behold, thou art fair.' The doubled 'behold' compels attention. The Bridegroom looks on his bride and pronounces her beautiful. This is justification's voice: the verdict of beauty resting on her not because of what she is in herself but because of how he sees her in covenant. Reformed exposition guards against sentimentality here: the praise is not flattery but truth, because he has clothed her in his own loveliness. The seven-fold description that follows (vv.1-5) is total—every feature renamed in love. Christ does not see his Church piece by piece but whole, and whole he calls her fair.
Song.4.7: 'Thou art all fair, my love; there is no spot in thee.' The summary verdict echoes Ephesians 5:27: a Church 'not having spot, or wrinkle, or any such thing.' This is not the bride's present empirical condition but her standing in the Beloved. Reformed theology calls this the imputed righteousness of Christ, by which the believer is reckoned spotless before the Father. The verse is a death-blow to every attempt to find peace by inspection of self. The bride looks not to her stains but to his pronouncement. To hear this verse spoken over the conscience by Christ himself is the marrow of evangelical assurance.
Song.4.8: 'Come with me from Lebanon, my spouse... from the lions' dens, from the mountains of the leopards.' The Bridegroom calls his bride out of dangerous places to himself. The pilgrim Church lives in lion country; the call is not to settle but to come.
