Heb.4.14: Since we have a great high priest who has passed through the heavens, Jesus the Son of God, let us hold fast our confession. His priesthood surpasses Aaron's because He entered not an earthly tabernacle but the heavenly sanctuary itself. His deity and humanity together qualify Him for this office. The exhortation to hold fast assumes perseverance of the saints, grounded not in our grip but in His. The pastoral application is steadfastness in confession because of the surpassing greatness of our priest.
Heb.5.9: Being made perfect, Christ became the source of eternal salvation to all who obey Him. His perfection is not moral improvement but vocational completion through suffering, fitting Him as covenant mediator. The salvation He authors is eternal, befitting His eternal priesthood. Obedience here is the obedience of faith, the response that necessarily flows from saving union with Christ. Reformed soteriology sees this obedience not as a meritorious condition but as the evidence and fruit of regeneration in those effectually called.
Heb.6.19: We have this hope as an anchor of the soul, sure and steadfast, entering into the inner place behind the curtain. The metaphor shifts heavenward; our anchor is cast not below but above, in the heavenly sanctuary where Jesus has gone as forerunner. Our security rests in His location, not in our circumstances. The torn veil grants access, and Christ's presence guarantees ours. Reformed assurance is grounded objectively in Christ's heavenly priesthood, not subjectively in fluctuating feelings or fragile resolve.
Heb.7.22: Jesus has become the guarantor of a better covenant. Egguos, guarantor, denotes one who pledges Himself as security for the covenant's fulfillment. Christ guarantees both God's promises to us and our covenant fidelity through His mediating work. The covenant is better because it rests on better promises, a better priest, and a better sacrifice. Unlike the old covenant, which depended on the people's obedience, the new covenant's success is secured in Christ Himself, ensuring its unbreakable efficacy for the elect.
Heb.7.27: Unlike the Levitical priests who needed daily sacrifices for their own sins and the people's, Christ has no such need, having offered Himself once for all. The hapax of Calvary terminates the sacrificial system. His sinlessness eliminates the need for personal atonement; His infinite worth eliminates the need for repetition. The Roman mass, claiming to re-present Christ's sacrifice, contradicts the once-for-all sufficiency proclaimed here. Reformed worship rests in the finality of the cross, proclaimed but never repeated.
Heb.8.6: Christ has obtained a ministry as much more excellent than the old as the covenant He mediates is better, since it is enacted on better promises. The new covenant's superiority lies in its unconditional, effectual promises: God will write His law on hearts, forgive sins decisively, and grant universal knowledge of Himself among His people. Christ's mediation actualizes these promises rather than merely offering them. Reformed covenant theology sees continuity in the covenant of grace yet decisive escalation in new covenant administration.
Heb.9.12: He entered once for all into the holy places, not by means of the blood of goats and calves but by means of His own blood, thus securing an eternal redemption. The aorist 'having obtained' depicts a completed achievement. Redemption is not made possible but actually secured. Particular redemption is implicit: Christ definitely accomplished what He intended, eternally redeeming His people. The heavenly sanctuary, not Jerusalem's, was His destination, and His blood, not animal substitutes, was His offering.
Heb.9.27: It is appointed for man to die once, and after that comes judgment. Death is divinely appointed, not random; it is singular, ruling out reincarnation; it is followed by judgment, ruling out annihilation or universalism upon death. This sober anthropology grounds the urgency of the gospel. The parallel with Christ's once-for-all death frames atonement against the backdrop of human mortality and accountability. Reformed eschatology affirms the immediate intermediate state and the certain final reckoning before the throne.
Heb.10.10: By God's will we have been sanctified through the offering of the body of Jesus Christ once for all. Sanctification here is positional, the definitive setting apart of God's people through the cross. The perfect tense 'have been sanctified' indicates an accomplished, abiding status. The Father's will, the Son's offering, and the Spirit's application converge in our consecration. Reformed theology distinguishes definitive sanctification, accomplished at the cross and applied at conversion, from progressive sanctification, the lifelong pursuit of holiness.
Heb.10.14: By a single offering He has perfected for all time those who are being sanctified. Christ's one sacrifice has eternally perfected the elect; their justification is complete, irrevocable, and final. Yet they are simultaneously being sanctified, progressing in actual holiness. This verse holds together the indicative of accomplished perfection and the imperative of ongoing transformation. Reformed soteriology rejoices that no further sacrifice is needed, and no genuine believer can ever be lost, for the perfecting work is Christ's, not ours.
Heb.10.19: Therefore, brothers, since we have confidence to enter the holy places by the blood of Jesus, we approach what was once forbidden. The torn veil signifies access; the high priest's once-yearly entry is now every believer's daily privilege. Confidence rests not on our worthiness but on Jesus' blood. The holy places are heavenly realities, accessed by faith in prayer and ultimately in glory. Reformed worship treasures this priesthood of all believers, the immediate access secured by the Mediator's accomplished work.
Heb.10.31: It is a fearful thing to fall into the hands of the living God. Following warnings against apostasy, this declaration confronts presumption with the terror of divine holiness. The same hands that hold believers safely become consuming fire to those who trample the Son. God's living character means His justice is active, not abstract. Reformed theology balances this fear with the comfort of adoption, recognizing that filial fear motivates obedience while servile fear marks the unregenerate facing inevitable judgment.
Acts.2.21: Peter cites Joel to declare that salvation is by calling upon the Lord, an open invitation grounded in sovereign mercy. The Lord here is Yahweh, yet Peter applies the title to Jesus, identifying the exalted Christ with the covenant God of Israel. Calling presupposes faith awakened by effectual grace, not a mere ceremonial appeal. The promise is universal in scope, embracing Jew and Gentile alike, yet particular in application to those whom God draws. This verse anchors evangelistic preaching in divine sovereignty and human responsibility.
Acts.5.29: The apostles' principled defiance establishes a vital ethic: when human authority commands what God forbids, allegiance to the divine King overrides civil compliance. This is not anarchic rebellion but ordered submission to the higher magistrate, God Himself. The apostles do not flee or curse their judges; they accept consequences while refusing to silence the gospel. Reformed political theology has long recognized this principle of conscientious resistance. Magistrates are God's servants, but their authority is derivative and limited. Christ's lordship transcends every earthly office and demands ultimate obedience.
Acts.7.59: Stephen's death mirrors his Lord's, both in committing his spirit and in praying forgiveness for executioners. Yet notably, Stephen prays to Jesus, addressing the exalted Christ as the receiver of departed souls, an implicit confession of His full deity. This is a remarkable testimony to the immediate intermediate state: the believer's spirit goes consciously to Christ at death. Stephen's serenity under stoning displays the Spirit's sustaining grace. The first martyr models how union with the crucified Lord transforms even violent death into worship and triumphant witness.
Acts.10.43: Peter declares the unanimous testimony of the prophets: everyone believing in Christ receives forgiveness through His name. Faith is the sole instrument of justification, not ethnic privilege or ceremonial observance. The phrase through His name highlights Christ's mediatorial work as the ground of pardon. Notice the universal scope, everyone, which prepares for the Spirit's outpouring upon Cornelius's household. Old Testament Scripture and apostolic preaching converge on this truth: the gospel is one across redemptive history, with Christ as its singular substance. Reformed theology rightly insists on sola fide here.
Acts.13.39: Paul contrasts the Mosaic law's inability to justify with Christ's effective justification through faith. The law revealed sin and demanded obedience but could not free anyone from condemnation. In Christ, every believer is justified from all things, a comprehensive forensic declaration. This anticipates the doctrinal precision of Romans and Galatians. Justification is not partial or progressive but total, grounded in Christ's perfect righteousness imputed. The law's role is preparatory and pedagogical; the gospel's role is consummating and liberating. Reformed soteriology rightly grounds assurance in this completed work.
Acts.13.48: Luke's editorial comment is one of Scripture's clearest statements of divine election: as many as were appointed to eternal life believed. Faith follows appointment, not the reverse. The Greek tetagmenoi indicates a prior divine ordering. Belief is the fruit of God's electing love, manifest in time. This text powerfully refutes synergistic accounts of conversion. Reformed theology celebrates this verse as a window into the secret counsel of God's saving purpose. The Gentiles' joy springs from grace they neither earned nor initiated, and their believing reveals the hidden decree.
Acts.17.27: Paul declares that God ordained nations and times so that humanity might seek and find Him, though He is not far from any. This is general revelation's appointed end, drawing creatures toward their Creator. Yet fallen humanity suppresses this knowledge, exchanging truth for idols. The seeking described here proves inadequate apart from special revelation in Christ. God's nearness underscores His universal accessibility through gospel proclamation. Reformed theology distinguishes general from special revelation while acknowledging that providence orders circumstances toward the gospel's effectual reach to elect souls scattered among the nations.
Acts.20.21: Paul summarizes his apostolic message as testifying to both Jews and Greeks of repentance toward God and faith in our Lord Jesus Christ. These two graces are inseparable yet distinct: repentance turns from sin and self toward God; faith embraces Christ as Savior and Lord. Both are gifts of sovereign grace, wrought by the Spirit through the Word. Notice the universal audience: ethnic distinctions yield to gospel necessity. Reformed catechetical tradition rightly emphasizes this dual response. Authentic conversion always involves both turning and trusting, never one without the other.
Acts.20.27: Paul declares his pastoral integrity: he did not shrink from declaring the whole counsel of God. This is the gold standard for faithful ministry. No doctrine is to be suppressed for popularity or comfort, including hard teachings on sin, judgment, election, and holiness. The whole counsel embraces the entire scope of biblical revelation in its systematic coherence. Reformed preaching aspires to this comprehensive fidelity, expounding the canon's full theological breadth. Pastors who selectively preach palatable truths bear bloodguilt; those who declare the whole counsel deliver their souls in faithful stewardship.
Acts.20.32: Paul commends the elders to God and to the word of His grace, which is able to build up and give an inheritance among all who are sanctified. Notice the means: God works through His Word, not apart from it. Sanctification and inheritance are linked; those being made holy are heirs. The word of His grace evangelizes and edifies; the gospel sustains believers throughout their pilgrimage. Reformed theology rightly insists on the Word's centrality in both conversion and growth. Pastors and saints alike depend wholly upon Scripture's transforming power.
1Cor.2.2: Paul deliberately narrowed his apostolic preaching to Christ crucified, refusing to compete with Corinthian rhetoricians on their own terms. This was no anti-intellectualism but principled focus, for the cross alone reveals God's saving purpose. Reformed preaching follows this apostolic pattern, refusing to substitute moralism, philosophy, or therapeutic counsel for the proclamation of the slain and risen Lord. Where Christ crucified is faithfully preached, the Spirit attends the word with power. Every other emphasis ultimately diminishes the glory of redemption accomplished at Calvary.
1Cor.5.7: Christ our Passover has been sacrificed, fulfilling the typology of the Exodus lamb whose blood averted divine wrath. Paul commands the church to purge old leaven, that they might be a new lump corresponding to their actual identity in Christ. The indicative grounds the imperative: because redemption is accomplished, holiness must follow. Reformed theology rightly perceives Christ as the substance of all Old Testament shadows. The believer's separation from sin flows not from legalistic striving but from gratitude for the once-for-all sacrifice already offered.
1Cor.6.20: You were bought with a price; therefore glorify God in your body. The redemption Christ accomplished was not abstract but commercial, a real payment securing real ownership. Believers belong wholly to their Redeemer, body and spirit. This purchase grounds Christian ethics in objective accomplishment rather than subjective feeling. The price was the precious blood of Christ, and such a transaction demands corresponding consecration. Reformed theology emphasizes that redemption was definite and effectual, securing not merely the possibility but the actuality of holy living through the Spirit's application.
1Cor.11.24: This is My body, which is broken for you; do this in remembrance of Me. The Lord's Supper proclaims a substitutionary sacrifice, the body given on behalf of His people. Reformed theology rejects both transubstantiation and bare memorialism, confessing instead a true spiritual feeding upon Christ by faith. The remembrance is not mere mental recall but covenantal participation in the benefits of redemption. The broken body signifies the violent cost of atonement, while the giving language emphasizes the personal directedness of Christ's love toward His covenant people.
1Cor.11.26: As often as you eat this bread and drink this cup, you proclaim the Lord's death till He comes. The Supper is fundamentally proclamation, a visible sermon declaring the gospel in elemental form. It looks backward to the cross, upward to the ascended Christ, and forward to His return. This eschatological dimension anchors the church's hope in the certain consummation. Reformed worship treasures the sacrament as a means of grace, where Christ truly meets His people, strengthening faith and renewing covenant fellowship until the marriage supper of the Lamb.
1Cor.12.13: By one Spirit we were all baptized into one body, whether Jews or Greeks, slaves or free. This Spirit-baptism effects vital union with Christ and incorporation into His body, transcending every ethnic, social, and economic barrier. The unity of the church is not manufactured by human effort but constituted by divine action. Reformed ecclesiology understands this baptism as the work of regeneration applied to the elect, signified outwardly by water baptism. The diversity of members serves the unity of the whole, each gifted by the same Spirit for the common good.
1Cor.13.7: Love bears all things, believes all things, hopes all things, endures all things. This fourfold description portrays love as resilient, trusting, expectant, and steadfast. Such love is not natural sentiment but supernatural fruit, possible only through the Spirit's indwelling. It mirrors the covenant love of God Himself, who bore our reproach and endured the cross. Reformed sanctification cultivates this love as evidence of regeneration, distinguishing genuine faith from spurious profession. Without such love, all gifts and sacrifices count for nothing in the divine economy of grace.
1Cor.13.12: Now we see in a mirror dimly, but then face to face. Present knowledge is partial and mediated, but eschatological vision will be direct and complete. Paul does not denigrate present revelation but acknowledges its proportionate character given our pilgrim condition. The beatific vision awaits glorification, when faith yields to sight. Reformed theology guards against both rationalistic overreach and mystical pretensions, contenting itself with what God has revealed in Scripture while longing for the consummation when we shall know even as we are known.
1Cor.15.3: Christ died for our sins according to the Scriptures. Paul delivers this as matter of first importance, the apostolic deposit received and transmitted. The phrase contains a complete soteriology: the historical death, its substitutionary purpose, and its scriptural foundation. Reformed theology insists upon the penal substitutionary character of the atonement, grounded in covenant fulfillment of Old Testament prophecy and typology. The objective accomplishment of redemption stands prior to any subjective application, and the gospel rests upon the unchanging facts of what Christ has actually done.
1Cor.15.10: By the grace of God I am what I am, and His grace toward me was not in vain; I labored more abundantly than they all, yet not I, but the grace of God which was with me. Paul navigates the paradox of divine sovereignty and human responsibility with consummate skill. Grace is the source, but grace produces real labor in the recipient. Reformed theology embraces both truths without compromise, rejecting the sluggard's appeal to sovereignty as well as the moralist's confidence in flesh. Grace empowers genuine effort.
1Cor.15.20: Now Christ is risen from the dead, and has become the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep. The resurrection is not merely Christ's vindication but the firstfruits of a coming harvest. His bodily rising guarantees the bodily rising of all united to Him. Reformed eschatology takes the firstfruits imagery seriously: the same Spirit who raised Jesus will quicken our mortal bodies. The resurrection is therefore not peripheral but central to Christian hope, securing the redemption of creation itself and the consummation of the covenant of grace.
1Cor.15.22: As in Adam all die, even so in Christ all shall be made alive. Paul's federal theology emerges with stark clarity: humanity stands either in Adam or in Christ, with consequences flowing from the representative head. The covenant of works was broken in Adam, plunging his posterity into death. The covenant of grace, fulfilled in the second Adam, bestows life upon all united to Him. This federal framework grounds Reformed soteriology, vindicating both the justice of imputed guilt and the wonder of imputed righteousness through Christ.
1Cor.15.26: The last enemy that will be destroyed is death. Death entered through sin and reigns as a hostile usurper, but Christ has already disarmed it through His own death and resurrection. Its final abolition awaits the consummation, when the dead are raised incorruptible. Reformed eschatology refuses to romanticize death as a friend; it remains an enemy, the wages of sin. Yet its defeat is certain, sealed by the empty tomb. The believer faces death not with stoic resignation but with confident expectation of its ultimate destruction.
1Cor.15.45: The first man Adam became a living being; the last Adam became a life-giving spirit. Paul's two-Adam theology distinguishes the original creation from the eschatological new creation. Adam received life; Christ bestows it. This Christological climax of redemptive history reveals that Jesus is not merely Adam restored but Adam transcended. Reformed theology cherishes this insight: the goal of redemption is not return to Eden but advance to glory. The risen Christ inaugurates a new humanity, sharing His Spirit-empowered, indestructible life with all His covenant people.
1Cor.15.55: O death, where is your sting? O grave, where is your victory? Paul taunts the vanquished foe with prophetic boldness drawn from Hosea. The sting of death is sin, and the strength of sin is the law, but Christ has fulfilled the law and borne sin's penalty. What once terrorized now lies disarmed. Reformed pastoral theology equips believers with this triumphant confidence at every graveside. Death cannot finally harm those whom Christ has redeemed; it has been transformed into the doorway to His unmediated presence.
1Cor.15.57: But thanks be to God, who gives us the victory through our Lord Jesus Christ. The victory is given, not earned; received, not achieved. This passive reception characterizes the entire economy of grace. Christ has conquered sin, death, and hell; believers participate in His triumph by union with Him. Reformed worship erupts in doxology at this point, recognizing that every spiritual blessing flows from the accomplished work of the Mediator. Thanksgiving is the only fitting response to such sovereign, gratuitous, and complete deliverance bestowed upon undeserving sinners.
Titus.1.1: Paul opens by identifying himself as a bondservant of God and apostle of Jesus Christ, framing his ministry as serving the faith of God's elect. This pairing of servanthood and apostolic authority is no contradiction; true authority in Christ flows from submission. The mention of election grounds the gospel in God's sovereign purpose, not human initiative. The knowledge of truth that accords with godliness reminds us that doctrine and life are inseparable, sound theology always producing holy practice.
Titus.1.6: An elder must be above reproach, the husband of one wife, with believing and well-ordered children. This is not sinless perfection but a settled reputation of integrity. Marital faithfulness demonstrates covenant fidelity in microcosm; a man who breaks vows at home cannot be trusted with Christ's bride. Faithful children evidence that gospel truth has been pressed home in his own household first. Public ministry presupposes private faithfulness; the home is the proving ground for shepherding the flock.
Titus.1.7: As God's steward, the overseer manages what is not his own. He must not be arrogant, quick-tempered, drunken, violent, or greedy for gain. These prohibitions strike at the self-exalting passions that destroy ministry. A steward holds entrusted goods accountable to his Master, never to be leveraged for personal advantage. Reformed ecclesiology guards against tyranny by demanding character that mirrors Christ's gentleness. The man who cannot govern his own appetites cannot be entrusted with governing God's household.
Titus.1.13: Paul commands sharp rebuke that the Cretans may be sound in the faith. Love does not coddle error but confronts it, for the goal is restoration to doctrinal health. The verb 'sound' is medical; false teaching is disease, and faithful pastors are physicians who lance the wound. Reformed pastoral care refuses sentimental tolerance of damning error. Such severity is not cruelty but compassion, refusing to let souls drift into ruin under the cover of polite silence.
Titus.2.1: Paul charges Titus to teach what accords with sound doctrine, contrasting him sharply with the empty talkers of chapter one. Sound doctrine is not abstract speculation but life-shaping truth applied to every station. The verb 'speak' implies ongoing, persistent instruction. Faithful ministry is fundamentally a teaching ministry, grounded in revealed truth. Reformed pulpits prize this above eloquence, novelty, or relevance. Where sound doctrine is spoken, godly lives follow; where it is silenced, the church withers into religious sentimentalism.
Titus.2.2: Older men are to be sober-minded, dignified, self-controlled, sound in faith, love, and steadfastness. Age does not automatically confer maturity; godly seasoning is cultivated through years of disciplined walking with Christ. The triad of faith, love, and steadfastness echoes the cardinal Christian graces, now matured by trial. These men set the tone of congregational life by their gravity and stability. The church needs fathers whose endurance bears witness that the gospel sustains through every season.
Titus.2.7: Titus himself must show himself a model of good works, with integrity and dignity in teaching. The minister's life either authenticates or undermines his message. Doctrine and demeanor are inseparable; a corrupt teacher cannot proclaim incorruptible truth credibly. Reformed ministry recognizes that the messenger embodies the message in some measure. Integrity here means the teaching is unmixed with personal motive or worldly compromise. Younger pastors particularly are reminded that authority is established not by title but by visible Christlikeness.
Titus.2.10: Bondservants are to show all good faith, that they may adorn the doctrine of God our Savior. Even slaves, the lowest social rung, can beautify the gospel by faithful service. The metaphor is striking: doctrine is a precious jewel, and obedient lives are the setting that displays its glory. No vocation is too humble to glorify God. Reformed teaching on vocation finds rich expression here, dignifying ordinary labor as a stage for adorning Christ before a watching world.
Titus.2.15: Titus is to declare these things, exhort, and rebuke with all authority, letting no one disregard him. Pastoral authority is not personal but derivative, flowing from the apostolic word being faithfully proclaimed. The threefold charge—teach, encourage, rebuke—encompasses the full ministry of the word. Cultural pressure to soften rebuke must be resisted. Where Scripture is preached, the minister speaks with Christ's borrowed authority. Congregations honor Christ by receiving such ministry rather than dismissing it as merely human opinion.
Titus.3.1: Believers must submit to rulers and authorities, be obedient, and ready for every good work. The Christian is not a revolutionary but a model citizen, recognizing that civil order reflects God's common grace. Submission has limits when authorities command sin, but the default posture is honor. Reformed political theology distinguishes the kingdoms while affirming both. Readiness for every good work shows that genuine piety overflows into public usefulness, contradicting any caricature of Christianity as withdrawn or socially indifferent.
Titus.3.8: Paul insists that those who have believed in God devote themselves to good works, which are excellent and profitable for people. Faith and works are distinguished but inseparable; justification produces sanctification. The trustworthy saying preceding this verse grounds works in grace, not the reverse. Good works benefit neighbors and adorn the gospel. Reformed teaching guards both flanks: against legalism that grounds salvation in works, and against antinomianism that severs salvation from them. Believers are to be intentional, not accidental, in doing good.
Titus.3.10: A divisive person is to be warned once, then twice, and then have nothing more to do with him. Church purity requires decisive action against persistent factionalism. Patience is shown through repeated warnings, but patience has limits when souls are endangered. Reformed church discipline follows this pattern, seeking restoration but ultimately protecting the flock. Heresy and faction are not minor offenses; they tear Christ's body. The shepherd who refuses to act in love for the wolf betrays love for the sheep.
Titus.3.14: Paul concludes urging believers to devote themselves to good works, meeting urgent needs, that they may not be unfruitful. The final word echoes the chapter's refrain: faith bears visible fruit. 'Urgent needs' grounds Christian charity in real human suffering, not abstract benevolence. Unfruitfulness is incompatible with genuine faith, as Christ's parables repeatedly warn. Reformed piety has always emphasized practical mercy alongside doctrinal precision. The letter closes pressing home that sound doctrine and active love together display the transforming power of saving grace.
Hag.1.7: Thus saith the LORD of hosts: Consider your ways. The summons to consider is repeated, for self-examination is the first step toward reformation. The covenant people had sown much and brought in little; their pockets had holes. God was withholding blessing to provoke reflection. The Reformed conscience knows this rhythm: providential frustration is often paternal discipline calling us back to first things. The remedy is not greater striving in misordered pursuits but a sober reckoning before the Lord and a return to obedience.
